Current benchmarks rarely test whether models grasp the step-by-step processes and physical dependencies required to actually build these artifacts, a capability essential for automating design-to-construction pipelines. To address this, we introduce DreamHouse, a novel benchmark for physical generative reasoning: the capacity to synthesize artifacts that concurrently satisfy geometric, structural, constructability, and code-compliance constraints. We ground this benchmark in residential timber-frame construction, a domain with fully codified engineering standards and objectively verifiable correctness. We curate over 26,000 structures spanning 13 architectural styles, ach verified to construction-document standards (LOD 350) and develop a deterministic 10-test structural validation framework. Unlike static benchmarks that assess only final outputs, DreamHouse supports iterative agentic interaction. Models observe intermediate build states, generate construction actions, and receive structured environmental feedback, enabling a fine-grained evaluation of planning, structural reasoning, and self-correction. Extensive experiments with state-of-the-art VLMs reveal substantial capability gaps that are largely invisible on existing leaderboards. These findings establish physical validity as a critical evaluation axis orthogonal to visual realism, highlighting physical generative reasoning as a distinct and underdeveloped frontier in multimodal intelligence. Available at https://luluyuyuyang.github.io/dreamhouse

AI.tldr: DreamHouse is a benchmark for physical generative reasoning in vision-language models, focusing on constructing artifacts that satisfy geometric, structural, constructability, and code-compliance constraints in residential timber-frame construction.
AI.motivation: Current VLM benchmarks emphasize perceptual realism but largely ignore step-by-step construction reasoning and compliance with structural/code constraints necessary for design-to-construction pipelines. The domain is grounded in codified standards (LOD 350) to enable objective verification.
AI.method: Curate over 26,000 timber-frame structures across 13 architectural styles that are verified to construction-document standards (LOD 350). Develop a deterministic 10-test structural validation framework. Enable iterative agentic interaction where models observe intermediate build states, propose construction actions, and receive feedback, allowing evaluation of planning, structural reasoning, and self-correction.
AI.result: Experiments show substantial capability gaps for state-of-the-art VLMs on physical validity that are not captured by existing leaderboards focused on visual realism.
AI.conclusion: Physical generative reasoning is a distinct, underdeveloped frontier in multimodal intelligence. DreamHouse provides a benchmark to evaluate planning, structural reasoning, and code-compliance in addition to perceptual quality, with potential to advance automated design-to-construction workflows.

title: $π$, But Make It Fly: Physics-Guided Transfer of VLA Models to Aerial Manipulation
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models such as $π_0$ have demonstrated remarkable generalization across diverse fixed-base manipulators. However, transferring these foundation models to aerial platforms remains an open challenge due to the fundamental mismatch between the quasi-static dynamics of fixed-base arms and the underactuated, highly dynamic nature of flight. In this work, we introduce AirVLA, a system that investigates the transferability of manipulation-pretrained VLAs to aerial pick-and-place tasks. We find that while visual representations transfer effectively, the specific control dynamics required for flight do not. To bridge this "dynamics gap" without retraining the foundation model, we introduce a Payload-Aware Guidance mechanism that injects payload constraints directly into the policy's flow-matching sampling process. To overcome data scarcity, we further utilize a Gaussian Splatting pipeline to synthesize navigation training data. We evaluate our method through a cumulative 460 real-world experiments which demonstrate that this synthetic data is a key enabler of performance, unlocking 100% success in navigation tasks where directly fine-tuning on teleoperation data alone attains 81% success. Our inference-time intervention, Payload-Aware Guidance, increases real-world pick-and-place task success from 23% to 50%. Finally, we evaluate the model on a long-horizon compositional task, achieving a 62% overall success rate. These results suggest that pre-trained manipulation VLAs, with appropriate data augmentation and physics-informed guidance, can transfer to aerial manipulation and navigation, as well as the composition of these tasks.

AI.tldr: AirVLA investigates transferring vision-language-action (VLA) models to aerial manipulation, finding visual features transfer well but control dynamics do not. The paper proposes Payload-Aware Guidance to inject payload constraints into flow-matching sampling, and uses Gaussian Splatting to synthesize navigation data. With 460 real-world tests, synthetic data significantly boosts navigation success (up to 100% vs 81% with teleop data), Payload-Aware Guidance raises pick-and-place success from 23% to 50%, and long-horizon tasks reach 62% overall. Concludes that pretrained VLA models can transfer to aerial manipulation with data augmentation and physics-informed guidance.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between quasi-static fixed-base manipulators used to train VLAs and the dynamic, underactuated nature of aerial platforms. Reuse manipulation-focused foundation models to enable aerial manipulation and navigation without extensive retraining.
AI.method: 1) Analyze transferability of manipulation-pretrained VLA models to aerial pick-and-place tasks. 2) Introduce Payload-Aware Guidance (PAG) that injects payload constraints into the policy's flow-matching sampling process, to align actions with payload requirements without retraining the base model. 3) Apply Gaussian Splatting to synthesize navigation training data to mitigate data scarcity. 4) Evaluate on real-world tasks with 460 experiments across navigation, pick-and-place, and long-horizon compositional tasks.
AI.result: Visual representations from pretrained VLAs transfer well; control dynamics for flight do not. Synthetic data via Gaussian Splatting substantially improves navigation performance, enabling 100% success in some navigation tasks where teleoperation-only data yields 81% success. Payload-Aware Guidance improves real-world pick-and-place success from 23% to 50%. Long-horizon compositional task achieves 62% overall success.
AI.conclusion: Pretrained manipulation VLAs can transfer to aerial manipulation and navigation with the aid of data augmentation and physics-informed guidance, reducing retraining requirements and enabling task composition across aerial manipulation scenarios.

title: On the Foundations of Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence
summary:
We prove that platform-deterministic inference is necessary and sufficient for
  trustworthy AI. We formalize this as the Determinism Thesis and introduce trust
  entropy to quantify the cost of non-determinism, proving that verification failure
  probability equals 1 - 2^{-H_T} exactly. We prove a Determinism-Verification
  Collapse: verification under determinism requires O(1) hash comparison; without it,
  the verifier faces an intractable membership problem. IEEE 754 floating-point
  arithmetic fundamentally violates the determinism requirement. We resolve this by
  constructing a pure integer inference engine that achieves bitwise identical output
  across ARM and x86. In 82 cross-architecture tests on models up to 6.7B parameters,
  we observe zero hash mismatches. Four geographically distributed nodes produce
  identical outputs, verified by 356 on-chain attestation transactions. Every major
  trust property of AI systems (fairness, robustness, privacy, safety, alignment)
  presupposes platform determinism. Our system, 99,000 lines of Rust deployed across
  three continents, establishes that AI trust is a question of arithmetic.

AI.tldr: Platform-deterministic inference is argued to be necessary and sufficient for trustworthy AI, via the Determinism Thesis and trust entropy; the work formalizes a relationship between non-determinism cost and verification failure, introduces a Determinism-Verification Collapse, resolves non-determinism with a pure integer inference engine achieving cross-architecture bitwise-identical output, and reports extensive cross-node and on-chain attestations—claiming trust is an arithmetic property implemented in Rust.
AI.motivation: To establish a formal, verifiable foundation for AI trust by asserting determinism as central to trust properties (fairness, robustness, privacy, safety, alignment) and to provide a practical, portable engine that preserves determinism across hardware.
AI.method: Formalize the Determinism Thesis and introduce trust entropy (H_T); prove verification failure probability equals 1−2^{−H_T}; prove a Determinism-Verification Collapse where verification under determinism reduces to constant-time hash comparisons while under non-determinism it becomes intractable; build a pure integer inference engine yielding bitwise-identical outputs across ARM and x86; conduct 82 cross-architecture tests on models up to 6.7B parameters; deploy on four geographically distributed nodes with 356 on-chain attestation transactions; implement in roughly 99,000 lines of Rust across three continents.
AI.result: Demonstrates zero hash mismatches in 82 tests; cross-architecture determinism; identical outputs across four nodes; verifiable through on-chain attestations; supports the claim that determinism enables trust properties of AI systems.
AI.conclusion: Platform determinism underpins AI trust; deterministic inference is presented as both necessary and sufficient for trustworthy AI; a practical deterministic engine is shown to implement and validate trust as an arithmetic property across architectures.

title: ThermoAct:Thermal-Aware Vision-Language-Action Models for Robotic Perception and Decision-Making
summary: In recent human-robot collaboration environments, there is a growing focus on integrating diverse sensor data beyond visual information to enable safer and more intelligent task execution. Although thermal data can be crucial for enhancing robot safety and operational efficiency, its integration has been relatively overlooked in prior research. This paper proposes a novel Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework that incorporates thermal information for robot task execution. The proposed system leverages a Vision-Language Model (VLM) as a high-level planner to interpret complex natural language commands and decompose them into simpler sub-tasks. This approach facilitates efficient data collection and robust reasoning for complex operations. Unlike conventional methods that rely solely on visual data, our approach integrates thermal information, enabling the robot to perceive physical properties and proactively ensure environmental safety. Experimental results from real-world task scenarios validate the feasibility of our proposed framework, suggesting its potential to enhance task success rates and safety compared to existing vision-based systems.

AI.tldr: A Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework augments robot task execution by integrating thermal data with a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to serve as high-level planner, improving safety and effectiveness over vision-only approaches.
AI.motivation: Current robotics perception relies heavily on vision; thermal information can reveal physical properties and hazardous conditions not captured by RGB, yet is underexplored in HRC settings.
AI.method: Introduce VLA framework combining VLM-based planning with multi-sensor perception; incorporate thermal data into perception and reasoning; use VLM to interpret natural-language instructions and decompose into subtasks; validate on real-world tasks.
AI.result: Feasibility demonstrated in real-world scenarios; reported improvements in task success and safety relative to vision-only baselines; demonstrates robust data collection and reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Thermal data integration within a VLA system enhances safety and efficiency in HRC tasks; framework shows promise and warrants further study and refinement.

title: Confidence-Based Mesh Extraction from 3D Gaussians
summary: Recently, 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) greatly accelerated mesh extraction from posed images due to its explicit representation and fast software rasterization. While the addition of geometric losses and other priors has improved the accuracy of extracted surfaces, mesh extraction remains difficult in scenes with abundant view-dependent effects. To resolve the resulting ambiguities, prior works rely on multi-view techniques, iterative mesh extraction, or large pre-trained models, sacrificing the inherent efficiency of 3DGS. In this work, we present a simple and efficient alternative by introducing a self-supervised confidence framework to 3DGS: within this framework, learnable confidence values dynamically balance photometric and geometric supervision. Extending our confidence-driven formulation, we introduce losses which penalize per-primitive color and normal variance and demonstrate their benefits to surface extraction. Finally, we complement the above with an improved appearance model, by decoupling the individual terms of the D-SSIM loss. Our final approach delivers state-of-the-art results for unbounded meshes while remaining highly efficient.

AI.tldr: Introduces a self-supervised confidence framework for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) to balance photometric and geometric supervision, adds per-primitive color/normal variance penalties, and decouples appearance terms in D-SSIM; achieves state-of-the-art unbounded mesh results while preserving efficiency.
AI.motivation: 3DGS is efficient but struggles with view-dependent effects; existing fixes (multi-view, iterative extraction, large models) reduce efficiency. Need an approach that maintains 3DGS efficiency while improving surface extraction in challenging scenes.
AI.method: Learnable confidence values dynamically balance supervision; penalties on per-primitive color and normal variance; decoupled D-SSIM appearance loss terms; improved appearance model.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance for unbounded meshes with high efficiency; robust extraction under view-dependent effects.
AI.conclusion: The confidence-driven, variance-penalized, and decoupled appearance enhancements yield robust, efficient 3D surface extraction with 3DGS, preserving its computational benefits.

title: Grokking as a Falsifiable Finite-Size Transition
summary: Grokking -- the delayed onset of generalization after early memorization -- is often described with phase-transition language, but that claim has lacked falsifiable finite-size inputs. Here we supply those inputs by treating the group order $p$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p$ as an admissible extensive variable and a held-out spectral head-tail contrast as a representation-level order parameter, then apply a condensed-matter-style diagnostic chain to coarse-grid sweeps and a dense near-critical addition audit. Binder-like crossings reveal a shared finite-size boundary, and susceptibility comparison strongly disfavors a smooth-crossover interpretation ($Δ\mathrm{AIC}=16.8$ in the near-critical audit). Phase-transition language in grokking can therefore be tested as a quantitative finite-size claim rather than invoked as analogy alone, although the transition order remains unresolved at present.

AI.tldr: Finite-size scaling evidence supports phase-transition-like grokking, via Binder-like crossings and a spectral-head-tail order parameter; smooth crossover rejected by higher ΔAIC; finite-size boundary observed, but the transition order remains unresolved.
AI.motivation: Provide falsifiable finite-size criteria for the grokking phenomenon, moving beyond analogy to a testable phase-transition claim.
AI.method: Treat p = order of Z_p as an extensive variable; use held-out spectral head-tail contrast as a representation-level order parameter; apply condensed-matter diagnostics (coarse-grid sweeps) and a near-critical addition audit; compute Binder-like cumulants and susceptibilities; compare models using ΔAIC (ΔAIC=16.8 for near-critical audit).
AI.result: Binder-like crossings indicate a shared finite-size boundary across experiments; susceptibility analysis disfavors a smooth crossover; near-critical audit yields ΔAIC=16.8, supporting a finite-size phase-transition interpretation; the evidence does not resolve the transition order.
AI.conclusion: Grokking’s phase-transition language can be treated as a falsifiable finite-size claim rather than mere analogy; however, the order of the transition remains unresolved, warranting further finite-size scaling and multiple-order-parameter analyses.

title: CTS-PLL: A Robust and Anytime Framework for Collaborative Task Sequencing and Multi-Agent Path Finding
summary: The Collaborative Task Sequencing and Multi-Agent Path Finding (CTS-MAPF) problem requires agents to accomplish sequences of tasks while avoiding collisions, posing significant challenges due to its combinatorial complexity. This work introduces CTS-PLL, a hierarchical framework that extends the configuration-based CTS-MAPF planning paradigm with two key enhancements: a lock agents detection and release mechanism leveraging a complete planning method for local re-planning, and an anytime refinement procedure based on Large Neighborhood Search (LNS). These additions ensure robustness in dense environments and enable continuous improvement of solution quality. Extensive evaluations across sparse and dense benchmarks demonstrate that CTS-PLL achieves higher success rates and solution quality compared with existing methods, while maintaining competitive runtime efficiency. Real-world robot experiments further demonstrate the feasibility of the approach in practice.

AI.tldr: CTS-PLL adds lock-aware local re-planning and LNS-based anytime refinement to CTS-MAPF, boosting success rate and solution quality in dense environments with real-world validation.
AI.motivation: CTS-MAPF faces combinatorial complexity and collision risk when coordinating task sequences for many agents, especially in dense settings. A robust, scalable framework with continual improvement is needed for practical deployment.
AI.method: Proposes CTS-PLL, a hierarchical, configuration-based CTS-MAPF framework augmented with: (1) a lock agent detection and release mechanism that triggers complete local re-planning to resolve deadlocks, and (2) an anytime refinement procedure based on Large Neighborhood Search to iteratively improve plan quality.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on sparse and dense benchmarks show CTS-PLL achieves higher success rates and better solution quality than existing methods, while maintaining competitive runtimes; real-world robot experiments validate practicality.
AI.conclusion: CTS-PLL provides robust, scalable CTS-MAPF planning in dense environments and supports continual improvement of solutions, making it suitable for real-world multi-robot systems.

title: Light Cones For Vision: Simple Causal Priors For Visual Hierarchy
summary: Standard vision models treat objects as independent points in Euclidean space, unable to capture hierarchical structure like parts within wholes. We introduce Worldline Slot Attention, which models objects as persistent trajectories through spacetime worldlines, where each object has multiple slots at different hierarchy levels sharing the same spatial position but differing in temporal coordinates. This architecture consistently fails without geometric structure: Euclidean worldlines achieve 0.078 level accuracy, below random chance (0.33), while Lorentzian worldlines achieve 0.479-0.661 across three datasets: a 6x improvement replicated over 20+ independent runs. Lorentzian geometry also outperforms hyperbolic embeddings showing visual hierarchies require causal structure (temporal dependency) rather than tree structure (radial branching). Our results demonstrate that hierarchical object discovery requires geometric structure encoding asymmetric causality, an inductive bias absent from Euclidean space but natural to Lorentzian light cones, achieved with only 11K parameters. The code is available at: https://github.com/iclrsubmissiongram/loco.

AI.tldr: Lorentzian Worldline Slot Attention models objects as persistent spacetime trajectories with hierarchical slots, using causal structure to discover hierarchies. It outperforms Euclidean baselines by large margins and exceeds hyperbolic embeddings, with ~11k parameters; code released.
AI.motivation: Standard vision treats objects as independent Euclidean points, failing to capture hierarchical structure and asymmetrical causality inherent in spatiotemporal data. A geometry incorporating causal structure is hypothesized to better support hierarchical object discovery.
AI.method: Propose Worldline Slot Attention: represent each object as a trajectory (worldline) with multiple slots at different hierarchy levels sharing spatial position but differing in time. Compare Euclidean vs Lorentzian worldlines across three datasets; demonstrate large gains with only about 11k parameters; code released at the provided repo.
AI.result: Lorentzian worldlines achieve 0.479–0.661 accuracy across three datasets, a ~6× improvement over Euclidean worldlines (0.078; which is below random chance 0.33) and superior to hyperbolic embeddings; results replicated over 20+ independent runs.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical object discovery requires geometric structure encoding asymmetric causality. Lorentzian light-cone geometry provides a natural inductive bias for hierarchical structure, enabling strong performance with a compact model (~11k params) and suggesting temporal causality, not tree-like radial branching, governs visual hierarchies.

title: Decoding Market Emotions in Cryptocurrency Tweets via Predictive Statement Classification with Machine Learning and Transformers
summary: The growing prominence of cryptocurrencies has triggered widespread public engagement and increased speculative activity, particularly on social media platforms. This study introduces a novel classification framework for identifying predictive statements in cryptocurrency-related tweets, focusing on five popular cryptocurrencies: Cardano, Matic, Binance, Ripple, and Fantom. The classification process is divided into two stages: Task 1 involves binary classification to distinguish between Predictive and Non-Predictive statements. Tweets identified as Predictive proceed to Task 2, where they are further categorized as Incremental, Decremental, or Neutral. To build a robust dataset, we combined manual and GPT-based annotation methods and utilized SenticNet to extract emotion features corresponding to each prediction category. To address class imbalance, GPT-generated paraphrasing was employed for data augmentation. We evaluated a wide range of machine learning, deep learning, and transformer-based models across both tasks. The results show that GPT-based balancing significantly enhanced model performance, with transformer models achieving the highest F1-score in Task 1, while traditional machine learning models performed best in Task 2. Furthermore, our emotion analysis revealed distinct emotional patterns associated with each prediction category across the different cryptocurrencies.

AI.tldr: A two-stage classification framework identifies predictive statements in crypto-related tweets across five currencies, leveraging manual and GPT-based annotations, SenticNet emotions, and GPT-based augmentation; transformers excel for predicting predictive statements, while traditional ML models perform best for finer-grained trend categorization; distinct emotional patterns emerge per category and currency.
AI.motivation: To address rising crypto discourse on social media and the need to automatically detect predictive statements that may signal market-relevant sentiment.
AI.method: Two-stage classification: Task 1 (Predictive vs Non-Predictive) and Task 2 (Incremental, Decremental, Neutral) on tweets about Cardano, Matic, Binance, Ripple, Fantom. Dataset built via manual and GPT-based annotations; emotion features extracted via SenticNet; class imbalance mitigated with GPT-based paraphrasing data augmentation; extensive evaluation of ML, DL, and transformer models.
AI.result: GPT-based balancing improves model performance; transformer models achieve the highest F1-score in Task 1, while traditional ML models perform best in Task 2; emotion analysis reveals category-specific emotional patterns across cryptocurrencies.
AI.conclusion: The proposed two-stage framework effectively detects predictive statements in crypto tweets and uncovers distinct emotion signatures by category and currency, with model choice depending on the classification stage.

title: Dissimilarity-Based Persistent Coverage Control of Multi-Robot Systems for Improving Solar Irradiance Prediction Accuracy in Solar Thermal Power Plants
summary: Accurate forecasting of future solar irradiance is essential for the effective control of solar thermal power plants. Although various kriging-based methods have been proposed to address the prediction problem, these methods typically do not provide an appropriate sampling strategy to dynamically position mobile sensors for optimizing prediction accuracy in real time, which is critical for achieving accurate forecasts with a minimal number of sensors. This paper introduces a dissimilarity map derived from a kriging model and proposes a persistent coverage control algorithm that effectively guides agents toward regions where additional observations are required to improve prediction performance. By means of experiments using mobile robots, the proposed approach was shown to obtain more accurate predictions than the considered baselines under various emulated irradiance fields.

AI.tldr: Kriging-based solar irradiance forecasting enhanced by a dissimilarity map and persistent coverage control to guide mobile sensors for real-time, accurate predictions with fewer sensors.
AI.motivation: Accurate solar irradiance forecasts are critical for efficient control of solar thermal plants. Traditional kriging approaches lack an effective, dynamic sampling strategy for mobile sensors to maintain high prediction accuracy with a limited sensor count.
AI.method: Introduce a dissimilarity map derived from a kriging model and a persistent coverage control algorithm that directs mobile agents toward regions needing additional observations to improve prediction performance. Validate via experiments with mobile robots across emulated irradiance fields.
AI.result: The proposed approach yields more accurate solar irradiance predictions than the considered baselines across various emulated irradiance fields.
AI.conclusion: Integrating a kriging-based dissimilarity map with persistent coverage control improves predictive accuracy and enables efficient sensor deployment for real-time solar irradiance forecasting with fewer sensors.

title: OpenCap Monocular: 3D Human Kinematics and Musculoskeletal Dynamics from a Single Smartphone Video
summary: Quantifying human movement (kinematics) and musculoskeletal forces (kinetics) at scale, such as estimating quadriceps force during a sit-to-stand movement, could transform prediction, treatment, and monitoring of mobility-related conditions. However, quantifying kinematics and kinetics traditionally requires costly, time-intensive analysis in specialized laboratories, limiting clinical translation. Scalable, accurate tools for biomechanical assessment are needed. We introduce OpenCap Monocular, an algorithm that estimates 3D skeletal kinematics and kinetics from a single smartphone video. The method refines 3D human pose estimates from a monocular pose estimation model (WHAM) via optimization, computes kinematics of a biomechanically constrained skeletal model, and estimates kinetics via physics-based simulation and machine learning. We validated OpenCap Monocular against marker-based motion capture and force plate data for walking, squatting, and sit-to-stand tasks. OpenCap Monocular achieved low kinematic error (4.8° mean absolute error for rotational degrees of freedom; 3.4 cm for pelvis translations), outperforming a regression-only computer vision baseline by 48% in rotational accuracy (p = 0.036) and 69% in translational accuracy (p < 0.001). OpenCap Monocular also estimated ground reaction forces during walking with accuracy comparable to, or better than, our prior two-camera OpenCap system. We demonstrate that the algorithm estimates important kinetic outcomes with clinically meaningful accuracy in applications related to frailty and knee osteoarthritis, including estimating knee extension moment during sit-to-stand transitions and knee adduction moment during walking. OpenCap Monocular is deployed via a smartphone app, web app, and secure cloud computing (https://opencap.ai), enabling free, accessible single-smartphone biomechanical assessments.

AI.tldr: Monocular OpenCap estimates 3D kinematics and kinetics from a single smartphone video by refining monocular pose estimates (WHAM) with biomechanical constraints and physics-based ML, validated against lab-based data across walking, squats, and sit-to-stand; achieves high accuracy and deploys via mobile/web/cloud for scalable biomechanical assessment.
AI.motivation: Scale and translate biomechanical analysis from costly lab-based systems to accessible tools suitable for clinical use, enabling prediction, treatment, and monitoring of mobility-related conditions.
AI.method: OpenCap Monocular refines 3D poses from a monocular video (WHAM) through optimization against a biomechanical skeletal model; computes kinematics of the model; estimates kinetics using physics-based simulation and machine learning. Validation against marker-based MoCap and force plates for walking, squatting, and sit-to-stand. Compared to a regression-only CV baseline. Deployed via smartphone app, web app, and secure cloud.
AI.result: Low kinematic error: 4.8° MAE for rotational DOF; 3.4 cm pelvis translation error. Outperform regression baseline by 48% in rotational accuracy (p=0.036) and 69% in translational accuracy (p<0.001). Ground reaction force estimates comparable to or better than prior two-camera OpenCap. Clinically meaningful kinase-related kinetics estimates (e.g., knee extension moment during sit-to-stand; knee adduction moment during walking) in frailty and knee OA contexts.
AI.conclusion: OpenCap Monocular enables accessible, single-smartphone biomechanical assessments with accuracy suitable for clinical screening and monitoring, expanding reach beyond specialized labs.

title: Transformers in the Dark: Navigating Unknown Search Spaces via Bandit Feedback
comment: Accepted for publication in Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR)
summary: Effective problem solving with Large Language Models (LLMs) can be enhanced when they are paired with external search algorithms. By viewing the space of diverse ideas and their follow-up possibilities as a tree structure, the search algorithm can navigate such a search space and guide the LLM toward better solutions more efficiently. While the search algorithm enables an effective balance between exploitation and exploration of a tree-structured space, the need for an external component can complicate the overall problem-solving process. We therefore pose the following question: Can LLMs or their underlying Transformer architectures approximate a search algorithm? To answer this question, we first introduce a simplified framework in which tree extensions and feedback signals are externally specified, allowing for controlled evaluation of search capabilities. We call this setting unknown tree search with bandit feedback. Within this setting, we show that Transformers are theoretically expressive enough to implement distinct search strategies and can be trained from scratch to approximate those strategies. Our Transformer models exhibit the possibility of generalizing to unseen conditions such as longer horizons or deeper trees. Furthermore, we demonstrate that continued task-focused training unlocks the complete capabilities of a pretrained LLM, by fine-tuning the LLM on search trajectories.

AI.tldr: Transformers can emulate external tree-search strategies under an unknown-tree-search with bandit feedback, and with task-focused fine-tuning, LLMs can realize full search capabilities and generalize to longer horizons and deeper trees.
AI.motivation: Assess whether LLMs/Transformers can internally approximate an external search algorithm to simplify problem solving and improve efficiency in tree-structured search spaces.
AI.method: Introduce a simplified framework: unknown tree search with bandit feedback. Externally specify tree extensions and feedback signals. Train Transformers from scratch to approximate distinct search strategies. Evaluate generalization to longer horizons/deeper trees. Fine-tune the LLM on search trajectories to unlock full capabilities.
AI.result: Transformers are theoretically expressive enough to implement distinct search strategies and can be trained from scratch to approximate those strategies. They generalize to unseen conditions such as longer horizons or deeper trees. Continued task-focused training (fine-tuning on search trajectories) unlocks the complete capabilities of a pretrained LLM.
AI.conclusion: LLMs/Transformers can approximate external search algorithms within a controlled framework. Training regimens that emphasize search trajectories enhance their ability to emulate and generalize search behavior, potentially reducing reliance on external components.

title: FinMCP-Bench: Benchmarking LLM Agents for Real-World Financial Tool Use under the Model Context Protocol
summary: This paper introduces \textbf{FinMCP-Bench}, a novel benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) in solving real-world financial problems through tool invocation of financial model context protocols. FinMCP-Bench contains 613 samples spanning 10 main scenarios and 33 sub-scenarios, featuring both real and synthetic user queries to ensure diversity and authenticity. It incorporates 65 real financial MCPs and three types of samples, single tool, multi-tool, and multi-turn, allowing evaluation of models across different levels of task complexity. Using this benchmark, we systematically assess a range of mainstream LLMs and propose metrics that explicitly measure tool invocation accuracy and reasoning capabilities. FinMCP-Bench provides a standardized, practical, and challenging testbed for advancing research on financial LLM agents.

AI.tldr: Introduces FinMCP-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating LLMs' ability to solve real-world financial problems via tool invocation, with 613 samples across 10 main scenarios and 33 sub-scenarios, 65 real MCPs, and three sample types; assesses mainstream LLMs and proposes metrics for tool invocation accuracy and reasoning, providing a standardized testbed for financial LLM agents.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of standardized evaluation for LLMs that must interact with external financial tools and context protocols, enabling realistic, diverse, and scalable assessment of financial decision-making capabilities.
AI.method: Constructs FinMCP-Bench comprising 613 samples (real and synthetic user queries) spanning 10 main scenarios and 33 sub-scenarios, includes 65 real financial MCPs, and supports three sample types: single tool, multi-tool, and multi-turn. The study evaluates mainstream LLMs against proposed metrics that quantify tool invocation accuracy and reasoning.
AI.result: Systematic evaluation across multiple mainstream LLMs using the benchmark; demonstrates distinct gaps in tool-invocation capability and reasoning, and shows the usefulness of the proposed performance metrics for guiding improvements.
AI.conclusion: FinMCP-Bench offers a standardized, practical, and challenging testbed that enables advancing research on financial LLM agents and their ability to invoke and utilize external tools effectively.

title: A Minimum-Energy Control Approach for Redundant Mobile Manipulators in Physical Human-Robot Interaction Applications
summary: Research on mobile manipulation systems that physically interact with humans has expanded rapidly in recent years, opening the way to tasks which could not be performed using fixed-base manipulators. Within this context, developing suitable control methodologies is essential since mobile manipulators introduce additional degrees of freedom, making the design of control approaches more challenging and more prone to performance optimization. This paper proposes a control approach for a mobile manipulator, composed of a mobile base equipped with a robotic arm mounted on the top, with the objective of minimizing the overall kinetic energy stored in the whole-body mobile manipulator in physical human-robot interaction applications. The approach is experimentally tested with reference to a peg-in-hole task, and the results demonstrate that the proposed approach reduces the overall kinetic energy stored in the whole-body robotic system and improves the system performance compared with the benchmark method.

AI.tldr: A control strategy for mobile manipulators is proposed to minimize total kinetic energy during human-robot interaction, validated on a peg-in-hole task, showing reduced energy and improved performance versus a benchmark.
AI.motivation: Mobile manipulators add extra degrees of freedom and energy considerations, increasing control complexity and safety concerns in human-robot interaction; energy-efficient, whole-body control is needed to enhance performance and safety.
AI.method: Develop a whole-body control approach that minimizes the overall kinetic energy of the mobile manipulator system; experimentally validate on a peg-in-hole task and compare performance to a benchmark method.
AI.result: The approach reduces the overall kinetic energy stored in the system and improves performance compared with the benchmark method.
AI.conclusion: An energy-minimizing whole-body control strategy for mobile manipulators is effective in HRI tasks, yielding energy reductions and performance gains.

title: TIGeR: A Unified Framework for Time, Images and Geo-location Retrieval
summary: Many real-world applications in digital forensics, urban monitoring, and environmental analysis require jointly reasoning about visual appearance, geolocation, and time. Beyond standard geo-localization and time-of-capture prediction, these applications increasingly demand more complex capabilities, such as retrieving an image captured at the same location as a query image but at a specified target time. We formalize this problem as Geo-Time Aware Image Retrieval and curate a diverse benchmark of 4.5M paired image-location-time triplets for training and 86k high-quality triplets for evaluation. We then propose TIGeR, a multi-modal-transformer-based model that maps image, geolocation, and time into a unified geo-temporal embedding space. TIGeR supports flexible input configurations (single-modality and multi-modality queries) and uses the same representation to perform (i) geo-localization, (ii) time-of-capture prediction, and (iii) geo-time-aware retrieval. By better preserving underlying location identity under large appearance changes, TIGeR enables retrieval based on where and when a scene is, rather than purely on visual similarity. Extensive experiments show that TIGeR consistently outperforms strong baselines and state-of-the-art methods by up to 16% on time-of-year, 8% time-of-day prediction, and 14% in geo-time aware retrieval recall, highlighting the benefits of unified geo-temporal modeling.

AI.tldr: TIGeR introduces a multi-modal transformer that learns a unified geo-temporal embedding for images, geolocation, and time, enabling geo-localization, time prediction, and geo-time-aware retrieval with strong gains on a large-scale dataset.
AI.motivation: Real-world tasks require reasoning about where and when a scene occurred. Existing methods largely address geo-localization or time prediction in isolation and struggle when appearance changes across time. A unified geo-temporal representation can enable retrieval of images by location and time, even under varying appearance.
AI.method: Propose TIGeR, a multi-modal-transformer model that maps image, geolocation, and time into a shared geo-temporal embedding space. Supports flexible input configurations (single- or multi-modality queries) and uses the same representation to perform (i) geo-localization, (ii) time-of-capture prediction, and (iii) geo-time-aware retrieval. Trains on a large curated dataset of 4.5M image-location-time triplets (training) and 86k high-quality triplets (evaluation).
AI.result: TIGeR consistently outperforms strong baselines and state-of-the-art methods, by up to 16% for time-of-year, 8% for time-of-day prediction, and 14% recall in geo-time-aware retrieval.
AI.conclusion: Unified geo-temporal modeling yields robust retrieval based on where and when a scene is, robust to large appearance changes, and enables versatile querying and predictions across geo-localization, time prediction, and retrieval tasks.

title: Shopping with a Platform AI Assistant: Who Adopts, When in the Journey, and What For
summary: This paper provides some of the first large-scale descriptive evidence on how consumers adopt and use platform-embedded shopping AI in e-commerce. Using data on 31 million users of Ctrip, China's largest online travel platform, we study "Wendao," an LLM-based AI assistant integrated into the platform. We document three empirical regularities. First, adoption is highest among older consumers, female users, and highly engaged existing users, reversing the younger, male-dominated profile commonly documented for general-purpose AI tools. Second, AI chat appears in the same broad phase of the purchase journey as traditional search and well before order placement; among journeys containing both chat and search, the most common pattern is interleaving, with users moving back and forth between the two modalities. Third, consumers disproportionately use the assistant for exploratory, hard-to-keyword tasks: attraction queries account for 42% of observed chat requests, and chat intent varies systematically with both the timing of chat relative to search and the category of products later purchased within the same journey. These findings suggest that embedded shopping AI functions less as a substitute for conventional search than as a complementary interface for exploratory product discovery in e-commerce.

AI.tldr: Embedded shopping AI (Wendao) on Ctrip is adopted by older, female, and highly engaged users; AI chat appears in the search phase and is frequently interleaved with search; used mainly for exploratory, hard-to-keyword tasks (e.g., attraction queries, 42% of chat requests); overall, the AI functions as a complement to search for discovery rather than a substitute.
AI.motivation: Fill a knowledge gap on large-scale, real-world usage of platform-embedded shopping AI and how consumers integrate such tools into their purchase journeys.
AI.method: Descriptive analysis of platform data from 31 million Ctrip users, focusing on Wendao, an LLM-based AI assistant. Examines adoption demographics, temporal placement in purchase journeys, modality interleaving (chat vs. search), and task types (attraction queries).
AI.result: Three empirical regularities emerge: (1) adoption skews toward older users, women, and highly engaged existing users; (2) AI chat appears in the same broad phase as traditional search and precedes order placement, with interleaving between chat and search being the most common pattern; (3) predominant use for exploratory, hard-to-keyword tasks—attraction queries constitute 42% of chat requests, and chat intent varies with timing and product category. These findings suggest embedded shopping AI acts more as a complementary interface for exploratory product discovery than as a substitute for conventional search.
AI.conclusion: Embedded shopping AI on e-commerce platforms primarily supports exploratory discovery and acts as a complement to traditional search within the purchase funnel. Design and policy implications include aligning chat capabilities with discovery tasks, timing within the journey, and targeting user segments more likely to benefit from exploration tools. Limitations include descriptive, non-causal evidence and platform-specific context.

title: Synthetic Cardiac MRI Image Generation using Deep Generative Models
summary: Synthetic cardiac MRI (CMRI) generation has emerged as a promising strategy to overcome the scarcity of annotated medical imaging data. Recent advances in GANs, VAEs, diffusion probabilistic models, and flow-matching techniques aim to generate anatomically accurate images while addressing challenges such as limited labeled datasets, vendor variability, and risks of privacy leakage through model memorization. Maskconditioned generation improves structural fidelity by guiding synthesis with segmentation maps, while diffusion and flowmatching models offer strong boundary preservation and efficient deterministic transformations. Cross-domain generalization is further supported through vendor-style conditioning and preprocessing steps like intensity normalization. To ensure privacy, studies increasingly incorporate membership inference attacks, nearest-neighbor analyses, and differential privacy mechanisms. Utility evaluations commonly measure downstream segmentation performance, with evidence showing that anatomically constrained synthetic data can enhance accuracy and robustness across multi-vendor settings. This review aims to compare existing CMRI generation approaches through the lenses of fidelity, utility, and privacy, highlighting current limitations and the need for integrated, evaluation-driven frameworks for reliable clinical workflows.

AI.tldr: Survey of synthetic cardiac MRI generation using GANs, VAEs, diffusion, and flow-matching with mask-conditioned synthesis, vendor-aware conditioning, and privacy-preserving evaluations; highlights fidelity, utility, and privacy trade-offs; calls for integrated evaluation frameworks for clinical reliability.
AI.motivation: Address data scarcity, vendor variability, and privacy risks in CMRI; improve anatomical fidelity and utility of synthetic data for downstream tasks like segmentation.
AI.method: Review of diverse generative approaches (GANs, VAEs, diffusion models, flow-matching) with mask-conditioned generation; cross-domain generalization via vendor-style conditioning; preprocessing normalization; privacy analyses (membership inference, nearest-neighbor analyses, differential privacy); utility by downstream segmentation performance.
AI.result: Evidence suggests anatomically constrained synthetic CMRI can improve segmentation accuracy and robustness across multi-vendor settings; diffusion/flow-matching offer boundary preservation; masking improves structural fidelity; privacy assessments are increasingly incorporated.
AI.conclusion: Need for integrated, evaluation-driven frameworks that balance fidelity, utility, and privacy; current limitations include lack of standardized benchmarks and standardized evaluation protocols for clinical deployment.

title: SafeGuard ASF: SR Agentic Humanoid Robot System for Autonomous Industrial Safety
summary: The rise of unmanned ``dark factories'' operating without human presence demands autonomous safety systems capable of detecting and responding to multiple hazard types. We present SafeGuard ASF (Agentic Security Fleet), a comprehensive framework deploying humanoid robots for autonomous hazard detection in industrial environments. Our system integrates multi-modal perception (RGB-D imaging), a ReAct-based agentic reasoning framework, and learned locomotion policies on the Unitree G1 humanoid platform. We address three critical hazard scenarios: fire and smoke detection, abnormal temperature monitoring in pipelines, and intruder detection in restricted zones. Our perception pipeline achieves 94.2% mAP for fire or smoke detection with 127ms latency. We train multiple locomotion policies, including dance motion tracking and velocity control, using Unitree RL Lab with PPO, demonstrating stable convergence within 80,000 training iterations. We validate our system in both simulation and real-world environments, demonstrating autonomous patrol, human detection with visual perception, and obstacle avoidance capabilities. The proposed ToolOrchestra action framework enables structured decision-making through perception, reasoning, and actuation tools.

AI.tldr: SafeGuard ASF is a humanoid-robot framework for autonomous hazard detection and response in unmanned factories, integrating RGB-D perception, a ReAct-based reasoning module, and learned locomotion on Unitree G1. It demonstrates high-detect performance, real-time operation, and validation in simulation and real environments.
AI.motivation: The rise of unmanned/“dark” factories lacking human operators necessitates autonomous safety systems capable of detecting hazards (fire, temperature anomalies, intruders) and responding without human intervention.
AI.method: Combines multi-modal RGB-D perception, a ReAct-based agentic reasoning framework, and learned locomotion policies (dance motion tracking, velocity control) trained via Unitree RL Lab using PPO on the Unitree G1. The ToolOrchestra action framework enables structured perception–reasoning–actuation workflows. Evaluated on fire/smoke detection, pipeline temperature anomalies, and intruder detection, with sim-to-real validation.
AI.result: Perception pipeline achieves 94.2% mAP for fire/smoke with 127 ms latency. Locomotion policies converge after ~80,000 training iterations. System demonstrated in simulation and real-world settings with autonomous patrol, visual human detection, and obstacle avoidance.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates effective autonomous hazard detection and response in industrial environments by tightly integrating perception, reasoning, and actuation, and supports safe operation in unmanned factories with potential for broader hazard types and workflows.

title: Bayesian Learning-Enhanced Navigation with Deep Smoothing for Inertial-Aided Navigation
summary: Accurate post-processing navigation is essential for applications such as survey and mapping, where the full measurement history can be exploited to refine past state estimates. Fixed-interval smoothing algorithms represent the theoretically optimal solution under Gaussian assumptions. However, loosely coupled INS/GNSS systems fundamentally inherit the systematic position bias of raw GNSS measurements, leaving a persistent accuracy gap that model-based smoothers cannot resolve. To address this limitation, we propose BLENDS, which integrates Bayesian learning with deep smoothing to enhance navigation performance. BLENDS is a a data-driven post-processing framework that augments the classical two-filter smoother with a transformer-based neural network. It learns to modify the filter covariance matrices and apply an additive correction to the smoothed error-state directly within the Bayesian framework. A novel Bayesian-consistent loss jointly supervises the smoothed mean and covariance, enforcing minimum-variance estimates while maintaining statistical consistency. BLENDS is evaluated on two real-world datasets spanning a mobile robot and a quadrotor. Across all unseen test trajectories, BLENDS achieves horizontal position improvements of up to 63% over the baseline forward EKF.

AI.tldr: BLENDS combines Bayesian learning with deep smoothing to improve post-processed INS/GNSS navigation by learning to adapt the smoother’s covariances and correct the smoothed state within a two-filter framework, guided by a Bayesian-consistent loss. It addresses GNSS bias in loosely coupled systems and shows up to 63% horizontal-position gains on unseen trajectories across robot and quadrotor datasets.
AI.motivation: Loosely coupled INS/GNSS systems inherit persistent GNSS bias that standard model-based smoothers cannot remove; fixed-interval smoothing is theoretically optimal under Gaussian assumptions but cannot recover systematic measurement biases.
AI.method: A transformer-based neural network is integrated into a two-filter smoother to learn covariance modulation and apply an additive correction to the smoothed error-state within a Bayesian framework. Training uses a Bayesian-consistent loss that jointly optimizes the smoothed mean and covariance to enforce minimum-variance estimates while preserving statistical consistency.
AI.result: Evaluations on two real-world datasets (mobile robot and quadrotor) show that BLENDS achieves up to 63% improvement in horizontal position accuracy on unseen trajectories compared to the baseline forward EKF.
AI.conclusion: BLENDS demonstrates that data-driven covariance adaptation and additive corrections within a Bayesian smoothing framework can bridge the accuracy gap caused by GNSS bias in loosely coupled systems, with demonstrated generalization across platforms and trajectories.

title: Reaching Beyond the Mode: RL for Distributional Reasoning in Language Models
summary: Given a question, a language model (LM) implicitly encodes a distribution over possible answers. In practice, post-training procedures for LMs often collapse this distribution onto a single dominant mode. While this is generally not a problem for benchmark-style evaluations that assume one correct answer, many real-world tasks inherently involve multiple valid answers or irreducible uncertainty. Examples include medical diagnosis, ambiguous question answering, and settings with incomplete information. In these cases, we would like LMs to generate multiple plausible hypotheses, ideally with confidence estimates for each one, and without computationally intensive repeated sampling to generate non-modal answers. This paper describes a multi-answer reinforcement learning approach for training LMs to perform distributional reasoning over multiple answers during inference. We modify the RL objective to enable models to explicitly generate multiple candidate answers in a single forward pass, internalizing aspects of inference-time search into the model's generative process. Across question-answering, medical diagnostic, and coding benchmarks, we observe improved diversity, coverage, and set-level calibration scores compared to single answer trained baselines. Models trained with our approach require fewer tokens to generate multiple answers than competing approaches. On coding tasks, they are also substantially more accurate. These results position multi-answer RL as a principled and compute-efficient alternative to inference-time scaling procedures such as best-of-k. Code and more information can be found at https://multi-answer-rl.github.io/.

AI.tldr: Proposes multi-answer RL to train LMs to generate multiple candidate answers in a single pass, improving diversity, coverage, and calibration while reducing inference-time compute; gains across QA, medical diagnosis, and coding tasks.
AI.motivation: Real-world tasks often have multiple valid answers or irreducible uncertainty; single-modal outputs fail to capture distributional reasoning and confidence across options.
AI.method: Modify the RL objective to enable a model to generate multiple candidate answers in one forward pass, embedding inference-time search-like behavior into training and avoiding costly sampling methods at inference.
AI.result: Across QA, medical diagnosis, and coding benchmarks, improved diversity, coverage, and set-level calibration versus single-answer baselines; fewer tokens needed to produce multiple answers; coding tasks show substantial accuracy gains.
AI.conclusion: Multi-answer RL is a principled, computation-efficient alternative to inference-time scaling methods (e.g., best-of-k) for producing multiple plausible LM outputs; code available at the project site.

title: The Anatomy of Uncertainty in LLMs
summary: Understanding why a large language model (LLM) is uncertain about the response is important for their reliable deployment. Current approaches, which either provide a single uncertainty score or rely on the classical aleatoric-epistemic dichotomy, fail to offer actionable insights for improving the generative model. Recent studies have also shown that such methods are not enough for understanding uncertainty in LLMs. In this work, we advocate for an uncertainty decomposition framework that dissects LLM uncertainty into three distinct semantic components: (i) input ambiguity, arising from ambiguous prompts; (ii) knowledge gaps, caused by insufficient parametric evidence; and (iii) decoding randomness, stemming from stochastic sampling. Through a series of experiments we demonstrate that the dominance of these components can shift across model size and task. Our framework provides a better understanding to audit LLM reliability and detect hallucinations, paving the way for targeted interventions and more trustworthy systems.

AI.tldr: Proposes a three-component uncertainty decomposition for LLMs—input ambiguity, knowledge gaps, and decoding randomness—and demonstrates that their relative importance varies with model size and task, enabling targeted interventions to audit reliability and mitigate hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Current uncertainty measures are limited (single scores, or aleatoric/epistemic split) and fail to guide improvements; understanding when prompts are ambiguous, when models lack knowledge, or when sampling randomness drives outputs is crucial for trustworthy LLMs.
AI.method: Define and operationalize an uncertainty decomposition framework; quantify each component; run experiments across model sizes and tasks; analyze dominance of components.
AI.result: Dominance of components shifts by model size and task; framework supports reliability auditing and hallucination detection; enables targeted fixes.
AI.conclusion: The three-component decomposition advances understanding of LLM uncertainty and supports targeted interventions to improve trustworthiness.

title: Calibri: Enhancing Diffusion Transformers via Parameter-Efficient Calibration
comment: Accepted to CVRP 2026, Project page: https://v-gen-ai.github.io/Calibri-page/
summary: In this paper, we uncover the hidden potential of Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) to significantly enhance generative tasks. Through an in-depth analysis of the denoising process, we demonstrate that introducing a single learned scaling parameter can significantly improve the performance of DiT blocks. Building on this insight, we propose Calibri, a parameter-efficient approach that optimally calibrates DiT components to elevate generative quality. Calibri frames DiT calibration as a black-box reward optimization problem, which is efficiently solved using an evolutionary algorithm and modifies just ~100 parameters. Experimental results reveal that despite its lightweight design, Calibri consistently improves performance across various text-to-image models. Notably, Calibri also reduces the inference steps required for image generation, all while maintaining high-quality outputs.

AI.tldr: Calibri is a lightweight, parameter-efficient calibration for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) using ~100 parameters optimized via black-box rewards with an evolutionary algorithm, improving generative quality and reducing inference steps across text-to-image models.
AI.motivation: Diffusion Transformers have untapped potential; a single learned scaling parameter can significantly boost DiT performance. A scalable, parameter-efficient calibration method could unlock quality gains without full fine-tuning.
AI.method: Calibrate DiTs by framing calibration as black-box optimization. Use an evolutionary algorithm to optimize around ~100 parameters that adjust DiT components, requiring no gradient access to the model and minimal parameter modification.
AI.result: CALIBRI consistently improves generative quality across various text-to-image models and reduces the required inference steps while preserving high output quality.
AI.conclusion: Calibri provides a practical, lightweight approach to enhance DiT-based generation, achieving performance gains with modest parameter updates and fewer inference steps, highlighting the viability of black-box, evolutionary calibration for diffusion models.

title: Rethinking Failure Attribution in Multi-Agent Systems: A Multi-Perspective Benchmark and Evaluation
summary: Failure attribution is essential for diagnosing and improving multi-agent systems (MAS), yet existing benchmarks and methods largely assume a single deterministic root cause for each failure. In practice, MAS failures often admit multiple plausible attributions due to complex inter-agent dependencies and ambiguous execution trajectories. We revisit MAS failure attribution from a multi-perspective standpoint and propose multi-perspective failure attribution, a practical paradigm that explicitly accounts for attribution ambiguity. To support this setting, we introduce MP-Bench, the first benchmark designed for multi-perspective failure attribution in MAS, along with a new evaluation protocol tailored to this paradigm. Through extensive experiments, we find that prior conclusions suggesting LLMs struggle with failure attribution are largely driven by limitations in existing benchmark designs. Our results highlight the necessity of multi-perspective benchmarks and evaluation protocols for realistic and reliable MAS debugging.

AI.tldr: Introduces multi-perspective failure attribution for multi-agent systems, plus MP-Bench and a tailored evaluation protocol; shows attribution ambiguity is essential and that past LLM-attribution claims were benchmark-driven.
AI.motivation: MAS failures often have multiple plausible attributions due to inter-agent dependencies and ambiguous execution trajectories; single-deterministic attribution is unrealistic; need benchmarks that capture attribution ambiguity.
AI.method: Define a multi-perspective attribution framework; create MP-Bench benchmark and an evaluation protocol; perform extensive experiments comparing LLMs and baselines under multi-perspective settings.
AI.result: Findings indicate prior claims that LLMs struggle were largely due to existing benchmarks; MP-Bench demonstrates the value of multiple perspectives; underscores need for multi-perspective benchmarks for realistic MAS debugging.
AI.conclusion: Advocates for multi-perspective benchmarks and evaluation protocols as essential for reliable MAS debugging; MP-Bench provides a practical paradigm to account for attribution ambiguity.

title: GraphER: An Efficient Graph-Based Enrichment and Reranking Method for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Semantic search in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems is often insufficient for complex information needs, particularly when relevant evidence is scattered across multiple sources. Prior approaches to this problem include agentic retrieval strategies, which expand the semantic search space by generating additional queries. However, these methods do not fully leverage the organizational structure of the data and instead rely on iterative exploration, which can lead to inefficient retrieval. Another class of approaches employs knowledge graphs to model non-semantic relationships through graph edges. Although effective in capturing richer proximities, such methods incur significant maintenance costs and are often incompatible with the vector stores used in most production systems. To address these limitations, we propose GraphER, a graph-based enrichment and reranking method that captures multiple forms of proximity beyond semantic similarity. GraphER independently enriches data objects during offline indexing and performs graph-based reranking over candidate objects at query time. This design does not require a knowledge graph, allowing GraphER to integrate seamlessly with standard vector stores. In addition, GraphER is retriever-agnostic and introduces negligible latency overhead. Experiments on multiple retrieval benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: GraphER is a graph-based enrichment and reranking framework for retrieval-augmented generation that enriches data offline, performs graph-based reranking at query time, does not require a knowledge graph, integrates with standard vector stores, is retriever-agnostic, and achieves effective retrieval with negligible latency overhead.
AI.motivation: Semantic search in RAG often fails for complex, multi-source evidence. Agentic query expansion is inefficient, while knowledge-graph approaches incur maintenance costs and clash with vector stores. A need exists for a scalable method that captures multiple forms of proximity beyond semantic similarity and integrates with existing infrastructure.
AI.method: Offline enrichment of data objects during indexing to build a graph with multiple proximity relationships; at query time, graph-based reranking over candidate objects. No explicit knowledge graph required; compatible with standard vector stores; retriever-agnostic; negligible latency overhead.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multiple retrieval benchmarks shows that GraphER improves retrieval effectiveness and maintains low latency across retrievers.
AI.conclusion: GraphER offers a practical, scalable solution that captures non-semantic proximities via graph-based enrichment and reranking, avoiding knowledge-graph maintenance while integrating with existing vector-index pipelines and preserving retriever flexibility.

title: System-Anchored Knee Estimation for Low-Cost Context Window Selection in PDE Forecasting
summary: Autoregressive neural PDE simulators predict the evolution of physical fields one step at a time from a finite history, but low-cost context-window selection for such simulators remains an unformalized problem. Existing approaches to context-window selection in time-series forecasting include exhaustive validation, direct low-cost search, and system-theoretic memory estimation, but they are either expensive, brittle, or not directly aligned with downstream rollout performance. We formalize explicit context-window selection for fixed-window autoregressive neural PDE simulators as an independent low-cost algorithmic problem, and propose \textbf{System-Anchored Knee Estimation (SAKE)}, a two-stage method that first identifies a small structured candidate set from physically interpretable system anchors and then performs knee-aware downstream selection within it. Across all eight PDEBench families evaluated under the shared \(L\in\{1,\dots,16\}\) protocol, SAKE is the strongest overall matched-budget low-cost selector among the evaluated methods, achieving 67.8\% Exact, 91.7\% Within-1, 6.1\% mean regret@knee, and a cost ratio of 0.051 (94.9\% normalized search-cost savings).

AI.tldr: SAKE is a two-stage algorithm for selecting fixed autoregressive context windows in neural PDE simulators, using system anchors to form a candidate set and knee-aware selection to pick the final window. It targets low-cost, budget-constrained rollout performance.
AI.motivation: Formalize and efficiently solve the context-window selection problem for fixed-window autoregressive neural PDE simulators, where existing methods are expensive, brittle, or misaligned with downstream rollout.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) identify a small, structured candidate set from physically interpretable system anchors; (2) perform knee-aware downstream selection within this candidate set to choose the context window that balances accuracy and rollout cost.
AI.result: Across eight PDEBench families under a shared L in {1,...,16} protocol, SAKE achieves 67.8% Exact, 91.7% Within-1, 6.1% mean regret@knee, cost ratio 0.051, and 94.9% normalized search-cost savings, making it the strongest low-cost selector among evaluated methods.
AI.conclusion: System-Anchored Knee Estimation (SAKE) provides an effective, low-cost solution for context-window selection in fixed-window autoregressive neural PDE simulators, with strong empirical performance and substantial search-cost savings.

title: From Stateless to Situated: Building a Psychological World for LLM-Based Emotional Support
summary: In psychological support and emotional companionship scenarios, the core limitation of large language models (LLMs) lies not merely in response quality, but in their reliance on local next-token prediction, which prevents them from maintaining the temporal continuity, stage awareness, and user consent boundaries required for multi-turn intervention. This stateless characteristic makes systems prone to premature advancement, stage misalignment, and boundary violations in continuous dialogue. To address this problem, we argue that the key challenge in process-oriented emotional support is not simply generating natural language, but constructing a sustainably updatable external situational structure for the model. We therefore propose LEKIA 2.0, a situated LLM architecture that separates the cognitive layer from the executive layer, thereby decoupling situational modeling from intervention execution. This design enables the system to maintain stable representations of the user's situation and consent boundaries throughout ongoing interaction. To evaluate this process-control capability, we further introduce a Static-to-Dynamic online evaluation protocol for multi-turn interaction. LEKIA achieved an average absolute improvement of approximately 31% over prompt-only baselines in deep intervention loop completion. The results suggest that an external situational structure is a key enabling condition for building stable, controllable, and situated emotional support systems.

AI.tldr: LEKIA 2.0 introduces a two-layer (cognitive/executive) situated LLM with an external, updatable situational structure to maintain user context and consent, improving multi-turn emotional support over prompt-only baselines.
AI.motivation: LLMs relying on next-token prediction produce stateless dialogue, leading to stage misalignment and boundary violations in multi-turn emotional support; a sustainably updatable external representation of user situation and consent is needed.
AI.method: Proposes LEKIA 2.0 architecture with a separated cognitive layer and executive layer, decoupling situation modeling from intervention execution; utilizes an external situational structure; introduces a Static-to-Dynamic online evaluation protocol for multi-turn interaction.
AI.result: Reported ~31% average absolute improvement over prompt-only baselines in deep intervention loop completion; supports the benefit of external situational structure for stable, controllable, and situated emotional support.
AI.conclusion: External situational structure is a key enabling condition for building stable, controllable, and situated emotional support systems; separating modeling from execution enhances continuity and consent management.

title: Mechanistically Interpreting Compression in Vision-Language Models
summary: Compressed vision-language models (VLMs) are widely used to reduce memory and compute costs, making them a suitable choice for real-world deployment. However, compressing these models raises concerns about whether internal computations and safety behaviors are preserved. In this work, we use causal circuit analysis and crosscoder-based feature comparisons to examine how pruning and quantization fundamentally change the internals across representative VLMs. We observe that pruning generally keeps circuit structure intact but rotates and attenuates internal features, while quantization modifies the circuits at a higher level yet leaves the surviving features better aligned. Leveraging this insight, we also introduce VLMSafe-420, a novel benchmark that pairs harmful inputs with matched benign counterfactuals across various safety categories. Our findings show that pruning causes a sharp drop in genuine refusal behavior, suggesting that the choice of compression has safety implications.

AI.tldr: Pruning and quantization affect compressed vision-language models differently at the representational level, with pruning preserving circuit structure but rotating/attenuating features while quantization changes circuitry more globally but preserves alignment of surviving features; a new safety benchmark (VLMSafe-420) reveals pruning degrades genuine refusal behavior, implying safety implications for compression strategies.
AI.motivation: To understand how model compression alters internal computations and safety behaviors in vision-language models, guiding safe deployment of compressed VLMs.
AI.method: Use causal circuit analysis and crosscoder-based feature comparisons to study pruning and quantization effects across representative VLMs; introduce VLMSafe-420 benchmark pairing harmful inputs with benign counterfactuals across safety categories.
AI.result: Pruning largely preserves circuit structure but rotates/attenuates internal features; quantization modifies circuits at a higher level yet preserves alignment among surviving features; pruning significantly reduces genuine refusal behavior.
AI.conclusion: Compression choices carry safety implications; pruning especially can compromise safety responses; the VLMSafe-420 benchmark provides a framework for evaluating safety under compression and informs safer deployment of compressed VLMs.

title: Visualizing Impedance Control in Augmented Reality for Teleoperation: Design and User Evaluation
comment: 6 pages, 5 figures, submitted to IEEE RO-MAN 2026
summary: Teleoperation for contact-rich manipulation remains challenging, especially when using low-cost, motion-only interfaces that provide no haptic feedback. Virtual reality controllers enable intuitive motion control but do not allow operators to directly perceive or regulate contact forces, limiting task performance. To address this, we propose an augmented reality (AR) visualization of the impedance controller's target pose and its displacement from each robot end effector. This visualization conveys the forces generated by the controller, providing operators with intuitive, real-time feedback without expensive haptic hardware. We evaluate the design in a dual-arm manipulation study with 17 participants who repeatedly reposition a box with and without the AR visualization. Results show that AR visualization reduces completion time by 24% for force-critical lifting tasks, with no significant effect on sliding tasks where precise force control is less critical. These findings indicate that making the impedance target visible through AR is a viable approach to improve human-robot interaction for contact-rich teleoperation.

AI.tldr: AR visualization of the impedance controller's target pose and displacement from each end effector improves teleoperation performance by providing force-related feedback, reducing task time in force-critical lifting tasks by 24%. Validated with 17 participants in a dual-arm box-repositioning study; no significant gain for sliding tasks; supports AR as a viable, low-cost feedback modality for contact-rich teleoperation.
AI.motivation: Motivates by the lack of haptic feedback in motion-only teleoperation interfaces and the need to convey contact forces to operators without expensive haptic hardware.
AI.method: Augmented reality visualization displays the impedance target pose and its displacement for each robot end effector. A dual-arm manipulation study with 17 participants compared performing a box-repositioning task with and without AR visualization, assessing effects on force-critical lifting versus sliding tasks.
AI.result: The AR visualization reduced completion time by 24% for force-critical lifting tasks; no significant benefit was observed for sliding tasks where precise force control is less critical; overall, AR provides intuitive, real-time feedback that enhances manipulation performance.
AI.conclusion: Visible impedance targets via AR constitute a viable approach to improve human–robot interaction in contact-rich teleoperation without requiring expensive haptic hardware.

title: MP-MoE: Matrix Profile-Guided Mixture of Experts for Precipitation Forecasting
summary: Precipitation forecasting remains a persistent challenge in tropical regions like Vietnam, where complex topography and convective instability often limit the accuracy of Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) models. While data-driven post-processing is widely used to mitigate these biases, most existing frameworks rely on point-wise objective functions, which suffer from the ``double penalty'' effect under minor temporal misalignments. In this work, we propose the Matrix Profile-guided Mixture of Experts (MP-MoE), a framework that integrates conventional intensity loss with a structural-aware Matrix Profile objective. By leveraging subsequence-level similarity rather than point-wise errors, the proposed loss facilitates more reliable expert selection and mitigates excessive penalization caused by phase shifts. We evaluate MP-MoE on rainfall datasets from two major river basins in Vietnam across multiple horizons, including 1-hour intensity and accumulated rainfall over 12, 24, and 48 hours. Experimental results demonstrate that MP-MoE outperforms raw NWP and baseline learning methods in terms of Mean Critical Success Index (CSI-M) for heavy rainfall events, while significantly reducing Dynamic Time Warping (DTW) values. These findings highlight the framework's efficacy in capturing peak rainfall intensities and preserving the morphological integrity of storm events.

AI.tldr: MP-MoE uses a Matrix Profile-guided, structure-aware loss combined with a conventional intensity loss within a Mixture of Experts framework to reduce phase-shift penalties in rainfall post-processing, improving heavy-rain CSI and reducing DTW on Vietnamese basins.
AI.motivation: To overcome the double-penalty issue of point-wise losses when temporally misaligned rainfall events occur, by leveraging subsequence similarity instead of point-wise errors.
AI.method: Introduce MP-MoE that blends standard intensity loss with a Matrix Profile-based structural loss and uses a Mixture of Experts to select among models. The Matrix Profile objective operates on subsequences to capture shape and alignment, mitigating phase-shift penalties.
AI.result: On rainfall data from two Vietnamese river basins across 1-hour and 12/24/48-hour horizons, MP-MoE outperforms raw NWP and baseline learners in Mean CSI for heavy rainfall and significantly reduces DTW distances, preserving storm morphology.
AI.conclusion: Structural-aware, subsequence-based loss improves post-processing for tropical rainfall forecasts, enabling better peak-intensity capture and waveform fidelity in forecasts.

title: WAFT-Stereo: Warping-Alone Field Transforms for Stereo Matching
summary: We introduce WAFT-Stereo, a simple and effective warping-based method for stereo matching.
