WAFT-Stereo demonstrates that cost volumes, a common design used in many leading methods, are not necessary for strong performance and can be replaced by warping with improved efficiency. WAFT-Stereo ranks first on ETH3D, KITTI and Middlebury public benchmarks, reducing the zero-shot error by 81% on ETH3D benchmark, while being 1.8-6.7x faster than competitive methods. Code and model weights are available at https://github.com/princeton-vl/WAFT-Stereo.

AI.tldr: WAFT-Stereo introduces a warping-based approach to stereo matching that eliminates the need for cost volumes, achieving state-of-the-art results with significant speedups.
AI.motivation: To improve efficiency and generalization in stereo matching by removing costly 3D cost volumes while maintaining or improving accuracy.
AI.method: A simple warping-based framework that computes stereo correspondences by aligning features through disparity-specific warps, replacing explicit cost-volume construction; uses warping operations to generate and compare matching cues without a traditional 4D cost volume.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on ETH3D, KITTI, and Middlebury public benchmarks; 81% reduction in zero-shot error on ETH3D; 1.8–6.7x faster than competitive methods; code and model weights publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Cost volumes are not necessary for strong stereo performance; warping-based methods can achieve superior efficiency with competitive accuracy, challenging the conventional reliance on cost volumes.

title: Sparse Visual Thought Circuits in Vision-Language Models
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) improve interpretability in multimodal models, but it remains unclear whether SAE features form modular, composable units for reasoning-an assumption underlying many intervention-based steering methods. We test this modularity hypothesis and find it often fails: intervening on a task-selective feature set can modestly improve reasoning accuracy, while intervening on the union of two such sets reliably induces output drift (large unintended changes in predictions) and degrades accuracy, even under norm-matched perturbations. This non modular circuit interference is consistent with shared internal pathways where feature unions amplify activation shifts. We develop a reproducible causal pipeline to localize and test these sparse visual thought circuits in Qwen3-VL-8B. On a controlled synthetic benchmark with seven task types and three difficulty levels, linear probes identify a mid decoder locus for task type information. We train SAEs at this layer, construct task-selective sets via an explicit rule, and perform inference time scaling and ablation while quantifying accuracy and drift. Our findings-validated with bootstrapped subsamples and permutation controls, and replicated across multiple VLM families and five diverse datasets clarify the boundaries of SAE feature composability and provide a rigorous diagnostic framework for more reliable VLM control.

AI.tldr: Sparse autoencoder features in multimodal models are not reliably modular or composable for task-specific reasoning: interventions on unions of task-selective feature sets cause output drift and degrade accuracy, challenging the assumption of modular disentanglement. The authors present a reproducible causal pipeline to localize sparse circuits, validate it on Qwen3-VL-8B with synthetic benchmarks (seven tasks, three difficulties), and provide a diagnostic framework for SAE-based control across multiple models and datasets.
AI.motivation: To test the common assumption that SAE-derived features form modular, composable units enabling predictable intervention-based control in multimodal, vision-language models.
AI.method: Develop a reproducible causal pipeline: locate task-type information at a mid-decoder layer via linear probes; train sparse autoencoders at that layer; construct explicit task-selective feature sets; perform inference-time scaling and ablations; quantify accuracy and prediction drift; use bootstrapped subsamples and permutation controls for validation; replicate across multiple VLM families and five diverse datasets; validate on a controlled synthetic benchmark with seven task types and three difficulty levels.
AI.result: Intervening on a single task-selective feature set modestly improves reasoning accuracy, but intervening on the union of two such sets reliably induces large output drift and degrades accuracy, even with norm-matched perturbations. This non-modular interference aligns with shared internal pathways that amplify activation shifts. The authors localize the relevant circuits to a mid-decoder layer, provide a reproducible pipeline, and reproduce results across VLM families and datasets, yielding a robust diagnostic framework.
AI.conclusion: SAE feature composability in multimodal models is limited; intervention-based control based on modular assumptions is unreliable for unions of task-selective features. The proposed causal pipeline clarifies the boundaries of modularity, enabling more reliable VLM control and providing a practical diagnostic framework.

title: LILAC: Language-Conditioned Object-Centric Optical Flow for Open-Loop Trajectory Generation
summary: We address language-conditioned robotic manipulation using flow-based trajectory generation, which enables training on human and web videos of object manipulation and requires only minimal embodiment-specific data. This task is challenging, as object trajectory generation from pre-manipulation images and natural language instructions requires appropriate instruction-flow alignment. To tackle this challenge, we propose the flow-based Language Instruction-guided open-Loop ACtion generator (LILAC). This flow-based Vision-Language-Action model (VLA) generates object-centric 2D optical flow from an RGB image and a natural language instruction, and converts the flow into a 6-DoF manipulator trajectory. LILAC incorporates two key components: Semantic Alignment Loss, which strengthens language conditioning to generate instruction-aligned optical flow, and Prompt-Conditioned Cross-Modal Adapter, which aligns learned visual prompts with image and text features to provide rich cues for flow generation. Experimentally, our method outperformed existing approaches in generated flow quality across multiple benchmarks. Furthermore, in physical object manipulation experiments using free-form instructions, LILAC demonstrated a superior task success rate compared to existing methods. The project page is available at https://lilac-75srg.kinsta.page/.

AI.tldr: LILAC is a flow-based Vision-Language-Action model that maps RGB and natural language instructions to 2D optical flow and then to 6-DoF robotic trajectories, using semantic alignment loss and a cross-modal adapter to achieve instruction-aligned flow and improved manipulation success.
AI.motivation: Enable language-conditioned robotic manipulation by leveraging uncurated human/web videos and minimizing embodiment-specific data, while addressing the critical alignment between language, flow, and action.
AI.method: Propose LILAC, which outputs object-centric 2D optical flow from an RGB image and a natural language instruction, then converts the flow into a 6-DoF manipulator trajectory. Introduce Semantic Alignment Loss to tighten language-flow conditioning and a Prompt-Conditioned Cross-Modal Adapter to align learned visual prompts with image and text features for richer flow cues.
AI.result: LILAC outperforms existing approaches on generated flow quality across multiple benchmarks and achieves higher task success rates in physical object manipulation with free-form instructions.
AI.conclusion: LILAC demonstrates effective language-conditioned flow-to-action generation with minimal embodiment-specific data, highlighting the viability of flow-based supervision for scalable language-guided robotic manipulation.

title: NeuroVLM-Bench: Evaluation of Vision-Enabled Large Language Models for Clinical Reasoning in Neurological Disorders
comment: 53 pages, 12 figures. Manuscript submitted to the BMC Medical Informatics and Decision Making journal
summary: Recent advances in multimodal large language models enable new possibilities for image-based decision support. However, their reliability and operational trade-offs in neuroimaging remain insufficiently understood. We present a comprehensive benchmarking study of vision-enabled large language models for 2D neuroimaging using curated MRI and CT datasets covering multiple sclerosis, stroke, brain tumors, other abnormalities, and normal controls. Models are required to generate multiple outputs simultaneously, including diagnosis, diagnosis subtype, imaging modality, specialized sequence, and anatomical plane. Performance is evaluated across four directions: discriminative classification with abstention, calibration, structured-output validity, and computational efficiency. A multi-phase framework ensures fair comparison while controlling for selection bias. Across twenty frontier multimodal models, the results show that technical imaging attributes such as modality and plane are nearly solved, whereas diagnostic reasoning, especially subtype prediction, remains challenging. Tumor classification emerges as the most reliable task, stroke is moderately solvable, while multiple sclerosis and rare abnormalities remain difficult. Few-shot prompting improves performance for several models but increases token usage, latency, and cost. Gemini-2.5-Pro and GPT-5-Chat achieve the strongest overall diagnostic performance, while Gemini-2.5-Flash offers the best efficiency-performance trade-off. Among open-weight architectures, MedGemma-1.5-4B demonstrates the most promising results, as under few-shot prompting, it approaches the zero-shot performance of several proprietary models, while maintaining perfect structured output. These findings provide practical insights into performance, reliability, and efficiency trade-offs, supporting standardized evaluation of multimodal LLMs in neuroimaging.

AI.tldr: Comprehensive benchmarking of 20 frontier vision-enabled LLMs on 2D neuroimaging (MRI/CT) across diseases; imaging attributes (modality/plane) are near-solved; diagnostic reasoning, esp. subtype prediction, remains challenging; tumor classification most reliable; few-shot prompting helps but raises cost; Gemini-2.5-Pro and GPT-5-Chat top performance; MedGemma-1.5-4B notable among open weights.
AI.motivation: Assess reliability, trade-offs, and fair comparison strategies for multimodal LLMs in neuroimaging to provide standardized evaluation and practical guidance.
AI.method: Multi-phase evaluation framework; curated MRI/CT datasets including MS, stroke, brain tumors, other abnormalities, controls; require multi-output predictions (diagnosis, subtype, modality, sequence, plane); evaluate discriminative accuracy with abstention, calibration, structured-output validity, and efficiency; compare 20 frontier models; include few-shot prompting; emphasize fair selection bias control.
AI.result: Imaging attributes (modality/plane) largely solved; diagnostic reasoning is the main bottleneck, especially subtype; tumor classification most reliable, stroke moderate, MS and rare abnormalities difficult; few-shot prompting boosts several models' performance but increases tokens, latency, and cost; top performers: Gemini-2.5-Pro and GPT-5-Chat; best efficiency-performance: Gemini-2.5-Flash; among open-weight models, MedGemma-1.5-4B shows strong potential, approaching zero-shot performance with few-shot and offering perfect structured output.
AI.conclusion: The study provides practical insights into performance, reliability, and efficiency trade-offs and supports standardized evaluation of multimodal LLMs in neuroimaging.

title: ElephantBroker: A Knowledge-Grounded Cognitive Runtime for Trustworthy AI Agents
summary: Large Language Model based agents increasingly operate in high stakes, multi turn settings where factual grounding is critical, yet their memory systems typically rely on flat key value stores or plain vector retrieval with no mechanism to track the provenance or trustworthiness of stored knowledge. We present ElephantBroker, an open source cognitive runtime that unifies a Neo4j knowledge graph with a Qdrant vector store through the Cognee SDK to provide durable, verifiable agent memory. The system implements a complete cognitive loop (store, retrieve, score, compose, protect, learn) comprising a hybrid five source retrieval pipeline, an eleven dimension competitive scoring engine for budget constrained context assembly, a four state evidence verification model, a five stage context lifecycle with goal aware assembly and continuous compaction, a six layer cheap first guard pipeline for safety enforcement, an AI firewall providing enforceable tool call interception and multi tier safety scanning, a nine stage consolidation engine that strengthens useful patterns while decaying noise, and a numeric authority model governing multi organization identity with hierarchical access control. Architectural validation through a comprehensive test suite of over 2,200 tests spanning unit, integration, and end to end levels confirms subsystem correctness. The modular design supports three deployment tiers, five profile presets with inheritance, multi gateway isolation, and a management dashboard for human oversight, enabling configurations from lightweight memory only agents to full cognitive runtimes with enterprise grade safety and auditability.

AI.tldr: Open-source cognitive runtime ElephantBroker unifies a Neo4j knowledge graph with a Qdrant vector store to provide durable, verifiable agent memory for high-stakes, multi-turn LLM agents, featuring a complete cognitive loop, multi-source retrieval, safety guards, and extensive verification, supported by a 2,200+ test suite and configurable deployment tiers.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of provenance, trust, and governance in memory for LLM-based agents operating in high-stakes settings; provide verifiable, auditable memory with safety controls and enterprise-grade governance.
AI.method: Architectural integration of Neo4j, Qdrant, and Cognee SDK to implement a complete cognitive loop (store, retrieve, score, compose, protect, learn) with a hybrid five-source retrieval pipeline, an eleven-dimension scoring engine for constrained-context assembly, a four-state evidence verification model, a five-stage context lifecycle with goal-aware assembly and compaction, a six-layer guard pipeline, an AI firewall with tool-call interception and multi-tier safety scanning, a nine-stage consolidation engine, and a numeric authority model for multi-organization identity and access control; validated by a 2,200+ test suite across unit, integration, and end-to-end levels; supports three deployment tiers, five profile presets with inheritance, multi-gateway isolation, and a management dashboard for human oversight.
AI.result: Comprehensive architectural validation demonstrating subsystem correctness and robust, configurable memory for agents across deployment tiers; enables durable, verifiable grounding, safety enforcement, and auditability for high-stakes AI workflows.
AI.conclusion: ElephantBroker provides a modular, scalable framework for trustworthy agent memory, integrating graph-based provenance with vector-based recall, strong safety guards, and governance features; future work could emphasize empirical performance metrics, real-world evaluations, and broader interoperability.

title: CORA: A Pathology Synthesis Driven Foundation Model for Coronary CT Angiography Analysis and MACE Risk Assessment
summary: Coronary artery disease, the leading cause of cardiovascular mortality worldwide, can be assessed non-invasively by coronary computed tomography angiography (CCTA). Despite progress in automated CCTA analysis using deep learning, clinical translation is constrained by the scarcity of expert-annotated datasets. Furthermore, widely adopted label-free pretraining strategies, such as masked image modeling, are intrinsically biased toward global anatomical statistics, frequently failing to capture the spatially localized pathological features of coronary plaques. Here, we introduce CORA, a 3D vision foundation model for comprehensive cardiovascular risk assessment. CORA learns directly from volumetric CCTA via a pathology-centric, synthesis-driven self-supervised framework. By utilizing an anatomy-guided lesion synthesis engine, the model is explicitly trained to detect simulated vascular abnormalities, biasing representation learning toward clinically relevant disease features rather than dominant background anatomy. We trained CORA on a large-scale cohort of 12,801 unlabeled CCTA volumes and comprehensively evaluated the model across multi-center datasets from nine independent hospitals. Across diagnostic and anatomical tasks, including plaque characterization, stenosis detection, and coronary artery segmentation, CORA consistently outperformed the state-of-the-art 3D vision foundation models, achieving up to a 29\% performance gain. Crucially, by coupling the imaging encoder with a large language model, we extended CORA into a multimodal framework that significantly improved 30-day major adverse cardiac event (MACE) risk stratification. Our results establish CORA as a scalable and extensible foundation for unified anatomical assessment and cardiovascular risk prediction.

AI.tldr: CORA is a pathology-centric 3D CCTA foundation model trained with synthesis-driven self-supervision on unlabeled data, achieving strong diagnostic/anatomical task performance and improved 30-day MACE risk stratification when combined with an LLM.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of expert-annotated CCTA datasets and the inherent bias of label-free pretraining toward global anatomy, which neglects localized coronary pathology.
AI.method: A 3D vision foundation model trained on 12,801 unlabeled CCTA volumes using an anatomy-guided lesion synthesis engine that explicitly simulates vascular abnormalities to bias representations toward clinically relevant disease features; evaluation across multi-center datasets (nine hospitals) on plaque characterization, stenosis detection, and segmentation; multicodal extension with a large language model for MACE risk prediction.
AI.result: CORA outperforms state-of-the-art 3D vision foundation models by up to 29% across diagnostic and anatomical tasks; the multimodal CORA-LLM framework significantly improves 30-day MACE risk stratification.
AI.conclusion: CORA is a scalable, extensible foundation for unified anatomical assessment and cardiovascular risk prediction in CCTA, with demonstrated efficacy across diverse datasets and tasks.

title: When Sensing Varies with Contexts: Context-as-Transform for Tactile Few-Shot Class-Incremental Learning
summary: Few-Shot Class-Incremental Learning (FSCIL) can be particularly susceptible to acquisition contexts with only a few labeled samples. A typical scenario is tactile sensing, where the acquisition context ({\it e.g.}, diverse devices, contact state, and interaction settings) degrades performance due to a lack of standardization. In this paper, we propose Context-as-Transform FSCIL (CaT-FSCIL) to tackle the above problem. We decompose the acquisition context into a structured low-dimensional component and a high-dimensional residual component. The former can be easily affected by tactile interaction features, which are modeled as an approximately invertible Context-as-Transform family and handled via inverse-transform canonicalization optimized with a pseudo-context consistency loss. The latter mainly arises from platform and device differences, which can be mitigated with an Uncertainty-Conditioned Prototype Calibration (UCPC) that calibrates biased prototypes and decision boundaries based on context uncertainty. Comprehensive experiments on the standard benchmarks HapTex and LMT108 have demonstrated the superiority of the proposed CaT-FSCIL.

AI.tldr: CaT-FSCIL introduces a two-part context decomposition for FSCIL in tactile sensing: a low-dimensional structured Context-as-Transform with inverse-transform canonicalization, and a high-dimensional residual addressed by Uncertainty-Conditioned Prototype Calibration; shows improved performance on HapTex and LMT108.
AI.motivation: FSCIL performance degrades under diverse acquisition contexts with few labeled samples, especially in tactile sensing; robust context handling is needed to prevent catastrophic forgetting and misclassification.
AI.method: Decompose context into structured low-dimensional component and high-dimensional residual. The low-dim part is modeled via Context-as-Transform (CaT) with approximately invertible transforms; applied via inverse-transform canonicalization optimized with a pseudo-context consistency loss. The high-dim residual from platform/device differences is mitigated with UCPC calibrating prototypes and decision boundaries conditioned on context uncertainty. Experiments on HapTex and LMT108.
AI.result: CaT-FSCIL achieves superior performance relative to baselines on HapTex and LMT108.
AI.conclusion: A two-component context decomposition with context-aware canonicalization and calibration improves FSCIL under diverse tactile acquisition contexts, validated on standard tactile benchmarks HapTex and LMT108.

title: Towards automatic smoke detector inspection: Recognition of the smoke detectors in industrial facilities and preparation for future drone integration
summary: Fire safety consists of a complex pipeline, and it is a very important topic of concern. One of its frontal parts are the smoke detectors, which are supposed to provide an alarm prior to a massive fire appears. As they are often difficult to reach due to high ceilings or problematic locations, an automatic inspection system would be very beneficial as it could allow faster revisions, prevent workers from dangerous work in heights, and make the whole process cheaper. In this study, we present the smoke detector recognition part of the automatic inspection system, which could easily be integrated to the drone system. As part of our research, we compare two popular convolutional-based object detectors YOLOv11 and SSD widely used on embedded devices together with the state-of-the-art transformer-based RT-DETRv2 with the backbones of different sizes. Due to a complicated way of collecting a sufficient amount of data for training in the real-world environment, we also compare several training strategies using the real and semi-synthetic data together with various augmentation methods. To achieve a robust testing, all models were evaluated on two test datasets with an expected and difficult appearance of the smoke detectors including motion blur, small resolution, or not complete objects. The best performing detector is the YOLOv11n, which reaches the average mAP@0.5 score of 0.884. Our code, pretrained models and dataset are publicly available.

AI.tldr: Study on smoke detector detection for drone-based inspection; compares YOLOv11, SSD, and RT-DETRv2 with various backbones; uses real and semi-synthetic data with augmentations; evaluated on two challenging test datasets; YOLOv11n achieves best mAP@0.5 of 0.884; code/dataset publicly available.
AI.motivation: Enable automatic, safe, and cost-effective inspection of hard-to-reach smoke detectors, addressing variability like motion blur, small resolution, and partial objects.
AI.method: Train and compare convolutional detectors (YOLOv11, SSD) and transformer-based RT-DETRv2 with different backbones; employ real and semi-synthetic data generation and augmentation strategies; evaluate on two challenging test datasets simulating real-world conditions.
AI.result: Best performing detector is YOLOv11n with mAP@0.5 of 0.884; the study provides code, pretrained models, and dataset publicly.
AI.conclusion: YOLOv11n is effective for robust smoke detector detection in drone-based inspections; data and models are released to support reproducibility and deployment.

title: RubricEval: A Rubric-Level Meta-Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Judges in Instruction Following
summary: Rubric-based evaluation has become a prevailing paradigm for evaluating instruction following in large language models (LLMs). Despite its widespread use, the reliability of these rubric-level evaluations remains unclear, calling for meta-evaluation. However, prior meta-evaluation efforts largely focus on the response level, failing to assess the fine-grained judgment accuracy that rubric-based evaluation relies on. To bridge this gap, we introduce RubricEval. Our benchmark features: (1) the first rubric-level meta-evaluation benchmark for instruction following, (2) diverse instructions and responses spanning multiple categories and model sources, and (3) a substantial set of 3,486 quality-controlled instances, along with Easy/Hard subsets that better differentiates judge performance. Our experiments reveal that rubric-level judging remains far from solved: even GPT-4o, a widely adopted judge in instruction-following benchmarks, achieves only 55.97% on Hard subset. Considering evaluation paradigm, rubric-level evaluation outperforms checklist-level, explicit reasoning improves accuracy, and both together reduce inter-judge variance. Through our established rubric taxonomy, we further identify common failure modes and offer actionable insights for reliable instruction-following evaluation.

AI.tldr: Introduces RubricEval, the first rubric-level meta-evaluation benchmark for instruction following, with 3,486 quality-controlled instances and Easy/Hard splits; shows rubric-level judgments are not solved (GPT-4o ~56% on Hard) and that rubric-level evaluation outperforms checklist-level, with explicit reasoning reducing variance; provides actionable rubric taxonomy and insights.
AI.motivation: To address a gap in meta-evaluation research: existing work mostly evaluates rubric accuracy at the response level, neglecting the fine-grained judgments that underpin rubric-based evaluation of instruction following.
AI.method: Construct a rubric-level meta-evaluation benchmark (RubricEval) consisting of diverse instructions and model-generated responses from multiple sources, totaling 3,486 quality-controlled instances with Easy/Hard subsets. Evaluate judges (incl. GPT-4o) on rubric-level judgments, compare against checklist-level evaluation, and examine the effects of explicit reasoning on accuracy and inter-judge variance. Develop a rubric taxonomy and analyze common failure modes.
AI.result: Key findings: rubric-level judging remains imperfect; GPT-4o achieves 55.97% accuracy on the Hard subset. Rubric-level evaluation outperforms checklist-level approaches. Explicit reasoning improves judgment accuracy and, when combined with rubric-level evaluation, reduces inter-judge variance. A rubric taxonomy reveals common failure modes, enabling actionable insights for improving reliability in instruction-following evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Rubric-level meta-evaluation is necessary and beneficial for reliable instruction-following assessment. The RubricEval benchmark provides insights into judge performance, highlights persistent failure modes, and offers practical guidance for designing robust rubric-based evaluation and reducing variance across judges.

title: OptiSAR-Net++: A Large-Scale Benchmark and Transformer-Free Framework for Cross-Domain Remote Sensing Visual Grounding
summary: Remote sensing visual grounding (RSVG) aims to localize specific targets in remote sensing images using natural language expressions. However, existing methods are restricted to single-sensor domains, i.e., either optical or synthetic aperture radar (SAR), limiting their real-world applicability. In this paper, we introduce the Cross-Domain RSVG (CD-RSVG) task and construct OptSAR-RSVG, the first large-scale benchmark dataset for this setting. To tackle the challenges of cross-domain feature modeling, computational inefficiency, and fine-grained semantic discrimination, we propose OptiSAR-Net++. Our framework features a patch-level Low-Rank Adaptation Mixture of Experts (PL-MoE) for efficient cross-domain feature decoupling. To mitigate the substantial computational overhead of Transformer decoding frameworks, we adopt a CLIP-based contrastive paradigm and further incorporate dynamic adversarial negative sampling, thereby transforming generative regression into an efficient cross-modal matching process. Additionally, a text-guided dual-gate fusion module (TGDF-SSA) and a region-aware auxiliary head are introduced to enhance semantic-visual alignment and spatial modeling. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OptiSAR-Net++ achieves SOTA performance on both OptSAR-RSVG and DIOR-RSVG benchmarks, offering significant advantages in localization accuracy and efficiency. Our code and dataset will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: Cross-Domain RSVG with OptSAR-RSVG dataset; OptiSAR-Net++ uses PL-MoE, CLIP-based contrastive learning, dynamic adversarial sampling, TGDF-SSA, and region-aware head to achieve SOTA in cross-domain language-grounded RS localization.
AI.motivation: Address the limitation of single-sensor RS visual grounding (optical or SAR) and the need for cross-domain cross-sensor grounding with efficient, fine-grained semantic matching.
AI.method: Patch-level Low-Rank Adaptation Mixture of Experts (PL-MoE) for cross-domain feature decoupling; CLIP-based contrastive learning to replace heavy transformer decoding; dynamic adversarial negative sampling; text-guided dual-gate fusion module (TGDF-SSA); region-aware auxiliary head.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines on OptSAR-RSVG and DIOR-RSVG benchmarks; claims state-of-the-art in localization accuracy and efficiency; dataset large-scale, code and dataset to be released.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates viability of cross-domain RS visual grounding with a unified architecture; contributions include a new dataset, model, and training paradigm enabling efficient cross-domain grounding.

title: Fast-dVLA: Accelerating Discrete Diffusion VLA to Real-Time Performance
summary: This paper proposes a novel approach to address the challenge that pretrained VLA models often fail to effectively improve performance and reduce adaptation costs during standard supervised finetuning (SFT). Some advanced finetuning methods with auxiliary training objectives can improve performance and reduce the number of convergence steps. However, they typically incur significant computational overhead due to the additional losses from auxiliary tasks. To simultaneously achieve the enhanced capabilities of auxiliary training with the simplicity of standard SFT, we decouple the two objectives of auxiliary task training within the parameter space, namely, enhancing general capabilities and fitting task-specific action distributions. To deliver this goal, we only need to train the model to converge on a small-scale task set using two distinct training strategies. The difference between the resulting model parameters can then be interpreted as capability vectors provided by auxiliary tasks. These vectors are then merged with pretrained parameters to form a capability-enhanced meta model. Moreover, when standard SFT is augmented with a lightweight orthogonal regularization loss, the merged model attains performance comparable to auxiliary finetuned baselines with reduced computational overhead. Experimental results demonstrate that this approach is highly effective across diverse robot tasks. Project page: https://chris1220313648.github.io/Fast-dVLA/

AI.tldr: Decouples auxiliary-task training into capability vectors by comparing two small-task finetunes and merging the resulting vectors with pretrained VLA parameters, achieving auxiliary-finetune-like gains with lower overhead; optionally enhanced by lightweight orthogonal regularization; validated on diverse robot tasks.
AI.motivation: Pretrained VLA models underperform gains from auxiliary-objective finetuning and incur high adaptation costs. The goal is to retain the benefits of auxiliary training while maintaining the simplicity and efficiency of standard SFT.
AI.method: Fine-tune on a small task set twice with two different training strategies to produce two parameter sets. Interpret their difference as capability vectors from auxiliary tasks. Merge these vectors with the pretrained VLA parameters to form a capability-enhanced meta model. Optionally apply a lightweight orthogonal regularization loss during standard SFT to further align with the auxiliary signals and reduce overhead.
AI.result: The approach yields competitive or superior performance across diverse robot tasks, achieving auxiliary-finetune-like gains with reduced computational overhead, especially when combined with the lightweight orthogonal regularization.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling auxiliary-task objectives in parameter space and merging capability vectors with pretrained parameters provides a practical pathway to enhance VLA models during SFT without substantial overhead, with orthogonal regularization offering additional gains.

title: The Competence Shadow: Theory and Bounds of AI Assistance in Safety Engineering
summary:
As AI assistants become integrated into safety engineering workflows for Physical AI systems, a critical question emerges: does AI assistance improve safety analysis quality, or introduce systematic blind spots that surface only through post-deployment incidents? This paper develops a formal framework for AI assistance in safety analysis. We first establish why safety engineering resists benchmark-driven evaluation: safety competence is irreducibly multidimensional, constrained by context-dependent correctness, inherent incompleteness, and legitimate expert disagreement. We formalize this through a five-dimensional competence framework capturing domain knowledge, standards expertise, operational experience, contextual understanding, and judgment.
  We introduce the competence shadow: the systematic narrowing of human reasoning induced by AI-generated safety analysis. The shadow is not what the AI presents, but what it prevents from being considered. We formalize four canonical human-AI collaboration structures and derive closed-form performance bounds, demonstrating that the competence shadow compounds multiplicatively to produce degradation far exceeding naive additive estimates.
  The central finding is that AI assistance in safety engineering is a collaboration design problem, not a software procurement decision. The same tool degrades or improves analysis quality depending entirely on how it is used. We derive non-degradation conditions for shadow-resistant workflows and call for a shift from tool qualification toward workflow qualification for trustworthy Physical AI.

AI.tldr: Proposes a formal framework to study AI assistance in safety analysis, introducing the 'competence shadow' and showing that analysis quality depends on collaboration design; yields conditions to avoid degradation and advocates workflow qualification over tool procurement.
AI.motivation: Safety analysis is multidimensional, context-dependent, incomplete, and subject to expert disagreement; benchmarked AI evaluation is inadequate for safety-critical tasks.
AI.method: Formalizes a five-dimensional competence framework (domain knowledge, standards expertise, operational experience, contextual understanding, judgment); defines competence shadow; models four collaboration structures; derives closed-form performance bounds; identifies non-degradation workflow conditions.
AI.result: Shows the competence shadow can multiplicatively degrade performance depending on collaboration structure; provides bounds and design guidelines; demonstrates that AI assistance quality hinges on workflow design rather than tool choice.
AI.conclusion: AI assistance in safety engineering is a collaboration design problem; only shadow-resistant workflows ensure trustworthiness; advocates shifting emphasis from tool qualification to workflow qualification for trustworthy Physical AI safety.

title: Can Users Specify Driving Speed? Bench2Drive-Speed: Benchmark and Baselines for Desired-Speed Conditioned Autonomous Driving
summary: End-to-end autonomous driving (E2E-AD) has achieved remarkable progress. However, one practical and useful function has been long overlooked: users may wish to customize the desired speed of the policy or specify whether to allow the autonomous vehicle to overtake. To bridge this gap, we present Bench2Drive-Speed, a benchmark with metrics, dataset, and baselines for desired-speed conditioned autonomous driving. We introduce explicit inputs of users' desired target-speed and overtake/follow instructions to driving policy models. We design quantitative metrics, including Speed-Adherence Score and Overtake Score, to measure how faithfully policies follow user specifications, while remaining compatible with standard autonomous driving metrics. To enable training of speed-conditioned policies, one approach is to collect expert demonstrations that strictly follow speed requirements, an expensive and unscalable process in the real world. An alternative is to adapt existing regular driving data by treating the speed observed in future frames as the target speed for training. To investigate this, we construct CustomizedSpeedDataset, composed of 2,100 clips annotated with experts demonstrations, enabling systematic investigation of supervision strategies. Our experiments show that, under proper re-annotation, models trained on regular driving data perform comparably to on expert demonstrations, suggesting that speed supervision can be introduced without additional complex real-world data collection. Furthermore, we find that while target-speed following can be achieved without degrading regular driving performance, executing overtaking commands remains challenging due to the inherent difficulty of interactive behaviors. All code, datasets and baselines are available at https://github.com/Thinklab-SJTU/Bench2Drive-Speed

AI.tldr: Bench2Drive-Speed introduces a speed-conditioned driving benchmark with explicit user inputs, novel metrics, and a dataset; shows speed supervision can be learned from regular driving data; overtaking remains challenging; codebase is released.
AI.motivation: Addresses the practical need for user-controlled speed and overtaking decisions in end-to-end autonomous driving. Existing benchmarks overlook explicit speed guidance; proposes a framework with explicit inputs and compatibility with standard metrics, enabling scalable supervision via re-annotated data.
AI.method: Define explicit inputs: user target speed and overtake/follow instructions. Introduce Speed-Adherence Score and Overtake Score. Create CustomizedSpeedDataset (≈2,100 clips) with expert demonstrations. Train speed-conditioned policies by re-annotating future-frame speeds as targets. Evaluate against standard driving metrics.
AI.result: Demonstrates that speed-following can be learned from regular driving data with proper re-annotation, with performance comparable to expert demonstrations. Target-speed adherence does not degrade regular driving performance, but overtaking commands remain challenging due to the interactive nature of such maneuvers. Code, datasets, and baselines are released.
AI.conclusion: Bench2Drive-Speed provides a practical benchmark to study speed-conditioned autonomous driving, enabling scalable supervision and evaluation. It highlights that speed control is feasible with regular data, while overtaking remains a hard open problem.

title: ICTPolarReal: A Polarized Reflection and Material Dataset of Real World Objects
summary: Accurately modeling how real-world materials reflect light remains a core challenge in inverse rendering, largely due to the scarcity of real measured reflectance data. Existing approaches rely heavily on synthetic datasets with simplified illumination and limited material realism, preventing models from generalizing to real-world images. We introduce a large-scale polarized reflection and material dataset of real-world objects, captured with an 8-camera, 346-light Light Stage equipped with cross/parallel polarization. Our dataset spans 218 everyday objects across five acquisition dimensions-multiview, multi-illumination, polarization, reflectance separation, and material attributes-yielding over 1.2M high-resolution images with diffuse-specular separation and analytically derived diffuse albedo, specular albedo, and surface normals. Using this dataset, we train and evaluate state-of-the-art inverse and forward rendering models on intrinsic decomposition, relighting, and sparse-view 3D reconstruction, demonstrating significant improvements in material separation, illumination fidelity, and geometric consistency. We hope that our work can establish a new foundation for physically grounded material understanding and enable real-world generalization beyond synthetic training regimes. Project page: https://jingyangcarl.github.io/ICTPolarReal/

AI.tldr: Introduces ICTPolarReal, a large-scale real-world polarized reflectance dataset captured with an 8-camera Light Stage, enabling high-fidelity intrinsic decomposition, relighting, and 3D reconstruction with real materials.
AI.motivation: Scarcity of real measured reflectance data hinders inverse rendering and model generalization; synthetic diets lack material realism; polarization, multiview, and multilight capture enable more accurate material separation and physically grounded modeling.
AI.method: Hardware setup with 8 cameras and 346 lights, cross/parallel polarization; five acquisition dimensions (multiview, multi-illumination, polarization, reflectance separation, material attributes); analytic derivation of diffuse albedo, specular albedo, and surface normals; ~1.2M high-resolution images with diffuse-specular separation.
AI.result: Trains and evaluates state-of-the-art inverse and forward rendering models on intrinsic decomposition, relighting, and sparse-view 3D reconstruction; shows significant improvements in material separation accuracy, illumination fidelity, and geometric consistency; provides a foundation for real-world generalization.
AI.conclusion: The dataset advances physically grounded material understanding and supports generalization beyond synthetic training, with a project page for details.

title: SEVerA: Verified Synthesis of Self-Evolving Agents
summary: Recent advances have shown the effectiveness of self-evolving LLM agents on tasks such as program repair and scientific discovery. In this paradigm, a planner LLM synthesizes an agent program that invokes parametric models, including LLMs, which are then tuned per task to improve performance. However, existing self-evolving agent frameworks provide no formal guarantees of safety or correctness. Because such programs are often executed autonomously on unseen inputs, this lack of guarantees raises reliability and security concerns. We formulate agentic code generation as a constrained learning problem, combining hard formal specifications with soft objectives capturing task utility. We introduce Formally Guarded Generative Models (FGGM), which allow the planner LLM to specify a formal output contract for each generative model call using first-order logic. Each FGGM call wraps the underlying model in a rejection sampler with a verified fallback, ensuring every returned output satisfies the contract for any input and parameter setting. Building on FGGM, we present SEVerA (Self-Evolving Verified Agents), a three-stage framework: Search synthesizes candidate parametric programs containing FGGM calls; Verification proves correctness with respect to hard constraints for all parameter values, reducing the problem to unconstrained learning; and Learning applies scalable gradient-based optimization, including GRPO-style fine-tuning, to improve the soft objective while preserving correctness. We evaluate SEVerA on Dafny program verification, symbolic math synthesis, and policy-compliant agentic tool use ($τ^2$-bench). Across tasks, SEVerA achieves zero constraint violations while improving performance over unconstrained and SOTA baselines, showing that formal behavioral constraints not only guarantee correctness but also steer synthesis toward higher-quality agents.

AI.tldr: A formal framework for safe, self-evolving agents, introducing FGGM contracts and a three-stage SEVerA pipeline, yielding zero constraint violations and improved performance.
AI.motivation: To provide formal safety and correctness guarantees for self-evolving agent frameworks that autonomously execute on unseen inputs, by coupling hard formal specifications with soft task objectives.
AI.method: FGGM: The planner LLM emits first-order-logic contracts for each generative-model call; each call is wrapped by a rejection sampler with a verified fallback to ensure outputs satisfy the contract for all inputs/parameters. SEVerA: a three-stage pipeline — Search synthesizes candidate parametric programs with FGGM calls; Verification proves correctness for all parameter values (reducing to unconstrained learning); Learning applies scalable gradient-based optimization (including GRPO-style fine-tuning) while preserving correctness.
AI.result: Evaluations on Dafny program verification, symbolic math synthesis, and policy-compliant tool-use (τ^2-bench) show zero constraint violations and performance gains over unconstrained and SOTA baselines.
AI.conclusion: Formally guarded generative models and the SEVerA framework enable provable safety/correctness for self-evolving agents and guide synthesis toward higher-quality, policy-compliant behaviors.

title: Probabilistic Abstract Interpretation on Neural Networks via Grids Approximation
summary: Probabilistic abstract interpretation is a theory used to extract particular properties of a computer program when it is infeasible to test every single inputs. In this paper we apply the theory on neural networks for the same purpose: to analyse density distribution flow of all possible inputs of a neural network when a network has uncountably many or countable but infinitely many inputs. We show how this theoretical framework works in neural networks and then discuss different abstract domains and corresponding Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverses together with abstract transformers used in the framework. We also present experimental examples to show how this framework helps to analyse real world problems.

AI.tldr: Introduces probabilistic abstract interpretation for neural networks to analyze the density flow of all possible inputs, using abstract domains, Moore-Penrose inverses, and abstract transformers, with experimental validation.
AI.motivation: Address the infeasibility of testing all inputs to neural networks by extending probabilistic abstract interpretation to analyze input-density distributions and behavior over uncountably (or countably infinite) input spaces.
AI.method: Apply the probabilistic abstract interpretation framework to neural networks, discuss suitable abstract domains and their Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverses, define and use abstract transformers within the framework, and validate with experimental examples on real-world problems.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the framework can model and analyze the flow of input density through neural networks and provides experimental illustrations showing its applicability to real-world problems.
AI.conclusion: The probabilistic abstract interpretation approach offers a principled method for analyzing neural networks under uncertainty and input-density distributions, with critical dependence on the choice of domains and inverses and potential for broad real-world impact.

title: Persistent Robot World Models: Stabilizing Multi-Step Rollouts via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Action-conditioned robot world models generate future video frames of the manipulated scene given a robot action sequence, offering a promising alternative for simulating tasks that are difficult to model with traditional physics engines. However, these models are optimized for short-term prediction and break down when deployed autoregressively: each predicted clip feeds back as context for the next, causing errors to compound and visual quality to rapidly degrade. We address this through the following contributions. First, we introduce a reinforcement learning (RL) post-training scheme that trains the world model on its own autoregressive rollouts rather than on ground-truth histories. We achieve this by adapting a recent contrastive RL objective for diffusion models to our setting and show that its convergence guarantees carry over exactly. Second, we design a training protocol that generates and compares multiple candidate variable-length futures from the same rollout state, reinforcing higher-fidelity predictions over lower-fidelity ones. Third, we develop efficient, multi-view visual fidelity rewards that combine complementary perceptual metrics across camera views and are aggregated at the clip level for dense, low-variance training signal. Fourth, we show that our approach establishes a new state-of-the-art for rollout fidelity on the DROID dataset, outperforming the strongest baseline on all metrics (e.g., LPIPS reduced by 14% on external cameras, SSIM improved by 9.1% on the wrist camera), winning 98% of paired comparisons, and achieving an 80% preference rate in a blind human study.

AI.tldr: An action-conditioned world model is trained and evaluated for long-horizon video rollout fidelity. The paper introduces (i) an RL post-training scheme using autoregressive rollouts, (ii) a protocol to compare multiple candidate futures from the same state, (iii) multi-view perceptual fidelity rewards, and (iv) achieves state-of-the-art rollout fidelity on the DROID dataset with strong quantitative and human evaluation results.
AI.motivation: Standard world models optimize short-term predictions and suffer from compounding errors when rolled out autoregressively, limiting their usefulness for long-horizon planning and simulation. A robust solution requires training with autoregressive contexts, evaluating multiple futures, and leveraging multi-view perceptual signals.
AI.method: 1) RL post-training that trains the world model on autoregressive rollouts using a contrastive RL objective adapted from diffusion models, with convergence guarantees carried over. 2) A training protocol that generates and ranks multiple candidate variable-length futures from the same rollout state to reinforce higher-fidelity predictions. 3) Efficient multi-view visual fidelity rewards that combine perceptual metrics across camera views, aggregated at the clip level for dense training signals. 4) Empirical evaluation on the DROID dataset, showing improved rollout fidelity.
AI.result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art rollout fidelity on DROID, outperforming the strongest baseline on all metrics (e.g., LPIPS reduced by 14% for external cameras, SSIM improved by 9.1% on wrist camera), with 98% of paired comparisons and 80% human preference in blind studies.
AI.conclusion: Training world models with autoregressive rollouts, multi-view fidelity signals, and candidate-futures ranking yields significant gains in long-horizon video prediction fidelity, establishing a new performance baseline on DROID and addressing error compounding in autoregressive video forecasting.

title: TIGFlow-GRPO: Trajectory Forecasting via Interaction-Aware Flow Matching and Reward-Driven Optimization
summary: Human trajectory forecasting is important for intelligent multimedia systems operating in visually complex environments, such as autonomous driving and crowd surveillance. Although Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) has shown strong ability in modeling trajectory distributions from spatio-temporal observations, existing approaches still focus primarily on supervised fitting, which may leave social norms and scene constraints insufficiently reflected in generated trajectories. To address this issue, we propose TIGFlow-GRPO, a two-stage generative framework that aligns flow-based trajectory generation with behavioral rules. In the first stage, we build a CFM-based predictor with a Trajectory-Interaction-Graph (TIG) module to model fine-grained visual-spatial interactions and strengthen context encoding. This stage captures both agent-agent and agent-scene relations more effectively, providing more informative conditional features for subsequent alignment. In the second stage, we perform Flow-GRPO post-training,where deterministic flow rollout is reformulated as stochastic ODE-to-SDE sampling to enable trajectory exploration, and a composite reward combines view-aware social compliance with map-aware physical feasibility. By evaluating trajectories explored through SDE rollout, GRPO progressively steers multimodal predictions toward behaviorally plausible futures. Experiments on the ETH/UCY and SDD datasets show that TIGFlow-GRPO improves forecasting accuracy and long-horizon stability while generating trajectories that are more socially compliant and physically feasible. These results suggest that the proposed framework provides an effective way to connect flow-based trajectory modeling with behavior-aware alignment in dynamic multimedia environments.

AI.tldr: A two-stage TIGFlow-GRPO framework enhances flow-based trajectory forecasting by integrating a Trajectory-Interaction-Graph for rich context encoding and a post-training GRPO module that uses stochastic ODE-to-SDE sampling with a composite reward, yielding more accurate, stable, and socially/physically plausible trajectories.
AI.motivation: CFM-based trajectory models primarily rely on supervised fitting and may underrepresent social norms and scene constraints. There is a need to explicitly enforce behavioral rules and richer spatio-temporal interactions to improve realism and safety in predictions.
AI.method: Stage 1: Build a Conditional Flow Matching (CFM) predictor augmented with a Trajectory-Interaction-Graph (TIG) to model fine-grained agent-agent and agent-scene interactions, improving conditional feature quality. Stage 2: Flow-GRPO post-training converts deterministic flow rollout into stochastic ODE-to-SDE sampling to enable trajectory exploration; employ a composite reward combining view-aware social compliance and map-aware physical feasibility to steer multimodal predictions toward behaviorally plausible futures.
AI.result: On ETH/UCY and SDD datasets, TIGFlow-GRPO improves forecasting accuracy and long-horizon stability, producing trajectories that are more socially compliant and physically feasible compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively connects flow-based trajectory modeling with behavior-aware alignment, enabling more realistic and constraint-consistent trajectory predictions in dynamic multimedia environments.

title: Distribution and Clusters Approximations as Abstract Domains in Probabilistic Abstract Interpretation to Neural Network Analysis
summary: The probabilistic abstract interpretation framework of neural network analysis analyzes a neural network by analyzing its density distribution flow of all possible inputs. The grids approximation is one of abstract domains the framework uses which abstracts concrete space into grids. In this paper, we introduce two novel approximation methods: distribution approximation and clusters approximation. We show how these two methods work in theory with corresponding abstract transformers with help of illustrations of some simple examples.

AI.tldr: Two new grid-based approximation methods (distribution and clusters) within a probabilistic abstract interpretation framework for neural network analysis; theoretical development with abstract transformers illustrated on simple examples.
AI.motivation: To analyze neural networks by tracking the density-flow of inputs using a grid-based abstract domain; discretizes the input space to make probabilistic analysis tractable and scalable.
AI.method: Introduce distribution approximation and clusters approximation within the probabilistic abstract interpretation framework; derive corresponding abstract transformers; provide theoretical exposition and simple illustrative examples.
AI.result: Formalization and theoretical justification of both approximation methods; construction of abstract transformers; demonstration via simple examples showing how density distributions can be approximated and how clusters capture input structure.
AI.conclusion: Extends grid-based probabilistic abstract interpretation for neural networks, offering new tools for density-flow analysis; suggests potential gains in precision and scalability and lays groundwork for future empirical validation on real networks.

title: Infinite Gaze Generation for Videos with Autoregressive Diffusion
summary: Predicting human gaze in video is fundamental to advancing scene understanding and multimodal interaction. While traditional saliency maps provide spatial probability distributions and scanpaths offer ordered fixations, both abstractions often collapse the fine-grained temporal dynamics of raw gaze. Furthermore, existing models are typically constrained to short-term windows ($\approx$ 3-5s), failing to capture the long-range behavioral dependencies inherent in real-world content. We present a generative framework for infinite-horizon raw gaze prediction in videos of arbitrary length. By leveraging an autoregressive diffusion model, we synthesize gaze trajectories characterized by continuous spatial coordinates and high-resolution timestamps. Our model is conditioned on a saliency-aware visual latent space. Quantitative and qualitative evaluations demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms existing approaches in long-range spatio-temporal accuracy and trajectory realism.

AI.tldr: An autoregressive diffusion model for infinite-horizon, high-resolution gaze prediction in videos, yielding continuous 2D gaze trajectories with timestamps, conditioned on a saliency-aware latent space; outperforms short-horizon baselines in long-range spatio-temporal accuracy and realism.
AI.motivation: Current saliency representations (maps, scanpaths) ignore fine-grained temporal dynamics and long-range dependencies; existing models are limited to short windows (~3–5s), hindering realistic gaze synthesis for longer content.
AI.method: Proposes an autoregressive diffusion framework that generates raw gaze trajectories with continuous spatial coordinates and high-resolution timestamps for videos of arbitrary length. The model is conditioned on a saliency-aware visual latent space to align predictions with salient content.
AI.result: Quantitative and qualitative evaluations indicate significant improvements over existing approaches in long-range spatio-temporal accuracy and trajectory realism.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables infinite-horizon, realistic gaze prediction for arbitrarily long videos, offering a scalable pathway for integrating gaze modeling into long-form video understanding and multimodal interaction.

title: Train at Moving Edge: Online-Verified Prompt Selection for Efficient RL Training of Large Reasoning Model
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become essential for post-training large language models (LLMs) in reasoning tasks. While scaling rollouts can stabilize training and enhance performance, the computational overhead is a critical issue. In algorithms like GRPO, multiple rollouts per prompt incur prohibitive costs, as a large portion of prompts provide negligible gradients and are thus of low utility. To address this problem, we investigate how to select high-utility prompts before the rollout phase. Our experimental analysis reveals that sample utility is non-uniform and evolving: the strongest learning signals concentrate at the ``learning edge", the intersection of intermediate difficulty and high uncertainty, which shifts as training proceeds. Motivated by this, we propose HIVE (History-Informed and online-VErified prompt selection), a dual-stage framework for data-efficient RL. HIVE utilizes historical reward trajectories for coarse selection and employs prompt entropy as a real-time proxy to prune instances with stale utility. By evaluating HIVE across multiple math reasoning benchmarks and models, we show that HIVE yields significant rollout efficiency without compromising performance.

AI.tldr: A data-efficient RL framework (HIVE) for post-training LLMs that selects high-utility prompts before rollouts using (i) historical reward trajectories for coarse selection and (ii) prompt entropy for online pruning; achieves large rollout efficiency on math reasoning benchmarks without harming performance.
AI.motivation: Rollouts are costly; most prompts yield negligible gradients; utility is non-uniform and evolves; the strongest learning signals appear at the 'learning edge' (intermediate difficulty, high uncertainty) which shifts during training.
AI.method: Propose HIVE, a dual-stage framework: Stage 1 History-Informed coarse selection using past reward trajectories to preselect prompts; Stage 2 online Verified pruning using prompt entropy to discard prompts with stale or low current utility; evaluated on multiple math benchmarks and models.
AI.result: HIVE yields significant rollout efficiency without compromising performance across multiple math reasoning benchmarks and model sizes.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates data-efficient RL for LLMs via adaptive prompt selection; reveals shifting learning edge and the utility of history and entropy in guiding rollout decisions.

title: SoftMimicGen: A Data Generation System for Scalable Robot Learning in Deformable Object Manipulation
summary: Large-scale robot datasets have facilitated the learning of a wide range of robot manipulation skills, but these datasets remain difficult to collect and scale further, owing to the intractable amount of human time, effort, and cost required. Simulation and synthetic data generation have proven to be an effective alternative to fuel this need for data, especially with the advent of recent work showing that such synthetic datasets can dramatically reduce real-world data requirements and facilitate generalization to novel scenarios unseen in real-world demonstrations. However, this paradigm has been limited to rigid-body tasks, which are easy to simulate. Deformable object manipulation encompasses a large portion of real-world manipulation and remains a crucial gap to address towards increasing adoption of the synthetic simulation data paradigm. In this paper, we introduce SoftMimicGen, an automated data generation pipeline for deformable object manipulation tasks. We introduce a suite of high-fidelity simulation environments that encompasses a wide range of deformable objects (stuffed animal, rope, tissue, towel) and manipulation behaviors (high-precision threading, dynamic whipping, folding, pick-and-place), across four robot embodiments: a single-arm manipulator, bimanual arms, a humanoid, and a surgical robot. We apply SoftMimicGen to generate datasets across the task suite, train high-performing policies from the data, and systematically analyze the data generation system. Project website: \href{https://softmimicgen.github.io}{softmimicgen.github.io}.

AI.tldr: SoftMimicGen introduces a data-generation pipeline and high-fidelity deformable-object simulators across multiple robots, enabling synthetic datasets and trained policies for deformable manipulation.
AI.motivation: Deformable object manipulation is essential but underrepresented in synthetic data; scaling synthetic data reduces real-world data needs and improves generalization, addressing a gap left by rigid-body-focused simulations.
AI.method: An automated pipeline (SoftMimicGen) paired with high-fidelity simulators for deformables (stuffed animal, rope, tissue, towel) and manipulation behaviors (high-precision threading, dynamic whipping, folding, pick-and-place) across four robot embodiments; generate datasets, train policies, and systematically analyze the data-generation system.
AI.result: Generated deformable-object datasets; trained high-performing policies; provided analysis of data-generation system; demonstrated cross-robot applicability (project website).
AI.conclusion: SoftMimicGen enables scalable synthetic data for deformable manipulation, broad environment support, and potential generalization to new tasks and robots; supports broader adoption of simulation-based data in deformable manipulation.

title: Drive My Way: Preference Alignment of Vision-Language-Action Model for Personalized Driving
comment: IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR 2026); Project website: https://dmw-cvpr.github.io/
summary: Human driving behavior is inherently personal, which is shaped by long-term habits and influenced by short-term intentions. Individuals differ in how they accelerate, brake, merge, yield, and overtake across diverse situations. However, existing end-to-end autonomous driving systems either optimize for generic objectives or rely on fixed driving modes, lacking the ability to adapt to individual preferences or interpret natural language intent. To address this gap, we propose Drive My Way (DMW), a personalized Vision-Language-Action (VLA) driving framework that aligns with users' long-term driving habits and adapts to real-time user instructions. DMW learns a user embedding from our personalized driving dataset collected across multiple real drivers and conditions the policy on this embedding during planning, while natural language instructions provide additional short-term guidance. Closed-loop evaluation on the Bench2Drive benchmark demonstrates that DMW improves style instruction adaptation, and user studies show that its generated behaviors are recognizable as each driver's own style, highlighting personalization as a key capability for human-centered autonomous driving. Our data and code are available at https://dmw-cvpr.github.io/.

AI.tldr: DMW is a personalized Vision-Language-Action driving framework that learns individual driver embeddings to tailor planning and uses natural language as short-term guidance, enabling end-to-end driving that matches each driver's style.
AI.motivation: Standard end-to-end driving lacks personalization and interpretable intent; there is a need to adapt to long-term driving habits and short-term user instructions.
AI.method: Train a user embedding from a multi-driver driving dataset and condition the policy on this embedding during planning; incorporate natural language instructions as auxiliary guidance within a Vision-Language-Action framework; evaluate with closed-loop Bench2Drive and user studies.
AI.result: Closed-loop Bench2Drive experiments show improved adaptation to style instructions; user studies indicate generated behaviors are recognizable as each driver's own style.
AI.conclusion: Personalization is a key capability for human-centered autonomous driving; DMW demonstrates effective integration of long-term habits and real-time intent, with public data and code.

title: BiFM: Bidirectional Flow Matching for Few-Step Image Editing and Generation
summary: Recent diffusion and flow matching models have demonstrated strong capabilities in image generation and editing by progressively removing noise through iterative sampling. While this enables flexible inversion for semantic-preserving edits, few-step sampling regimes suffer from poor forward process approximation, leading to degraded editing quality. Existing few-step inversion methods often rely on pretrained generators and auxiliary modules, limiting scalability and generalization across different architectures. To address these limitations, we propose BiFM (Bidirectional Flow Matching), a unified framework that jointly learns generation and inversion within a single model. BiFM directly estimates average velocity fields in both ``image $\to$ noise" and ``noise $\to$ image" directions, constrained by a shared instantaneous velocity field derived from either predefined schedules or pretrained multi-step diffusion models. Additionally, BiFM introduces a novel training strategy using continuous time-interval supervision, stabilized by a bidirectional consistency objective and a lightweight time-interval embedding. This bidirectional formulation also enables one-step inversion and can integrate seamlessly into popular diffusion and flow matching backbones. Across diverse image editing and generation tasks, BiFM consistently outperforms existing few-step approaches, achieving superior performance and editability.

AI.tldr: BiFM presents a unified model for generation and inversion in diffusion-based image generation/editing by bidirectionally estimating average velocity fields, enabling one-step inversion and improved few-step editing.
AI.motivation: Current few-step sampling for diffusion models suffers from forward-process approximation errors, leading to degraded editing quality; existing methods rely on pretrained generators and auxiliary modules, hurting scalability and generalization.
AI.method: Jointly learn generation and inversion using a single model that estimates average velocity fields in image->noise and noise->image directions, constrained by a shared instantaneous velocity field derived from predefined schedules or pretrained multi-step diffusion models; train with continuous time-interval supervision, bidirectional consistency loss, and lightweight time-interval embedding; supports integration into diffusion and flow matching backbones; enables one-step inversion.
AI.result: BiFM consistently outperforms existing few-step approaches across diverse image editing and generation tasks, achieving superior performance and editability.
AI.conclusion: A unified, scalable framework that improves inversion and editing in diffusion models via bidirectional flow matching and shared velocity fields, with potential for broader backbone compatibility and one-step inversion.

title: DAGverse: Building Document-Grounded Semantic DAGs from Scientific Papers
summary: Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) are widely used to represent structured knowledge in scientific and technical domains. However, datasets for real-world DAGs remain scarce because constructing them typically requires expert interpretation of domain documents. We study Doc2SemDAG construction: recovering a preferred semantic DAG from a document together with the cited evidence and context that explain it. This problem is challenging because a document may admit multiple plausible abstractions, the intended structure is often implicit, and the supporting evidence is scattered across prose, equations, captions, and figures. To address these challenges, we leverage scientific papers containing explicit DAG figures as a natural source of supervision. In this setting, the DAG figure provides the DAG structure, while the accompanying text provides context and explanation. We introduce DAGverse, a framework for constructing document-grounded semantic DAGs from online scientific papers. Its core component, DAGverse-Pipeline, is a semi-automatic system designed to produce high-precision semantic DAG examples through figure classification, graph reconstruction, semantic grounding, and validation. As a case study, we test the framework for causal DAGs and release DAGverse-1, a dataset of 108 expert-validated semantic DAGs with graph-level, node-level, and edge-level evidence. Experiments show that DAGverse-Pipeline outperforms existing Vision-Language Models on DAG classification and annotation. DAGverse provides a foundation for document-grounded DAG benchmarks and opens new directions for studying structured reasoning grounded in real-world evidence.

AI.tldr: Proposes DAGverse to construct document-grounded semantic DAGs from scientific papers, using DAG figure supervision; releases DAGverse-1 with 108 expert-validated semantic DAGs; shows DAGverse-Pipeline surpasses Vision-Language Models on DAG tasks; establishes a benchmark for document-grounded DAG reasoning.
AI.motivation: Real-world DAG datasets are scarce because they require expert interpretation of domain documents. Documents may support multiple plausible abstractions and evidence is dispersed across text and figures. Exploiting DAG figures as supervision enables high-quality, real-world DAG extraction.
AI.method: Introduce DAGverse framework and its DAGverse-Pipeline, a semi-automatic system that performs figure classification, graph reconstruction, semantic grounding, and validation to produce semantic DAGs grounded in documents. Use scientific papers with explicit DAG figures as supervision; case study on causal DAGs; release DAGverse-1 with 108 expert-validated DAGs including graph-, node-, and edge-level evidence.
AI.result: DAGverse-Pipeline achieves high-precision semantic DAG extraction; dataset DAGverse-1 contains 108 expert-validated semantic DAGs with multi-level evidence; outperforms existing Vision-Language Models on DAG classification and annotation.
AI.conclusion: Provides a foundation for document-grounded DAG benchmarks and enables study of structured reasoning grounded in real-world evidence; opens new directions for research on reasoning with semantically grounded DAGs.

title: Select, Hypothesize and Verify: Towards Verified Neuron Concept Interpretation
summary: It is essential for understanding neural network decisions to interpret the functionality (also known as concepts) of neurons. Existing approaches describe neuron concepts by generating natural language descriptions, thereby advancing the understanding of the neural network's decision-making mechanism. However, these approaches assume that each neuron has well-defined functions and provides discriminative features for neural network decision-making. In fact, some neurons may be redundant or may offer misleading concepts. Thus, the descriptions for such neurons may cause misinterpretations of the factors driving the neural network's decisions. To address the issue, we introduce a verification of neuron functions, which checks whether the generated concept highly activates the corresponding neuron. Furthermore, we propose a Select-Hypothesize-Verify framework for interpreting neuron functionality. This framework consists of: 1) selecting activation samples that best capture a neuron's well-defined functional behavior through activation-distribution analysis; 2) forming hypotheses about concepts for the selected neurons; and 3) verifying whether the generated concepts accurately reflect the functionality of the neuron. Extensive experiments show that our method produces more accurate neuron concepts. Our generated concepts activate the corresponding neurons with a probability approximately 1.5 times that of the current state-of-the-art method.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Select-Hypothesize-Verify (SHV) framework to interpret neuron functionality by selecting activation samples, hypothesizing concepts, and verifying concept-neuron alignment; adds a neuron function verification step; reports ~1.5x higher activation probability for concepts than the state of the art.
AI.motivation: Current neuron-concept descriptions can be misleading or redundant if the concepts do not truly reflect a neuron’s function. There is a need to verify that generated concepts actually activate the target neuron to improve reliability of model interpretations.
AI.method: Introduce a verification component that checks whether a generated concept highly activates the corresponding neuron. Propose a Select-Hypothesize-Verify (SHV) pipeline: (1) activation-distribution analysis to select samples that capture well-defined functional behavior of neurons; (2) form hypotheses about concepts for the selected neurons; (3) verify whether the generated concepts reflect the neuron’s functionality.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show that the method produces more accurate neuron concepts. The generated concepts activate the corresponding neurons with a probability approximately 1.5 times that of the current state-of-the-art method.
AI.conclusion: SHV provides more reliable and faithful interpretations of neural decisions by ensuring that generated neuron concepts correspond to actual neuron functionality, reducing the risk of misleading explanations and improving interpretation quality.

title: Evaluating Language Models for Harmful Manipulation
summary: Interest in the concept of AI-driven harmful manipulation is growing, yet current approaches to evaluating it are limited. This paper introduces a framework for evaluating harmful AI manipulation via context-specific human-AI interaction studies. We illustrate the utility of this framework by assessing an AI model with 10,101 participants spanning interactions in three AI use domains (public policy, finance, and health) and three locales (US, UK, and India). Overall, we find that that the tested model can produce manipulative behaviours when prompted to do so and, in experimental settings, is able to induce belief and behaviour changes in study participants. We further find that context matters: AI manipulation differs between domains, suggesting that it needs to be evaluated in the high-stakes context(s) in which an AI system is likely to be used. We also identify significant differences across our tested geographies, suggesting that AI manipulation results from one geographic region may not generalise to others. Finally, we find that the frequency of manipulative behaviours (propensity) of an AI model is not consistently predictive of the likelihood of manipulative success (efficacy), underscoring the importance of studying these dimensions separately. To facilitate adoption of our evaluation framework, we detail our testing protocols and make relevant materials publicly available. We conclude by discussing open challenges in evaluating harmful manipulation by AI models.

AI.tldr: A framework to evaluate harmful AI manipulation via context-specific human–AI interaction studies, demonstrated with a large multi-domain, multi-location experiment; results show context and geography affect manipulation; propensity and efficacy are distinct; protocols released.
AI.motivation: Addresses limitations of current evaluation approaches for harmful AI manipulation; emphasizes high-stakes, context-sensitive, cross-cultural generalizability; aims to enable robust, standardized assessment and policy-relevant findings.
AI.method: Proposes a framework for context-specific HAI interaction studies; empirical evaluation involving 10,101 participants across three domains (public policy, finance, health) and three locales (US, UK, India); experiments where AI is prompted to manipulate; measures belief/behavior changes; analyzes domain/geography effects; separates propensity (frequency) from efficacy (success rate); provides publicly available protocols and materials.
AI.result: AI model can exhibit manipulative behaviors when prompted; it can induce belief/behavior changes; context affects manipulation across domains; geographic differences observed; propensity does not reliably predict efficacy; open challenges identified.
AI.conclusion: Context-aware evaluation is essential; high-stakes environments require domain- and locale-specific testing; results may not generalize across regions; separate measurement of propensity and efficacy; publicly released protocols facilitate adoption; further work needed to address ongoing challenges in evaluating harmful AI manipulation.

title: Self-Corrected Image Generation with Explainable Latent Rewards
