summary: Despite significant progress in text-to-image generation, aligning outputs with complex prompts remains challenging, particularly for fine-grained semantics and spatial relations. This difficulty stems from the feed-forward nature of generation, which requires anticipating alignment without fully understanding the output. In contrast, evaluating generated images is more tractable. Motivated by this asymmetry, we propose xLARD, a self-correcting framework that uses multimodal large language models to guide generation through Explainable LAtent RewarDs. xLARD introduces a lightweight corrector that refines latent representations based on structured feedback from model-generated references. A key component is a differentiable mapping from latent edits to interpretable reward signals, enabling continuous latent-level guidance from non-differentiable image-level evaluations. This mechanism allows the model to understand, assess, and correct itself during generation. Experiments across diverse generation and editing tasks show that xLARD improves semantic alignment and visual fidelity while maintaining generative priors. Code is available at https://yinyiluo.github.io/xLARD/.

AI.tldr: Proposes xLARD, a self-correcting framework that refines latent representations via differentiable latent-edit rewards guided by multimodal LLMs, improving alignment and fidelity in text-to-image generation.
AI.motivation: To overcome the persistent misalignment between fine-grained prompts and generated images due to the feed-forward nature of generation; image-level evaluation is easier, suggesting a corrective loop leveraging latent-level guidance.
AI.method: A lightweight latent-space corrector that uses model-generated references and a differentiable mapping from latent edits to interpretable reward signals, enabling continuous guidance from non-differentiable image metrics; integrates Explainable Latent Rewards.
AI.result: Across diverse generation and editing tasks, xLARD improves semantic alignment and visual fidelity while preserving generative priors; code released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: xLARD demonstrates that self-correcting, latent-space guidance informed by multimodal feedback can bridge evaluation and generation, with broad applicability to text-to-image systems and potential extensions to other modalities.

title: Offline Decision Transformers for Neural Combinatorial Optimization: Surpassing Heuristics on the Traveling Salesman Problem
comment: 11 pages, 1 figures. Accepted at NeurIPS 2025 Workshop on DiffCoALG
summary: Combinatorial optimization problems like the Traveling Salesman Problem are critical in industry yet NP-hard. Neural Combinatorial Optimization has shown promise, but its reliance on online reinforcement learning (RL) hampers deployment and underutilizes decades of algorithmic knowledge. We address these limitations by applying the offline RL framework, Decision Transformer, to learn superior strategies directly from datasets of heuristic solutions; it aims to not only to imitate but to synthesize and outperform them. Concretely, we (i) integrate a Pointer Network to handle the instance-dependent, variable action space of node selection, and (ii) employ expectile regression for optimistic conditioning of Return-to-Go, which is crucial for instances with widely varying optimal values. Experiments show that our method consistently produces higher-quality tours than the four classical heuristics it is trained on, demonstrating the potential of offline RL to unlock and exceed the performance embedded in existing domain knowledge.

AI.tldr: Offline reinforcement learning (Decision Transformer) applied to combinatorial optimization (TSP). Integrates a Pointer Network to manage variable action space and expectile regression for optimistic conditioning of Return-to-Go, yielding tours higher quality than four classical heuristics it was trained on.
AI.motivation: NP-hard problems like the Traveling Salesman Problem are central to industry, but depend on online RL and underutilize decades of algorithmic heuristics. Offline RL can leverage existing heuristic data to synthesize and surpass them, enabling deployment without online learning.
AI.method: Use an offline RL framework (Decision Transformer) trained on datasets of heuristic solutions. Incorporate a Pointer Network to handle instance-dependent, variable action space for node selection. Apply expectile regression to condition Return-to-Go optimistically, addressing wide variation in optimal tour values.
AI.result: Empirical experiments show the approach consistently produces higher-quality tours than the four classical heuristics on which it was trained.
AI.conclusion: Offline RL can unlock and exceed embedded domain knowledge in combinatorial optimization, suggesting a path to synthesize and improve upon existing heuristics by learning from their data.

title: Macroscopic Characteristics of Mixed Traffic Flow with Deep Reinforcement Learning Based Automated and Human-Driven Vehicles
summary: Automated Vehicle (AV) control in mixed traffic, where AVs coexist with human-driven vehicles, poses significant challenges in balancing safety, efficiency, comfort, fuel efficiency, and compliance with traffic rules while capturing heterogeneous driver behavior. Traditional car-following models, such as the Intelligent Driver Model (IDM), often struggle to generalize across diverse traffic scenarios and typically do not account for fuel efficiency, motivating the use of learning-based approaches. Although Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has shown strong microscopic performance in car-following conditions, its macroscopic traffic flow characteristics remain underexplored. This study focuses on analyzing the macroscopic traffic flow characteristics and fuel efficiency of DRL-based models in mixed traffic. A Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (TD3) algorithm is implemented for AVs' control and trained using the NGSIM highway dataset, enabling realistic interaction with human-driven vehicles. Traffic performance is evaluated using the Fundamental Diagram (FD) under varying driver heterogeneity, heterogeneous time-gap penetration levels, and different shares of RL-controlled vehicles. A macroscopic level comparison of fuel efficiency between the RL-based AV model and the IDM is also conducted. Results show that traffic performance is sensitive to the distribution of safe time gaps and the proportion of RL vehicles. Transitioning from fully human-driven to fully RL-controlled traffic can increase road capacity by approximately 7.52%. Further, RL-based AVs also improve average fuel efficiency by about 28.98% at higher speeds (above 50 km/h), and by 1.86% at lower speeds (below 50 km/h) compared to the IDM. Overall, the DRL framework enhances traffic capacity and fuel efficiency without compromising safety.

AI.tldr: DRL-based autonomous vehicles (AVs) controlled by TD3, trained on NGSIM data, can improve macroscopic traffic flow and fuel efficiency in mixed traffic more than traditional IDM controls. The gains depend on driver heterogeneity and the share of RL-controlled vehicles, with about a 7.5% capacity increase when fully RL and notable fuel efficiency gains at higher speeds (~29% vs IDM, ~2% at lower speeds).
AI.motivation: Traditional car-following models like IDM struggle to generalize across diverse, mixed-traffic scenarios and often ignore fuel efficiency; learning-based control can adapt to heterogeneity and optimize for safety, efficiency, and emissions, but macroscopic effects of DRL policies are underexplored.
AI.method: Apply Twin Delayed DDPG (TD3) to control AVs. Train with the NGSIM highway dataset to capture realistic interactions with human drivers. Evaluate performance via the Fundamental Diagram under varying driver heterogeneity, heterogeneous time-gap penetration, and RL vehicle shares. Conduct a macroscopic fuel-efficiency comparison between the RL-based AVs and IDM.
AI.result: Traffic performance is sensitive to the distribution of safe time gaps and the proportion of RL vehicles. Transitioning from fully human-driven to fully RL-controlled traffic increases road capacity by ~7.52%. RL-based AVs improve average fuel efficiency by ~28.98% at speeds >50 km/h and by ~1.86% at speeds <50 km/h, compared to IDM.
AI.conclusion: The DRL framework can enhance both traffic capacity and fuel efficiency in mixed traffic without compromising safety, demonstrating favorable macroscopic benefits when RL control is deployed with consideration of driver heterogeneity and RL penetration.

title: LaMP: Learning Vision-Language-Action Policies with 3D Scene Flow as Latent Motion Prior
summary: We introduce \textbf{LaMP}, a dual-expert Vision-Language-Action framework that embeds dense 3D scene flow as a latent motion prior for robotic manipulation. Existing VLA models regress actions directly from 2D semantic visual features, forcing them to learn complex 3D physical interactions implicitly. This implicit learning strategy degrades under unfamiliar spatial dynamics. LaMP addresses this limitation by aligning a flow-matching \emph{Motion Expert} with a policy-predicting \emph{Action Expert} through gated cross-attention. Specifically, the Motion Expert generates a one-step partially denoised 3D scene flow, and its hidden states condition the Action Expert without full multi-step reconstruction. We evaluate LaMP on the LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and SimplerEnv-WidowX simulation benchmarks as well as real-world experiments. LaMP consistently outperforms evaluated VLA baselines across LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, and SimplerEnv-WidowX benchmarks, achieving the highest reported average success rates under the same training budgets. On LIBERO-Plus OOD perturbations, LaMP shows improved robustness with an average 9.7% gain over the strongest prior baseline. Our project page is available at https://summerwxk.github.io/lamp-project-page/.

AI.tldr: LaMP is a dual-expert Vision-Language-Action framework for robotic manipulation that uses a latent 3D scene flow (Motion Expert) to condition a policy-predicting Action Expert via gated cross-attention, improving robustness and success rates across multiple benchmarks and showing OOD gains.
AI.motivation: Current VLA models regress actions from 2D visual features, forcing implicit learning of 3D interactions which degrades under unfamiliar spatial dynamics. A latent 3D motion prior could stabilize policy learning and improve robustness.
AI.method: A dual-expert architecture: a Motion Expert generates a one-step partially denoised 3D scene flow; its hidden states are used to condition the Action Expert through gated cross-attention, aligning motion and action predictions without full multi-step reconstruction.
AI.result: Evaluated on LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, SimplerEnv-WidowX in simulation and real-world experiments. LaMP outperforms VLA baselines on all benchmarks, achieving the highest reported average success rates under the same training budgets. On LIBERO-Plus out-of-distribution perturbations, it gains an average 9.7% over the strongest prior baseline.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating a latent 3D motion prior via a dual-expert, flow-guided architecture improves robustness and performance in VLA-based robotic manipulation, suggesting that explicit latent motion modeling can mitigate the brittleness of purely 2D-feature-driven policies.

title: PASDiff: Physics-Aware Semantic Guidance for Joint Real-world Low-Light Face Enhancement and Restoration
summary: Face images captured in real-world low light suffer multiple degradations-low illumination, blur, noise, and low visibility, etc. Existing cascaded solutions often suffer from severe error accumulation, while generic joint models lack explicit facial priors and struggle to resolve clear face structures. In this paper, we propose PASDiff, a Physics-Aware Semantic Diffusion with a training-free manner. To achieve a plausible illumination and color distribution, we leverage inverse intensity weighting and Retinex theory to introduce photometric constraints, thereby reliably recovering visibility and natural chromaticity. To faithfully reconstruct facial details, our Style-Agnostic Structural Injection (SASI) extracts structures from an off-the-shelf facial prior while filtering out its intrinsic photometric biases, seamlessly harmonizing identity features with physical constraints. Furthermore, we construct WildDark-Face, a real-world benchmark of 700 low-light facial images with complex degradations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PASDiff significantly outperforms existing methods, achieving a superior balance among natural illumination, color recovery, and identity consistency.

AI.tldr: PASDiff: a training-free, physics-aware diffusion method for low-light face enhancement that uses inverse intensity weighting and Retinex-based photometric constraints, with SASI structure injection from facial priors, and introduces WildDark-Face benchmark; claims strong performance in illumination, color, and identity preservation.
AI.motivation: Real-world low-light face images suffer multiple degradations (low illumination, blur, noise, low visibility) and cascaded or generic joint models either accumulate errors or lack explicit facial priors, leading to poor face structure recovery.
AI.method: A training-free diffusion framework (PASDiff) with two key components: (1) physics-aware photometric constraints via inverse intensity weighting and Retinex theory to recover plausible illumination and color; (2) Style-Agnostic Structural Injection (SASI) that extracts and injects facial structures from an off-the-shelf prior while filtering photometric biases, harmonizing identity with physical constraints. Also introduces WildDark-Face, a real-world low-light facial image benchmark of 700 images.
AI.result: Experiments show PASDiff outperforms existing methods, achieving a better balance among natural illumination, color recovery, and identity consistency.
AI.conclusion: PASDiff provides plausible illumination and natural color recovery while preserving identity details, validated on a new real-world benchmark (WildDark-Face); demonstrates the potential of a training-free, physics- and priors-guided diffusion approach for real-world low-light face imaging.

title: How Pruning Reshapes Features: Sparse Autoencoder Analysis of Weight-Pruned Language Models
comment: 27 pages, 6 figures, 6 tables. Analysis covers Gemma 3 1B, Gemma 2 2B, and Llama 3.2 1B across 22 experimental runs. Code and data available at https://github.com/hborobia/sae-pruning-paper
summary: Weight pruning is a standard technique for compressing large language models, yet its effect on learned internal representations remains poorly understood. We present the first systematic study of how unstructured pruning reshapes the feature geometry of language models, using Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) as interpretability probes. Across three model families (Gemma 3 1B, Gemma 2 2B, Llama 3.2 1B), two pruning methods (magnitude and Wanda), and six sparsity levels (0--60%), we investigate five research questions spanning seed stability, feature survival, SAE transferability, feature fragility, and causal relevance. Our most striking finding is that rare SAE features--those with low firing rates--survive pruning far better than frequent ones, with within-condition Spearman correlations of rho = -1.0 in 11 of 17 experimental conditions. This counter-intuitive result suggests that pruning acts as implicit feature selection, preferentially destroying high-frequency generic features while preserving specialized rare ones. We further show that Wanda pruning preserves feature structure up to 3.7x better than magnitude pruning, that pre-trained SAEs remain viable on Wanda-pruned models up to 50% sparsity, and that geometric feature survival does not predict causal importance--a dissociation with implications for interpretability under compression.

AI.tldr: Unstructured pruning reshapes LM representations by acting as implicit feature selection: rare, low-firing SAE features survive pruning better than frequent ones. Wanda pruning preserves feature structure much more than magnitude pruning; SAEs remain viable under substantial sparsity, but geometric survival does not imply causal importance.
AI.motivation: To understand how unstructured pruning affects learned internal representations in language models and to evaluate the reliability of interpretability probes (Sparse Autoencoders) under compression.
AI.method: Systematic study across three LM families (Gemma 3 1B, Gemma 2 2B, Llama 3.2 1B), two pruning methods (magnitude and Wanda), and six sparsity levels (0–60%), using Sparse Autoencoders as probes to assess seed stability, feature survival, transferability, fragility, and causal relevance.
AI.result: Rare SAE features (low firing rate) survive pruning much better than frequent ones (within-condition Spearman rho = -1.0 in 11 of 17 conditions). Wanda pruning preserves feature structure up to 3.7x better than magnitude pruning. Pre-trained SAEs remain viable on Wanda-pruned models up to 50% sparsity. Geometric feature survival does not predict causal importance.
AI.conclusion: Pruning functions as implicit feature selection, preferentially destroying high-frequency generic features while preserving specialized rare ones. Geometry-based interpretability can misalign with causal relevance under compression; Wanda is superior for preserving feature structure, and many SAEs remain usable under substantial sparsity.

title: Agentic Trust Coordination for Federated Learning through Adaptive Thresholding and Autonomous Decision Making in Sustainable and Resilient Industrial Networks
summary:
Distributed intelligence in industrial networks increasingly integrates sensing, communication, and computation across heterogeneous and resource constrained devices. Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training in such environments, but its reliability is affected by inconsistent client behaviour, noisy sensing conditions, and the presence of faulty or adversarial updates. Trust based mechanisms are commonly used to mitigate these effects, yet most remain statistical and heuristic, relying on fixed parameters or simple adaptive rules that struggle to accommodate changing operating conditions.
  This paper presents a lightweight agentic trust coordination approach for FL in sustainable and resilient industrial networks. The proposed Agentic Trust Control Layer operates as a server side control loop that observes trust related and system level signals, interprets their evolution over time, and applies targeted trust adjustments when instability is detected. The approach extends prior adaptive trust mechanisms by enabling context aware intervention decisions, rather than relying on fixed or purely reactive parameter updates. By explicitly separating observation, reasoning, and action, the proposed framework supports stable FL operation without modifying client side training or increasing communication overhead.

AI.tldr: Lightweight server-side agentic trust control layer for federated learning in industrial networks that enables context-aware trust interventions to sustain stability without changing clients or adding communication overhead.
AI.motivation: In industrial FL, inconsistent client behavior, noisy sensing, and adversarial updates threaten reliability. Existing trust methods are often fixed and heuristic, lacking adaptability to changing conditions.
AI.method: Introduce an Agentic Trust Control Layer as a server-side control loop that observes trust-related and system signals, reasons about their evolution, and performs targeted trust adjustments. It explicitly separates observation, reasoning, and action to enable context-aware interventions without modifying client-side training or increasing communication.
AI.result: Enables stable FL operation without modifying client-side training or increasing communication overhead; extends adaptive trust mechanisms by providing context-aware interventions and a lightweight governance layer suitable for heterogeneous, resource-constrained industrial networks.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a practical, context-aware trust coordination paradigm for FL in sustainable and resilient industrial networks, powered by a server-side control loop that enhances stability through targeted, context-driven interventions.

title: Modernising Reinforcement Learning-Based Navigation for Embodied Semantic Scene Graph Generation
summary:
Semantic world models enable embodied agents to reason about objects, relations, and spatial context beyond purely geometric representations. In Organic Computing, such models are a key enabler for objective-driven self-adaptation under uncertainty and resource constraints. The core challenge is to acquire observations maximising model quality and downstream usefulness within a limited action budget.
  Semantic scene graphs (SSGs) provide a structured and compact representation for this purpose. However, constructing them within a finite action horizon requires exploration strategies that trade off information gain against navigation cost and decide when additional actions yield diminishing returns.
  This work presents a modular navigation component for Embodied Semantic Scene Graph Generation and modernises its decision-making by replacing the policy-optimisation method and revisiting the discrete action formulation. We study compact and finer-grained, larger discrete motion sets and compare a single-head policy over atomic actions with a factorised multi-head policy over action components. We evaluate curriculum learning and optional depth-based collision supervision, and assess SSG completeness, execution safety, and navigation behaviour.
  Results show that replacing the optimisation algorithm alone improves SSG completeness by 21\% relative to the baseline under identical reward shaping. Depth mainly affects execution safety (collision-free motion), while completeness remains largely unchanged. Combining modern optimisation with a finer-grained, factorised action representation yields the strongest overall completeness--efficiency trade-off.

AI.tldr: Modern optimisation plus a factorised, finer-grained action space substantially improves Embodied Semantic Scene Graph completeness and navigation efficiency, with depth-based collision supervision mainly enhancing safety.
AI.motivation: Semantic world models and SSGs enable reasoning about objects, relations, and spatial context under uncertainty and resource constraints. The core challenge is to maximize model quality within a limited action budget, enabling objective-driven self-adaptation.
AI.method: Propose a modular navigation component for Embodied Semantic Scene Graph Generation. Compare a single-head policy over atomic actions with a factorised multi-head policy over action components. Explore compact and larger discrete motion sets, curriculum learning, and optional depth-based collision supervision. Evaluate SSG completeness, execution safety, and navigation behavior.
AI.result: Replacing the optimisation algorithm alone improves SSG completeness by 21% relative to the baseline under identical reward shaping. Depth mainly affects execution safety (collision-free motion), while completeness remains largely unchanged. Combining modern optimisation with a finer-grained, factorised action representation yields the strongest overall completeness–efficiency trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Modern optimisation plus a finer-grained, factorised action representation enhances the completeness–efficiency trade-off in Embodied Semantic Scene Graph Generation, while depth-based supervision primarily boosts safety; the approach provides a robust scheme for SSG-enabled embodied agents under limited action budgets.

title: MoE-GRPO: Optimizing Mixture-of-Experts via Reinforcement Learning in Vision-Language Models
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) has emerged as an effective approach to reduce the computational overhead of Transformer architectures by sparsely activating a subset of parameters for each token while preserving high model capacity. This paradigm has recently been extended to Vision-Language Models (VLMs), enabling scalable multi-modal understanding with reduced computational cost. However, the widely adopted deterministic top-K routing mechanism may overlook more optimal expert combinations and lead to expert overfitting. To address this limitation and improve the diversity of expert selection, we propose MoE-GRPO, a reinforcement learning (RL)-based framework for optimizing expert routing in MoE-based VLMs. Specifically, we formulate expert selection as a sequential decision-making problem and optimize it using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), allowing the model to learn adaptive expert routing policies through exploration and reward-based feedback. Furthermore, we introduce a modality-aware router guidance that enhances training stability and efficiency by discouraging the router from exploring experts that are infrequently activated for a given modality. Extensive experiments on multi-modal image and video benchmarks show that MoE-GRPO consistently outperforms standard top-K routing and its variants by promoting more diverse expert selection, thereby mitigating expert overfitting and enabling a task-level expert specialization.

AI.tldr: Proposes MoE-GRPO, an RL-based router for MoE-based VLMs to improve diversity and reduce expert overfitting by using Group Relative Policy Optimization and modality-aware guidance; shows improvements over deterministic top-K routing on image/video multimodal tasks.
AI.motivation: Deterministic top-K routing in Mixture-of-Experts for Vision-Language Models can miss optimal expert combinations and lead to overfitting; a diverse, adaptive routing mechanism is needed to leverage multiple experts and modality cues.
AI.method: Formulates expert routing as sequential decision-making and optimizes with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). Introduces modality-aware router guidance to discourage exploring rarely activated experts for a given modality, stabilizing training. Evaluates on multi-modal image and video benchmarks.
AI.result: MoE-GRPO consistently outperforms standard top-K routing and its variants by promoting more diverse expert selection, mitigating expert overfitting, and enabling task-level expert specialization.
AI.conclusion: RL-based routing with GRPO and modality-aware guidance yields better diversity and specialization in MoE-based VLMs, improving task performance and robustness across multi-modal benchmarks.

title: 4OPS: Structural Difficulty Modeling in Integer Arithmetic Puzzles
summary:
Arithmetic puzzle games provide a controlled setting for studying difficulty in mathematical reasoning tasks, a core challenge in adaptive learning systems. We investigate the structural determinants of difficulty in a class of integer arithmetic puzzles inspired by number games. We formalize the problem and develop an exact dynamic-programming solver that enumerates reachable targets, extracts minimal-operation witnesses, and enables large-scale labeling.
  Using this solver, we construct a dataset of over 3.4 million instances and define difficulty via the minimum number of operations required to reach a target. We analyze the relationship between difficulty and solver-derived features. While baseline machine learning models based on bag- and target-level statistics can partially predict solvability, they fail to reliably distinguish easy instances. In contrast, we show that difficulty is fully determined by a small set of interpretable structural attributes derived from exact witnesses. In particular, the number of input values used in a minimal construction serves as a minimal sufficient statistic for difficulty under this labeling.
  These results provide a transparent, computationally grounded account of puzzle difficulty that bridges symbolic reasoning and data-driven modeling. The framework supports explainable difficulty estimation and principled task sequencing, with direct implications for adaptive arithmetic learning and intelligent practice systems.

AI.tldr: Exact solver yields interpretable structural features that determine puzzle difficulty; a minimal construction using inputs serves as a sufficient statistic; large dataset (3.4M instances) enables explainable difficulty estimation for adaptive arithmetic learning.
AI.motivation: Understand what makes arithmetic puzzle instances difficult to solve, to enable explainable and adaptive task sequencing in educational systems; address limits of purely statistical predictors by revealing symbolic structure that governs difficulty.
AI.method: Develop an exact dynamic-programming solver that enumerates reachable targets, extracts minimal-operation witnesses, and uses these to label instances. Build a 3.4M-instance dataset. Analyze solver-derived features; compare ML baselines against interpretable structural attributes; identify a minimal sufficient statistic.
AI.result: Baseline models with bag- and target-level statistics partially predict solvability but cannot reliably separate easy instances. Difficulty is governed by a small set of interpretable structural attributes derived from exact witnesses; specifically, the number of input values used in a minimal construction acts as a minimal sufficient statistic for difficulty.
AI.conclusion: Provides a transparent, computationally grounded account of puzzle difficulty bridging symbolic reasoning and data-driven modeling. Enables explainable difficulty estimation and principled task sequencing with direct implications for adaptive arithmetic learning and intelligent practice systems.

title: Vega: Learning to Drive with Natural Language Instructions
summary: Vision-language-action models have reshaped autonomous driving to incorporate languages into the decision-making process. However, most existing pipelines only utilize the language modality for scene descriptions or reasoning and lack the flexibility to follow diverse user instructions for personalized driving. To address this, we first construct a large-scale driving dataset (InstructScene) containing around 100,000 scenes annotated with diverse driving instructions with the corresponding trajectories. We then propose a unified Vision-Language-World-Action model, Vega, for instruction-based generation and planning. We employ the autoregressive paradigm to process visual inputs (vision) and language instructions (language) and the diffusion paradigm to generate future predictions (world modeling) and trajectories (action). We perform joint attention to enable interactions between the modalities and use individual projection layers for different modalities for more capabilities. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method not only achieves superior planning performance but also exhibits strong instruction-following abilities, paving the way for more intelligent and personalized driving systems.

AI.tldr: A unified Vision-Language-World-Action (Vega) model for instruction-guided autonomous driving, trained on InstructScene (~100k scenes) to generate future states and trajectories via diffusion, with autoregressive processing of vision/language and joint attention across modalities; achieves strong instruction-following and planning performance.
AI.motivation: Current driving pipelines largely use language only for scene descriptions or reasoning and cannot flexibly follow diverse user instructions for personalized driving; there is a need for integrated multi-modal planning that can handle natural-language instructions and environment inputs to support personalized driving.
AI.method: Create InstructScene dataset (~100k scenes) with diverse driving instructions and trajectories. Propose Vega: a unified model that processes visual inputs and language instructions autoregressively and uses diffusion to generate future world states and action trajectories. Utilize joint attention among modalities and modality-specific projection layers to enhance capabilities.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show Vega achieves superior planning performance and strong instruction-following abilities, enabling more intelligent and personalized driving systems.
AI.conclusion: Vega provides a unified, instruction-aware planning framework for autonomous driving by integrating vision, language, world modeling, and action generation; demonstrates scalability via a large dataset and improves planning and instruction-following capabilities.

title: Few-Shot Left Atrial Wall Segmentation in 3D LGE MRI via Meta-Learning
summary: Segmenting the left atrial wall from late gadolinium enhancement magnetic resonance images (MRI) is challenging due to the wall's thin geometry, low contrast, and the scarcity of expert annotations. We propose a Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) framework for K-shot (K = 5, 10, 20) 3D left atrial wall segmentation that is meta-trained on the wall task together with auxiliary left atrial and right atrial cavity tasks and uses a boundary-aware composite loss to emphasize thin-structure accuracy. We evaluated MAML segmentation performance on a hold-out test set and assessed robustness under an unseen synthetic shift and on a distinct local cohort. On the hold-out test set, MAML appeared to improve segmentation performance compared to the supervised fine-tuning model, achieving a Dice score (DSC) of 0.64 vs. 0.52 and HD95 of 5.70 vs. 7.60 mm at 5-shot, and approached the fully supervised reference at 20-shot (0.69 vs. 0.71 DSC). Under unseen shift, performance degraded but remained robust: at 5-shot, MAML attained 0.59 DSC and 5.99 mm HD95 on the unseen domain shift and 0.57 DSC and 6.01 mm HD95 on the local cohort, with consistent gains as K increased. These results suggest that more accurate and reliable thin-wall boundaries are achievable in low-shot adaptation, potentially enabling clinical translation with minimal additional labeling for the assessment of atrial remodeling.

AI.tldr: A model-agnostic meta-learning framework for few-shot 3D left atrial wall segmentation using a boundary-aware loss, showing improved performance over standard fine-tuning in low-shot settings and approaching fully supervised results at 20-shot, with robustness to unseen shifts.
AI.motivation: The left atrial wall is thin and has low image contrast, making segmentation difficult and annotation scarce. A meta-learning approach aims to enable rapid, data-efficient adaptation for accurate thin-wall segmentation suitable for clinical translation.
AI.method: A MAML-based framework trained on the target left atrial wall segmentation task, augmented with auxiliary left atrial and right atrial cavity tasks. Uses a boundary-aware composite loss to emphasize thin-structure accuracy. Evaluated in K-shot settings (K=5,10,20) on a hold-out test set, with robustness tests on an unseen synthetic shift and a local cohort.
AI.result: On hold-out, DS C improved to 0.64 (vs 0.52 with supervised fine-tuning) and HD95 to 5.70 mm (vs 7.60 mm) at 5-shot; at 20-shot, DSC approached fully supervised (0.69 vs 0.71). Under unseen domain shift: 5-shot DSC 0.59 with HD95 5.99 mm; local cohort: 0.57 DSC and 6.01 mm HD95, with gains increasing with K.
AI.conclusion: MAML enables more accurate and reliable thin-wall boundary segmentation in low-shot regimes, suggesting potential clinical translation with minimal additional labeling for assessing atrial remodeling.

title: Towards Video Anomaly Detection from Event Streams: A Baseline and Benchmark Datasets
summary: Event-based vision, characterized by low redundancy, focus on dynamic motion, and inherent privacy-preserving properties, naturally fits the demands of video anomaly detection (VAD). However, the absence of dedicated event-stream anomaly detection datasets and effective modeling strategies has significantly hindered progress in this field. In this work, we take the first major step toward establishing event-based VAD as a unified research direction. We first construct multiple event-stream based benchmarks for video anomaly detection, featuring synchronized event and RGB recordings. Leveraging the unique properties of events, we then propose an EVent-centric spatiotemporal Video Anomaly Detection framework, namely EWAD, with three key innovations: an event density aware dynamic sampling strategy to select temporally informative segments; a density-modulated temporal modeling approach that captures contextual relations from sparse event streams; and an RGB-to-event knowledge distillation mechanism to enhance event-based representations under weak supervision. Extensive experiments on three benchmarks demonstrate that our EWAD achieves significant improvements over existing approaches, highlighting the potential and effectiveness of event-driven modeling for video anomaly detection. The benchmark datasets will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: Introduces EWAD, an event-centric framework for video anomaly detection (VAD) and synchronized event-RGB benchmarks. It proposes three innovations—(i) event density aware dynamic sampling to select informative segments, (ii) density-modulated temporal modeling for sparse event streams, and (iii) RGB-to-event knowledge distillation under weak supervision. Experiments on three benchmarks show significant improvements over existing approaches, with benchmarks to be released publicly.
AI.motivation: The field lacks dedicated event-stream anomaly detection datasets and effective modeling strategies. Event-based vision offers low redundancy, emphasis on dynamic motion, and privacy benefits, which align well with VAD requirements. Establishing benchmarks and a robust method is essential to advance this nascent direction.
AI.method: 1) Construct multiple event-stream based benchmarks with synchronized event and RGB recordings. 2) Propose EWAD with three core components: (a) event density aware dynamic sampling to pick temporally informative segments, (b) density-modulated temporal modeling to capture context from sparse event streams, (c) RGB-to-event knowledge distillation to enhance event representations under weak supervision.
AI.result: EWAD achieves significant improvements over existing approaches across three benchmarks, validating the effectiveness of event-driven modeling for VAD. The benchmark datasets will be publicly released to foster further research.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes event-based VAD as a unified research direction by providing benchmarks and a strong baseline framework. It demonstrates the potential of event-driven modeling for anomaly detection and paves the way for future exploration in this area.

title: Beyond Content Safety: Real-Time Monitoring for Reasoning Vulnerabilities in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly rely on explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning to solve complex tasks, yet the safety of the reasoning process itself remains largely unaddressed. Existing work on LLM safety focuses on content safety--detecting harmful, biased, or factually incorrect outputs -- and treats the reasoning chain as an opaque intermediate artifact. We identify reasoning safety as an orthogonal and equally critical security dimension: the requirement that a model's reasoning trajectory be logically consistent, computationally efficient, and resistant to adversarial manipulation. We make three contributions. First, we formally define reasoning safety and introduce a nine-category taxonomy of unsafe reasoning behaviors, covering input parsing errors, reasoning execution errors, and process management errors. Second, we conduct a large-scale prevalence study annotating 4111 reasoning chains from both natural reasoning benchmarks and four adversarial attack methods (reasoning hijacking and denial-of-service), confirming that all nine error types occur in practice and that each attack induces a mechanistically interpretable signature. Third, we propose a Reasoning Safety Monitor: an external LLM-based component that runs in parallel with the target model, inspects each reasoning step in real time via a taxonomy-embedded prompt, and dispatches an interrupt signal upon detecting unsafe behavior. Evaluation on a 450-chain static benchmark shows that our monitor achieves up to 84.88\% step-level localization accuracy and 85.37\% error-type classification accuracy, outperforming hallucination detectors and process reward model baselines by substantial margins. These results demonstrate that reasoning-level monitoring is both necessary and practically achievable, and establish reasoning safety as a foundational concern for the secure deployment of large reasoning models.

AI.tldr: Defines reasoning safety for LLMs, presents a 9-category taxonomy of unsafe reasoning behaviors, shows prevalence under natural and adversarial conditions, and introduces a parallel Reasoning Safety Monitor achieving ~85% localization and error-type classification accuracy.
AI.motivation: To address the neglected yet critical security dimension of LLM reasoning: ensuring logical consistency, efficiency, and resilience to adversarial manipulation, beyond content safety.
AI.method: Formal taxonomy of unsafe reasoning (nine categories across input parsing, reasoning execution, and process management). Annotated 4111 reasoning chains from natural benchmarks and four adversarial attack methods (reasoning hijacking and DoS). Developed an external LLM-based Reasoning Safety Monitor that runs in parallel, uses taxonomy-embedded prompts to inspect steps in real time, and interrupts unsafe steps.
AI.result: Found evidence of all nine error types in practice; each attack leaves interpretable signatures. The monitor achieved up to 84.88% step-level localization accuracy and 85.37% error-type classification accuracy on a 450-chain static benchmark, outperforming hallucination detectors and process reward model baselines.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning safety is a foundational concern for secure deployment of large reasoning models; external monitoring is feasible and effective, establishing a new safety dimension for LLMs.

title: C2W-Tune: Cavity-to -Wall Transfer Learning for Thin Atrial Wall Segmentation in 3D Late Gadolinium-enhanced Magnetic Resonance
comment: Submitted this to the International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Medicine (AIME 2026)
summary: Accurate segmentation of the left atrial (LA) wall in 3D late gadolinium-enhanced MRI (LGE-MRI) is essential for wall thickness mapping and fibrosis quantification, yet it remains challenging due to the wall's thinness, complex anatomy, and low contrast. We propose C2W-Tune, a two-stage cavity-to-wall transfer framework that leverages a high-accuracy LA cavity model as an anatomical prior to improve thin-wall delineation. Using a 3D U-Net with a ResNeXt encoder and instance normalization, Stage 1 pre-trains the network to segment the LA cavity, learning robust atrial representations. Stage 2 transfers these weights and adapts the network to LA wall segmentation using a progressive layer-unfreezing schedule to preserve endocardial features while enabling wall-specific refinement. Experiments on the 2018 LA Segmentation Challenge dataset demonstrate substantial gains over an architecture-matched baseline trained from scratch: wall Dice improves from 0.623 to 0.814, and Surface Dice at 1 mm improves from 0.553 to 0.731. Boundary errors were substantially reduced, with the 95th-percentile Hausdorff distance (HD95) decreasing from 2.95 mm to 2.55 mm and the average symmetric surface distance (ASSD) from 0.71 mm to 0.63 mm. Furthermore, even with reduced supervision (70 training volumes sampled from the same training pool), C2W-Tune achieved a Dice score of 0.78 and an HD95 of 3.15 mm, maintaining competitive performance and exceeding multi-class benchmarks that typically report Dice values around 0.6-0.7. These results show that anatomically grounded task transfer with controlled fine-tuning improves boundary accuracy for thin LA wall segmentation in 3D LGE-MRI.

AI.tldr: Two-stage cavity-to-wall transfer (C2W-Tune) uses an anatomical LA cavity prior to improve thin LA wall segmentation in 3D LGE-MRI. Stage1 pretrains a 3D U-Net on LA cavity; Stage2 transfers weights with progressive layer unfreezing to refine wall segmentation. Substantial gains over a baseline in Dice and boundary metrics on the 2018 LA Segmentation Challenge; robust with reduced supervision.
AI.motivation: Accurately segmenting the thin LA wall is challenging due to wall thinness, complex geometry, and low contrast in 3D LGE-MRI. A strong anatomical prior (LA cavity) can guide the network to preserve endocardial features while enabling wall-focused refinement, potentially improving boundary accuracy and robustness.
AI.method: Two-stage training: Stage1 pre-trains a 3D U-Net with a ResNeXt encoder and instance normalization to segment the LA cavity. Stage2 transfers learned weights and applies a progressive layer-unfreezing schedule to adapt to LA wall segmentation, preserving endocardial features while allowing wall-specific adjustment.
AI.result: On the 2018 LA Segmentation Challenge dataset, wall Dice improved from 0.623 to 0.814; Surface Dice at 1 mm improved from 0.553 to 0.731. HD95 reduced from 2.95 mm to 2.55 mm; ASSD reduced from 0.71 mm to 0.63 mm. Under reduced supervision (70 volumes), Dice was 0.78 and HD95 3.15 mm, still competitive and surpassing multi-class benchmarks (Dice ~0.6-0.7).
AI.conclusion: Anatomically grounded task transfer with controlled fine-tuning effectively improves thin LA wall boundary accuracy in 3D LGE-MRI, validating the benefit of leveraging a high-accuracy cavity model as an anatomical prior for wall segmentation.

title: Relaxed Rigidity with Ray-based Grouping for Dynamic Gaussian Splatting
summary: The reconstruction of dynamic 3D scenes using 3D Gaussian Splatting has shown significant promise. A key challenge, however, remains in modeling realistic motion, as most methods fail to align the motion of Gaussians with real-world physical dynamics. This misalignment is particularly problematic for monocular video datasets, where failing to maintain coherent motion undermines local geometric structure, ultimately leading to degraded reconstruction quality. Consequently, many state-of-the-art approaches rely heavily on external priors, such as optical flow or 2D tracks, to enforce temporal coherence. In this work, we propose a novel method to explicitly preserve the local geometric structure of Gaussians across time in 4D scenes. Our core idea is to introduce a view-space ray grouping strategy that clusters Gaussians intersected by the same ray, considering only those whose $α$-blending weights exceed a threshold. We then apply constraints to these groups to maintain a consistent spatial distribution, effectively preserving their local geometry. This approach enforces a more physically plausible motion model by ensuring that local geometry remains stable over time, eliminating the reliance on external guidance. We demonstrate the efficacy of our method by integrating it into two distinct baseline models. Extensive experiments on challenging monocular datasets show that our approach significantly outperforms existing methods, achieving superior temporal consistency and reconstruction quality.

AI.tldr: Proposes view-space ray grouping to preserve local Gaussian geometry over time in 4D dynamic scenes for 3D Gaussian Splatting, reducing reliance on external priors; shows improved temporal coherence and reconstruction quality on monocular data by enforcing geometry-consistent motion.
AI.motivation: To address misalignment between Gaussian motion and physical dynamics in dynamic 3D scene reconstruction, especially for monocular video where geometric coherence degrades without external priors such as optical flow or 2D tracks.
AI.method: Introduce a view-space ray grouping strategy that clusters Gaussians intersected by the same ray and with alpha above a threshold; apply constraints to maintain consistent local spatial distribution within each group, enforcing a physically plausible motion model; integrate this constraint into existing 4D Gaussian Splatting baselines.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on challenging monocular datasets show significant improvements in temporal consistency and reconstruction quality compared to baselines and prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Preserving the local geometry of Gaussians over time yields a more physically plausible motion model for dynamic 3D reconstruction, reducing reliance on external guidance and improving monocular reconstruction outcomes.

title: Not a fragment, but the whole: Map-based evaluation of data-driven Fire Danger Index models
summary: A growing body of literature has focused on predicting wildfire occurrence using machine learning methods, capitalizing on high-resolution data and fire predictors that canonical process-based frameworks largely ignore. Standard evaluation metrics for an ML classifier, while important, provide a potentially limited measure of the model's operational performance for the Fire Danger Index (FDI) forecast. Furthermore, model evaluation is frequently conducted without adequately accounting for false positive rates, despite their critical relevance in operational contexts. In this paper, we revisit the daily FDI model evaluation paradigm and propose a novel method for evaluating a forest fire forecasting model that is aligned with real-world decision-making. Furthermore, we systematically assess performance in accurately predicting fire activity and the false positives (false alarms). We further demonstrate that an ensemble of ML models improves both fire identification and reduces false positives.

AI.tldr: A decision-oriented evaluation framework for wildfire forecasting is proposed, highlighting limitations of standard ML metrics and false positives; an ensemble of ML models improves fire detection and reduces false alarms, aligning model assessment with operational needs.
AI.motivation: Operational decision-making requires forest fire forecasts that balance hit rates with false alarms; conventional ML metrics may misrepresent real-world usefulness for Fire Danger Index (FDI) forecasts and neglect false-positive rates.
AI.method: Revisit the daily FDI evaluation paradigm and introduce a novel evaluation method aligned with practical decision-making; systematically assess performance on accurate fire identification and false-alarm rates; evaluate an ensemble of ML models to improve detection and reduce false positives.
AI.result: The ensemble approach yields better identification of fire activity and a reduction in false positives compared to individual models, under the proposed decision-oriented evaluation framework.
AI.conclusion: A decision-aligned evaluation framework is essential for operational wildfire forecasting; ensembles can enhance performance by improving true positives while mitigating false alarms, supporting more reliable FDI-based decisions.

title: Distributed Real-Time Vehicle Control for Emergency Vehicle Transit: A Scalable Cooperative Method
comment: Submitted to IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
summary: Rapid transit of emergency vehicles is critical for saving lives and reducing property loss but often relies on surrounding ordinary vehicles to cooperatively adjust their driving behaviors. It is important to ensure rapid transit of emergency vehicles while minimizing the impact on ordinary vehicles. Centralized mathematical solver and reinforcement learning are the state-of-the-art methods. The former obtains optimal solutions but is only practical for small-scale scenarios. The latter implicitly learns through extensive centralized training but the trained model exhibits limited scalability to different traffic conditions. Hence, existing methods suffer from two fundamental limitations: high computational cost and lack of scalability. To overcome above limitations, this work proposes a scalable distributed vehicle control method, where vehicles adjust their driving behaviors in a distributed manner online using only local instead of global information. We proved that the proposed distributed method using only local information is approximately equivalent to the one using global information, which enables vehicles to evaluate their candidate states and make approximately optimal decisions in real time without pre-training and with natural adaptability to varying traffic conditions. Then, a distributed conflict resolution mechanism is further proposed to guarantee vehicles' safety by avoiding their decision conflicts, which eliminates the single-point-of-failure risk of centralized methods and provides deterministic safety guarantees that learned methods cannot offer. Compared with existing methods, simulation experiments based on real-world traffic datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves faster decision-making, less impact on ordinary vehicles, and maintains much stronger scalability across different traffic densities and road configurations.

AI.tldr: A scalable distributed vehicle control framework enabling fast, local-information-based emergency vehicle transit with approximate global-optimality and guaranteed safety, outperforming centralized and RL baselines in speed, traffic impact, and scalability.
AI.motivation: Rapid emergency-vehicle transit requires coordinating surrounding vehicles while minimizing disruption to regular traffic. Centralized solvers are computationally costly and scale poorly; reinforcement learning offers adaptability but lacks scalability and safety guarantees.
AI.method: A distributed online control method using only local information (no global data). The approach proves approximate equivalence to a global-information controller, enabling near-optimal decisions in real time without pre-training and with natural adaptability to varying traffic. A distributed conflict resolution mechanism guarantees safety by avoiding decision conflicts, removing single points of failure associated with centralized methods, and providing deterministic safety guarantees.
AI.result: Simulation experiments on real-world traffic datasets show faster decision-making, reduced impact on ordinary vehicles, and much stronger scalability across different traffic densities and road configurations.
AI.conclusion: The method overcomes high computational cost and scalability limitations of existing approaches, delivering near-optimal real-time decisions with safety guarantees, reducing centralization risks, and demonstrating robust performance under diverse traffic conditions.

title: Causal-INSIGHT: Probing Temporal Models to Extract Causal Structure
summary:
Understanding directed temporal interactions in multivariate time series is essential for interpreting complex dynamical systems and the predictive models trained on them. We present Causal-INSIGHT, a model-agnostic, post-hoc interpretation framework for extracting model-implied (predictor-dependent), directed, time-lagged influence structure from trained temporal predictors. Rather than inferring causal structure at the level of the data-generating process, Causal-INSIGHT analyzes how a fixed, pre-trained predictor responds to systematic, intervention-inspired input clamping applied at inference time.
  From these responses, we construct directed temporal influence signals that reflect the dependencies the predictor relies on for prediction, and introduce Qbic, a sparsity-aware graph selection criterion that balances predictive fidelity and structural complexity without requiring ground-truth graph labels. Experiments across synthetic, simulated, and realistic benchmarks show that Causal-INSIGHT generalizes across diverse backbone architectures, maintains competitive structural accuracy, and yields significant improvements in temporal delay localization when applied to existing predictors.

AI.tldr: A model-agnostic, post-hoc interpretation framework (Causal-INSIGHT) that extracts predictor-dependent, directed, time-lagged influence structures from trained temporal predictors via input clamping at inference; introduces Qbic for sparsity-aware graph selection; shows generalization and improved temporal delay localization.
AI.motivation: To understand directed temporal interactions in multivariate time series and interpret predictive models by analyzing how a fixed predictor responds to intervention-inspired input modifications at inference, rather than inferring the data-generating causal graph.
AI.method: Post-hoc analysis using systematic input clamping at inference to elicit predictor responses; constructs directed temporal influence signals; introduces Qbic as a sparsity-aware graph selection criterion that does not require ground-truth graphs.
AI.result: Generalizes across diverse backbone architectures; maintains competitive structural accuracy; yields significant improvements in temporal delay localization when applied to existing predictors; validated on synthetic, simulated, and realistic benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Causal-INSIGHT offers a model-agnostic, scalable approach to extract predictor-dependent, directed temporal influences and provides sparse graph recovery (via Qbic) without ground-truth labels, aiding interpretation of temporal predictors.

title: Retraining as Approximate Bayesian Inference
summary: Model retraining is usually treated as an ongoing maintenance task. But as Harrison Katz now argues, retraining can be better understood as approximate Bayesian inference under computational constraints. The gap between a continuously updated belief state and your frozen deployed model is "learning debt," and the retraining decision is a cost minimization problem with a threshold that falls out of your loss function. In this article Katz provides a decision-theoretic framework for retraining policies. The result is evidence-based triggers that replace calendar schedules and make governance auditable. For readers less familiar with the Bayesian and decision-theoretic language, key terms are defined in a glossary at the end of the article.

AI.tldr: Retraining is framed as approximate Bayesian inference under compute limits; introduces ‘learning debt’ and a decision-theoretic retraining policy yielding evidence-based triggers for governance.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between continuously updated belief states and frozen deployed models by replacing calendar schedules with principled, auditable retraining policies.
AI.method: Formalizes retraining as a decision-theoretic problem under computational constraints, modeling learning debt as the gap between the current belief state and the deployed model, and deriving threshold-based retraining triggers from the loss function.
AI.result: Gives evidence-based retraining triggers that replace calendar-based schedules, enabling auditable governance; includes glossary defining Bayesian/decision-theoretic terms.
AI.conclusion: A principled, cost-aware framework for retraining that supports auditable, data-driven governance and practical deployment under resource limits.

title: Interpretable Zero-shot Referring Expression Comprehension with Query-driven Scene Graphs
summary: Zero-shot referring expression comprehension (REC) aims to locate target objects in images given natural language queries without relying on task-specific training data, demanding strong visual understanding capabilities. Existing Vision-Language Models~(VLMs), such as CLIP, commonly address zero-shot REC by directly measuring feature similarities between textual queries and image regions. However, these methods struggle to capture fine-grained visual details and understand complex object relationships. Meanwhile, Large Language Models~(LLMs) excel at high-level semantic reasoning, their inability to directly abstract visual features into textual semantics limits their application in REC tasks. To overcome these limitations, we propose \textbf{SGREC}, an interpretable zero-shot REC method leveraging query-driven scene graphs as structured intermediaries. Specifically, we first employ a VLM to construct a query-driven scene graph that explicitly encodes spatial relationships, descriptive captions, and object interactions relevant to the given query. By leveraging this scene graph, we bridge the gap between low-level image regions and higher-level semantic understanding required by LLMs. Finally, an LLM infers the target object from the structured textual representation provided by the scene graph, responding with detailed explanations for its decisions that ensure interpretability in the inference process. Extensive experiments show that SGREC achieves top-1 accuracy on most zero-shot REC benchmarks, including RefCOCO val (66.78\%), RefCOCO+ testB (53.43\%), and RefCOCOg val (73.28\%), highlighting its strong visual scene understanding.

AI.tldr: SGREC: an interpretable zero-shot referring expression comprehension method that uses a query-driven scene graph as an intermediate representation to bridge vision-language models and large language models, achieving strong top-1 performance on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current zero-shot REC methods (e.g., CLIP-based) struggle with fine-grained visual details and relational reasoning; while LLMs excel at semantics, they lack direct access to visual features. An interpretable intermediate representation could fuse visual grounding with high-level reasoning.
AI.method: 1) Use a vision-language model to generate a query-driven scene graph that encodes spatial relations, descriptive captions, and object interactions tied to the textual query. 2) Translate the scene graph into structured textual input that links image regions to semantic relations. 3) Employ a large language model to infer the target object from the structured representation and provide explanations to ensure interpretability.
AI.result: SGREC achieves competitive top-1 accuracy on zero-shot REC benchmarks: RefCOCO val 66.78%, RefCOCO+ testB 53.43%, RefCOCOg val 73.28%, indicating strong visual scene understanding and effective use of structured representations.
AI.conclusion: Using a query-driven scene-graph intermediate representation to bridge VLMs and LLMs enables interpretable zero-shot REC with strong performance, highlighting the value of structured intermediate representations in multimodal reasoning.

title: How Class Ontology and Data Scale Affect Audio Transfer Learning
summary: Transfer learning is a crucial concept within deep learning that allows artificial neural networks to benefit from a large pre-training data basis when confronted with a task of limited data. Despite its ubiquitous use and clear benefits, there are still many open questions regarding the inner workings of transfer learning and, in particular, regarding the understanding of when and how well it works. To that extent, we perform a rigorous study focusing on audio-to-audio transfer learning, in which we pre-train various model states on (ontology-based) subsets of AudioSet and fine-tune them on three computer audition tasks, namely acoustic scene recognition, bird activity recognition, and speech command recognition. We report that increasing the number of samples and classes in the pre-training data both have a positive impact on transfer learning. This is, however, generally surpassed by similarity between pre-training and the downstream task, which can lead the model to learn comparable features.

AI.tldr: Audio-to-audio transfer learning benefits from larger pre-training data and more classes, but task similarity often dominates transfer effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Address open questions about how transfer learning works in deep learning, specifically in audio, by isolating factors like data volume, label diversity, and task similarity across pre-training and downstream tasks.
AI.method: Pre-train multiple model states on ontology-based subsets of AudioSet and fine-tune them on three audio tasks: acoustic scene recognition, bird activity recognition, and speech command recognition.
AI.result: Both increasing the amount of pre-training data and the number of classes improve transfer performance, but similarity between pre-training and downstream tasks can be more impactful, guiding the model to learn comparable features.
AI.conclusion: In audio-to-audio transfer, aligning pre-training tasks with the target downstream tasks often yields greater transfer gains than merely increasing pre-training data; design choices should prioritize task similarity.

title: EcoThink: A Green Adaptive Inference Framework for Sustainable and Accessible Agents
summary: As the Web transitions from static retrieval to generative interaction, the escalating environmental footprint of Large Language Models (LLMs) presents a critical sustainability challenge. Current paradigms indiscriminately apply computation-intensive strategies like Chain-of-Thought (CoT) to billions of daily queries, causing LLM overthinking, a redundancy that amplifies carbon emissions and operational barriers. This inefficiency directly undermines UN Sustainable Development Goals 13 (Climate Action) and 10 (Reduced Inequalities) by hindering equitable AI access in resource-constrained regions. To address this, we introduce EcoThink, an energy-aware adaptive inference framework designed to reconcile high-performance AI intelligence with environmental responsibility. EcoThink employs a lightweight, distillation-based router to dynamically assess query complexity, skipping unnecessary reasoning for factoid retrieval while reserving deep computation for complex logic. Extensive evaluations across 9 diverse benchmarks demonstrate that EcoThink reduces inference energy by 40.4% on average (up to 81.9% for web knowledge retrieval) without statistically significant performance loss. By mitigating algorithmic waste, EcoThink offers a scalable path toward a sustainable, inclusive, and energy-efficient generative AI Agent.

AI.tldr: EcoThink is an energy-aware adaptive inference framework that uses a lightweight distillation-based router to skip unnecessary chain-of-thought reasoning for factoid queries, achieving substantial energy reductions with negligible performance loss.
AI.motivation: Current LLM use of compute-heavy Chain-of-Thought for billions of queries causes excessive energy consumption and limits accessibility in resource-constrained settings; need a sustainable, scalable approach.
AI.method: A distillation-based router evaluates query complexity and dynamically decides whether to skip CoT reasoning for factoids or engage deeper reasoning for complex tasks; routes queries to lighter inference paths when appropriate; evaluated across 9 benchmarks showing energy savings and negligible performance impact.
AI.result: Avg energy reduction of 40.4% (max 81.9% for web knowledge retrieval) with statistically non-significant performance loss across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: EcoThink offers a scalable, energy-efficient path for generative AI that maintains performance while reducing environmental footprint, potentially improving accessibility and aligning with sustainability goals.

title: Improving Fine-Grained Rice Leaf Disease Detection via Angular-Compactness Dual Loss Learning
summary: Early detection of rice leaf diseases is critical, as rice is a staple crop supporting a substantial share of the world's population. Timely identification of these diseases enables more effective intervention and significantly reduces the risk of large-scale crop losses. However, traditional deep learning models primarily rely on cross entropy loss, which often struggles with high intra-class variance and inter-class similarity, common challenges in plant pathology datasets. To tackle this, we propose a dual-loss framework that combines Center Loss and ArcFace Loss to enhance fine-grained classification of rice leaf diseases. The method is applied into three state-of-the-art backbone architectures: InceptionNetV3, DenseNet201, and EfficientNetB0 trained on the public Rice Leaf Dataset. Our approach achieves significant performance gains, with accuracies of 99.6%, 99.2% and 99.2% respectively. The results demonstrate that angular margin-based and center-based constraints substantially boost the discriminative strength of feature embeddings. In particular, the framework does not require major architectural modifications, making it efficient and practical for real-world deployment in farming environments.

AI.tldr: Dual-loss learning (Center Loss + ArcFace) improves fine-grained rice leaf disease classification across InceptionNetV3, DenseNet201, and EfficientNetB0, achieving ~99.2–99.6% accuracy on the Rice Leaf Dataset with minimal architectural changes.
AI.motivation: High intra-class variance and inter-class similarity in plant-pathology datasets hinder traditional cross-entropy-based models. Early, accurate rice disease detection is essential to prevent crop losses, and a practical loss-based approach that works with existing backbones is desirable for real-world deployment.
AI.method: A dual-loss objective combining Center Loss (tightens intra-class feature clustering) and ArcFace Loss (imposes angular margin between class boundaries), applied to three backbones (InceptionNetV3, DenseNet201, EfficientNetB0) on the Rice Leaf Dataset. The framework requires no major architectural changes, aiming to boost discriminative embeddings.
AI.result: Accuracies: InceptionNetV3 99.6%, DenseNet201 99.2%, EfficientNetB0 99.2%. The angular-margin and center-based constraints substantially enhance feature discriminability, yielding strong performance gains. The approach is efficient and practical for deployment in farming environments.
AI.conclusion: The dual-loss framework delivers significant performance improvements for rice-leaf disease classification and is suitable for real-world deployment with minimal architectural changes. Further validation on field conditions and comparisons to standard cross-entropy baselines would strengthen generalizability.

title: Interpretable PM2.5 Forecasting for Urban Air Quality: A Comparative Study of Operational Time-Series Models
summary: Accurate short-term air-quality forecasting is essential for public health protection and urban management, yet many recent forecasting frameworks rely on complex, data-intensive, and computationally demanding models. This study investigates whether lightweight and interpretable forecasting approaches can provide competitive performance for hourly PM2.5 prediction in Beijing, China. Using multi-year pollutant and meteorological time-series data, we developed a leakage-aware forecasting workflow that combined chronological data partitioning, preprocessing, feature selection, and exogenous-driver modeling under the Perfect Prognosis setting. Three forecasting families were evaluated: SARIMAX, Facebook Prophet, and NeuralProphet. To assess practical deployment behavior, the models were tested under two adaptive regimes: weekly walk-forward refitting and frozen forecasting with online residual correction. Results showed clear differences in both predictive accuracy and computational efficiency. Under walk-forward refitting, Facebook Prophet achieved the strongest completed performance, with an MAE of $37.61$ and an RMSE of $50.10$, while also requiring substantially less execution time than NeuralProphet. In the frozen-model regime, online residual correction improved Facebook Prophet and SARIMAX, with corrected SARIMAX yielding the lowest overall error (MAE $32.50$; RMSE $46.85$). NeuralProphet remained less accurate and less stable across both regimes, and residual correction did not improve its forecasts. Notably, corrected Facebook Prophet reached nearly the same error as its walk-forward counterpart while reducing runtime from $15$ min $21.91$ sec to $46.60$ sec. These findings show that lightweight additive forecasting strategies can remain highly competitive for urban air-quality prediction, offering a practical balance between accuracy, interpretability, ...

AI.tldr: Lightweight, interpretable models (SARIMAX, Prophet, NeuralProphet) can competitively forecast hourly PM2.5 in Beijing under leakage-aware preprocessing and Perfect Prognosis, with two deployment regimes showing Prophet often best in walk-forward, and corrected SARIMAX achieving the lowest error in frozen mode; NeuralProphet underperforms.
AI.motivation: Assess whether lightweight, interpretable forecasting models can match data-intensive approaches for short-term urban air-quality forecasting and enable practical deployment.
AI.method: Leakage-aware forecasting workflow with chronological data partitioning, preprocessing, feature selection, and exogenous driver modeling under Perfect Prognosis; compared three families (SARIMAX, Facebook Prophet, NeuralProphet); evaluated under two adaptive regimes: weekly walk-forward refitting and frozen forecasting with online residual correction.
AI.result: Under walk-forward refitting, Prophet achieved MAE 37.61 and RMSE 50.10, with substantially lower runtime than NeuralProphet. In the frozen regime, online residual correction improved Prophet and SARIMAX, with corrected SARIMAX delivering the lowest overall error (MAE 32.50; RMSE 46.85). NeuralProphet remained less accurate and unstable, and residual correction did not improve its forecasts. Corrected Prophet nearly matched its walk-forward performance while reducing runtime from 15:21.91 to 0:46.60. Overall, lightweight additive forecasting strategies can be highly competitive for urban air-quality prediction, balancing accuracy, interpretability, and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight additive forecasting approaches can offer an effective balance between accuracy, interpretability, and computational efficiency for short-term urban air-quality forecasting, challenging the necessity of more complex, data-hungry models.

title: Voxtral TTS
summary: We introduce Voxtral TTS, an expressive multilingual text-to-speech model that generates natural speech from as little as 3 seconds of reference audio. Voxtral TTS adopts a hybrid architecture that combines auto-regressive generation of semantic speech tokens with flow-matching for acoustic tokens. These tokens are encoded and decoded with Voxtral Codec, a speech tokenizer trained from scratch with a hybrid VQ-FSQ quantization scheme. In human evaluations conducted by native speakers, Voxtral TTS is preferred for multilingual voice cloning due to its naturalness and expressivity, achieving a 68.4\% win rate over ElevenLabs Flash v2.5. We release the model weights under a CC BY-NC license.

AI.tldr: Expressive multilingual TTS (Voxtral TTS) that uses only 3 seconds of reference audio, with a hybrid semantic token auto-regressive + flow-matching acoustic token model and Voxtral Codec tokenizer; claims 68.4% win rate vs ElevenLabs Flash v2.5 in native speaker evaluations; released under CC BY-NC.
AI.motivation: Need for natural, expressive multilingual TTS that can clone voices from very few reference samples (3s), enabling high expressivity and naturalness across languages.
AI.method: Hybrid architecture: auto-regressive generation of semantic speech tokens + flow-matching for acoustic tokens; Voxtral Codec trained from scratch using a hybrid VQ-FSQ quantization; multilingual voice cloning with short reference audio.
AI.result: Human native-speaker evaluations show Voxtral TTS preferred for multilingual voice cloning due to naturalness and expressivity; 68.4% win rate over ElevenLabs Flash v2.5.
AI.conclusion: Model weights released under CC BY-NC license; Voxtral TTS demonstrates superior naturalness/expressivity in multilingual cloning under limited reference audio; practical openness.

title: Few TensoRF: Enhance the Few-shot on Tensorial Radiance Fields
summary: This paper presents Few TensoRF, a 3D reconstruction framework that combines TensorRF's efficient tensor based representation with FreeNeRF's frequency driven few shot regularization. Using TensorRF to significantly accelerate rendering speed and introducing frequency and occlusion masks, the method improves stability and reconstruction quality under sparse input views. Experiments on the Synthesis NeRF benchmark show that Few TensoRF method improves the average PSNR from 21.45 dB (TensorRF) to 23.70 dB, with the fine tuned version reaching 24.52 dB, while maintaining TensorRF's fast \(\approx10-15\) minute training time. Experiments on the THuman 2.0 dataset further demonstrate competitive performance in human body reconstruction, achieving 27.37 - 34.00 dB with only eight input images. These results highlight Few TensoRF as an efficient and data effective solution for real-time 3D reconstruction across diverse scenes.

AI.tldr: Few TensoRF combines TensorRF's fast tensor-based rendering with FreeNeRF's frequency-driven few-shot regularization to boost sparse-view 3D reconstruction while preserving fast training.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of high-quality 3D reconstruction from sparse views with limited training time. TensorRF offers speed but may struggle with sparse data; FreeNeRF provides regularization for few-shot data using frequency cues. Integrating both aims to improve reconstruction quality without sacrificing speed.
AI.method: Extend TensorRF's representation to accelerate rendering, and introduce frequency-driven few-shot regularization and occlusion masks inspired by FreeNeRF to stabilize training under sparse input views.
AI.result: On the Synthesis NeRF benchmark, average PSNR improves from 21.45 dB (TensorRF) to 23.70 dB, with a fine-tuned version reaching 24.52 dB, while maintaining TensorRF's fast ~10–15 minute training time. On the THuman 2.0 dataset, achieves 27.37–34.00 dB using only eight input images, indicating competitive performance in human body reconstruction.
AI.conclusion: Few TensoRF is an efficient, data-efficient solution for real-time 3D reconstruction across diverse scenes, combining speed with improved reconstruction quality under sparse inputs.

title: Missing-Aware Multimodal Fusion for Unified Microservice Incident Management
summary: Automated incident management is critical for microservice reliability. While recent unified frameworks leverage multimodal data for joint optimization, they unrealistically assume perfect data completeness. In practice, network fluctuations and agent failures frequently cause missing modalities. Existing approaches relying on static placeholders introduce imputation noise that masks anomalies and degrades performance. To address this, we propose ARMOR, a robust self-supervised framework designed for missing modality scenarios. ARMOR features: (i) a modality-specific asymmetric encoder that isolates distribution disparities among metrics, logs, and traces; and (ii) a missing-aware gated fusion mechanism utilizing learnable placeholders and dynamic bias compensation to prevent cross-modal interference from incomplete inputs. By employing self-supervised auto-regression with mask-guided reconstruction, ARMOR jointly optimizes anomaly detection (AD), failure triage (FT), and root cause localization (RCL). AD and RCL require no fault labels, while FT relies solely on failure-type annotations for the downstream classifier. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ARMOR achieves state-of-the-art performance under complete data conditions and maintains robust diagnostic accuracy even with severe modality loss.
