AI.tldr: A robust self-supervised framework, ARMOR, for missing-modality data in automated incident management; introduces modality-specific encoders and a missing-aware gated fusion, achieving state-of-the-art performance even with severe modality loss.
AI.motivation: Unified multimodal AD frameworks assume perfect data; in practice modalities are missing due to network fluctuations and agent failures, and placeholders for missing data introduce imputation noise that degrades performance.
AI.method: Modality-specific asymmetric encoders isolate distribution disparities among metrics, logs, and traces; a missing-aware gated fusion mechanism with learnable placeholders and dynamic bias compensation prevents cross-modal interference from incomplete inputs; self-supervised auto-regression with mask-guided reconstruction; jointly optimizes anomaly detection (AD), failure triage (FT), and root cause localization (RCL); AD and RCL require no fault labels, FT uses failure-type annotations for the downstream classifier.
AI.result: ARMOR achieves state-of-the-art performance under complete data and maintains robust diagnostic accuracy even with severe modality loss, as shown by extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: ARMOR provides a robust, self-supervised, multi-task framework for AD, FT, and RCL in scenarios with missing modalities, mitigating imputation noise and preserving diagnostic accuracy despite substantial modality loss.

title: GDPO-Listener: Expressive Interactive Head Generation via Auto-Regressive Flow Matching and Group reward-Decoupled Policy Optimization
summary: Generating realistic 3D head motion for dyadic interactions is a significant challenge in virtual human synthesis. While recent methods achieve impressive results with speaking heads, they frequently suffer from the `Regression-to-the-Mean' problem in listener motions, collapsing into static faces, and lack the parameter space for complex nonverbal motions. In this paper, we propose GDPO-Listener, a novel framework that achieves highly expressive speaking and listening motion generation. First, we introduce an Auto-Regressive Flow Matching architecture enabling stable supervised learning. Second, to overcome kinematic stillness, we apply the Group reward-Decoupled Policy Optimization (GDPO). By isolating reward normalization across distinct FLAME parameter groups, GDPO explicitly incentivizes high variance expressive generations. Finally, we enable explicit semantic text control for customizable responses. Extensive evaluations across the Seamless Interaction and DualTalk datasets demonstrate superior performance compared to existing baselines on long-term kinematic variance, visual expressivity and semantic controllability.

AI.tldr: GDPO-Listener introduces Auto-Regressive Flow Matching and Group reward-Decoupled Policy Optimization to generate expressive speaking and listening head motion with semantic text control, addressing regression-to-the-mean and kinematic stillness; evaluated on Seamless Interaction and DualTalk, showing improved long-term variance, expressivity, and controllability.
AI.motivation: The field lacks methods that produce highly expressive, long-range dyadic head motion without collapsing into static faces. The paper targets listener stillness and limited nonverbal motion space by stabilizing supervision via flow-based sequence modeling and by shaping rewards per parameter groups to encourage variance, along with semantic control for customizable responses.
AI.method: Proposes Auto-Regressive Flow Matching architecture for stable supervised learning of motion sequences. Introduces Group reward-Decoupled Policy Optimization (GDPO) that normalizes rewards across distinct FLAME parameter groups to incentivize high-variance expressive outputs. Adds explicit semantic text control for customizable responses. Evaluated on Seamless Interaction and DualTalk datasets, with comparisons to baselines.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior performance over baselines in long-term kinematic variance, visual expressivity, and semantic controllability, indicating more expressive and controllable dyadic head motion generation.
AI.conclusion: GDPO-Listener presents a robust framework for expressive speaking and listening motion generation in virtual humans, mitigating regression-to-the-mean and kinematic stillness while enabling semantic control; promising for naturalistic social interactions but requires further validation on generalization and multi-modal expressivity.

title: An Integrative Genome-Scale Metabolic Modeling and Machine Learning Framework for Predicting and Optimizing Biofuel-Relevant Biomass Production in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
summary: Saccharomyces cerevisiae is a cornerstone organism in industrial biotechnology, valued for its genetic tractability and robust fermentative capacity. Accurately predicting biomass flux across diverse environmental and genetic perturbations remains a significant challenge for rational strain design. We present a computational framework combining the Yeast9 genome-scale metabolic model with machine learning and optimization to predict, interpret, and enhance biomass flux. Flux balance analysis generated 2,000 flux profiles by varying glucose, oxygen, and ammonium uptake rates. Random Forest and XGBoost regressors achieved R2 of 0.99989 and 0.9990, respectively. A variational autoencoder revealed four distinct metabolic clusters, and SHAP analysis identified glycolysis, the TCA cycle, and lipid biosynthesis as key biomass determinants. In silico overexpression achieved a biomass flux of 0.979 gDW/hr, while Bayesian optimization of nutrient constraints produced a 12-fold increase (0.0858 to 1.041 gDW/hr). A generative adversarial network proposed stoichiometrically feasible novel flux configurations. This framework demonstrates how genome-scale simulation, interpretable ML, and generative modeling can advance yeast metabolic engineering.

AI.tldr: Integrated GEM-based simulations with ML and generative models to predict, interpret, and enhance yeast biomass flux; achieves near-perfect predictive accuracy and substantial flux improvements, illustrating a workflow for strain design.
AI.motivation: Biomass flux prediction under diverse environmental and genetic perturbations is hard; integrating genome-scale modeling with interpretable ML and generative modeling can enable accurate prediction, interpretation, and design-guided optimization in yeast.
AI.method: Use the Yeast9 genome-scale metabolic model with flux balance analysis to generate 2,000 flux profiles by varying glucose, oxygen, and ammonium uptake. Train Random Forest and XGBoost regressors to predict biomass flux. Apply a variational autoencoder to uncover metabolic clusters. Use SHAP to identify key determinants. Evaluate in silico overexpression and perform Bayesian optimization of nutrient constraints. Employ a generative adversarial network to propose novel, stoichiometrically feasible flux configurations.
AI.result: Random Forest and XGBoost achieved R2 of 0.99989 and 0.9990. VAE revealed four metabolic clusters. SHAP highlighted glycolysis, TCA cycle, and lipid biosynthesis as main determinants. In silico overexpression yielded biomass flux of 0.979 gDW/hr. Bayesian optimization increased flux from 0.0858 to 1.041 gDW/hr (12-fold). GAN proposed additional feasible novel flux configurations.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates a cohesive framework that combines genome-scale simulation, interpretable machine learning, and generative modeling to advance yeast metabolic engineering and strain design.

title: Agent Factories for High Level Synthesis: How Far Can General-Purpose Coding Agents Go in Hardware Optimization?
summary:
We present an empirical study of how far general-purpose coding agents -- without hardware-specific training -- can optimize hardware designs from high-level algorithmic specifications. We introduce an agent factory, a two-stage pipeline that constructs and coordinates multiple autonomous optimization agents.
  In Stage~1, the pipeline decomposes a design into sub-kernels, independently optimizes each using pragma and code-level transformations, and formulates an Integer Linear Program (ILP) to assemble globally promising configurations under an area constraint. In Stage~2, it launches $N$ expert agents over the top ILP solutions, each exploring cross-function optimizations such as pragma recombination, loop fusion, and memory restructuring that are not captured by sub-kernel decomposition.
  We evaluate the approach on 12 kernels from HLS-Eval and Rodinia-HLS using Claude Code (Opus~4.5/4.6) with AMD Vitis HLS. Scaling from 1 to 10 agents yields a mean $8.27\times$ speedup over baseline, with larger gains on harder benchmarks: streamcluster exceeds $20\times$ and kmeans reaches approximately $10\times$. Across benchmarks, agents consistently rediscover known hardware optimization patterns without domain-specific training, and the best designs often do not originate from top-ranked ILP candidates, indicating that global optimization exposes improvements missed by sub-kernel search. These results establish agent scaling as a practical and effective axis for HLS optimization.

AI.tldr: Two-stage agent factory for HLS optimization: sub-kernel ILP-based assembly plus cross-function expert search yields large speedups (mean 8.27x; up to >20x on some benchmarks) and finds improvements beyond top ILP candidates.
AI.motivation: Address the barrier of hardware design optimization from high-level specifications without domain-specific training; test whether general-purpose coding agents can scale to improve HLS outcomes by coordinating sub-kernel optimization with global, cross-function exploration.
AI.method: Stage 1 decomposes designs into sub-kernels, applies pragma and code-level transformations, and formulates an ILP to assemble promising configurations under an area constraint. Stage 2 launches N expert agents over top ILP solutions to explore cross-function optimizations (pragma recombination, loop fusion, memory restructuring) not captured by sub-kernel search. Evaluation on 12 kernels from HLS-Eval and Rodinia-HLS using Claude Code (Opus 4.5/4.6) with AMD Vitis HLS.
AI.result: Mean speedup of 8.27× as agents scale from 1 to 10; larger gains on harder benchmarks (streamcluster >20×, kmeans ≈10×). Agents consistently rediscover known hardware optimization patterns without domain-specific training; best designs often do not originate from top-ranked ILP candidates, indicating global optimization uncovers improvements missed by sub-kernel search.
AI.conclusion: Agent scaling is a practical and effective axis for HLS optimization, enabling substantial gains by leveraging cross-kernel interactions beyond sub-kernel scope.

title: VideoTIR: Accurate Understanding for Long Videos with Efficient Tool-Integrated Reasoning
summary: Existing Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) often suffer from hallucinations in long video understanding (LVU), primarily due to the imbalance between textual and visual tokens. Observing that MLLMs handle short visual inputs well, recent LVU works alleviate hallucinations by automatically parsing the vast visual data into manageable segments that can be effectively processed by MLLMs. SFT-based tool-calling methods can serve this purpose, but they typically require vast amounts of fine-grained, high-quality data and suffer from constrained tool-calling trajectories. We propose a novel VideoTIR that leverages Reinforcement Learning (RL) to encourage proper usage of comprehensive multi-level toolkits for efficient long video understanding. VideoTIR explores both Zero-RL and SFT cold-starting to enable MLLMs to retrieve and focus on meaningful video segments/images/regions, enhancing long video understanding both accurately and efficiently. To reduce redundant tool-calling, we propose Toolkit Action Grouped Policy Optimization (TAGPO), which enhances the efficiency of the calling process through stepwise reward assignment and reuse of failed rollouts. Additionally, we develop a sandbox-based trajectory synthesis framework to generate high-quality trajectories data. Extensive experiments on three long-video QA benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our method.

AI.tldr: VideoTIR uses reinforcement learning to optimize tool-calling for long video understanding in multimodal LLMs, addressing hallucinations by focusing on meaningful segments via multi-level toolkits, with TAGPO to improve efficiency, sandbox trajectory synthesis, and zero-shot/SFT cold-start strategies, validated on three long-video QA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: MLLMs struggle with hallucinations in long video understanding due to imbalance between textual and visual tokens and processing of vast visual data. Short-input handling is good, but long videos require effective parsing into segments. Existing SFT-based tool calling needs large, fine-grained data and suffers from constrained trajectories, hindering scalable LVU.
AI.method: VideoTIR employs reinforcement learning to train MLLMs to invoke a comprehensive, multi-level toolkit to retrieve and focus on meaningful video segments/images/regions. It supports zero-RL and SFT-based cold-starts to bootstrap tool usage. TAGPO (Toolkit Action Grouped Policy Optimization) enhances call efficiency via stepwise reward assignment and reuse of failed rollouts. A sandbox-based trajectory synthesis framework generates high-quality trajectories data for training and evaluation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on three long-video QA benchmarks demonstrate improved accuracy and efficiency in long video understanding through guided, efficient tool calling.
AI.conclusion: RL-guided tool calling with multi-level toolkits and TAGPO, plus sandbox trajectory synthesis, effectively mitigates hallucinations in LVU by enabling precise, economical retrieval and processing of long video content.

title: Revisiting On-Policy Distillation: Empirical Failure Modes and Simple Fixes
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) is appealing for large language model (LLM) post-training because it evaluates teacher feedback on student-generated rollouts rather than fixed teacher traces. In long-horizon settings, however, the common sampled-token variant is fragile: it reduces distribution matching to a one-token signal and becomes increasingly unreliable as rollouts drift away from prefixes the teacher commonly visits. We revisit OPD from the estimator and implementation sides. Theoretically, token-level OPD is biased relative to sequence-level reverse-KL, but it has a much tighter worst-case variance bound; our toy study shows the same tradeoff empirically, with stronger future-reward coupling producing higher gradient variance and less stable learning. Empirically, we identify three failure modes of sampled-token OPD: an imbalanced one-token signal, unreliable teacher guidance on student-generated prefixes, and distortions caused by tokenizer or special-token mismatch. We address these issues with teacher top-K local support matching, implemented as truncated reverse-KL with top-p rollout sampling and special-token masking. Across single-task math reasoning and multi-task agentic-plus-math training, this objective yields more stable optimization and better downstream performance than sampled-token OPD.

AI.tldr: Token-level on-policy distillation has bias but lower variance; propose top-K local support matching using truncated reverse-KL with top-p rollout and masking; improves stability and downstream performance vs standard sampled-token OPD.
AI.motivation: Stabilize long-horizon LLM post-training by evaluating teacher feedback on student rollouts. Address fragility and drift in sampled-token OPD when rollouts depart from teacher prefixes.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis shows token-level OPD is biased relative to sequence-level reverse-KL but has tighter worst-case variance. Empirical toy study confirms bias-variance tradeoff; identify three failure modes of sampled-token OPD (imbalanced one-token signal, unreliable guidance on prefixes, tokenizer/special-token mismatch). Propose teacher top-K local support matching as truncated reverse-KL with top-p rollout sampling and special-token masking.
AI.result: Identification of three failure modes; introduction of top-K local support matching improves stability and downstream performance. Demonstrated on single-task math reasoning and multi-task agentic-plus-math training, outperforming sampled-token OPD.
AI.conclusion: Top-K local support matching mitigates the weaknesses of sampled-token OPD by trading some bias for substantial variance reduction, yielding more stable optimization and better downstream results in long-horizon distillation settings.

title: R-C2: Cycle-Consistent Reinforcement Learning Improves Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Robust perception and reasoning require consistency across sensory modalities. Yet current multimodal models often violate this principle, yielding contradictory predictions for visual and textual representations of the same concept. Rather than masking these failures with standard voting mechanisms, which can amplify systematic biases, we show that cross-modal inconsistency provides a rich and natural signal for learning. We introduce RC2, a reinforcement learning framework that resolves internal conflicts by enforcing cross-modal cycle consistency. By requiring a model to perform backward inference, switch modalities, and reliably reconstruct the answer through forward inference, we obtain a dense, label-free reward. This cyclic constraint encourages the model to align its internal representations autonomously. Optimizing for this structure mitigates modality-specific errors and improves reasoning accuracy by up to 7.6 points. Our results suggest that advanced reasoning emerges not only from scaling data, but also from enforcing a structurally consistent understanding of the world.

AI.tldr: Introduce RC2, a reinforcement-learning framework that enforces cross-modal cycle consistency to resolve internal conflicts between visual and textual predictions, using backward inference, modality switching, and forward reconstruction to produce a dense, label-free reward; yields up to 7.6 point improvement in reasoning accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal models suffer from cross-modal inconsistencies that bias predictions; standard voting can amplify biases. The paper posits that cross-modal inconsistency is a rich signal for learning and alignment.
AI.method: Propose RC2, which enforces cycle consistency across modalities via backward inference, modality switching, and reliable reconstruction of the answer through forward inference; uses a dense reward signal that is label-free; optimizes the model to align internal representations.
AI.result: Achieves up to 7.6-point improvement in reasoning accuracy; mitigates modality-specific errors; demonstrates that structured consistency improves reasoning beyond data scaling.
AI.conclusion: Advanced reasoning can emerge from enforcing structural cross-modal consistency; aligning modalities leads to robust representations and better generalization in multimodal reasoning tasks.

title: CARE: Training-Free Controllable Restoration for Medical Images via Dual-Latent Steering
summary: Medical image restoration is essential for improving the usability of noisy, incomplete, and artifact-corrupted clinical scans, yet existing methods often rely on task-specific retraining and offer limited control over the trade-off between faithful reconstruction and prior-driven enhancement. This lack of controllability is especially problematic in clinical settings, where overly aggressive restoration may introduce hallucinated details or alter diagnostically important structures. In this work, we propose CARE, a training-free controllable restoration framework for real-world medical images that explicitly balances structure preservation and prior-guided refinement during inference. CARE uses a dual-latent restoration strategy, in which one branch enforces data fidelity and anatomical consistency while the other leverages a generative prior to recover missing or degraded information. A risk-aware adaptive controller dynamically adjusts the contribution of each branch based on restoration uncertainty and local structural reliability, enabling conservative or enhancement-focused restoration modes without additional model training. We evaluate CARE on noisy and incomplete medical imaging scenarios and show that it achieves strong restoration quality while better preserving clinically relevant structures and reducing the risk of implausible reconstructions and show that it achieves strong restoration quality while better preserving clinically relevant structures and reducing the risk of implausible reconstructions. The proposed approach offers a practical step toward safer, more controllable, and more deployment-ready medical image restoration.

AI.tldr: Training-free, controllable medical image restoration (CARE) with a dual-latent strategy and a risk-aware controller to balance data fidelity and prior-driven refinement, enabling safe, inference-time control without retraining.
AI.motivation: Addresses the need for controllable restoration in clinical settings, where aggressive enhancement risks hallucinations and alteration of diagnostically important structures; existing methods rely on task-specific retraining and offer limited fidelity-control.
AI.method: Introduces CARE with dual-latent restoration: one branch enforces data fidelity/anatomical consistency, the other uses a generative prior to recover missing/degraded information. An inference-time, risk-aware adaptive controller balances contributions between branches based on restoration uncertainty and local structural reliability, enabling conservative or enhancement-focused modes without extra training.
AI.result: Evaluated on noisy/incomplete medical images, CARE achieves strong restoration quality while better preserving clinically relevant structures and reducing the risk of implausible reconstructions; summarized as a practical, deployment-ready approach.
AI.conclusion: A practical step toward safer, more controllable, and deployment-ready medical image restoration that avoids retraining for different scenarios.

title: Spatiotemporal System Forecasting with Irregular Time Steps via Masked Autoencoder
summary: Predicting high-dimensional dynamical systems with irregular time steps presents significant challenges for current data-driven algorithms. These irregularities arise from missing data, sparse observations, or adaptive computational techniques, reducing prediction accuracy. To address these limitations, we propose a novel method: a Physics-Spatiotemporal Masked Autoencoder. This method integrates convolutional autoencoders for spatial feature extraction with masked autoencoders optimised for irregular time series, leveraging attention mechanisms to reconstruct the entire physical sequence in a single prediction pass. The model avoids the need for data imputation while preserving physical integrity of the system. Here, 'physics' refers to high-dimensional fields generated by underlying dynamical systems, rather than the enforcement of explicit physical constraints or PDE residuals. We evaluate this approach on multiple simulated datasets and real-world ocean temperature data. The results demonstrate that our method achieves significant improvements in prediction accuracy, robustness to nonlinearities, and computational efficiency over traditional convolutional and recurrent network methods. The model shows potential for capturing complex spatiotemporal patterns without requiring domain-specific knowledge, with applications in climate modelling, fluid dynamics, ocean forecasting, environmental monitoring, and scientific computing.

AI.tldr: A hybrid CNN-based spatial encoder plus masked autoencoder for irregular-time-series, predicting high-dimensional dynamical systems without data imputation by reconstructing full spatiotemporal sequences in one pass.
AI.motivation: Irregular time steps from missing data, sparse observations, or adaptive sampling degrade performance of standard models; need accurate, efficient predictions for high-dimensional spatiotemporal fields without relying on explicit PDE constraints or imputation.
AI.method: Proposes a Physics-Spatiotemporal Masked Autoencoder that combines convolutional autoencoders for spatial feature extraction with masked autoencoders optimized for irregular time series. It uses attention to reconstruct the entire physical sequence in a single prediction pass, and avoids data imputation. ‘Physics’ denotes high-dimensional fields from underlying dynamical systems rather than explicit physical constraints. Evaluated on simulated datasets and real-world ocean temperature data.
AI.result: Shows significant gains in prediction accuracy, robustness to nonlinearities, and computational efficiency over traditional convolutional and recurrent networks. Demonstrates capability to capture complex spatiotemporal patterns without domain-specific knowledge; validated on multiple simulations and real ocean temperature data.
AI.conclusion: The approach offers a data-efficient, domain-agnostic pathway for predicting high-dimensional spatiotemporal dynamics under irregular sampling, with broad potential applications in climate modelling, fluid dynamics, ocean forecasting, environmental monitoring, and scientific computing. Future work may explore integrating explicit physical constraints or extending to other data modalities.

title: Back to Basics: Revisiting ASR in the Age of Voice Agents
summary: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) systems have achieved near-human accuracy on curated benchmarks, yet still fail in real-world voice agents under conditions that current evaluations do not systematically cover. Without diagnostic tools that isolate specific failure factors, practitioners cannot anticipate which conditions, in which languages, will cause what degree of degradation. We introduce WildASR, a multilingual (four-language) diagnostic benchmark sourced entirely from real human speech that factorizes ASR robustness along three axes: environmental degradation, demographic shift, and linguistic diversity. Evaluating seven widely used ASR systems, we find severe and uneven performance degradation, and model robustness does not transfer across languages or conditions. Critically, models often hallucinate plausible but unspoken content under partial or degraded inputs, creating concrete safety risks for downstream agent behavior. Our results demonstrate that targeted, factor-isolated evaluation is essential for understanding and improving ASR reliability in production systems. Besides the benchmark itself, we also present three analytical tools that practitioners can use to guide deployment decisions.

AI.tldr: WildASR introduces a four-language, real-speech diagnostic benchmark that factors ASR robustness along environmental degradation, demographic shift, and linguistic diversity; reveals severe, uneven degradation across models and languages, plus hallucinations under partial inputs; provides three deployment-guidance analytical tools.
AI.motivation: Current ASR benchmarks underestimate real-world failure modes. A diagnostic, factorized evaluation is needed to anticipate language- and condition-specific degradation and safety risks in production systems.
AI.method: Develop WildASR from real human speech across four languages, factorize ASR robustness along three axes (environmental, demographic, linguistic); evaluate seven widely used ASR systems; analyze cross-language transfer of robustness; document hallucination risks; introduce three analytical tools for deployment decisions.
AI.result: Observes severe and uneven performance degradation across languages and conditions; robustness does not transfer across languages or conditions; models often hallucinate plausible but unspoken content under degraded inputs; establishes that factorized evaluation is essential for understanding ASR reliability; provides three analytical tools for practitioners.
AI.conclusion: A targeted, factorized evaluation framework is essential for reliable ASR deployment; WildASR and its tools enable more informed deployment decisions and highlight safety risks in real-world ASR usage.

title: GeoNDC: A Queryable Neural Data Cube for Planetary-Scale Earth Observation
summary: Satellite Earth observation has accumulated massive spatiotemporal archives essential for monitoring environmental change, yet these remain organized as discrete raster files, making them costly to store, transmit, and query. We present GeoNDC, a queryable neural data cube that encodes planetary-scale Earth observation data as a continuous spatiotemporal implicit neural field, enabling on-demand queries and continuous-time reconstruction without full decompression. Experiments on a 20-year global MODIS MCD43A4 reflectance record (7 bands, 5\,km, 8-day sampling) show that the learned representation supports direct spatiotemporal queries on consumer hardware. On Sentinel-2 imagery (10\,m), continuous temporal parameterization recovers cloud-free dynamics with high fidelity ($R^2 > 0.85$) under simulated 2-km cloud occlusion. On HiGLASS biophysical products (LAI and FPAR), GeoNDC attains near-perfect accuracy ($R^2 > 0.98$). The representation compresses the 20-year MODIS archive to 0.44\,GB -- approximately 95:1 relative to an optimized Int16 baseline -- with high spectral fidelity (mean $R^2 > 0.98$, mean RMSE $= 0.021$). These results suggest GeoNDC offers a unified AI-native representation for planetary-scale Earth observation, complementing raw archives with a compact, analysis-ready data layer integrating query, reconstruction, and compression in a single framework.

AI.tldr: GeoNDC encodes large Earth observation archives as a continuous spatiotemporal implicit neural field, enabling on-demand queries and reconstruction without full decompression, while achieving substantial compression with high fidelity across multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Massive EO raster archives are bulky, hard to store, transmit, and query. An AI-native, unified representation could reduce storage, enable fast queries, and support continuous-time reconstruction.
AI.method: Train a continuous spatiotemporal implicit neural field (neural data cube) that encodes planetary-scale EO data. Evaluate on MODIS MCD43A4 (20 years, 7 bands, 5 km, 8-day), Sentinel-2 (10 m, cloud occlusion simulation), and HiGLASS LAI/FPAR. Demonstrate on-demand spatiotemporal queries, cloud occlusion robustness, and compression performance (e.g., 0.44 GB for MODIS, ~95:1 compression vs Int16).
AI.result: Learned representation supports direct spatiotemporal queries on commodity hardware. For Sentinel-2, continuous temporal parameterization recovers cloud-free dynamics with R^2>0.85 under simulated 2-km occlusion. For HiGLASS products, R^2>0.98. MODIS archive compressed to 0.44 GB (~95× reduction) with mean R^2>0.98 and RMSE=0.021, indicating high spectral fidelity.
AI.conclusion: GeoNDC offers a unified AI-native data layer that couples query, reconstruction, and compression, complementing raw archives and enabling analysis-ready access to planetary-scale EO data.

title: Training the Knowledge Base through Evidence Distillation and Write-Back Enrichment
summary: The knowledge base in a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system is typically assembled once and never revised, even though the facts a query requires are often fragmented across documents and buried in irrelevant content. We argue that the knowledge base should be treated as a trainable component and propose WriteBack-RAG, a framework that uses labeled examples to identify where retrieval succeeds, isolate the relevant documents, and distill them into compact knowledge units that are indexed alongside the original corpus. Because the method modifies only the corpus, it can be applied once as an offline preprocessing step and combined with any RAG pipeline. Across four RAG methods, six benchmarks, and two LLM backbones, WriteBack-RAG improves every evaluated setting, with gains averaging +2.14%. Cross-method transfer experiments further show that the distilled knowledge benefits RAG pipelines other than the one used to produce it, confirming that the improvement resides in the corpus itself.

AI.tldr: Proposes WriteBack-RAG: treat knowledge base as trainable, distill relevant documents into compact units, improving RAG performance offline; gains ~2.14% on average across settings.
AI.motivation: In RAG, the knowledge base is often static and not revised, while query-relevant facts may be scattered across documents; there is a need to refine the corpus to improve retrieval and answer quality.
AI.method: Label-based identification of where retrieval succeeds, isolate the relevant documents, and distill them into compact knowledge units that are indexed alongside the original corpus. This offline preprocessing step modifies only the corpus and is compatible with any RAG pipeline.
AI.result: Across four RAG methods, six benchmarks, and two LLM backbones, WriteBack-RAG improves every evaluated setting, with an average gain of +2.14%. Cross-method transfer experiments show distilled knowledge benefits other RAG pipelines, indicating the improvement resides in the corpus.
AI.conclusion: Corpus-level distillation can be performed offline to create a trainable knowledge base; the gains arise from the refined corpus rather than changes to the retrieval algorithm, and the approach generalizes across pipelines.

title: MoRGS: Efficient Per-Gaussian Motion Reasoning for Streamable Dynamic 3D Scenes
summary: Online reconstruction of dynamic scenes aims to learn from streaming multi-view inputs under low-latency constraints. The fast training and real-time rendering capabilities of 3D Gaussian Splatting have made on-the-fly reconstruction practically feasible, enabling online 4D reconstruction. However, existing online approaches, despite their efficiency and visual quality, fail to learn per-Gaussian motion that reflects true scene dynamics. Without explicit motion cues, appearance and motion are optimized solely under photometric loss, causing per-Gaussian motion to chase pixel residuals rather than true 3D motion. To address this, we propose MoRGS, an efficient online per-Gaussian motion reasoning framework that explicitly models per-Gaussian motion to improve 4D reconstruction quality. Specifically, we leverage optical flow on a sparse set of key views as lightweight motion cues that regularize per-Gaussian motion beyond photometric supervision. To compensate for the sparsity of flow supervision, we learn a per-Gaussian motion offset field that reconciles discrepancies between projected 3D motion and observed flow across views and time. In addition, we introduce a per-Gaussian motion confidence that separates dynamic from static Gaussians and weights Gaussian attribute residual updates, thereby suppressing redundant motion in static regions for better temporal consistency and accelerating the modeling of large motions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MoRGS achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality and motion fidelity among online methods, while maintaining streamable performance.

AI.tldr: MoRGS enables online per-Gaussian motion reasoning for 3D Gaussian Splatting to improve online 4D reconstruction by introducing sparse optical-flow supervision, per-Gaussian motion offset fields, and per-Gaussian motion confidence to distinguish dynamic vs static Gaussians and weight updates.
AI.motivation: Existing online methods optimize appearance and motion under photometric loss without explicit per-Gaussian motion, causing motion to chase residuals rather than true 3D dynamics; need efficient, dynamics-aware online reconstruction.
AI.method: Leverage optical flow on a sparse set of key views as lightweight motion cues to regularize per-Gaussian motion; learn per-Gaussian motion offset field to reconcile discrepancies between projected 3D motion and observed flow across views/time; introduce per-Gaussian motion confidence to separate dynamic/static Gaussians and weight updates to suppress static-region motion and improve temporal consistency; enable streamable online reconstruction.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art reconstruction quality and motion fidelity among online methods while maintaining streamable (real-time) performance.
AI.conclusion: MoRGS provides explicit, efficient motion-aware dynamics modeling for online 3D Gaussian Splatting, improving 4D reconstruction quality, motion fidelity, and robustness to sparse supervision; supports large motions.

title: DyMRL: Dynamic Multispace Representation Learning for Multimodal Event Forecasting in Knowledge Graph
comment: Accepted to The ACM Web Conference 2026 (WWW '26). This version is published under a CC BY license
summary: Accurate representation of multimodal knowledge is crucial for event forecasting in real-world scenarios. However, existing studies have largely focused on static settings, overlooking the dynamic acquisition and fusion of multimodal knowledge. 1) At the knowledge acquisition level, how to learn time-sensitive information of different modalities, especially the dynamic structural modality. Existing dynamic learning methods are often limited to shallow structures across heterogeneous spaces or simple unispaces, making it difficult to capture deep relation-aware geometric features. 2) At the knowledge fusion level, how to learn evolving multimodal fusion features. Existing knowledge fusion methods based on static coattention struggle to capture the varying historical contributions of different modalities to future events. To this end, we propose DyMRL, a Dynamic Multispace Representation Learning approach to efficiently acquire and fuse multimodal temporal knowledge. 1) For the former issue, DyMRL integrates time-specific structural features from Euclidean, hyperbolic, and complex spaces into a relational message-passing framework to learn deep representations, reflecting human intelligences in associative thinking, high-order abstracting, and logical reasoning. Pretrained models endow DyMRL with time-sensitive visual and linguistic intelligences. 2) For the latter concern, DyMRL incorporates advanced dual fusion-evolution attention mechanisms that assign dynamic learning emphases equally to different modalities at different timestamps in a symmetric manner. To evaluate DyMRL's event forecasting performance through leveraging its learned multimodal temporal knowledge in history, we construct four multimodal temporal knowledge graph benchmarks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DyMRL outperforms state-of-the-art dynamic unimodal and static multimodal baseline methods.

AI.tldr: DyMRL: a dynamic, multi-space representation learning framework for time-sensitive multimodal knowledge acquisition and fusion, improving event forecasting; validated on four benchmarks and outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Accurate event forecasting requires capturing evolving multimodal knowledge; existing dynamic methods are limited to shallow structures or static fusion, lacking deep, time-sensitive, cross-space representations.
AI.method: DyMRL integrates time-specific structural features from Euclidean, hyperbolic, and complex spaces into a relational message-passing framework. Pretrained models provide time-sensitive visual and linguistic intelligences. It employs dual fusion-evolution attention mechanisms that dynamically adjust modality emphasis over time in a symmetric manner. Four multimodal temporal knowledge graph benchmarks are used for evaluation.
AI.result: DyMRL outperforms state-of-the-art dynamic unimodal and static multimodal baselines on four benchmarks, demonstrating effective learning and fusion of evolving multimodal temporal knowledge.
AI.conclusion: DyMRL offers a scalable approach to acquire and fuse time-sensitive multimodal knowledge across heterogeneous geometric spaces, advancing event forecasting by capturing dynamic modality contributions over time.

title: GaussFusion: Improving 3D Reconstruction in the Wild with A Geometry-Informed Video Generator
comment: CVPR 2026 main paper camera-ready. Project page: http://research.zhuliyuan.net/projects/GaussFusion/
summary: We present GaussFusion, a novel approach for improving 3D Gaussian splatting (3DGS) reconstructions in the wild through geometry-informed video generation. GaussFusion mitigates common 3DGS artifacts, including floaters, flickering, and blur caused by camera pose errors, incomplete coverage, and noisy geometry initialization. Unlike prior RGB-based approaches limited to a single reconstruction pipeline, our method introduces a geometry-informed video-to-video generator that refines 3DGS renderings across both optimization-based and feed-forward methods. Given an existing reconstruction, we render a Gaussian primitive video buffer encoding depth, normals, opacity, and covariance, which the generator refines to produce temporally coherent, artifact-free frames. We further introduce an artifact synthesis pipeline that simulates diverse degradation patterns, ensuring robustness and generalization. GaussFusion achieves state-of-the-art performance on novel-view synthesis benchmarks, and an efficient variant runs in real time at 21 FPS while maintaining similar performance, enabling interactive 3D applications.

AI.tldr: GaussFusion introduces a geometry-informed video-to-video generator to refine 3D Gaussian splatting reconstructions, reducing artifacts and enabling real-time interactive rendering.
AI.motivation: Address artifacts in 3D Gaussian splatting caused by camera pose errors, incomplete coverage, and noisy geometry initialization; limitations of RGB-based single pipelines; need for temporally coherent, robust reconstructions in the wild.
AI.method: Render a Gaussian primitive video buffer encoding depth, normals, opacity, and covariance; feed this into a geometry-informed video-to-video generator that refines renderings across both optimization-based and feed-forward pipelines; include an artifact synthesis pipeline to simulate diverse degradation patterns; provides a real-time variant at 21 FPS.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on novel-view synthesis benchmarks; a real-time variant runs at 21 FPS with similar performance.
AI.conclusion: GaussFusion provides robust, temporally coherent refinements for 3D Gaussian splatting reconstructions, enabling interactive 3D applications and generalizing across reconstruction pipelines.

title: Experiential Reflective Learning for Self-Improving LLM Agents
comment: Published as a conference paper at the ICLR 2026 MemAgents Workshop
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled the development of autonomous agents capable of complex reasoning and multi-step problem solving. However, these agents struggle to adapt to specialized environments and do not leverage past interactions, approaching each new task from scratch regardless of their accumulated experience. We introduce Experiential Reflective Learning (ERL), a simple self-improvement framework that enables rapid environment adaptation through experiential learning. ERL reflects on task trajectories and outcomes to generate heuristics, capturing actionable lessons that transfer across tasks. At test time, relevant heuristics are retrieved based on the current task and injected into the agent's context to guide execution. On the Gaia2 benchmark, ERL improves success rate by 7.8% over a ReAct baseline, with large gains in task completion reliability, and outperforms prior experiential learning methods. Through systematic ablations, we find that selective retrieval is essential and that heuristics provide more transferable abstractions than few-shot trajectory prompting. These results demonstrate that reflecting on single-attempt experiences to extract transferable heuristics enables effective agent self-improvement.

AI.tldr: ERL is a self-improvement framework for LLM agents that extracts transferable heuristics from past task trajectories and retrieves them at test time to rapidly adapt to new environments; it achieves notable improvements on Gaia2 over ReAct, with ablations highlighting the importance of selective retrieval and the superiority of heuristics over few-shot prompts.
AI.motivation: LLM agents often start fresh on each task and struggle to leverage accumulated experience or adapt to specialized environments; there is a need for simple, transferable self-improvement signals.
AI.method: ERL reflects on task trajectories and outcomes to derive heuristics; at test time, relevant heuristics are retrieved based on the current task and injected into the agent's context to guide execution.
AI.result: On Gaia2, ERL improves success rate by 7.8% over a ReAct baseline, with large gains in task completion reliability and outperformed prior experiential learning methods; ablations show selective retrieval is essential and heuristics offer better transferability than few-shot trajectory prompting.
AI.conclusion: Reflecting on single-attempt experiences to extract transferable heuristics enables effective agent self-improvement and rapid adaptation to new environments via selective retrieval of useful heuristics.

title: Synergistic Event-SVE Imaging for Quantitative Propellant Combustion Diagnostics
summary: Real-time monitoring of high-energy propellant combustion is difficult. Extreme high dynamic range (HDR), microsecond-scale particle motion, and heavy smoke often occur together. These conditions drive saturation, motion blur, and unstable particle extraction in conventional imaging. We present a closed-loop Event--SVE measurement system that couples a spatially variant exposure (SVE) camera with a stereo pair of neuromorphic event cameras. The SVE branch produces HDR maps with an explicit smoke-aware fusion strategy. A multi-cue smoke-likelihood map is used to separate particle emission from smoke scattering, yielding calibrated intensity maps for downstream analysis. The resulting HDR maps also provide the absolute-intensity reference missing in event cameras. This reference is used to suppress smoke-driven event artifacts and to improve particle-state discrimination. Based on the cleaned event observations, a stereo event-based 3D pipeline estimates separation height and equivalent particle size through feature extraction and triangulation (maximum calibration error 0.56%). Experiments on boron-based propellants show multimodal equivalent-radius statistics. The system also captures fast separation transients that are difficult to observe with conventional sensors. Overall, the proposed framework provides a practical, calibration-consistent route to microsecond-resolved 3D combustion measurement under smoke-obscured HDR conditions.

AI.tldr: A real-time HDR-event fusion system combining an SVE camera with stereo neuromorphic event cameras enables microsecond-precision 3D measurements of particle emissions in smoke-louden propellant combustion, achieving 0.56% calibration error in height/size triangulation.
AI.motivation: High-energy propellant combustion produces extreme dynamic range, rapid microsecond motion, and heavy smoke, causing traditional imaging to saturate, blur, or misinterpret particle signals. A calibrated, high-temporal-resolution 3D diagnostic under smoke is needed for insightful analysis of transient phenomena.
AI.method: Integrate a spatially variant exposure (SVE) camera with a stereo pair of neuromorphic event cameras in a closed-loop system. The SVE branch generates HDR maps with a smoke-aware fusion strategy; a multi-cue smoke-likelihood map separates particle emission from smoke scattering and yields calibrated intensity maps. An absolute-intensity reference from HDR addresses missing information in event cameras, enabling suppression of smoke-driven artifacts. A stereo event-based 3D pipeline performs feature extraction and triangulation to estimate separation height and equivalent particle size (calibration error ≤0.56%).
AI.result: HDR maps enable calibrated intensity and improved particle-state discrimination; smoke artifacts in events are suppressed; the system obtains microsecond-resolved 3D measurements, including separation transients and multimodal equivalent-radius statistics in boron-based propellants.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a calibration-consistent, practical route for microsecond-resolved 3D combustion measurement under smoke-obscured HDR conditions, enabling observation of fast transient phenomena previously challenging with conventional sensors.

title: Learning Explicit Continuous Motion Representation for Dynamic Gaussian Splatting from Monocular Videos
summary: We present an approach for high-quality dynamic Gaussian Splatting from monocular videos. To this end, we in this work go one step further beyond previous methods to explicitly model continuous position and orientation deformation of dynamic Gaussians, using an SE(3) B-spline motion bases with a compact set of control points. To improve computational efficiency while enhancing the ability to model complex motions, an adaptive control mechanism is devised to dynamically adjust the number of motion bases and control points. Besides, we develop a soft segment reconstruction strategy to mitigate long-interval motion interference, and employ a multi-view diffusion model to provide multi-view cues for avoiding overfitting to training views. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods in novel view synthesis. Our code is available at https://github.com/hhhddddddd/se3bsplinegs.

AI.tldr: High-quality dynamic Gaussian Splatting from monocular video using SE(3) B-spline motion bases with adaptive control, soft segment reconstruction, and multi-view diffusion to improve novel-view synthesis; outperforms state-of-the-art.
AI.motivation: Address limitations in dynamic scene reconstruction: model continuous pose/orientation changes of dynamic Gaussians, improve computational efficiency, reduce motion interference, and leverage multi-view cues to avoid overfitting to training views.
AI.method: Represent dynamic Gaussians with SE(3) B-spline motion bases controlled by a compact set of control points; adaptively adjust the number of bases/points; use soft segment reconstruction to mitigate long-interval motion interference; incorporate a multi-view diffusion model to supply multi-view cues; train on monocular video for novel-view synthesis.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show superiority over state-of-the-art methods in novel-view synthesis; code released at provided repository.
AI.conclusion: The approach advances dynamic Gaussian splatting by enabling continuous deformation via SE(3) B-splines, while ensuring efficiency and robustness through adaptive control, soft segmentation, and diffusion-based multi-view cues; demonstrates improved generalization to novel views.

title: Is Geometry Enough? An Evaluation of Landmark-Based Gaze Estimation
summary: Appearance-based gaze estimation frequently relies on deep Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). These models are accurate, but computationally expensive and act as "black boxes", offering little interpretability. Geometric methods based on facial landmarks are a lightweight alternative, but their performance limits and generalization capabilities remain underexplored in modern benchmarks. In this study, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of landmark-based gaze estimation. We introduce a standardized pipeline to extract and normalize landmarks from three large-scale datasets (Gaze360, ETH-XGaze, and GazeGene) and train lightweight regression models, specifically Extreme Gradient Boosted trees and two neural architectures: a holistic Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) and a siamese MLP designed to capture binocular geometry. We find that landmark-based models exhibit lower performance in within-domain evaluation, likely due to noise introduced into the datasets by the landmark detector. Nevertheless, in cross-domain evaluation, the proposed MLP architectures show generalization capabilities comparable to those of ResNet18 baselines. These findings suggest that sparse geometric features encode sufficient information for robust gaze estimation, paving the way for efficient, interpretable, and privacy-friendly edge applications. The source code and generated landmark-based datasets are available at https://github.com/daniele-agostinelli/LandmarkGaze.git.

AI.tldr: Landmark-based gaze estimation with a standardized pipeline and lightweight models shows cross-domain generalization comparable to ResNet18, but lower within-domain performance due to landmark detector noise; supports efficient, interpretable edge applications; code released.
AI.motivation: Assess the viability and generalization of landmark-based gaze estimation in modern benchmarks, balancing accuracy, efficiency, and interpretability, and to establish a standardized evaluation across datasets.
AI.method: Standardized pipeline to extract and normalize facial landmarks on Gaze360, ETH-XGaze, and GazeGene; train lightweight regressors (XGBoost), a holistic MLP, and a siamese MLP to exploit binocular geometry; evaluate within- and cross-domain performance and analyze landmark noise impact.
AI.result: Within-domain performance degrades due to noise from the landmark detector; across-domain, MLP variants generalize as well as ResNet18 baselines; landmark features suffice for robust gaze estimation in cross-domain settings; code and datasets released.
AI.conclusion: Sparse geometric (landmark-based) features can deliver accurate, efficient, and privacy-friendly gaze estimation, enabling robust edge deployments; encourages further study and standardization across datasets.

title: GIFT: Global Irreplaceability Frame Targeting for Efficient Video Understanding
summary: Video Large Language Models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable success in video understanding, but the significant computational cost from processing dense frames severely limits their practical application. Existing methods alleviate this by selecting keyframes, but their greedy decision-making, combined with a decoupled evaluation of relevance and diversity, often falls into local optima and results in erroneously selecting irrelevant noise frames. To address these challenges, we propose GIFT: Global Irreplaceability Frame Targeting, a novel training-free framework that selects frames by assessing their intrinsic irreplaceability. Specifically, we first introduce Directed Diversity to quantify a frame's uniqueness conditioned on relevance, which allows us to formulate a unified irreplaceability score. Subsequently, our Budget-Aware Refinement strategy employs a adaptive iterative process that first secures a core set of frames with the highest irreplaceability, and then shifts its priority to building crucial temporal context around these selections as the budget expands. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GIFT achieves a maximum average improvement of 12.5% across long-form video benchmarks on LLaVA-Video-7B compared to uniform sampling.

AI.tldr: GIFT is a training-free frame-selection framework for video LLMs that improves long-form video understanding by selecting frames based on frame irreplaceability, using Directed Diversity and Budget-Aware Refinement to outperform uniform sampling by up to 12.5% on LLaVA-Video-7B.
AI.motivation: High computational cost of dense-frame processing in video LLMs and reliance on greedy, decoupled relevance/diversity for keyframe selection often leads to local optima and inclusion of noisy, irrelevant frames.
AI.method: Introduce Directed Diversity to quantify a frame's uniqueness conditioned on relevance and formulate a unified irreplaceability score. Implement Budget-Aware Refinement with an adaptive iterative process that first secures a core set of highly irreplaceable frames and then expands temporal context around these selections as budget increases.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show up to 12.5% average improvement across long-form video benchmarks on LLaVA-Video-7B compared to uniform sampling.
AI.conclusion: GIFT provides a training-free, irreplaceability-based frame selection strategy that improves long-form video understanding efficiency and accuracy, demonstrating effective allocation of limited frames through Directed Diversity and Budget-Aware Refinement.

title: A Unified Memory Perspective for Probabilistic Trustworthy AI
summary: Trustworthy artificial intelligence increasingly relies on probabilistic computation to achieve robustness, interpretability, security and privacy. In practical systems, such workloads interleave deterministic data access with repeated stochastic sampling across models, data paths and system functions, shifting performance bottlenecks from arithmetic units to memory systems that must deliver both data and randomness. Here we present a unified data-access perspective in which deterministic access is treated as a limiting case of stochastic sampling, enabling both modes to be analyzed within a common framework. This view reveals that increasing stochastic demand reduces effective data-access efficiency and can drive systems into entropy-limited operation. Based on this insight, we define memory-level evaluation criteria, including unified operation, distribution programmability, efficiency, robustness to hardware non-idealities and parallel compatibility. Using these criteria, we analyze limitations of conventional architectures and examine emerging probabilistic compute-in-memory approaches that integrate sampling with memory access, outlining pathways toward scalable hardware for trustworthy AI.

AI.tldr: A unified probabilistic data-access framework shows stochastic demand reduces data-access efficiency and can drive entropy-limited operation; defines memory-level evaluation criteria and outlines paths toward scalable probabilistic compute-in-memory hardware for trustworthy AI.
AI.motivation: Probabilistic computation is central to trustworthy AI, but workloads interleave deterministic data access with stochastic sampling, shifting bottlenecks to memory. A common framework is needed to analyze both modes and guide hardware design.
AI.method: Propose a unified view where deterministic access is a limiting case of stochastic sampling; derive memory-level evaluation criteria (unified operation, distribution programmability, efficiency, robustness, parallel compatibility); analyze traditional architectures; survey emerging probabilistic compute-in-memory (CIM) approaches; outline design pathways.
AI.result: Reveals that increasing stochastic demand reduces effective data-access efficiency and can push systems into entropy-limited operation; provides a set of memory-level evaluation criteria; identifies limitations of conventional architectures and highlights potential CIM-based approaches as scalable paths for trustworthy AI.
AI.conclusion: The unified framework enables systematic assessment and guideposts for scalable hardware; probabilistic CIM approaches offer a promising route to meet the demands of trustworthy AI, with future work directed at optimizing data-randomness integration and hardware robustness.

title: Z-Erase: Enabling Concept Erasure in Single-Stream Diffusion Transformers
summary: Concept erasure serves as a vital safety mechanism for removing unwanted concepts from text-to-image (T2I) models. While extensively studied in U-Net and dual-stream architectures (e.g., Flux), this task remains under-explored in the recent emerging paradigm of single-stream diffusion transformers (e.g., Z-Image). In this new paradigm, text and image tokens are processed as a single unified sequence via shared parameters. Consequently, directly applying prior erasure methods typically leads to generation collapse. To bridge this gap, we introduce Z-Erase, the first concept erasure method tailored for single-stream T2I models. To guarantee stable image generation, Z-Erase first proposes a Stream Disentangled Concept Erasure Framework that decouples updates and enables existing methods on single-stream models. Subsequently, within this framework, we introduce Lagrangian-Guided Adaptive Erasure Modulation, a constrained algorithm that further balances the sensitive erasure-preservation trade-off. Moreover, we provide a rigorous convergence analysis proving that Z-Erase can converge to a Pareto stationary point. Experiments demonstrate that Z-Erase successfully overcomes the generation collapse issue, achieving state-of-the-art performance across a wide range of tasks.

AI.tldr: Z-Erase is the first concept erasure method for single-stream text-to-image diffusion transformers. It introduces a Stream Disentangled Concept Erasure Framework to decouple updates, and a Lagrangian-Guided Adaptive Erasure Modulation to balance erasure with preservation. The authors prove convergence to a Pareto stationary point and demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and avoidance of generation collapse across diverse tasks.
AI.motivation: Single-stream diffusion transformers process text and image tokens in a unified sequence, causing traditional erasure methods (designed for U-Nets or dual-stream models) to fail or cause generation collapse. There is a need for safe, effective concept erasure in this new architecture.
AI.method: 1) Propose Stream Disentangled Concept Erasure Framework to decouple erasure updates from the unified token stream, enabling compatibility with existing erasure methods on single-stream models. 2) Develop Lagrangian-Guided Adaptive Erasure Modulation, a constrained optimization algorithm that balances erasure strength with preservation of useful information. 3) Provide a theoretical convergence analysis showing Z-Erase converges to a Pareto stationary point.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate Z-Erase avoids generation collapse in single-stream T2I models and achieves state-of-the-art performance across a range of erasure tasks.
AI.conclusion: Z-Erase fills a critical gap for safe concept erasure in single-stream diffusion transformers, supported by convergence guarantees and strong empirical performance; it enables stable, effective removal of unwanted concepts while preserving generation quality.

title: Neural Network Conversion of Machine Learning Pipelines
comment: Submitted and accepted to AutoML 2018 @ ICML/IJCAI-ECAI
summary: Transfer learning and knowledge distillation has recently gained a lot of attention in the deep learning community. One transfer approach, the student-teacher learning, has been shown to successfully create ``small'' student neural networks that mimic the performance of a much bigger and more complex ``teacher'' networks. In this paper, we investigate an extension to this approach and transfer from a non-neural-based machine learning pipeline as teacher to a neural network (NN) student, which would allow for joint optimization of the various pipeline components and a single unified inference engine for multiple ML tasks. In particular, we explore replacing the random forest classifier by transfer learning to a student NN. We experimented with various NN topologies on 100 OpenML tasks in which random forest has been one of the best solutions. Our results show that for the majority of the tasks, the student NN can indeed mimic the teacher if one can select the right NN hyper-parameters. We also investigated the use of random forest for selecting the right NN hyper-parameters.

AI.tldr: Neural network student can imitate a non-neural random forest teacher via transfer learning across 100 OpenML tasks; success hinges on proper NN hyperparameters; random forest also guides hyperparameter selection.
AI.motivation: Extend student-teacher learning to transfer knowledge from non-neural pipelines to neural networks, enabling joint optimization and a unified inference engine for multiple ML tasks.
AI.method: Train neural network students with various topologies to mimic a random forest teacher on tasks where RF performs well; use RF to assist in selecting NN hyperparameters.
AI.result: On the majority of tasks, the student NN can replicate the teacher if suitable hyperparameters are found; RF was leveraged to guide hyperparameter selection.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of cross-domain teacher-student transfer from non-neural to neural models and highlights the critical role of hyperparameter optimization for effective distillation.

title: Dissecting Model Failures in Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm Segmentation through Explainability-Driven Analysis
summary: Computed tomography image segmentation of complex abdominal aortic aneurysms (AAA) often fails because the models assign internal focus to irrelevant structures or do not focus on thin, low-contrast targets. Where the model looks is the primary training signal, and thus we propose an Explainable AI (XAI) guided encoder shaping framework. Our method computes a dense, attribution-based encoder focus map ("XAI field") from the final encoder block and uses it in two complementary ways: (i) we align the predicted probability mass to the XAI field to promote agreement between focus and output; and (ii) we route the field into a lightweight refinement pathway and a confidence prior that modulates logits at inference, suppressing distractors while preserving subtle structures. The objective terms serve only as control signals; the contribution is the integration of attribution guidance into representation and decoding. We evaluate clinically validated challenging cases curated for failure-prone scenarios. Compared to a base SAM setup, our implementation yields substantial improvements. The observed gains suggest that explicitly optimizing encoder focus via XAI guidance is a practical and effective principle for reliable segmentation in complex scenarios.

AI.tldr: XAI-guided encoder shaping improves CT AAA segmentation by aligning model focus with an attribution field and using it to refine predictions and suppress distractors.
AI.motivation: Address failures in segmentation where models attend to irrelevant structures or miss thin, low-contrast targets; leverage attribution-based guidance to steer representation and decoding.
AI.method: Compute a dense attribution field from the final encoder block (XAI field); (i) align predicted probability mass to the XAI field to promote focus-output agreement; (ii) route the field into a lightweight refinement pathway and a confidence prior that modulates logits at inference, suppressing distractors while preserving subtle structures. The objective terms act as control signals; core idea is integrating attribution guidance into representation and decoding.
AI.result: Compared to a base SAM setup, the approach yields substantial improvements on clinically validated challenging cases curated for failure-prone scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly optimizing encoder focus via XAI guidance is a practical, effective principle for reliable segmentation in complex CT scenarios.

title: Bridging Perception and Reasoning: Token Reweighting for RLVR in Multimodal LLMs
summary: Extending Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) to multimodal large language models (MLLMs) faces a fundamental challenge: their responses inherently interleave perception-related tokens, which ground visual content, with reasoning-related tokens, which construct reasoning chains. These token types instantiate distinct yet interdependent capacities -- visual grounding and symbolic reasoning -- making isolated optimization insufficient. Through token-level empirical analysis, we demonstrate that optimizing either perception- or reasoning-only tokens consistently underperforms full optimization, underscoring their inherent coupling. To address this, we propose a plug-and-play Token-Reweighting (ToR) strategy that explicitly models this interdependence by identifying critical tokens of both types and dynamically reweighting them during RLVR training. Applied on top of existing methods (e.g., GRPO and DAPO), ToR delivers consistent performance gains across multiple multi-modal reasoning benchmarks, achieving state-of-the-art performance with both accurate visual grounding and coherent reasoning.

AI.tldr: Token-Reweighting (ToR) for RL-based multimodal LLMs improves grounding and reasoning by dynamically reweighting key perception and reasoning tokens during RLVR training, yielding consistent gains and state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: In RLVR for multimodal LLMs, perception-grounding tokens and reasoning tokens are interdependent; optimizing one type in isolation underperforms due to their coupling, necessitating methods that capture token-level interdependence.
AI.method: Introduce ToR, a plug-and-play strategy that identifies critical tokens from both perception and reasoning categories and dynamically reweights them during RLVR training; it is compatible with existing methods (e.g., GRPO, DAPO).
AI.result: ToR delivers consistent performance gains across multiple multimodal reasoning benchmarks and achieves state-of-the-art results with accurate visual grounding and coherent reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Token interdependence between grounding and reasoning tokens is fundamental; a token-level reweighting approach effectively leverages this coupling, improving RLVR for multimodal LLMs and potentially generalizing to other RL-based multimodal settings.

title: GoldiCLIP: The Goldilocks Approach for Balancing Explicit Supervision for Language-Image Pretraining
summary: Until recently, the success of large-scale vision-language models (VLMs) has primarily relied on billion-sample datasets, posing a significant barrier to progress. Latest works have begun to close this gap by improving supervision quality, but each addresses only a subset of the weaknesses in contrastive pretraining. We present GoldiCLIP, a framework built on a Goldilocks principle of finding the right balance of supervision signals. Our multifaceted training framework synergistically combines three key innovations: (1) a text-conditioned self-distillation method to align both text-agnostic and text-conditioned features; (2) an encoder integrated decoder with Visual Question Answering (VQA) objective that enables the encoder to generalize beyond the caption-like queries; and (3) an uncertainty-based weighting mechanism that automatically balances all heterogeneous losses. Trained on just 30 million images, 300x less data than leading methods, GoldiCLIP achieves state-of-the-art among data-efficient approaches, improving over the best comparable baseline by 2.2 points on MSCOCO retrieval, 2.0 on fine-grained retrieval, and 5.9 on question-based retrieval, while remaining competitive with billion-scale models. Project page: https://petsi.uk/goldiclip.

AI.tldr: GoldiCLIP is a data-efficient vision-language model that achieves strong performance with only 30M images by integrating three synergistic supervision signals: text self-distillation, an encoder–decoder with VQA objective, and an uncertainty-based loss weighting scheme.
AI.motivation: Address the data-hungry nature of large-scale vision-language pretraining by improving supervision quality and balancing heterogeneous losses, aiming to close the gap between data-efficient and billion-scale models.
AI.method: Three core innovations under the Goldilocks principle: (1) text-conditioned self-distillation to align text-agnostic and text-conditioned features; (2) an encoder-integrated decoder with a Visual Question Answering objective so the encoder learns to handle non-caption-style queries; (3) an uncertainty-based weighting mechanism that automatically balances multiple, heterogeneous losses.
AI.result: Trained on 30 million images (≈300× less data than leading methods). Achieves state-of-the-art among data-efficient approaches with improvements of 2.2 points on MSCOCO retrieval, 2.0 points on fine-grained retrieval, and 5.9 points on question-based retrieval compared with the best comparable baseline, while remaining competitive with billion-scale models.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that carefully balancing diverse supervision signals and incorporating VQA-oriented objectives enables strong data-efficient VLM performance, suggesting a viable path to high-quality vision-language models without massive data requirements.

title: Learning domain-invariant features through channel-level sparsification for Out-Of Distribution Generalization
summary: Out-of-Distribution (OOD) generalization has become a primary metric for evaluating image analysis systems. Since deep learning models tend to capture domain-specific context, they often develop shortcut dependencies on these non-causal features, leading to inconsistent performance across different data sources. Current techniques, such as invariance learning, attempt to mitigate this. However, they struggle to isolate highly mixed features within deep latent spaces. This limitation prevents them from fully resolving the shortcut learning problem.In this paper, we propose Hierarchical Causal Dropout (HCD), a method that uses channel-level causal masks to enforce feature sparsity. This approach allows the model to separate causal features from spurious ones, effectively performing a causal intervention at the representation level. The training is guided by a Matrix-based Mutual Information (MMI) objective to minimize the mutual information between latent features and domain labels, while simultaneously maximizing the information shared with class labels.To ensure stability, we incorporate a StyleMix-driven VICReg module, which prevents the masks from accidentally filtering out essential causal data. Experimental results on OOD benchmarks show that HCD performs better than existing top-tier methods.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical Causal Dropout (HCD) uses channel-level causal masks to enforce feature sparsity and separate causal from spurious features at the representation level, guided by a Matrix-based Mutual Information objective and stabilized by a StyleMix-driven VICReg module. It achieves better OOD generalization than current techniques on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Deep models tend to latch onto non-causal, domain-specific features (shortcuts), causing degraded performance on out-of-distribution data. Existing invariance methods struggle to disentangle highly entangled features in latent spaces, limiting their ability to resolve shortcut learning.
AI.method: Introduce channel-level causal masks to enforce sparsity and intervene at the representation level. Train with a Matrix-based Mutual Information objective to minimize mutual information between latent features and domain labels while maximizing information with class labels. Incorporate a StyleMix-driven VICReg module to stabilize masks and preserve essential causal information.
AI.result: Experimental results on OOD benchmarks indicate that HCD outperforms existing top-tier methods.
AI.conclusion: HCD offers a practical mechanism to disentangle causal from spurious features through hierarchical dropout and a robust objective, improving OOD generalization with added stability from StyleMix-VICReg.

title: AutoSAM: an Agentic Framework for Automating Input File Generation for the SAM Code with Multi-Modal Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: In the design and safety analysis of advanced reactor systems, constructing input files for system-level thermal-hydraulics codes such as the System Analysis Module (SAM) remains a labor-intensive task. Analysts must extract and reconcile design data from heterogeneous engineering documents and manually translate it into solver-specific syntax. In this paper, we present AutoSAM, an agentic framework that automates SAM input file generation. The framework combines a large language model agent with retrieval-augmented generation over the solver's user guide and theory manual, together with specialized tools for analyzing PDFs, images, spreadsheets, and text files. AutoSAM ingests unstructured engineering documents, including system diagrams, design reports, and data tables, extracts simulation-relevant parameters into a human-auditable intermediate representation, and synthesizes validated, solver-compatible input decks. Its multimodal retrieval pipeline integrates scientific text extraction, vision-based figure interpretation, semantic embedding, and query answering. We evaluate AutoSAM on four case studies of increasing complexity: a single-pipe steady-state model, a solid-fuel channel with temperature reactivity feedback, the Advanced Burner Test Reactor core, and the Molten Salt Reactor Experiment primary loop. Across all cases, the agent produces runnable SAM models consistent with expected thermal-hydraulic behavior while explicitly identifying missing data and labeling assumed values. The framework achieves 100% utilization of structured inputs, about 88% extraction from PDF text, and 100% completeness in vision-based geometric extraction. These results demonstrate a practical path toward prompt-driven reactor modeling, in which analysts provide system descriptions and supporting documentation while the agent translates them into transparent, and executable, SAM simulations.

AI.tldr: AutoSAM automates SAM input file generation using an LLM agent with retrieval-augmented generation over solver docs and multimodal tools, producing runnable, auditable SAM models from unstructured engineering documents.
AI.motivation: Streamline and reduce errors in translating heterogeneous engineering documentation into solver-specific SAM input decks; enhance transparency, audibility, and completeness of the modeling inputs.
AI.method: Agentic framework combining a large language model agent with retrieval-augmented generation over the solver's user guide and theory manual, plus specialized tools for analyzing PDFs, images, spreadsheets, and text files. Ingests unstructured documents, extracts simulation-relevant parameters into an intermediate representation, then generates solver-compatible input decks. Multimodal retrieval pipeline includes text extraction, vision-based figure interpretation, semantic embeddings, and QA.
AI.result: Evaluated on four case studies of increasing complexity (single-pipe steady-state, solid-fuel channel with temperature reactivity feedback, Advanced Burner Test Reactor core, Molten Salt Reactor Experiment primary loop). The agent produced runnable SAM models consistent with expected thermal-hydraulic behavior, identified missing data, and labeled assumed values. Achieved 100% utilization of structured inputs, ~88% extraction from PDF text, and 100% completeness in vision-based geometric extraction.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a practical path toward prompt-driven reactor modeling where analysts provide system descriptions and documentation and the agent translates them into transparent, executable SAM simulations, using auditable intermediate representations.

title: Visual Attention Drifts,but Anchors Hold:Mitigating Hallucination in Multimodal Large Language Models via Cross-Layer Visual Anchors
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models often suffer from object hallucination. While existing research utilizes attention enhancement and visual retracing, we find these works lack sufficient interpretability regarding attention drift in final model stages.
