In this paper, we investigate the layer wise evolution of visual features and discover that hallucination stems from deep layer attention regressing toward initial visual noise from early layers. We observe that output reliability depends on acquiring visual anchors at intermediate layers rather than final layers. Based on these insights, we propose CLVA, which stands for Cross-Layer Visual Anchors, a training free method that reinforces critical mid layer features while suppressing regressive noise. This approach effectively pulls deep layer attention back to correct visual regions by utilizing essential anchors captured from attention dynamics. We evaluate our method across diverse architectures and benchmarks, demonstrating outstanding performance without significant increase in computational time and GPU memory.

AI.tldr: A training-free cross-layer approach (CLVA) mitigates object hallucination in multimodal LLMs by stabilizing mid-layer visual anchors to guide deep-layer attention, improving reliability with no major computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Object hallucination arises from late-stage attention drift toward early visual noise; there is a need for interpretable, layer-wise understanding and a compute-efficient remedy.
AI.method: Perform layer-wise analysis of visual features; identify that deep-layer attention regresses to initial visual noise from early layers; propose CLVA, a training-free technique that reinforces mid-layer visual anchors and suppresses regressive noise, guiding deep-layer attention using anchors from attention dynamics.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across diverse architectures and benchmarks shows improved performance with little to no additional computational cost or GPU memory usage.
AI.conclusion: Cross-layer visual anchors offer an effective, efficient solution to visual-language misalignment by stabilizing mid-layer representations and steering final attention, with broad applicability across models.

title: Generative Adversarial Perturbations with Cross-paradigm Transferability on Localized Crowd Counting
summary: State-of-the-art crowd counting and localization are primarily modeled using two paradigms: density maps and point regression. Given the field's security ramifications, there is active interest in model robustness against adversarial attacks. Recent studies have demonstrated transferability across density-map-based approaches via adversarial patches, but cross-paradigm attacks (i.e., across both density map-based models and point regression-based models) remain unexplored. We introduce a novel adversarial framework that compromises both density map and point regression architectural paradigms through a comprehensive multi-task loss optimization. For point-regression models, we employ scene-density-specific high-confidence logit suppression; for density-map approaches, we use peak-targeted density map suppression. Both are combined with model-agnostic perceptual constraints to ensure that perturbations are effective and imperceptible to the human eye. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our attack, achieving on average a 7X increase in Mean Absolute Error compared to clean images while maintaining competitive visual quality, and successfully transferring across seven state-of-the-art crowd models with transfer ratios ranging from 0.55 to 1.69. Our approach strikes a balance between attack effectiveness and imperceptibility compared to state-of-the-art transferable attack strategies. The source code is available at https://github.com/simurgh7/CrowdGen

AI.tldr: Cross-paradigm adversarial attack on crowd counting that jointly targets density-map and point-regression models using a multi-task loss, achieving ~7x MAE degradation and transfer across seven models (0.55–1.69), with perceptual constraints for imperceptibility; code at GitHub.
AI.motivation: Address security concerns in crowd counting by exploring cross-paradigm adversarial robustness gaps. While prior work examined transfers within a paradigm (density maps), this work investigates attacks that affect both density-map and point-regression models, highlighting systemic vulnerabilities and transferability.
AI.method: A joint multi-task loss optimization that (1) suppresses scene-density-specific high-confidence logits for point-regression models, (2) suppresses peak-targeted density-map responses for density-map models, combined with model-agnostic perceptual constraints to keep perturbations imperceptible. The framework is tested for transferability across seven state-of-the-art models under cross-paradigm settings.
AI.result: Demonstrates effective cross-paradigm attack: on average 7x increase in mean absolute error (MAE) on attacked images vs clean; preserves competitive visual quality; transfers across seven crowd-counting models with transfer ratios ranging from 0.55 to 1.69.
AI.conclusion: Reveals a cross-paradigm vulnerability in crowd-counting systems and offers a unified, transferable attack framework that compromises both density-map and point-regression models; underscores the need for robust defenses against cross-paradigm adversarial threats. Code available at the provided GitHub link.

title: THEMIS: Towards Holistic Evaluation of MLLMs for Scientific Paper Fraud Forensics
summary: We present THEMIS, a novel multi-task benchmark designed to comprehensively evaluate multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on visual fraud reasoning within real-world academic scenarios. Compared to existing benchmarks, THEMIS introduces three major advances. (1) Real-World Scenarios and Complexity: Our benchmark comprises over 4,000 questions spanning seven scenarios, derived from authentic retracted-paper cases and carefully curated multimodal synthetic data. With 60.47% complex-texture images, THEMIS bridges the critical gap between existing benchmarks and the complexity of real-world academic fraud. (2) Fraud-Type Diversity and Granularity: THEMIS systematically covers five challenging fraud types and introduces 16 fine-grained manipulation operations. On average, each sample undergoes multiple stacked manipulation operations, with the diversity and difficulty of these manipulations demanding a high level of visual fraud reasoning from the models. (3) Multi-Dimensional Capability Evaluation: We establish a mapping from fraud types to five core visual fraud reasoning capabilities, thereby enabling an evaluation that reveals the distinct strengths and specific weaknesses of different models across these core capabilities. Experiments on 16 leading MLLMs show that even the best-performing model, GPT-5, achieves an overall performance of only 56.15%, demonstrating that our benchmark presents a stringent test. We expect THEMIS to advance the development of MLLMs for complex, real-world fraud reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: THEMIS is a new multi-task benchmark to evaluate multimodal LLMs on visual fraud reasoning in real-world academic contexts. It features 4,000+ questions across 7 scenarios with real and synthetic data, 5 fraud types with 16 manipulation operations, and a mapping to five core visual reasoning capabilities. Evaluations on 16 MLLMs (GPT-5 best at 56.15%) show the benchmark is highly challenging.
AI.motivation: There is a gap between existing, often synthetic multimodal benchmarks and the real-world complexity of detecting and reasoning about visual fraud in academic settings. THEMIS aims to stress-test MLLMs on authentic scenarios, diverse fraud types, and manipulatives to reveal strengths and weaknesses.
AI.method: Construct a dataset of 4,000+ questions across seven real-world and curated synthetic scenarios derived from authentic retracted-paper cases. Use images with 60.47% complex textures. Cover five fraud types and 16 fine-grained manipulation operations, with samples often having multiple stacked manipulations. Map fraud types to five core visual fraud reasoning capabilities and evaluate 16 leading MLLMs (including GPT-5).
AI.result: GPT-5 achieves 56.15% overall performance, indicating the benchmark is stringent and highlights substantial room for improvement in visual fraud reasoning across models. The variation across capabilities can reveal model-specific weaknesses.
AI.conclusion: THEMIS provides a challenging, real-world-focused benchmark for multimodal fraud reasoning in academic contexts, expected to drive targeted improvements in MLLMs and to better reflect real-world investigative needs.

title: A Practical Guide Towards Interpreting Time-Series Deep Clinical Predictive Models: A Reproducibility Study
summary: Clinical decisions are high-stakes and require explicit justification, making model interpretability essential for auditing deep clinical models prior to deployment. As the ecosystem of model architectures and explainability methods expands, critical questions remain: Do architectural features like attention improve explainability? Do interpretability approaches generalize across clinical tasks? While prior benchmarking efforts exist, they often lack extensibility and reproducibility, and critically, fail to systematically examine how interpretability varies across the interplay of clinical tasks and model architectures. To address these gaps, we present a comprehensive benchmark evaluating interpretability methods across diverse clinical prediction tasks and model architectures. Our analysis reveals that: (1) attention when leveraged properly is a highly efficient approach for faithfully interpreting model predictions; (2) black-box interpreters like KernelSHAP and LIME are computationally infeasible for time-series clinical prediction tasks; and (3) several interpretability approaches are too unreliable to be trustworthy. From our findings, we discuss several guidelines on improving interpretability within clinical predictive pipelines. To support reproducibility and extensibility, we provide our implementations via PyHealth, a well-documented open-source framework: https://github.com/sunlabuiuc/PyHealth.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive benchmark of interpretability methods across clinical tasks and architectures; finds attention-based explanations are efficient and faithful when used correctly; black-box explainers (KernelSHAP, LIME) are often impractical for time-series clinical tasks; many interpretability methods are unreliable; provides guidelines and releases PyHealth for reproducibility.
AI.motivation: Address gaps in extensibility, reproducibility, and cross-task/architecture evaluation of interpretability in clinical models, enabling auditing before deployment.
AI.method: Systematic benchmark across diverse clinical prediction tasks and model architectures, evaluating multiple interpretability approaches (including attention-based methods and black-box explainers like KernelSHAP and LIME); assess faithfulness, efficiency, and reliability; implement and share code via PyHealth.
AI.result: (1) Attention, when leveraged properly, is a highly efficient and faithful interpreter for model predictions. (2) Black-box interpreters such as KernelSHAP and LIME are computationally infeasible for time-series clinical prediction tasks. (3) Several interpretability approaches are too unreliable to be trustworthy.
AI.conclusion: Provide guidelines to improve interpretability in clinical predictive pipelines and promote reproducibility/extensibility through open-source PyHealth framework.

title: Pixelis: Reasoning in Pixels, from Seeing to Acting
summary: Most vision-language systems are static observers: they describe pixels, do not act, and cannot safely improve under shift. This passivity limits generalizable, physically grounded visual intelligence. Learning through action, not static description, is essential beyond curated data. We present Pixelis, a pixel-space agent that operates directly on images and videos via a compact set of executable operations (zoom/crop, segment, track, OCR, temporal localization) and learns from its consequences. Pixelis trains in three phases: (1) Supervised Fine-Tuning learns a pixel-tool grammar from Chain-of-Thought-Action traces with a masked imitation loss that upweights operation/argument tokens and auxiliary heads to stabilize pixel-grounded arguments; (2) Curiosity-Coherence Reward Fine-Tuning optimizes a dual-drive objective marrying prediction-error curiosity with adjacent-step coherence and a mild efficiency prior under a KL anchor, yielding short, valid, structured toolchains; (3) Pixel Test-Time RL performs label-free adaptation by retrieving neighbors, voting over complete trajectories rather than answers, and updating toward short, high-fidelity exemplars while constraining drift with a KL-to-EMA safety control. Across six public image and video benchmarks, Pixelis yields consistent improvements: the average relative gain is +4.08% over the same 8B baseline (peaking at +6.03% on VSI-Bench), computed as (ours-baseline)/baseline, while producing shorter, auditable toolchains and maintaining in-corridor KL during test-time learning. Acting within pixels, rather than abstract tokens, grounds multimodal perception in the physical world, linking visual reasoning with actionable outcomes, and enables embodied adaptation without external feedback.

AI.tldr: Pixelis introduces a pixel-space agent that operates directly on images and videos with a compact executable operation set, learns via three training phases, and reports consistent improvements across six benchmarks, with shorter auditable toolchains and KL-regularized test-time adaptation.
AI.motivation: Static observation in vision-language models limits generalization and safety under distribution shift; need to ground perception through action and embodied, data-efficient learning.
AI.method: Three-phase training: (1) Supervised Fine-Tuning using Chain-of-Thought-Action traces and masked imitation loss emphasizing operation tokens; (2) Curiosity-Coherence Reward Fine-Tuning combining prediction-error curiosity with short-term coherence and an efficiency prior under a KL anchor; (3) Pixel Test-Time RL with label-free adaptation using neighbor retrieval, trajectory voting, and KL-to-EMA safety to constrain drift.
AI.result: Empirical gains across six benchmarks: average relative improvement ~4.08% over an 8B baseline (peaking ~6.03% on VSI-Bench); shorter, auditable toolchains; maintains in-corridor KL during test-time learning.
AI.conclusion: Grounding perception in pixel space enables embodied visual intelligence, linking reasoning to actionable outcomes and enabling adaptation without external feedback, at the cost of computational and data requirements.

title: Label What Matters: Modality-Balanced and Difficulty-Aware Multimodal Active Learning
summary: Multimodal learning integrates complementary information from different modalities such as image, text, and audio to improve model performance, but its success relies on large-scale labeled data, which is costly to obtain. Active learning (AL) mitigates this challenge by selectively annotating informative samples. In multimodal settings, many approaches implicitly assume that modality importance is stable across rounds and keep selection rules fixed at the fusion stage, which leaves them insensitive to the dynamic nature of multimodal learning, where the relative value of modalities and the difficulty of instances shift as training proceeds. To address this issue, we propose RL-MBA, a reinforcement-learning framework for modality-balanced, difficulty-aware multimodal active learning. RL-MBA models sample selection as a Markov Decision Process, where the policy adapts to modality contributions, uncertainty, and diversity, and the reward encourages accuracy gains and balance. Two key components drive this adaptability: (1) Adaptive Modality Contribution Balancing (AMCB), which dynamically adjusts modality weights via reinforcement feedback, and (2) Evidential Fusion for DifficultyAware Policy Adjustment (EFDA), which estimates sample difficulty via uncertainty-based evidential fusion to prioritize informative samples. Experiments on Food101, KineticsSound, and VGGSound demonstrate that RL-MBA consistently outperforms strong baselines, improving both classification accuracy and modality fairness under limited labeling budgets.

AI.tldr: RL-MBA: a reinforcement-learning framework for modality-balanced, difficulty-aware multimodal active learning that adaptively weights modalities and prioritizes informative, difficult samples, improving accuracy and modality fairness under labeling budgets.
AI.motivation: In multimodal learning, fixed modality fusion and static selection rules fail to capture shifting modality value and instance difficulty during training, and large labeled data are expensive; there is a need for adaptive AL in multimodal settings.
AI.method: Model sample selection as an MDP; adaptive modality contribution balancing (AMCB) to adjust modality weights with reinforcement feedback; evidential fusion for difficulty-aware policy adjustment (EFDA) to estimate sample difficulty via uncertainty-based evidential fusion; reward shaped to maximize accuracy gains and modality balance.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on Food101, KineticsSound, VGGSound shows consistent improvements over strong AL baselines in both accuracy and modality fairness under limited labels.
AI.conclusion: RL-MBA demonstrates effective adaptation to modality contributions and sample difficulty, yielding superior performance and balanced modality usage in multimodal active learning with limited annotation budgets.

title: MSRL: Scaling Generative Multimodal Reward Modeling via Multi-Stage Reinforcement Learning
summary: Recent advances in multimodal reward modeling have been largely driven by a paradigm shift from discriminative to generative approaches. Building on this progress, recent studies have further employed reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) to enhance multimodal reward models (MRMs). Despite their success, RLVR-based training typically relies on labeled multimodal preference data, which are costly and labor-intensive to obtain, making it difficult to scale MRM training. To overcome this limitation, we propose a Multi-Stage Reinforcement Learning (MSRL) approach, which can achieve scalable RL for MRMs with limited multimodal data. MSRL replaces the conventional RLVR-based training paradigm by first learning a generalizable reward reasoning capability from large-scale textual preference data, and then progressively transferring this capability to multimodal tasks through caption-based and fully multimodal reinforcement-learning stages. Furthermore, we introduce a cross-modal knowledge distillation approach to improve preference generalization within MSRL. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MSRL effectively scales the RLVR-based training of generative MRMs and substantially improves their performance across both visual understanding and visual generation tasks (e.g., from 66.6% to 75.9% on VL-RewardBench and from 70.2% to 75.7% on GenAI-Bench), without requiring additional multimodal preference annotations. Our code is available at: https://github.com/wangclnlp/MSRL.

AI.tldr: A staged reinforcement learning framework (MSRL) enables scalable multimodal reward modeling by transferring reward reasoning from textual preferences to caption-based and fully multimodal RL, aided by cross-modal distillation; achieves notable gains without additional multimodal preference data.
AI.motivation: RLVR-based training for generative MRMs is data-hungry due to reliance on labeled multimodal preferences; scalable training requires leveraging large-scale textual data and knowledge transfer across modalities.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: 1) learn reward reasoning from large-scale textual preferences; 2) caption-based RL to bridge text-to-image; 3) full multimodal RL; cross-modal knowledge distillation to improve generalization; no need for extra multimodal preference annotations.
AI.result: Significant performance gains on both visual understanding and generation benchmarks (VL-RewardBench 66.6%→75.9%; GenAI-Bench 70.2%→75.7%), demonstrating scalable RL for MRMs.
AI.conclusion: MSRL substantially improves scalability and performance of generative MRMs under limited multimodal data, applicable to broad tasks; code released.

title: MoireMix: A Formula-Based Data Augmentation for Improving Image Classification Robustness
summary: Data augmentation is a key technique for improving the robustness of image classification models. However, many recent approaches rely on diffusion-based synthesis or complex feature mixing strategies, which introduce substantial computational overhead or require external datasets. In this work, we explore a different direction: procedural augmentation based on analytic interference patterns. Unlike conventional augmentation methods that rely on stochastic noise, feature mixing, or generative models, our approach exploits Moire interference to generate structured perturbations spanning a wide range of spatial frequencies. We propose a lightweight augmentation method that procedurally generates Moire textures on-the-fly using a closed-form mathematical formulation. The patterns are synthesized directly in memory with negligible computational cost (0.0026 seconds per image), mixed with training images during training, and immediately discarded, enabling a storage-free augmentation pipeline without external data. Extensive experiments with Vision Transformers demonstrate that the proposed method consistently improves robustness across multiple benchmarks, including ImageNet-C, ImageNet-R, and adversarial benchmarks, outperforming standard augmentation baselines and existing external-data-free augmentation approaches. These results suggest that analytic interference patterns provide a practical and efficient alternative to data-driven generative augmentation methods.

AI.tldr: A lightweight, data-free augmentation using on-the-fly Moire interference patterns to improve Vision Transformer robustness with negligible compute.
AI.motivation: Improve robustness of image classifiers without heavy diffusion/generative models or external data, and with low computational/storage overhead.
AI.method: Procedurally generate Moire textures on-the-fly via closed-form mathematics (~0.0026 s per image), mix with training images during training, and discard; no storage or external data required.
AI.result: Robustness gains across ImageNet-C, ImageNet-R, and adversarial benchmarks, outperforming standard augmentations and existing external-data-free methods.
AI.conclusion: Analytic interference patterns provide a practical, efficient alternative to data-driven augmentation for robust image classification.

title: CVA: Context-aware Video-text Alignment for Video Temporal Grounding
summary: We propose Context-aware Video-text Alignment (CVA), a novel framework to address a significant challenge in video temporal grounding: achieving temporally sensitive video-text alignment that remains robust to irrelevant background context. Our framework is built on three key components. First, we propose Query-aware Context Diversification (QCD), a new data augmentation strategy that ensures only semantically unrelated content is mixed in. It builds a video-text similarity-based pool of replacement clips to simulate diverse contexts while preventing the ``false negative" caused by query-agnostic mixing. Second, we introduce the Context-invariant Boundary Discrimination (CBD) loss, a contrastive loss that enforces semantic consistency at challenging temporal boundaries, making their representations robust to contextual shifts and hard negatives. Third, we introduce the Context-enhanced Transformer Encoder (CTE), a hierarchical architecture that combines windowed self-attention and bidirectional cross-attention with learnable queries to capture multi-scale temporal context. Through the synergy of these data-centric and architectural enhancements, CVA achieves state-of-the-art performance on major VTG benchmarks, including QVHighlights and Charades-STA. Notably, our method achieves a significant improvement of approximately 5 points in Recall@1 (R1) scores over state-of-the-art methods, highlighting its effectiveness in mitigating false negatives.

AI.tldr: Proposes CVA for temporally sensitive video-text grounding with QCD, CBD loss, and CTE; achieves SOTA on VTG benchmarks with ~5-point R1 gain.
AI.motivation: To address misalignment due to irrelevant background context and brittle temporal boundaries in VTG, by making alignment more context-aware and robust.
AI.method: Three components: Query-aware Context Diversification (QCD) data augmentation using a similarity-based pool of replacement clips to avoid false negatives; Context-invariant Boundary Discrimination (CBD) contrastive loss for boundary robustness; Context-enhanced Transformer Encoder (CTE) a hierarchical transformer with windowed self-attention, bidirectional cross-attention, and learnable queries for multi-scale context.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on QVHighlights and Charades-STA VTG benchmarks; about 5 points improvement in Recall@1 over prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Combining data-centric augmentation with architecture innovations yields robust, context-aware VTG performance and reduces false negatives.

title: AnyDoc: Enhancing Document Generation via Large-Scale HTML/CSS Data Synthesis and Height-Aware Reinforcement Optimization
summary: Document generation has gained growing attention in the field of AI-driven content creation. In this work, we push its boundaries by introducing AnyDoc, a framework capable of handling multiple generation tasks across a wide spectrum of document categories, all represented in a unified HTML/CSS format. To overcome the limited coverage and scale of existing human-crafted document datasets, AnyDoc first establishes a scalable data synthesis pipeline to automatically generate documents in HTML/CSS form. This pipeline yields DocHTML, a large-scale dataset containing 265,206 document samples, while spanning 111 categories and 32 distinct styles. Additionally, all documents are equipped with comprehensive metadata, including design intentions, HTML/CSS source code, visual assets, and rendered screenshots. Building on the curated dataset, AnyDoc fine-tunes multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) to achieve three practical document generation tasks: intention-to-document, document derendering, and element-to-document. To address the content overflow issue observed during fine-tuning, AnyDoc further incorporates a height-aware reinforcement learning (HARL) post-training procedure. By defining a reward function based on the difference between predicted and target document heights, overflow is penalized and gradually mitigated during HARL, thereby enhancing overall performance. Qualitative and quantitative experiments demonstrate that AnyDoc outperforms both general-purpose MLLMs and task-specific baselines across all three tasks.

AI.tldr: AnyDoc presents a unified framework for multi-task document generation using a large-scale synthetic DocHTML dataset to fine-tune multi-modal LLMs for three tasks: intention-to-document, derendering, and element-to-document, with height-aware RL to mitigate content overflow; it outperforms general-purpose and task-specific baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the fragmentation of document generation capabilities across tasks and domains, the scarcity of large, diverse, labeled document datasets, and the need to generate documents in a consistent HTML/CSS representation for broad applicability.
AI.method: Create DocHTML by synthetically generating 265,206 HTML/CSS documents across 111 categories and 32 styles with rich metadata (design intent, HTML/CSS, assets, screenshots). Fine-tune multi-modal LLMs on three tasks (intention-to-document, derendering, element-to-document). Introduce height-aware reinforcement learning (HARL) that rewards closeness of predicted vs. target document heights to reduce content overflow.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show AnyDoc surpasses both general-purpose MLLMs and task-specific baselines on all three tasks in qualitative and quantitative metrics.
AI.conclusion: A scalable synthetic HTML/CSS document dataset, coupled with targeted fine-tuning and HARL, enables robust, multi-task document generation across diverse categories with improved performance and stability.

title: AirSplat: Alignment and Rating for Robust Feed-Forward 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary:
While 3D Vision Foundation Models (3DVFMs) have demonstrated remarkable zero-shot capabilities in visual geometry estimation, their direct application to generalizable novel view synthesis (NVS) remains challenging.
  In this paper, we propose AirSplat, a novel training framework that effectively adapts the robust geometric priors of 3DVFMs into high-fidelity, pose-free NVS. Our approach introduces two key technical contributions:
  (1) Self-Consistent Pose Alignment (SCPA), a training-time feedback loop that ensures pixel-aligned supervision to resolve pose-geometry discrepancy; and
  (2) Rating-based Opacity Matching (ROM), which leverages the local 3D geometry consistency knowledge from a sparse-view NVS teacher model to filter out degraded primitives.
  Experimental results on large-scale benchmarks demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art pose-free NVS approaches in reconstruction quality.
  Our AirSplat highlights the potential of adapting 3DVFMs to enable simultaneous visual geometry estimation and high-quality view synthesis.

AI.tldr: AirSplat repurposes 3D Vision Foundation Model priors for pose-free novel view synthesis via two innovations: Self-Consistent Pose Alignment (SCPA) and Rating-based Opacity Matching (ROM), achieving superior reconstruction quality over state-of-the-art pose-free NVS methods.
AI.motivation: Generalizable novel view synthesis without camera poses is challenging; 3DVFM priors offer strong geometric knowledge that can be leveraged if aligned with pixel-level supervision and robust pruning of primitives.
AI.method: SCPA: a training-time feedback loop ensuring pixel-aligned supervision to resolve pose-geometry discrepancies. ROM: uses local 3D geometry cues from a sparse-view NVS teacher to rate and filter degraded primitives, improving primitive selection during synthesis.
AI.result: Experiments on large-scale benchmarks show AirSplat significantly outperforms state-of-the-art pose-free NVS approaches in reconstruction quality.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of adapting 3DVFMs to achieve simultaneous geometry estimation and high-fidelity, pose-free view synthesis; indicates promising directions for integrating strong geometric priors into NVS.

title: A Public Theory of Distillation Resistance via Constraint-Coupled Reasoning Architectures
summary: Knowledge distillation, model extraction, and behavior transfer have become central concerns in frontier AI. The main risk is not merely copying, but the possibility that useful capability can be transferred more cheaply than the governance structure that originally accompanied it. This paper presents a public, trade-secret-safe theoretical framework for reducing that asymmetry at the architectural level. The core claim is that distillation becomes less valuable as a shortcut when high-level capability is coupled to internal stability constraints that shape state transitions over time. To formalize this idea, the paper introduces a constraint-coupled reasoning framework with four elements: bounded transition burden, path-load accumulation, dynamically evolving feasible regions, and a capability-stability coupling condition. The paper is intentionally public-safe: it omits proprietary implementation details, training recipes, thresholds, hidden-state instrumentation, deployment procedures, and confidential system design choices. The contribution is therefore theoretical rather than operational. It offers a falsifiable architectural thesis, a clear threat model, and a set of experimentally testable hypotheses for future work on distillation resistance, alignment, and model governance.

AI.tldr: Presents a theoretical framework to resist distillation-based leakage by embedding high-level capabilities into dynamic, stability-constrained architectures, predicting reduced distillation value when capabilities are coupled to time-evolving constraints.
AI.motivation: Address the governance risk from knowledge distillation, model extraction, and behavior transfer by ensuring architecture-level barriers to cheap capability transfer, rather than relying on operational details.
AI.method: Introduce a constraint-coupled reasoning framework with four elements: bounded transition burden, path-load accumulation, dynamically evolving feasible regions, and a capability-stability coupling condition. Provide a falsifiable architectural thesis, formal threat model, and testable hypotheses.
AI.result: The work is theoretical and public-safe, outlining a new architectural approach to distillation resistance and model governance, with defined hypotheses for future empirical validation.
AI.conclusion: Offers a falsifiable, architecture-centric thesis for reducing distillation risk, laying groundwork for future experiments in distillation resistance, alignment, and governance; does not provide implementation details or operational recipes.

title: Denoise and Align: Towards Source-Free UDA for Robust Panoramic Semantic Segmentation
summary: Panoramic semantic segmentation is pivotal for comprehensive 360° scene understanding in critical applications like autonomous driving and virtual reality. However, progress in this domain is constrained by two key challenges: the severe geometric distortions inherent in panoramic projections and the prohibitive cost of dense annotation. While Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) from label-rich pinhole-camera datasets offers a viable alternative, many real-world tasks impose a stricter source-free (SFUDA) constraint where source data is inaccessible for privacy or proprietary reasons. This constraint significantly amplifies the core problems of domain shift, leading to unreliable pseudo-labels and dramatic performance degradation, particularly for minority classes. To overcome these limitations, we propose the DAPASS framework. DAPASS introduces two synergistic modules to robustly transfer knowledge without source data. First, our Panoramic Confidence-Guided Denoising (PCGD) module generates high-fidelity, class-balanced pseudo-labels by enforcing perturbation consistency and incorporating neighborhood-level confidence to filter noise. Second, a Contextual Resolution Adversarial Module (CRAM) explicitly addresses scale variance and distortion by adversarially aligning fine-grained details from high-resolution crops with global semantics from low-resolution contexts. DAPASS achieves state-of-the-art performances on outdoor (Cityscapes-to-DensePASS) and indoor (Stanford2D3D) benchmarks, yielding 55.04% (+2.05%) and 70.38% (+1.54%) mIoU, respectively.

AI.tldr: DAPASS is a source-free panoramic semantic segmentation framework that denoises pseudo-labels and aligns multi-scale distortions, achieving state-of-the-art results on outdoor and indoor benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Panoramic projections introduce severe geometric distortions and dense annotations are costly; SFUDA worsens domain shift and pseudo-label reliability, especially for minority classes.
AI.method: Two modules: Panoramic Confidence-Guided Denoising (PCGD) to produce high-fidelity, class-balanced pseudo-labels via perturbation consistency and neighborhood-level confidence; and Contextual Resolution Adversarial Module (CRAM) to address scale variance and distortion by adversarially aligning high-resolution crop details with low-resolution contextual semantics.
AI.result: State-of-the-art on outdoor Cityscapes-to-DensePASS (55.04% mIoU, +2.05) and indoor Stanford2D3D (70.38% mIoU, +1.54).
AI.conclusion: DAPASS enables effective source-free cross-domain panoramic segmentation by generating reliable pseudo-labels and aligning heterogeneous representations, delivering robust performance across domains.

title: Robust Principal Component Completion
summary: Robust principal component analysis (RPCA) seeks a low-rank component and a sparse component from their summation. Yet, in many applications of interest, the sparse foreground actually replaces, or occludes, elements from the low-rank background. To address this mismatch, a new framework is proposed in which the sparse component is identified indirectly through determining its support. This approach, called robust principal component completion (RPCC), is solved via variational Bayesian inference applied to a fully probabilistic Bayesian sparse tensor factorization. Convergence to a hard classifier for the support is shown, thereby eliminating the post-hoc thresholding required of most prior RPCA-driven approaches. Experimental results reveal that the proposed approach delivers near-optimal estimates on synthetic data as well as robust foreground-extraction and anomaly-detection performance on real color video and hyperspectral datasets, respectively. Source implementation and Appendices are available at https://github.com/WongYinJ/BCP-RPCC.

AI.tldr: RPCC reframes RPCA as completing the sparse support via variational Bayesian inference on a probabilistic sparse tensor factorization, yielding automatic support classification and strong empirical performance.
AI.motivation: RPCA suffers when sparse foreground occludes parts of the low-rank background; instead of directly modeling a sparse foreground, RPCC targets the support of the sparse component to better handle occlusion and reduce post-hoc thresholding.
AI.method: Variational Bayesian inference on a fully probabilistic Bayesian sparse tensor factorization to identify the support of the sparse component; convergence to a hard classifier for the support, eliminating post-hoc thresholding.
AI.result: Near-optimal estimates on synthetic data; robust foreground extraction in color video; anomaly detection in hyperspectral data; source code and appendices available.
AI.conclusion: RPCC provides a principled sparse-support completion framework that reduces reliance on thresholding and improves robustness in RPCA-like tasks, with demonstrated empirical gains and an accessible implementation.

title: EgoXtreme: A Dataset for Robust Object Pose Estimation in Egocentric Views under Extreme Conditions
comment: Camera ready version for CVPR 2026, appendix included
summary: Smart glass is emerging as an useful device since it provides plenty of insights under hands-busy, eyes-on-task situations. To understand the context of the wearer, 6D object pose estimation in egocentric view is becoming essential. However, existing 6D object pose estimation benchmarks fail to capture the challenges of real-world egocentric applications, which are often dominated by severe motion blur, dynamic illumination, and visual obstructions. This discrepancy creates a significant gap between controlled lab data and chaotic real-world application. To bridge this gap, we introduce EgoXtreme, a new large-scale 6D pose estimation dataset captured entirely from an egocentric perspective. EgoXtreme features three challenging scenarios - industrial maintenance, sports, and emergency rescue - designed to introduce severe perceptual ambiguities through extreme lighting, heavy motion blur, and smoke. Evaluations of state-of-the-art generalizable pose estimators on EgoXtreme indicate that their generalization fails to hold in extreme conditions, especially under low light. We further demonstrate that simply applying image restoration (e.g., deblurring) offers no positive improvement for extreme conditions. While performance gain has appeared in tracking-based approach, implying using temporal information in fast-motion scenarios is meaningful. We conclude that EgoXtreme is an essential resource for developing and evaluating the next generation of pose estimation models robust enough for real-world egocentric vision. The dataset and code are available at https://taegyoun88.github.io/EgoXtreme/

AI.tldr: EgoXtreme introduces a large-scale egocentric 6D pose estimation dataset across three challenging scenarios (industrial, sports, emergency rescue) to stress-test generalizable models under severe motion blur, lighting, and occlusions; standard deblurring is ineffective; tracking with temporal information yields gains; dataset and code are released.
AI.motivation: Bridging the gap between controlled lab datasets and real-world egocentric vision by capturing extreme perceptual ambiguities (motion blur, low light, smoke/obstructions) that hinder 6D pose estimation.
AI.method: Collect egocentric 6D pose data in three scenarios (industrial maintenance, sports, emergency rescue) with extreme lighting, motion blur, and obstructions (smoke). Evaluate state-of-the-art generalizable pose estimators; test image restoration (deblurring); analyze the benefit of temporal tracking.
AI.result: Generalization of current pose estimators degrades under extreme conditions, especially in low light. Image restoration (deblurring) provides no positive improvement. Tracking-based methods show performance gains by leveraging temporal information.
AI.conclusion: EgoXtreme is a valuable resource for developing robust 6D pose estimation in real-world egocentric vision; the dataset and code enable benchmarking and advancement toward models capable of handling extreme perceptual challenges.

title: SAVe: Self-Supervised Audio-visual Deepfake Detection Exploiting Visual Artifacts and Audio-visual Misalignment
summary: Multimodal deepfakes can exhibit subtle visual artifacts and cross-modal inconsistencies, which remain challenging to detect, especially when detectors are trained primarily on curated synthetic forgeries. Such synthetic dependence can introduce dataset and generator bias, limiting scalability and robustness to unseen manipulations. We propose SAVe, a self-supervised audio-visual deepfake detection framework that learns entirely on authentic videos. SAVe generates on-the-fly, identity-preserving, region-aware self-blended pseudo-manipulations to emulate tampering artifacts, enabling the model to learn complementary visual cues across multiple facial granularities. To capture cross-modal evidence, SAVe also models lip-speech synchronization via an audio-visual alignment component that detects temporal misalignment patterns characteristic of audio-visual forgeries. Experiments on FakeAVCeleb and AV-LipSync-TIMIT demonstrate competitive in-domain performance and strong cross-dataset generalization, highlighting self-supervised learning as a scalable paradigm for multimodal deepfake detection.

AI.tldr: SAVe is a self-supervised audio-visual deepfake detector trained only on authentic data, using on-the-fly, identity-preserving self-blended pseudo-manipulations and an audio-visual alignment module to capture cross-modal tampering cues, achieving competitive in-domain results and strong cross-dataset generalization.
AI.motivation: Traditional deepfake detectors rely on synthetic-forgeries, leading to dataset and generator bias and poor generalization to unseen manipulations. A scalable approach should learn from authentic data and simulate plausible artifacts.
AI.method: SAVe comprises two components: (1) self-blended pseudo-manipulations that are generated on the fly from authentic videos, preserve identity, and focus on region-level tampering across facial granularities; (2) an audio-visual alignment module that learns lip-speech synchronization and detects temporal misalignments typical of audio-visual forgeries.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on FakeAVCeleb and AV-LipSync-TIMIT shows competitive in-domain performance and strong cross-dataset generalization, highlighting the viability of self-supervised learning for multimodal deepfake detection.
AI.conclusion: Self-supervised learning from authentic data, augmented with self-generated, realistic manipulations and cross-modal alignment signals, can improve robustness and generalization in multimodal deepfake detection without reliance on curated synthetic datasets.

title: Learning to Rank Caption Chains for Video-Text Alignment
summary: Direct preference optimization (DPO) is an effective technique to train language models to generate preferred over dispreferred responses. However, this binary "winner-takes-all" approach is suboptimal for vision-language models whose response quality is highly dependent on visual content. In particular, a response may still be faithful to the visual inputs even if it is less preferable than an alternative. The standard Bradley-Terry DPO formulation lacks this nuance, upweighting winning responses without sufficient regard for whether the "losing" response still maintains high visual fidelity. In this work, we investigate ranking optimization as an alternative that more precisely situates responses' faithfulness to visual inputs. We focus on video-text alignment using detailed video captions, proposing a method to generate challenging, totally ordered caption chains at scale through repeated caption degradation. Our results show ranking optimization outperforms binary DPO for long-form content generation and assessment, and importantly, we find that these approaches require finetuning of the vision encoder to be effective, challenging the view of DPO as purely a language-reweighting process.

AI.tldr: Ranking-based optimization provides a nuanced alternative to DPO for vision-language models, improving faithfulness-aware evaluation and long-form generation; effectiveness hinges on finetuning the vision encoder.
AI.motivation: Binary DPO focuses on a win/lose outcome and may neglect the visual fidelity of losing responses. For video-text alignment, faithful adherence to visual content is essential, so a ranking objective that governs the relative quality of responses with respect to visual input is desirable.
AI.method: Introduce ranking optimization for video-text alignment by creating totally ordered caption chains through repeated caption degradation to generate challenging training data. Train with a ranking loss that emphasizes relative faithfulness to visual inputs, and compare to DPO across long-form content tasks. Investigate the role of finetuning the vision encoder in enabling the ranking signal to influence the model.
AI.result: Ranking optimization outperforms binary DPO on long-form content generation and assessment. The gains are contingent on finetuning the vision encoder, challenging the notion that DPO is purely a language-reweighting process.
AI.conclusion: Ranking optimization is a viable and potentially superior alternative to DPO for vision-language models, as it explicitly accounts for visual fidelity in rankings and suggests that vision encoder adaptation is necessary for best performance.

title: FD$^2$: A Dedicated Framework for Fine-Grained Dataset Distillation
summary: Dataset distillation (DD) compresses a large training set into a small synthetic set, reducing storage and training cost, and has shown strong results on general benchmarks. Decoupled DD further improves efficiency by splitting the pipeline into pretraining, sample distillation, and soft-label generation. However, existing decoupled methods largely rely on coarse class-label supervision and optimize samples within each class in a nearly identical manner. On fine-grained datasets, this often yields distilled samples that (i) retain large intra-class variation with subtle inter-class differences and (ii) become overly similar within the same class, limiting localized discriminative cues and hurting recognition. To solve the above-mentioned problems, we propose FD$^{2}$, a dedicated framework for Fine-grained Dataset Distillation. FD$^{2}$ localizes discriminative regions and constructs fine-grained representations for distillation. During pretraining, counterfactual attention learning aggregates discriminative representations to update class prototypes. During distillation, a fine-grained characteristic constraint aligns each sample with its class prototype while repelling others, and a similarity constraint diversifies attention across same-class samples. Experiments on multiple fine-grained and general datasets show that FD$^{2}$ integrates seamlessly with decoupled DD and improves performance in most settings, indicating strong transferability.

AI.tldr: FD^2 is a fine-grained dataset distillation framework that localizes discriminative regions and enforces fine-grained representations to improve decoupled dataset distillation on fine-grained data.
AI.motivation: Decoupled dataset distillation (DD) reduces storage and training cost but typically relies on coarse class-label supervision and treats samples within a class similarly, which fails on fine-grained data where subtle inter-class differences and intra-class variation hurt performance.
AI.method: FD^2 introduces (1) counterfactual attention learning during pretraining to aggregate discriminative representations into class prototypes; (2) a fine-grained characteristic constraint that aligns each sample with its class prototype while repelling others; (3) a similarity constraint that diversifies attention across same-class samples; applied within the decoupled DD pipeline.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple fine-grained and general datasets shows that FD^2 integrates with decoupled DD and improves performance in most settings, demonstrating strong transferability and robustness.
AI.conclusion: FD^2 effectively addresses fine-grained pitfalls in decoupled DD by localizing discriminative cues and enforcing nuanced sample-prototype relationships, yielding improved distillation quality and generalization.

title: An Image Dataset of Common Skin Diseases of Bangladesh and Benchmarking Performance with Machine Learning Models
summary: Skin diseases are a major public health concern worldwide, and their detection is often challenging without access to dermatological expertise. In countries like Bangladesh, which is highly populated, the number of qualified skin specialists and diagnostic instruments is insufficient to meet the demand. Due to the lack of proper detection and treatment of skin diseases, that may lead to severe health consequences including death. Common properties of skin diseases are, changing the color, texture, and pattern of skin and in this era of artificial intelligence and machine learning, we are able to detect skin diseases by using image processing and computer vision techniques. In response to this challenge, we develop a publicly available dataset focused on common skin disease detection using machine learning techniques. We focus on five prevalent skin diseases in Bangladesh: Contact Dermatitis, Vitiligo, Eczema, Scabies, and Tinea Ringworm. The dataset consists of 1612 images (of which, 250 are distinct while others are augmented), collected directly from patients at the outpatient department of Faridpur Medical College, Faridpur, Bangladesh. The data comprises of 302, 381, 301, 316, and 312 images of Dermatitis, Eczema, Scabies, Tinea Ringworm, and Vitiligo, respectively. Although the data are collected regionally, the selected diseases are common across many countries especially in South Asia, making the dataset potentially valuable for global applications in machine learning-based dermatology. We also apply several machine learning and deep learning models on the dataset and report classification performance. We expect that this research would garner attention from machine learning and deep learning researchers and practitioners working in the field of automated disease diagnosis.

AI.tldr: Publicly available dataset of 1612 skin-disease images from Bangladesh for five prevalent conditions; includes baseline ML/DL model evaluations.
AI.motivation: Addresses the scarcity of dermatology expertise and diagnostic tools in Bangladesh. Provides a publicly available image dataset to enable machine learning-based automated diagnosis of common skin diseases with potential global relevance in South Asia.
AI.method: Data collection from outpatient department at Faridpur Medical College, Faridpur, Bangladesh. The dataset contains 1612 images (250 unique; the rest augmented) distributed as Dermatitis 302, Eczema 381, Scabies 301, Tinea Ringworm 316, and Vitiligo 312. Images were used to train/evaluate several ML and deep learning models; the dataset is publicly available.
AI.result: The study reports classification performance of multiple ML/DL models on the dataset, demonstrating feasibility of automated skin-disease detection and highlighting the dataset's value for ML researchers and practitioners.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a regionally collected yet globally relevant dataset for common skin diseases, facilitating automated dermatology research in resource-limited settings and inviting further benchmarking and model development.

title: Activation Matters: Test-time Activated Negative Labels for OOD Detection with Vision-Language Models
comment: CVPR 2026 main track, Codes are available at https://github.com/YBZh/OpenOOD-VLM
summary: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection aims to identify samples that deviate from in-distribution (ID). One popular pipeline addresses this by introducing negative labels distant from ID classes and detecting OOD based on their distance to these labels. However, such labels may present poor activation on OOD samples, failing to capture the OOD characteristics. To address this, we propose \underline{T}est-time \underline{A}ctivated \underline{N}egative \underline{L}abels (TANL) by dynamically evaluating activation levels across the corpus dataset and mining candidate labels with high activation responses during the testing process. Specifically, TANL identifies high-confidence test images online and accumulates their assignment probabilities over the corpus to construct a label activation metric. Such a metric leverages historical test samples to adaptively align with the test distribution, enabling the selection of distribution-adaptive activated negative labels. By further exploring the activation information within the current testing batch, we introduce a more fine-grained, batch-adaptive variant. To fully utilize label activation knowledge, we propose an activation-aware score function that emphasizes negative labels with stronger activations, boosting performance and enhancing its robustness to the label number. Our TANL is training-free, test-efficient, and grounded in theoretical justification. Experiments on diverse backbones and wide task settings validate its effectiveness. Notably, on the large-scale ImageNet benchmark, TANL significantly reduces the FPR95 from 17.5\% to 9.8\%. Codes are available at \href{https://github.com/YBZh/OpenOOD-VLM}{YBZh/OpenOOD-VLM}.

AI.tldr: TANL: a training-free, test-time method that dynamically mines activated negative labels for OOD detection by leveraging online high-confidence test samples and batch/corpus activation cues to adapt to the test distribution, improving robustness to label count.
AI.motivation: Standard OOD pipelines rely on negative labels distant from in-distribution classes, but these labels can exhibit weak activation on OOD samples, limiting detection. A distribution-adaptive, activation-aware strategy at test time aims to better capture OOD characteristics without retraining.
AI.method: Identify high-confidence test images online and accumulate their assignment probabilities across the corpus to build a label activation metric. Use this corpus-driven activation to select distribution-adaptive activated negative labels. Also develop a batch-adaptive variant by leveraging current-test-batch activations. Propose an activation-aware score function that prioritizes negative labels with stronger activations. The approach is training-free and test-efficient with theoretical grounding.
AI.result: Empirical validation across diverse backbones and tasks shows improvements. On ImageNet, TANL reduces FPR95 from 17.5% to 9.8%, indicating substantial gains in OOD detection. Code is provided at the authors’ GitHub.
AI.conclusion: TANL is a training-free, test-efficient framework that leverages activation dynamics to select and weight negative labels in OOD detection, with theoretical justification and strong empirical gains on large-scale benchmarks.

title: Photon: Speedup Volume Understanding with Efficient Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Multimodal large language models are promising for clinical visual question answering tasks, but scaling to 3D imaging is hindered by high computational costs. Prior methods often rely on 2D slices or fixed-length token compression, disrupting volumetric continuity and obscuring subtle findings. We present Photon, a framework that represents 3D medical volumes with token sequences of variable length. Photon introduces instruction-conditioned token scheduling and surrogate gradient propagation to adaptively reduce tokens during both training and inference, which lowers computational cost while mitigating the attention dilution caused by redundant tokens. It incorporates a custom backpropagation rule with gradient restoration to enable differentiable optimization despite discrete token drop. To stabilize token compression and ensure reliable use of visual evidence, Photon further applies regularization objectives that mitigate language-only bias and improve reliability. Experiments on diverse medical visual question answering tasks show that Photon achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing resource usage and accelerating both training and inference.

AI.tldr: Photon is a framework for 3D medical VQA that represents volumes as variable-length token sequences and adaptively compresses tokens during training and inference, using instruction-conditioned scheduling and gradient restoration to maintain differentiability. It mitigates language bias with regularization and achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing compute.
AI.motivation: Extending multimodal LLMs to 3D medical imaging is computationally expensive. 2D slice or fixed-length token approaches disrupt volumetric continuity and can miss subtle findings, necessitating a scalable, lossless representation of 3D data for VQA.
AI.method: Represent 3D volumes as variable-length token sequences. Use instruction-conditioned token scheduling to adaptively drop tokens. Employ surrogate gradient propagation and a custom backpropagation rule with gradient restoration to enable differentiable optimization despite discrete token dropping. Apply regularization to reduce language-only bias and improve reliability.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on diverse medical VQA tasks while reducing resource usage and accelerating both training and inference.
AI.conclusion: Photon provides a scalable, efficient framework for 3D medical VQA by adaptive token compression with gradient restoration, maintaining evidence reliability and reducing computation, addressing the core bottleneck in volumetric multimodal LLMs.

title: Hyperspectral Trajectory Image for Multi-Month Trajectory Anomaly Detection
summary: Trajectory anomaly detection underpins applications from fraud detection to urban mobility analysis. Dense GPS methods preserve fine-grained evidence such as abnormal speeds and short-duration events, but their quadratic cost makes multi-month analysis intractable; consequently, no existing approach detects anomalies over multi-month dense GPS trajectories. The field instead relies on scalable sparse stay-point methods that discard this evidence, forcing separate architectures for each regime and preventing knowledge transfer. We argue this bottleneck is unnecessary: human trajectories, dense or sparse, share a natural two-dimensional cyclic structure along within-day and across-day axes. We therefore propose TITAnD (Trajectory Image Transformer for Anomaly Detection), which reformulates trajectory anomaly detection as a vision problem by representing trajectories as a Hyperspectral Trajectory Image (HTI): a day x time-of-day grid whose channels encode spatial, semantic, temporal, and kinematic information from either modality, unifying both under a single representation. Under this formulation, agent-level detection reduces to image classification and temporal localization to semantic segmentation. To model this representation, we introduce the Cyclic Factorized Transformer (CFT), which factorizes attention along the two temporal axes, encoding the cyclic inductive bias of human routines, while reducing attention cost by orders of magnitude and enabling dense multi-month anomaly detection for the first time. Empirically, TITAnD achieves the best AUC-PR across sparse and dense benchmarks, surpassing vision models like UNet while being 11-75x faster than the Transformer with comparable memory, demonstrating that vision reformulation and structure-aware modeling are jointly essential. Code will be made public soon.

AI.tldr: Unifies dense and sparse trajectory anomaly detection by reformulating trajectories as a Hyperspectral Trajectory Image and modeling with a Cyclic Factorized Transformer, enabling multi-month analysis with improved speed and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Dense GPS data contain rich evidence (e.g., abnormal speeds, short events) but are computationally expensive; sparse stay-point methods discard this evidence and require separate architectures per regime. A unified approach leveraging the 2D cyclic structure of daily routines can enable scalable, knowledge-transferable anomaly detection.
AI.method: Represent trajectories as a Hyperspectral Trajectory Image (HTI): a day x time-of-day grid with channels encoding spatial, semantic, temporal, and kinematic information. Treat agent-level detection as image classification and temporal localization as semantic segmentation. Introduce Cyclic Factorized Transformer (CFT) that factorizes attention along the two temporal axes to capture cyclic routines while reducing attention cost, enabling dense multi-month analysis.
AI.result: TITAnD achieves the best AUC-PR across both sparse and dense benchmarks, outperforming vision models like UNet. It is 11–75× faster than a standard Transformer with comparable memory. Indicates that vision reformulation plus structure-aware modeling yields substantial practical gains; code to be released.
AI.conclusion: Vision-based reformulation combined with cycle-aware modeling is essential to unify dense and sparse trajectory anomaly detection and enable scalable multi-month analysis.

title: Layer-Specific Lipschitz Modulation for Fault-Tolerant Multimodal Representation Learning
summary: Modern multimodal systems deployed in industrial and safety-critical environments must remain reliable under partial sensor failures, signal degradation, or cross-modal inconsistencies. This work introduces a mathematically grounded framework for fault-tolerant multimodal representation learning that unifies self-supervised anomaly detection and error correction within a single architecture. Building upon a theoretical analysis of perturbation propagation, we derive Lipschitz- and Jacobian-based criteria that determine whether a neural operator amplifies or attenuates localized faults. Guided by this theory, we propose a two-stage self-supervised training scheme: pre-training a multimodal convolutional autoencoder on clean data to preserve localized anomaly signals in the latent space, and expanding it with a learnable compute block composed of dense layers for correction and contrastive objectives for anomaly identification. Furthermore, we introduce layer-specific Lipschitz modulation and gradient clipping as principled mechanisms to control sensitivity across detection and correction modules. Experimental results on multimodal fault datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach improves both anomaly detection accuracy and reconstruction under sensor corruption. Overall, this framework bridges the gap between analytical robustness guarantees and practical fault-tolerant multimodal learning.

AI.tldr: A unified, theory-guided framework for fault-tolerant multimodal learning that combines self-supervised anomaly detection and error correction via a Lipschitz/Jacobian analysis, a two-stage training scheme, and layer-wise sensitivity control, achieving improved detection and reconstruction under sensor corruption.
AI.motivation: Industrial and safety-critical multimodal systems must remain reliable under sensor faults, degradations, or cross-modal inconsistencies. A principled approach that provides robustness guarantees while enabling practical anomaly detection and correction is needed.
AI.method: Theoretical perturbation analysis yields Lipschitz- and Jacobian-based criteria to predict when a neural operator amplifies or attenuates faults. A two-stage self-supervised training scheme is proposed: (1) pre-train a multimodal convolutional autoencoder on clean data to preserve localized anomaly signals, (2) augment with a learnable dense compute block for correction and contrastive objectives for anomaly identification. Layer-specific Lipschitz modulation and gradient clipping are introduced to balance detection and correction sensitivity.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multimodal fault datasets shows improved anomaly detection accuracy and reconstruction quality under sensor corruption, indicating the approach effectively detects and corrects faults. The method demonstrates practical robustness with analytical grounding.
AI.conclusion: The work bridges analytical robustness guarantees with practical fault-tolerant multimodal learning, unifying anomaly detection and correction within a single architecture and providing principled mechanisms to modulate sensitivity across modules.

title: A Semantically Disentangled Unified Model for Multi-category 3D Anomaly Detection
summary: 3D anomaly detection targets the detection and localization of defects in 3D point clouds trained solely on normal data. While a unified model improves scalability by learning across multiple categories, it often suffers from Inter-Category Entanglement (ICE)-where latent features from different categories overlap, causing the model to adopt incorrect semantic priors during reconstruction and ultimately yielding unreliable anomaly scores. To address this issue, we propose the Semantically Disentangled Unified Model for 3D Anomaly Detection, which reconstructs features conditioned on disentangled semantic representations. Our framework consists of three key components: (i) Coarse-to-Fine Global Tokenization for forming instance-level semantic identity, (ii) Category-Conditioned Contrastive Learning for disentangling category semantics, and (iii) a Geometry-Guided Decoder for semantically consistent reconstruction. Extensive experiments on Real3D-AD and Anomaly-ShapeNet demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art for both unified and category-specific models, improving object-level AUROC by 2.8% and 9.1%, respectively, while enhancing the reliability of unified 3D anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Semantically Disentangled Unified Model for 3D anomaly detection to combat Inter-Category Entanglement (ICE) by learning disentangled semantic representations within a unified framework, using coarser-to-finer tokenization, category-conditioned contrastive learning, and a geometry-guided decoder; achieves state-of-the-art results on Real3D-AD and Anomaly-ShapeNet.
AI.motivation: Inter-Category Entanglement (ICE) in unified 3D anomaly detection causes latent features from different categories to overlap, leading to incorrect semantic priors during reconstruction and unreliable anomaly scores. A semantically disentangled representation within a unified model is needed to improve reliability while maintaining scalability across categories.
AI.method: Three components: (i) Coarse-to-Fine Global Tokenization to form instance-level semantic identity; (ii) Category-Conditioned Contrastive Learning to disentangle category semantics; (iii) Geometry-Guided Decoder for semantically consistent reconstruction. Trained on normal data; evaluated on Real3D-AD and Anomaly-ShapeNet.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance for both unified and category-specific models; object-level AUROC improves by 2.8% for unified and 9.1% for category-specific models. Demonstrates improved reliability of unified 3D anomaly detection.
AI.conclusion: The semantically disentangled unified framework effectively mitigates ICE, enabling reliable anomaly scores and improved AUROC across datasets, supporting both unified scalability and category-specific performance.

title: SportSkills: Physical Skill Learning from Sports Instructional Videos
summary: Current large-scale video datasets focus on general human activity, but lack depth of coverage on fine-grained activities needed to address physical skill learning. We introduce SportSkills, the first large-scale sports dataset geared towards physical skill learning with in-the-wild video. SportSkills has more than 360k instructional videos containing more than 630k visual demonstrations paired with instructional narrations explaining the know-how behind the actions from 55 varied sports. Through a suite of experiments, we show that SportSkills unlocks the ability to understand fine-grained differences between physical actions. Our representation achieves gains of up to 4x with the same model trained on traditional activity-centric datasets. Crucially, building on SportSkills, we introduce the first large-scale task formulation of mistake-conditioned instructional video retrieval, bridging representation learning and actionable feedback generation (e.g., "here's my execution of a skill; which video clip should I watch to improve it?"). Formal evaluations by professional coaches show our retrieval approach significantly advances the ability of video models to personalize visual instructions for a user query.

AI.tldr: SportSkills: a large-scale, in-the-wild sports video dataset (360k instructional videos, 630k visual demonstrations, 55 sports) designed for fine-grained physical skill learning; enables significant performance gains (up to 4x) over traditional activity datasets and introduces a mistake-conditioned instructional video retrieval task evaluated by professional coaches.
AI.motivation: Current large-scale video datasets emphasize general activity and lack fine-grained coverage of physical skills needed for instructive learning. There is a need for data that captures explicit know-how and actionable feedback for skill improvement.
AI.method: Create SportSkills dataset with paired instructional narrations; conduct experiments showing fine-grained action understanding improvements; develop a mistake-conditioned instructional video retrieval task; validate retrieval with professional coaches.
AI.result: Representation trained on SportSkills achieves up to 4x gains on fine-grained action understanding compared to models trained on traditional datasets; first large-scale retrieval task for mistake-conditioned instructional videos; retrieval approach enhances personalization of visual instructions, with professional coaches validating the method.
AI.conclusion: SportSkills enables better modeling and feedback for physical skill learning, and provides a foundational resource bridging representation learning and actionable coaching feedback in video understanding.

title: Knowledge-Guided Failure Prediction: Detecting When Object Detectors Miss Safety-Critical Objects
summary: Object detectors deployed in safety-critical environments can fail silently, e.g. missing pedestrians, workers, or other safety-critical objects without emitting any warning. Traditional Out Of Distribution (OOD) detection methods focus on identifying unfamiliar inputs, but do not directly predict functional failures of the detector itself. We introduce Knowledge Guided Failure Prediction (KGFP), a representation-based monitoring framework that treats missed safety-critical detections as anomalies to be detected at runtime. KGFP measures semantic misalignment between internal object detector features and visual foundation model embeddings using a dual-encoder architecture with an angular distance metric. A key property is that when either the detector is operating outside its competence or the visual foundation model itself encounters novel inputs, the two embeddings diverge, producing a high-angle signal that reliably flags unsafe images. We compare our novel KGFS method to baseline OOD detection methods. On COCO person detection, applying KGFP as a selective-prediction gate raises person recall among accepted images from 64.3% to 84.5% at 5% False Positive Rate (FPR), and maintains strong performance across six COCO-O visual domains, outperforming OOD baselines by large margins. Our code, models, and features are published at https://gitlab.cc-asp.fraunhofer.de/iosb_public/KGFP.

AI.tldr: KGFP is a dual-encoder framework that predicts detector failures by measuring semantic misalignment between detector features and visual foundation model embeddings, serving as a runtime gate to improve safety-critical object detection.
AI.motivation: Safety-critical detectors can fail silently, and standard OOD methods do not directly predict functional detector failures; a runtime failure-prediction mechanism is needed.
AI.method: A dual-encoder architecture computes an angular distance between internal detector features and foundation-model embeddings to detect semantic misalignment. When the detector is out of competence or inputs are novel, embeddings diverge, yielding a high angular signal to flag unsafe images and gate predictions.
AI.result: In COCO person detection, applying KGFP as a selective-prediction gate raises recall from 64.3% to 84.5% at 5% FPR and remains strong across six COCO-O visual domains, outperforming OOD baselines by large margins.
AI.conclusion: KGFP provides effective runtime failure prediction for safety-critical detectors and a reliable selective-prediction gate, with code and models publicly released.

title: Towards Foundation Models for 3D Scene Understanding: Instance-Aware Self-Supervised Learning for Point Clouds
comment: The paper was accepted by CVPR2026
summary: Recent advances in self-supervised learning (SSL) for point clouds have substantially improved 3D scene understanding without human annotations. Existing approaches emphasize semantic awareness by enforcing feature consistency across augmented views or by masked scene modeling. However, the resulting representations transfer poorly to instance localization, and often require full finetuning for strong performance. Instance awareness is a fundamental component of 3D perception, thus bridging this gap is crucial for progressing toward true 3D foundation models that support all downstream tasks on 3D data. In this work, we introduce PointINS, an instance-oriented self-supervised framework that enriches point cloud representations through geometry-aware learning. PointINS employs an orthogonal offset branch to jointly learn high-level semantic understanding and geometric reasoning, yielding instance awareness. We identify two consistent properties essential for robust instance localization and formulate them as complementary regularization strategies, Offset Distribution Regularization (ODR), which aligns predicted offsets with empirically observed geometric priors, and Spatial Clustering Regularization (SCR), which enforces local coherence by regularizing offsets with pseudo-instance masks. Through extensive experiments across five datasets, PointINS achieves on average +3.5% mAP improvement for indoor instance segmentation and +4.1% PQ gain for outdoor panoptic segmentation, paving the way for scalable 3D foundation models.

AI.tldr: PointINS presents an instance-oriented SSL framework for point clouds that uses an orthogonal offset branch plus two regularizers (ODR and SCR) to learn geometry-aware representations, improving instance localization without heavy finetuning. Across five datasets, it delivers on average +3.5% mAP for indoor instance segmentation and +4.1% PQ for outdoor panoptic segmentation.
AI.motivation: SSL for 3D point clouds often emphasizes semantic consistency but struggles with instance-level transfer; achieving robust instance localization without finetuning is essential for scalable 3D foundation models.
AI.method: Introduce an orthogonal offset branch to jointly learn semantic and geometric cues; propose Offset Distribution Regularization (ODR) to align predicted offsets with geometric priors; propose Spatial Clustering Regularization (SCR) to enforce local coherence using pseudo-instance masks.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on five datasets show average improvements: +3.5% mAP for indoor instance segmentation and +4.1% PQ for outdoor panoptic segmentation, indicating strong gains and better instance awareness.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-aware SSL with complementary regularizers can produce instance-aware representations suitable for 3D foundation models; further work needed to assess computational cost, ablations, and generalization to more tasks.

title: Challenges in Hyperspectral Imaging for Autonomous Driving: The HSI-Drive Case
summary: The use of hyperspectral imaging (HSI) in autonomous driving (AD), while promising, faces many challenges related to the specifics and requirements of this application domain. On the one hand, non-controlled and variable lighting conditions, the wide depth-of-field ranges, and dynamic scenes with fast-moving objects. On the other hand, the requirements for real-time operation and the limited computational resources of embedded platforms. The combination of these factors determines both the criteria for selecting appropriate HSI technologies and the development of custom vision algorithms that leverage the spectral and spatial information obtained from the sensors. In this article, we analyse several techniques explored in the research of HSI-based vision systems with application to AD, using as an example results obtained from experiments using data from the most recent version of the HSI-Drive dataset.

AI.tldr: A survey of hyperspectral imaging (HSI) for autonomous driving (AD), analyzing challenges (lighting, motion, depth-of-field, real-time constraints) and evaluating several HSI-based vision techniques on the HSI-Drive dataset to guide technology and algorithm choices.
AI.motivation: To assess the suitability of HSI technologies and develop/adapt vision algorithms for AD under non-ideal, real-world conditions, enabling effective use of spectral-spatial information while meeting real-time and resource constraints.
AI.method: Literature/systems analysis of multiple HSI-based AD techniques; empirical evaluation using the HSI-Drive dataset, comparing approaches under variable lighting, dynamic scenes, and embedded platform constraints.
AI.result: Experimental results on HSI-Drive demonstrating the relative performance and trade-offs of the analyzed techniques, illustrating feasibility and limitations of current HSI-based AD systems.
AI.conclusion: Despite challenges, HSI offers valuable spectral-spatial cues for AD; selecting suitable sensors and crafting tailored algorithms is essential, and the analyzed results provide guidance for future development.
