AI.motivation: There is a need for long-term, cross-task CV tools to monitor individual animals in the wild, motivated by conservation and evolutionary biology; current datasets are narrow in scope and lack biologically meaningful evaluation metrics.
AI.method: CHIRP-curated dataset from Siberian jays supporting re-id, action recognition, 2D keypoint estimation, object detection, and instance segmentation; application-specific benchmarking using biologically relevant metrics (e.g., feeding rates, co-occurrence); CORVID pipeline identifies individuals by segmentation and classification of colored leg rings, offering probabilistic ID tracking by matching color-ring combinations to a database.
AI.result: CORVID outperforms state-of-the-art re-id methods on application-specific benchmarks; CHIRP provides a blueprint for ethically approved, real-world biological datasets and enables multiple CV tasks for long-term behavioral analysis.
AI.conclusion: The work bridges computer vision and biology by providing a real-world, multi-task dataset and a practical re-id method that leverages traditional visual markers (color rings), promoting deployment in conservation research and setting a blueprint for future cross-disciplinary datasets.

title: DeepFAN, a transformer-based deep learning model for human-artificial intelligence collaborative assessment of incidental pulmonary nodules in CT scans: a multi-reader, multi-case trial
comment: 28 pages for main text and 37 pages for supplementary information, 7 figures in main text and 9 figures in supplementary information
summary: The widespread adoption of CT has notably increased the number of detected lung nodules. However, current deep learning methods for classifying benign and malignant nodules often fail to comprehensively integrate global and local features, and most of them have not been validated through clinical trials. To address this, we developed DeepFAN, a transformer-based model trained on over 10K pathology-confirmed nodules and further conducted a multi-reader, multi-case clinical trial to evaluate its efficacy in assisting junior radiologists. DeepFAN achieved diagnostic area under the curve (AUC) of 0.939 (95% CI 0.930-0.948) on an internal test set and 0.954 (95% CI 0.934-0.973) on the clinical trial dataset involving 400 cases across three independent medical institutions. Explainability analysis indicated higher contributions from global than local features. Twelve readers' average performance significantly improved by 10.9% (95% CI 8.3%-13.5%) in AUC, 10.0% (95% CI 8.9%-11.1%) in accuracy, 7.6% (95% CI 6.1%-9.2%) in sensitivity, and 12.6% (95% CI 10.9%-14.3%) in specificity (P<0.001 for all). Nodule-level inter-reader diagnostic consistency improved from fair to moderate (overall k: 0.313 vs. 0.421; P=0.019). In conclusion, DeepFAN effectively assisted junior radiologists and may help homogenize diagnostic quality and reduce unnecessary follow-up of indeterminate pulmonary nodules. Chinese Clinical Trial Registry: ChiCTR2400084624.

AI.tldr: Transformer-based DeepFAN improves radiologist performance in lung nodule classification; clinical validation shows high AUC and improved inter-reader agreement.
AI.motivation: Address limited integration of global and local features in deep learning nodules classification and lack of clinical trial validation.
AI.method: DeepFAN, a transformer-based model trained on >10k pathology-confirmed nodules; evaluated on internal test set and a 400-case multi-institution clinical trial; explainability analysis; twelve junior radiologists.
AI.result: Internal AUC 0.939 (CI 0.930-0.948); clinical trial AUC 0.954 (CI 0.934-0.973); reader performance gains in AUC 10.9%, accuracy 10.0%, sensitivity 7.6%, specificity 12.6%; inter-reader kappa improved from 0.313 to 0.421; P<0.001 for all.
AI.conclusion: DeepFAN assists junior radiologists and may homogenize diagnostic quality and reduce unnecessary follow-ups for indeterminate nodules; Chinese Clinical Trial Registry: ChiCTR2400084624.

title: Demographic Fairness in Multimodal LLMs: A Benchmark of Gender and Ethnicity Bias in Face Verification
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have recently been explored as face verification systems that determine whether two face images are of the same person. Unlike dedicated face recognition systems, MLLMs approach this task through visual prompting and rely on general visual and reasoning abilities. However, the demographic fairness of these models remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we present a benchmarking study that evaluates nine open-source MLLMs from six model families, ranging from 2B to 8B parameters, on the IJB-C and RFW face verification protocols across four ethnicity groups and two gender groups. We measure verification accuracy with the Equal Error Rate and True Match Rate at multiple operating points per demographic group, and we quantify demographic disparity with four FMR-based fairness metrics. Our results show that FaceLLM-8B, the only face-specialised model in our study, substantially outperforms general-purpose MLLMs on both benchmarks. The bias patterns we observe differ from those commonly reported for traditional face recognition, with different groups being most affected depending on the benchmark and the model. We also note that the most accurate models are not necessarily the fairest and that models with poor overall accuracy can appear fair simply because they produce uniformly high error rates across all demographic groups.

AI.tldr: Among nine open-source MLLMs, a face-specialized model (FaceLLM-8B) achieves the best verification performance on IJB-C and RFW, but demographic fairness is inconsistent and not guaranteed by higher accuracy; some low-accuracy models can appear fair due to uniformly high errors.
AI.motivation: Assess demographic fairness of multimodal large language models for face verification, an underexplored area given the increasing use of MLLMs for biometric tasks.
AI.method: Evaluate nine open-source MLLMs (2B–8B, six families) on IJB-C and RFW face verification benchmarks across four ethnicity groups and two gender groups. Measure verification accuracy using Equal Error Rate (EER) and True Match Rate (TMR) at multiple operating points, and quantify demographic disparity with four FMR-based fairness metrics.
AI.result: FaceLLM-8B (face-specialized) substantially outperforms general-purpose MLLMs on both benchmarks. Bias patterns differ from traditional face recognition, with different groups affected depending on the benchmark and model. The most accurate models are not necessarily the fairest, and poorly performing models can appear fair due to uniformly high error rates across all demographic groups.
AI.conclusion: Fairness in MLLMs for face verification is nuanced and benchmark-dependent. Caution is required in deploying such models, as high overall accuracy does not guarantee demographic fairness; fairness assessment should be reported alongside accuracy, and specialized face models show advantages but still exhibit demographic biases.

title: Towards Practical Lossless Neural Compression for LiDAR Point Clouds
summary: LiDAR point clouds are fundamental to various applications, yet the extreme sparsity of high-precision geometric details hinders efficient context modeling, thereby limiting the compression speed and performance of existing methods. To address this challenge, we propose a compact representation for efficient predictive lossless coding. Our framework comprises two lightweight modules. First, the Geometry Re-Densification Module iteratively densifies encoded sparse geometry, extracts features at a dense scale, and then sparsifies the features for predictive coding. This module avoids costly computation on highly sparse details while maintaining a lightweight prediction head. Second, the Cross-scale Feature Propagation Module leverages occupancy cues from multiple resolution levels to guide hierarchical feature propagation, enabling information sharing across scales and reducing redundant feature extraction. Additionally, we introduce an integer-only inference pipeline to enable bit-exact cross-platform consistency, which avoids the entropy-coding collapse observed in existing neural compression methods and further accelerates coding. Experiments demonstrate competitive compression performance at real-time speed. Code will be released upon acceptance. Code is available at https://github.com/pengpeng-yu/FastPCC.

AI.tldr: Two lightweight modules enable fast, bit-exact predictive lossless LiDAR compression: (1) geometry re-densification for sparse-to-dense features, (2) cross-scale feature propagation, plus integer-only inference for cross-platform consistency; achieves real-time speed with competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: The extreme sparsity of high-precision LiDAR geometry hampers efficient context modeling and slows neural compression; existing methods struggle with entropy-coding instability and computational cost on sparse details.
AI.method: Geometry Re-Densification Module densifies encoded sparse geometry to extract dense-scale features, then sparsifies features for predictive coding, reducing computation on sparse details; Cross-scale Feature Propagation Module uses occupancy cues from multiple resolution levels to guide hierarchical feature propagation for cross-scale information sharing and reduced redundant feature extraction; Integer-only inference pipeline ensures bit-exact cross-platform consistency and avoids entropy-coding collapse, accelerating coding.
AI.result: Experiments show competitive compression performance at real-time speed; code will be released upon acceptance; implementation available at https://github.com/pengpeng-yu/FastPCC.
AI.conclusion: The proposed compact representation enables efficient predictive lossless coding for LiDAR point clouds with real-time performance and cross-platform bit-exactness, addressing sparsity-induced inefficiencies and entropy-coding issues.

title: A Mentalistic Interface for Probing Folk-Psychological Attribution to Non-Humanoid Robots
comment: Preprint submitted to IEEE. 8 pages, 21 figures
summary: This paper presents an experimental platform for studying intentional-state attribution toward a non-humanoid robot. The system combines a simulated robot, realistic task environments, and large language model-based explanatory layers that can express the same behavior in mentalistic, teleological, or mechanistic terms. By holding behavior constant while varying the explanatory frame, the platform provides a controlled way to investigate how language and framing shape the adoption of the intentional stance in robotics.

AI.tldr: An experimental platform to study how explanatory framing (mentalistic, teleological, mechanistic) affects intentional-state attribution to a non-humanoid robot, while keeping behavior constant using LLM-generated explanations.
AI.motivation: To understand how language and framing shape the adoption of the intentional stance in human-robot interaction, informing design of explanations and user experience.
AI.method: Hybrid setup with a simulated robot, realistic task environments, and LLM-based explanatory layers that express the same behavior in different frames. Experimental manipulation varies the explanatory frame while keeping the robot's behavior constant; participants' attribution of intentional states is measured.
AI.result: The abstract presents the platform and approach; it does not report empirical results. Pilot demonstrations or intended outcomes are not specified.
AI.conclusion: This platform enables controlled investigation of framing effects on intentional-state attribution in robotics and can inform explainable AI/robot design, but its validity depends on measurement choices, ecological validity, and careful handling of potential framing biases.

title: ViewSplat: View-Adaptive Dynamic Gaussian Splatting for Feed-Forward Synthesis
summary: We present ViewSplat, a view-adaptive 3D Gaussian splatting network for novel view synthesis from unposed images. While recent feed-forward 3D Gaussian splatting has significantly accelerated 3D scene reconstruction by bypassing per-scene optimization, a fundamental fidelity gap remains. We attribute this bottleneck to the limited capacity of single-step feed-forward networks to regress static Gaussian primitives that satisfy all viewpoints. To address this limitation, we shift the paradigm from static primitive regression to view-adaptive dynamic splatting. Instead of a rigid Gaussian representation, our pipeline learns a view-adaptable latent representation. Specifically, ViewSplat initially predicts base Gaussian primitives alongside the weights of dynamic MLPs. During rendering, these MLPs take target view coordinates as input and predict view-dependent residual updates for each Gaussian attribute (i.e., 3D position, scale, rotation, opacity, and color). This mechanism, which we term view-adaptive dynamic splatting, allows each primitive to rectify initial estimation errors, effectively capturing high-fidelity appearances. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ViewSplat achieves state-of-the-art fidelity while maintaining fast inference (17 FPS) and real-time rendering (154 FPS).

AI.tldr: ViewSplat introduces view-adaptive dynamic Gaussian splatting for novel view synthesis from unposed images, using view-conditioned MLPs to refine base Gaussian primitives per-view, achieving higher fidelity with fast inference and real-time rendering.
AI.motivation: Address the fidelity gap in single-step feed-forward Gaussian splatting caused by static primitives that cannot satisfy all viewpoints; seek higher representational capacity without costly per-scene optimization.
AI.method: Predict base Gaussian primitives and the weights of dynamic MLPs; during rendering, MLPs take target view coordinates and output view-dependent residual updates for Gaussian attributes (position, scale, rotation, opacity, color), enabling each primitive to adapt to the view.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art fidelity while maintaining fast inference speeds, reporting 17 FPS rendering and 154 FPS real-time rendering.
AI.conclusion: ViewSplat’s view-adaptive dynamic splatting effectively bridging the fidelity gap of static Gaussians, enabling high-quality novel view synthesis with efficient, per-view refinement through view-conditioned MLPs.

title: Just Zoom In: Cross-View Geo-Localization via Autoregressive Zooming
summary: Cross-view geo-localization (CVGL) estimates a camera's location by matching a street-view image to geo-referenced overhead imagery, enabling GPS-denied localization and navigation. Existing methods almost universally formulate CVGL as an image-retrieval problem in a contrastively trained embedding space. This ties performance to large batches and hard negative mining, and it ignores both the geometric structure of maps and the coverage mismatch between street-view and overhead imagery. In particular, salient landmarks visible from the street view can fall outside a fixed satellite crop, making retrieval targets ambiguous and limiting explicit spatial inference over the map. We propose Just Zoom In, an alternative formulation that performs CVGL via autoregressive zooming over a city-scale overhead map. Starting from a coarse satellite view, the model takes a short sequence of zoom-in decisions to select a terminal satellite cell at a target resolution, without contrastive losses or hard negative mining. We further introduce a realistic benchmark with crowd-sourced street views and high-resolution satellite imagery that reflects real capture conditions. On this benchmark, Just Zoom In achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving Recall@1 within 50 m by 5.5% and Recall@1 within 100 m by 9.6% over the strongest contrastive-retrieval baseline. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of sequential coarse-to-fine spatial reasoning for cross-view geo-localization.

AI.tldr: Autoregressive zooming on city-scale overhead maps (Just Zoom In) reframes cross-view geo-localization as a coarse-to-fine sequential search, avoiding contrastive learning and hard negatives, yielding state-of-the-art Recall@k on a new realistic benchmark.
AI.motivation: Limitations of standard image-retrieval CVGL: dependency on large batches and hard negative mining, ignoring map geometry and coverage mismatch between street-view and overhead imagery; salient street-view landmarks may lie outside fixed satellite crops, hindering explicit spatial reasoning.
AI.method: Autoregressive zooming starting from a coarse satellite view, performing a short sequence of zoom-in decisions to select a terminal satellite cell at a target resolution over a city-scale overhead map; no contrastive losses or hard negative mining.
AI.result: On a realistic benchmark with crowd-sourced street views and high-resolution satellite imagery, Just Zoom In achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving Recall@1 within 50 m by 5.5% and Recall@1 within 100 m by 9.6% over the strongest contrastive-retrieval baseline.
AI.conclusion: Sequential coarse-to-fine spatial reasoning is effective for cross-view geo-localization and can outperform contrastive retrievers by leveraging explicit geometric search over maps.

title: EagleNet: Energy-Aware Fine-Grained Relationship Learning Network for Text-Video Retrieval
summary: Text-video retrieval tasks have seen significant improvements due to the recent development of large-scale vision-language pre-trained models. Traditional methods primarily focus on video representations or cross-modal alignment, while recent works shift toward enriching text expressiveness to better match the rich semantics in videos. However, these methods use only interactions between text and frames/video, and ignore rich interactions among the internal frames within a video, so the final expanded text cannot capture frame contextual information, leading to disparities between text and video. In response, we introduce Energy-Aware Fine-Grained Relationship Learning Network (EagleNet) to generate accurate and context-aware enriched text embeddings. Specifically, the proposed Fine-Grained Relationship Learning mechanism (FRL) first constructs a text-frame graph by the generated text candidates and frames, then learns relationships among texts and frames, which are finally used to aggregate text candidates into an enriched text embedding that incorporates frame contextual information. To further improve fine-grained relationship learning in FRL, we design Energy-Aware Matching (EAM) to model the energy of text-frame interactions and thus accurately capture the distribution of real text-video pairs. Moreover, for more effective cross-modal alignment and stable training, we replace the conventional softmax-based contrastive loss with the sigmoid loss. Extensive experiments have demonstrated the superiority of EagleNet across MSRVTT, DiDeMo, MSVD, and VATEX. Codes are available at https://github.com/draym28/EagleNet.

AI.tldr: EagleNet introduces Fine-Grained Relationship Learning (FRL) with a text-frame graph and Energy-Aware Matching (EAM) to produce context-aware enriched text embeddings for text-video retrieval. It replaces softmax-based contrastive loss with sigmoid loss and demonstrates superior performance on multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing text-video retrieval methods focus on video representations or cross-modal alignment and neglect rich interactions among frames within a video, causing text to mismatch with video context. The goal is to produce text embeddings that are contextually enriched by frame interactions.
AI.method: Construct a text-frame graph from generated text candidates and video frames using FRL to learn relationships among texts and frames, aggregating text candidates into an enriched text embedding that captures frame context. Introduce Energy-Aware Matching (EAM) to model the energy of text-frame interactions and better reflect real text-video pair distributions. Use sigmoid loss instead of traditional softmax-based contrastive loss for cross-modal alignment to improve training stability.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on MSRVTT, DiDeMo, MSVD, and VATEX show that EagleNet achieves superior text-video retrieval performance compared to baselines. Code is released at the provided URL.
AI.conclusion: FRL with text-frame graphs and EAM enhances text expressiveness and cross-modal alignment by leveraging frame-level context and energy modeling, yielding improved retrieval performance and stable training.

title: V2U4Real: A Real-world Large-scale Dataset for Vehicle-to-UAV Cooperative Perception
summary: Modern autonomous vehicle perception systems are often constrained by occlusions, blind spots, and limited sensing range. While existing cooperative perception paradigms, such as Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) and Vehicle-to-Infrastructure (V2I), have demonstrated their effectiveness in mitigating these challenges, they remain limited to ground-level collaboration and cannot fully address large-scale occlusions or long-range perception in complex environments. To advance research in cross-view cooperative perception, we present V2U4Real, the first large-scale real-world multi-modal dataset for Vehicle-to-UAV (V2U) cooperative object perception. V2U4Real is collected by a ground vehicle and a UAV equipped with multi-view LiDARs and RGB cameras. The dataset covers urban streets, university campuses, and rural roads under diverse traffic scenarios, comprising over 56K LiDAR frames, 56K multi-view camera images, and 700K annotated 3D bounding boxes across four classes. To support a wide range of research tasks, we establish benchmarks for single-agent 3D object detection, cooperative 3D object detection, and object tracking. Comprehensive evaluations of several state-of-the-art models demonstrate the effectiveness of V2U cooperation in enhancing perception robustness and long-range awareness. The V2U4Real dataset and codebase is available at https://github.com/VjiaLi/V2U4Real.

AI.tldr: Introduces V2U4Real, a large-scale real-world multi-modal dataset for Vehicle-to-UAV cooperative perception, with ground vehicle and UAV sensors, enabling single-agent and cooperative 3D detection and tracking benchmarks; code and data released.
AI.motivation: Existing V2V/V2I systems are limited to ground-level collaboration and struggle with large occlusions and long-range perception; there is a need for cross-view perception data that includes UAV perspectives to enhance robustness in diverse environments.
AI.method: Data collection using a ground vehicle and a UAV equipped with multi-view LiDARs and RGB cameras across urban, campus, and rural settings. The dataset contains over 56K LiDAR frames, 56K multi-view images, and 700K annotated 3D bounding boxes over four classes. Benchmark suites for single-agent 3D detection, cooperative 3D detection, and object tracking; baseline evaluations of state-of-the-art models; codebase release.
AI.result: Demonstrates that V2U cooperation improves perception robustness and long-range awareness; provides a scalable real-world dataset and benchmarks to advance cross-view cooperative perception research.
AI.conclusion: V2U4Real enables broader research on cross-view cooperation, offers comprehensive benchmarks and code for reproducible experiments, and is a valuable resource for improving long-range perception in autonomous systems.

title: Out of Sight but Not Out of Mind: Hybrid Memory for Dynamic Video World Models
summary: Video world models have shown immense potential in simulating the physical world, yet existing memory mechanisms primarily treat environments as static canvases. When dynamic subjects hide out of sight and later re-emerge, current methods often struggle, leading to frozen, distorted, or vanishing subjects. To address this, we introduce Hybrid Memory, a novel paradigm requiring models to simultaneously act as precise archivists for static backgrounds and vigilant trackers for dynamic subjects, ensuring motion continuity during out-of-view intervals. To facilitate research in this direction, we construct HM-World, the first large-scale video dataset dedicated to hybrid memory. It features 59K high-fidelity clips with decoupled camera and subject trajectories, encompassing 17 diverse scenes, 49 distinct subjects, and meticulously designed exit-entry events to rigorously evaluate hybrid coherence. Furthermore, we propose HyDRA, a specialized memory architecture that compresses memory into tokens and utilizes a spatiotemporal relevance-driven retrieval mechanism. By selectively attending to relevant motion cues, HyDRA effectively preserves the identity and motion of hidden subjects. Extensive experiments on HM-World demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both dynamic subject consistency and overall generation quality.

AI.tldr: Hybrid Memory for video world models: a memory-augmented approach combining static background archival with dynamic subject tracking, via HyDRA, a token-based memory with spatiotemporal relevance retrieval; HM-World is a large-scale dataset (59K clips, 17 scenes, 49 subjects) designed to test hybrid coherence with decoupled camera and subject trajectories and exit-entry events; results show improvements over state-of-the-art on dynamic subject consistency and generation quality.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitation of current video world models that treat scenes as static canvases, failing to preserve identity and motion of subjects that disappear and reappear, thereby causing distortions during occlusions or re-entries.
AI.method: Introduce HyDRA: a memory architecture that compresses memory into tokens and employs a spatiotemporal relevance-driven retrieval mechanism to selectively attend to motion cues. Develop HM-World: a large-scale dataset with decoupled camera and subject trajectories and carefully designed exit-entry events to rigorously evaluate hybrid coherence.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on HM-World show HyDRA significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in dynamic subject consistency and overall generation quality.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that a hybrid memory approach with token-based compression and relevance-driven retrieval enables persistent identity and motion tracking for hidden subjects, and provides a benchmark dataset to facilitate research in hybrid memory for video world models.

title: Adaptive Learned Image Compression with Graph Neural Networks
summary: Efficient image compression relies on modeling both local and global redundancy. Most state-of-the-art (SOTA) learned image compression (LIC) methods are based on CNNs or Transformers, which are inherently rigid. Standard CNN kernels and window-based attention mechanisms impose fixed receptive fields and static connectivity patterns, which potentially couple non-redundant pixels simply due to their proximity in Euclidean space. This rigidity limits the model's ability to adaptively capture spatially varying redundancy across the image, particularly at the global level. To overcome these limitations, we propose a content-adaptive image compression framework based on Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Specifically, our approach constructs dual-scale graphs that enable flexible, data-driven receptive fields. Furthermore, we introduce adaptive connectivity by dynamically adjusting the number of neighbors for each node based on local content complexity. These innovations empower our Graph-based Learned Image Compression (GLIC) model to effectively model diverse redundancy patterns across images, leading to more efficient and adaptive compression. Experiments demonstrate that GLIC achieves state-of-the-art performance, achieving BD-rate reductions of 19.29%, 21.69%, and 18.71% relative to VTM-9.1 on Kodak, Tecnick, and CLIC, respectively. Code will be released at https://github.com/UnoC-727/GLIC.

AI.tldr: Proposes a content-adaptive image compression framework using Graph Neural Networks with dual-scale graphs and adaptive neighbor counts to capture local and global redundancy, achieving state-of-the-art BD-rate reductions.
AI.motivation: CNNs/Transformers impose fixed receptive fields and rigid connectivity, limiting adaptation to spatially varying redundancy, especially at the global scale.
AI.method: Construct dual-scale graphs to enable flexible, data-driven receptive fields and dynamically adjust the number of neighbors per node based on local content complexity, forming a Graph-based Learned Image Compression (GLIC) pipeline.
AI.result: Achieves BD-rate reductions of 19.29% (Kodak), 21.69% (Tecnick), and 18.71% (CLIC) relative to VTM-9.1, indicating state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: Graph-based LC with adaptive connectivity effectively models diverse redundancy patterns, enabling more efficient and adaptive compression; code will be released on GitHub.

title: PixelSmile: Toward Fine-Grained Facial Expression Editing
comment: 21 Pages; Project Page: https://ammmob.github.io/PixelSmile/ Code: https://github.com/Ammmob/PixelSmile
summary: Fine-grained facial expression editing has long been limited by intrinsic semantic overlap. To address this, we construct the Flex Facial Expression (FFE) dataset with continuous affective annotations and establish FFE-Bench to evaluate structural confusion, editing accuracy, linear controllability, and the trade-off between expression editing and identity preservation. We propose PixelSmile, a diffusion framework that disentangles expression semantics via fully symmetric joint training. PixelSmile combines intensity supervision with contrastive learning to produce stronger and more distinguishable expressions, achieving precise and stable linear expression control through textual latent interpolation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PixelSmile achieves superior disentanglement and robust identity preservation, confirming its effectiveness for continuous, controllable, and fine-grained expression editing, while naturally supporting smooth expression blending.

AI.tldr: PixelSmile: a diffusion-based, symmetry-trained framework for continuous, controllable, fine-grained facial expression editing, enabled by the Flex Facial Expression dataset (FFE) and FFE-Bench for evaluating structural confusion, editing accuracy, linear controllability, and identity trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Intrinsic semantic overlap limits fine-grained expression editing; need continuous affect annotations and benchmarks to assess editing disentanglement and identity preservation.
AI.method: PixelSmile employs fully symmetric joint training to disentangle expression semantics; combines intensity supervision with contrastive learning; enables precise linear control via textual latent interpolation.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines in disentanglement and identity preservation; achieves precise, stable linear expression control; supports smooth expression blending.
AI.conclusion: PixelSmile demonstrates effective continuous, controllable, fine-grained expression editing, with the FFE dataset and FFE-Bench providing rigorous evaluation; the approach preserves identity while enabling nuanced expression manipulation.

title: MACRO: Advancing Multi-Reference Image Generation with Structured Long-Context Data
summary: Generating images conditioned on multiple visual references is critical for real-world applications such as multi-subject composition, narrative illustration, and novel view synthesis, yet current models suffer from severe performance degradation as the number of input references grows. We identify the root cause as a fundamental data bottleneck: existing datasets are dominated by single- or few-reference pairs and lack the structured, long-context supervision needed to learn dense inter-reference dependencies. To address this, we introduce MacroData, a large-scale dataset of 400K samples, each containing up to 10 reference images, systematically organized across four complementary dimensions -- Customization, Illustration, Spatial reasoning, and Temporal dynamics -- to provide comprehensive coverage of the multi-reference generation space. Recognizing the concurrent absence of standardized evaluation protocols, we further propose MacroBench, a benchmark of 4,000 samples that assesses generative coherence across graded task dimensions and input scales. Extensive experiments show that fine-tuning on MacroData yields substantial improvements in multi-reference generation, and ablation studies further reveal synergistic benefits of cross-task co-training and effective strategies for handling long-context complexity. The dataset and benchmark will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: Introduces MacroData and MacroBench to address long-context multi-reference image generation; a 400k-sample dataset with up to 10 references across four dimensions and a 4k-sample benchmark, enabling substantial performance gains via fine-tuning and cross-task co-training.
AI.motivation: Current multi-reference generation suffers severe performance degradation as references grow due to a lack of dense inter-reference supervision and standardized evaluation.
AI.method: MacroData: 400k samples with up to 10 reference images, organized across four dimensions (Customization, Illustration, Spatial reasoning, Temporal dynamics). MacroBench: a 4k-sample benchmark for evaluating generative coherence across task dimensions and input scales. Additional experiments include fine-tuning on MacroData, cross-task co-training, and long-context handling strategies.
AI.result: Fine-tuning on MacroData yields substantial improvements in multi-reference generation; ablation studies show synergistic benefits from cross-task co-training and effective long-context strategies.
AI.conclusion: The dataset and benchmark address data and evaluation bottlenecks in multi-reference generation and will be publicly released to facilitate research and development in this area.

title: PackForcing: Short Video Training Suffices for Long Video Sampling and Long Context Inference
summary: Autoregressive video diffusion models have demonstrated remarkable progress, yet they remain bottlenecked by intractable linear KV-cache growth, temporal repetition, and compounding errors during long-video generation. To address these challenges, we present PackForcing, a unified framework that efficiently manages the generation history through a novel three-partition KV-cache strategy. Specifically, we categorize the historical context into three distinct types: (1) Sink tokens, which preserve early anchor frames at full resolution to maintain global semantics; (2) Mid tokens, which achieve a massive spatiotemporal compression (32x token reduction) via a dual-branch network fusing progressive 3D convolutions with low-resolution VAE re-encoding; and (3) Recent tokens, kept at full resolution to ensure local temporal coherence. To strictly bound the memory footprint without sacrificing quality, we introduce a dynamic top-$k$ context selection mechanism for the mid tokens, coupled with a continuous Temporal RoPE Adjustment that seamlessly re-aligns position gaps caused by dropped tokens with negligible overhead. Empowered by this principled hierarchical context compression, PackForcing can generate coherent 2-minute, 832x480 videos at 16 FPS on a single H200 GPU. It achieves a bounded KV cache of just 4 GB and enables a remarkable 24x temporal extrapolation (5s to 120s), operating effectively either zero-shot or trained on merely 5-second clips. Extensive results on VBench demonstrate state-of-the-art temporal consistency (26.07) and dynamic degree (56.25), proving that short-video supervision is sufficient for high-quality, long-video synthesis. https://github.com/ShandaAI/PackForcing

AI.tldr: PackForcing introduces a three-partition KV-cache to efficiently generate long videos: Sink tokens for full-resolution anchor frames, Mid tokens compressed via a dual-branch network with 32x token reduction and dynamic top-k selection, and Recent tokens at full resolution; combined with Temporal RoPE adjustments, this yields bounded memory (4 GB), 24x temporal extrapolation (5s to 120s), and state-of-the-art temporal consistency on VBench with short-video supervision.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive video diffusion models suffer from intractable KV-cache growth, temporal repetition, and error accumulation during long-video generation. A memory-efficient, scalable context management strategy is needed to enable long-duration video synthesis without large memory footprints or degraded quality.
AI.method: Propose PackForcing with a three-partition KV-cache: Sink tokens (full-resolution anchors), Mid tokens (32x compression via a dual-branch network combining progressive 3D convolutions with low-resolution VAE re-encoding), and Recent tokens (full-resolution). Introduce dynamic top-k context selection for mid tokens and continuous Temporal RoPE Adjustment to re-align position gaps caused by token dropping, enabling memory bounding while preserving quality.
AI.result: Empirically enables coherent 2-minute, 832x480 videos at 16 FPS on a single H200 GPU with a bounded KV-cache of 4 GB. Achieves 24x temporal extrapolation (5s to 120s) and demonstrates state-of-the-art temporal consistency (26.07) and dynamic degree (56.25) on VBench, with effectiveness in zero-shot or 5-second clip supervision.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical context compression with adaptive token management enables efficient, high-quality long-video synthesis from short clips, showing that controlled memory budgets and dynamic context selection can match or surpass prior long-video generation baselines.

title: HeSS: Head Sensitivity Score for Sparsity Redistribution in VGGT
summary: Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer (VGGT) has advanced 3D vision, yet its global attention layers suffer from quadratic computational costs that hinder scalability. Several sparsification-based acceleration techniques have been proposed to alleviate this issue, but they often suffer from substantial accuracy degradation. We hypothesize that the accuracy degradation stems from the heterogeneity in head-wise sparsification sensitivity, as the existing methods apply a uniform sparsity pattern across all heads. Motivated by this hypothesis, we present a two-stage sparsification pipeline that effectively quantifies and exploits headwise sparsification sensitivity. In the first stage, we measure head-wise sparsification sensitivity using a novel metric, the Head Sensitivity Score (HeSS), which approximates the Hessian with respect to two distinct error terms on a small calibration set. In the inference stage, we perform HeSS-Guided Sparsification, leveraging the pre-computed HeSS to reallocate the total attention budget-assigning denser attention to sensitive heads and sparser attention to more robust ones. We demonstrate that HeSS effectively captures head-wise sparsification sensitivity and empirically confirm that attention heads in the global attention layers exhibit heterogeneous sensitivity characteristics. Extensive experiments further show that our method effectively mitigates performance degradation under high sparsity, demonstrating strong robustness across varying sparsification levels. Code is available at https://github.com/libary753/HeSS.

AI.tldr: A two-stage sparsification approach for Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer (VGGT) that accounts for headwise sensitivity via the Head Sensitivity Score (HeSS) to preserve accuracy under high sparsity.
AI.motivation: VGGT's global attention incurs quadratic computational cost; existing sparsification often degrades accuracy. The authors hypothesize that headwise heterogeneity in sensitivity causes this degradation and aim to quantify and exploit it.
AI.method: Stage 1: introduce Head Sensitivity Score (HeSS) to approximate Hessian-based sensitivity on a small calibration set for each attention head. Stage 2: during inference, perform HeSS-guided sparsification by reallocating the total attention budget—denser attention to sensitive heads and sparser attention to robust ones, using pre-computed HeSS.
AI.result: HeSS effectively captures headwise sparsification sensitivity; empirical analysis shows heterogeneous sensitivity across heads in global attention layers. Extensive experiments demonstrate robustness of the method across varying sparsity levels, mitigating performance loss at high sparsity. Code is provided by the authors.
AI.conclusion: Accounting for headwise sensitivity via HeSS enables effective and robust sparsification of VGGT without major accuracy loss, highlighting the importance of per-head analysis for scalable transformer inference.

title: InstanceAnimator: Multi-Instance Sketch Video Colorization
summary: We propose InstanceAnimator, a novel Diffusion Transformer framework for multi-instance sketch video colorization. Existing methods suffer from three core limitations: inflexible user control due to heavy reliance on single reference frames, poor instance controllability leading to misalignment in multi-character scenarios, and degraded detail fidelity in fine-grained regions. To address these challenges, we introduce three corresponding innovations. First, a Canvas Guidance Condition eliminates workflow fragmentation by allowing free placement of reference elements and background, enabling unprecedented user flexibility. Second, an Instance Matching Mechanism resolves misalignment by integrating instance features with the sketches, ensuring precise control over multiple characters. Third, an Adaptive Decoupled Control Module enhances detail fidelity by injecting semantic features from characters, backgrounds, and text conditions into the diffusion process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that InstanceAnimator achieves superior multi-instance colorization with enhanced user control, high visual quality, and strong instance consistency.

AI.tldr: InstanceAnimator is a Diffusion Transformer framework for multi-instance sketch video colorization that introduces three innovations to improve control, alignment, and detail: Canvas Guidance Condition, Instance Matching Mechanism, and Adaptive Decoupled Control Module.
AI.motivation: Address three core issues in multi-instance sketch colorization: inflexible user control due to dependence on a single reference frame, misalignment and lack of precise control in multi-character scenes, and degraded detail fidelity in fine-grained regions.
AI.method: 1) Canvas Guidance Condition enables free placement of reference elements and background for flexible workflows. 2) Instance Matching Mechanism integrates instance features with sketches to resolve misalignment across multiple characters. 3) Adaptive Decoupled Control Module injects semantic features from characters, backgrounds, and text into the diffusion process to enhance detail fidelity.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show InstanceAnimator achieves superior multi-instance colorization with enhanced user control, high visual quality, and strong instance consistency compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates effective fusion of user-driven cues and semantic information within a diffusion-based pipeline, enabling flexible, accurate, and high-quality multi-instance sketch video colorization and suggesting potential for broader animation workflows.

title: CLIP-RD: Relational Distillation for Efficient CLIP Knowledge Distillation
summary: CLIP aligns image and text embeddings via contrastive learning and demonstrates strong zero-shot generalization. Its large-scale architecture requires substantial computational and memory resources, motivating the distillation of its capabilities into lightweight student models. However, existing CLIP distillation methods do not explicitly model multi-directional relational dependencies between teacher and student embeddings, limiting the student's ability to preserve the structural relationships encoded by the teacher. To address this, we propose a relational knowledge distillation framework that introduces two novel methods, Vertical Relational Distillation (VRD) and Cross Relational Distillation (XRD). VRD enforces consistency of teacher-student distillation strength across modalities at the distribution level, while XRD imposes bidirectional symmetry on cross-modal teacher-student similarity distributions. By jointly modeling multi-directional relational structures, CLIP-RD promotes faithful alignment of the student embedding geometry with that of the teacher, outperforming existing methods by 0.8%p.

AI.tldr: A relational knowledge distillation framework for CLIP, CLIP-RD, with Vertical Relational Distillation (VRD) and Cross Relational Distillation (XRD) to preserve teacher-student relational structures, achieving +0.8 percentage points over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Large CLIP models are computationally heavy; existing distillation methods neglect multi-directional relational dependencies between teacher and student embeddings, hurting the preservation of the teacher's embedding geometry.
AI.method: Introduce VRD and XRD. VRD enforces consistent distillation strength across modalities at the distribution level; XRD enforces bidirectional symmetry on cross-modal teacher-student similarity distributions. The two components jointly model multi-directional relational structures to align student embeddings with the teacher.
AI.result: CLIP-RD outperforms existing distillation methods by 0.8 percentage points, indicating improved preservation of embedding geometry and teacher-student relationships.
AI.conclusion: Modeling multi-directional relational structures between teacher and student embeddings yields more faithful alignment of the student to the teacher’s geometry, enabling lighter CLIP models to retain stronger performance.

title: Multimodal Dataset Distillation via Phased Teacher Models
summary: Multimodal dataset distillation aims to construct compact synthetic datasets that enable efficient compression and knowledge transfer from large-scale image-text data. However, existing approaches often fail to capture the complex, dynamically evolving knowledge embedded in the later training stages of teacher models. This limitation leads to degraded student performance and compromises the quality of the distilled data. To address critical challenges such as pronounced cross-stage performance gaps and unstable teacher trajectories, we propose Phased Teacher Model with Shortcut Trajectory (PTM-ST) -- a novel phased distillation framework. PTM-ST leverages stage-aware teacher modeling and a shortcut-based trajectory construction strategy to accurately fit the teacher's learning dynamics across distinct training phases. This enhances both the stability and expressiveness of the distillation process. Through theoretical analysis and comprehensive experiments, we show that PTM-ST significantly mitigates optimization oscillations and inter-phase knowledge gaps, while also reducing storage overhead. Our method consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines on Flickr30k and COCO, achieving up to 13.5% absolute improvement and an average gain of 9.53% on Flickr30k. Code: https://github.com/Previsior/PTM-ST.

AI.tldr: PTM-ST introduces a phased distillation framework with stage-aware teacher models and shortcut trajectory construction to fit learning dynamics across training phases, improving stability and multimodal data distillation performance with substantial gains and reduced storage.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal dataset distillation often fails to capture evolving knowledge in later training stages, leading to cross-stage performance gaps and unstable teacher trajectories that hurt student performance and data quality.
AI.method: Formulate Phased Teacher Model with Shortcut Trajectory (PTM-ST): train stage-specific teachers representing distinct training phases and construct a shortcut-based trajectory to approximate the teacher's dynamics across phases; provide theoretical analysis and validate via experiments on Flickr30k and COCO; code release.
AI.result: PTM-ST reduces optimization oscillations and inter-phase knowledge gaps, lowers storage overhead, and achieves state-of-the-art gains on Flickr30k and COCO—up to 13.5% absolute improvement and an average 9.53% on Flickr30k.
AI.conclusion: By aligning distillation with phase-specific learning dynamics through stage-aware teachers and shortcut trajectories, PTM-ST enhances stability and expressiveness in multimodal dataset distillation and demonstrates strong cross-stage knowledge transfer and efficiency.

title: FSGNet: A Frequency-Aware and Semantic Guidance Network for Infrared Small Target Detection
summary: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) aims to identify and distinguish small targets from complex backgrounds. Leveraging the powerful multi-scale feature fusion capability of the U-Net architecture, IRSTD has achieved significant progress. However, U-Net suffers from semantic degradation when transferring high-level features from deep to shallow layers, limiting the precise localization of small targets. To address this issue, this paper proposes FSGNet, a lightweight and effective detection framework incorporating frequency-aware and semantic guidance mechanisms. Specifically, a multi-directional interactive attention module is proposed throughout the encoder to capture fine-grained and directional features, enhancing the network's sensitivity to small, low-contrast targets. To suppress background interference propagated through skip connections, a multi-scale frequency-aware module leverages Fast Fourier transform to filter out target-similar clutter while preserving salient target structures. At the deepest layer, a global pooling module captures high-level semantic information, which is subsequently upsampled and propagated to each decoder stage through the global semantic guidance flows, ensuring semantic consistency and precise localization across scales. Extensive experiments on four public IRSTD datasets demonstrate that FSGNet achieves superior detection performance and maintains high efficiency, highlighting its practical applicability and robustness. The codes will be released on https://github.com/Wangtao-Bao/FSGNet.

AI.tldr: FSGNet enhances infrared small target detection by integrating a frequency-aware module and semantic guidance with a multi-directional attention-equipped encoder to alleviate semantic degradation in U-Net, achieving better accuracy and efficiency across four IRSTD datasets.
AI.motivation: To overcome semantic degradation and background interference in U-Net-based IRSTD due to deep-to-shallow feature transfer and skip-connections, enabling precise localization of small, low-contrast targets.
AI.method: Introduce FSGNet: (1) multi-directional interactive attention module in the encoder to capture fine-grained directional features; (2) multi-scale frequency-aware module using FFT to suppress target-similar background clutter while preserving salient target structures; (3) global pooling module at the deepest layer to extract high-level semantics and propagate them to all decoders via global semantic guidance flows for cross-scale semantic consistency.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on four public IRSTD datasets show superior detection performance and high efficiency; code will be released.
AI.conclusion: FSGNet effectively mitigates semantic degradation, improves localization and robustness for infrared small targets, offering a practical, efficient solution for IRSTD.

title: PMT: Plain Mask Transformer for Image and Video Segmentation with Frozen Vision Encoders
summary: Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) pre-trained at scale enable a single frozen encoder to serve multiple downstream tasks simultaneously. Recent VFM-based encoder-only models for image and video segmentation, such as EoMT and VidEoMT, achieve competitive accuracy with remarkably low latency, yet they require finetuning the encoder, sacrificing the multi-task encoder sharing that makes VFMs practically attractive for large-scale deployment. To reconcile encoder-only simplicity and speed with frozen VFM features, we propose the Plain Mask Decoder (PMD), a fast Transformer-based segmentation decoder that operates on top of frozen VFM features. The resulting model, the Plain Mask Transformer (PMT), preserves the architectural simplicity and low latency of encoder-only designs while keeping the encoder representation unchanged and shareable. The design seamlessly applies to both image and video segmentation, inheriting the generality of the encoder-only framework. On standard image segmentation benchmarks, PMT matches the frozen-encoder state of the art while running up to ~3x faster. For video segmentation, it even performs on par with fully finetuned methods, while being up to 8x faster than state-of-the-art frozen-encoder models. Code: https://github.com/tue-mps/pmt.

AI.tldr: Introduces Plain Mask Decoder (PMD) and Plain Mask Transformer (PMT) to enable fast, encoder-freezing segmentation on frozen Vision Foundation Model features, applicable to image and video tasks with strong speed/accuracy trade-offs.
AI.motivation: To retain the benefits of frozen, generalizable Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) for multi-task downstream segmentation without finetuning the encoder, achieving high throughput and simplicity.
AI.method: Proposes PMD, a lightweight Transformer-based segmentation decoder that operates on frozen VFM features, forming PMT when combined with the encoder. The approach preserves encoder representations, enabling shared, frozen features for both image and video segmentation and maintaining a simple, fast pipeline.
AI.result: On image segmentation benchmarks, PMT matches the frozen-encoder state-of-the-art while being ~3× faster. In video segmentation, PMT matches fully finetuned methods and is up to ~8× faster than state-of-the-art frozen-encoder models.
AI.conclusion: PMD/PMT demonstrate that encoder-freezing with a fast Transformer-based decoder can rival or exceed the performance of finetuned encoders while delivering substantial speedups, for both image and video segmentation, with publicly available code.

title: HiSpatial: Taming Hierarchical 3D Spatial Understanding in Vision-Language Models
comment: Accepted by CVPR 2026. Project page: https://microsoft.github.io/HiSpatial
summary: Achieving human-like spatial intelligence for vision-language models (VLMs) requires inferring 3D structures from 2D observations, recognizing object properties and relations in 3D space, and performing high-level spatial reasoning. In this paper, we propose a principled hierarchical framework that decomposes the learning of 3D spatial understanding in VLMs into four progressively complex levels, from geometric perception to abstract spatial reasoning. Guided by this framework, we construct an automated pipeline that processes approximately 5M images with over 45M objects to generate 3D spatial VQA pairs across diverse tasks and scenes for VLM supervised fine-tuning. We also develop an RGB-D VLM incorporating metric-scale point maps as auxiliary inputs to further enhance spatial understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple spatial understanding and reasoning benchmarks, surpassing specialized spatial models and large proprietary systems such as Gemini-2.5-pro and GPT-5. Moreover, our analysis reveals clear dependencies among hierarchical task levels, offering new insights into how multi-level task design facilitates the emergence of 3D spatial intelligence.

AI.tldr: A four-level hierarchical framework enables 3D spatial understanding in vision-language models (VLMs), coupled with an automated pipeline to create 3D spatial VQA data from ~5M images/45M objects and an RGB-D VLM using metric-scale point maps; achieves state-of-the-art on spatial reasoning benchmarks and offers insights into multi-level task design.
AI.motivation: To achieve human-like spatial intelligence in VLMs by inferring 3D structures from 2D observations, recognizing 3D properties/relations, and performing high-level spatial reasoning, which current models struggle to systematize across tasks.
AI.method: Propose a principled four-level hierarchical framework for 3D spatial understanding (from geometric perception to abstract spatial reasoning). Build an automated data pipeline processing ~5M images with >45M objects to generate 3D spatial VQA pairs for supervised fine-tuning. Develop an RGB-D VLM that uses metric-scale point maps as auxiliary inputs to enhance spatial understanding.
AI.result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple spatial understanding and reasoning benchmarks, surpassing specialized spatial models and large proprietary systems (e.g., Gemini-2.5-pro, GPT-5). Also reveals dependencies among hierarchical task levels, indicating multi-level task design facilitates emergence of 3D spatial intelligence.
AI.conclusion: A hierarchical, multi-level training and data-generation framework, coupled with RGB-D inputs, can significantly advance 3D spatial understanding in VLMs, with structured dependencies across levels offering guidance for future model and data design.

title: VideoWeaver: Multimodal Multi-View Video-to-Video Transfer for Embodied Agents
summary:
Recent progress in video-to-video (V2V) translation has enabled realistic resimulation of embodied AI demonstrations, a capability that allows pretrained robot policies to be transferable to new environments without additional data collection. However, prior works can only operate on a single view at a time, while embodied AI tasks are commonly captured from multiple synchronized cameras to support policy learning. Naively applying single-view models independently to each camera leads to inconsistent appearance across views, and standard transformer architectures do not scale to multi-view settings due to the quadratic cost of cross-view attention.
  We present VideoWeaver, the first multimodal multi-view V2V translation framework. VideoWeaver is initially trained as a single-view flow-based V2V model. To achieve an extension to the multi-view regime, we propose to ground all views in a shared 4D latent space derived from a feed-forward spatial foundation model, namely, Pi3. This encourages view-consistent appearance even under wide baselines and dynamic camera motion. To scale beyond a fixed number of cameras, we train views at distinct diffusion timesteps, enabling the model to learn both joint and conditional view distributions. This in turn allows autoregressive synthesis of new viewpoints conditioned on existing ones.
  Experiments show superior or similar performance to the state-of-the-art on the single-view translation benchmarks and, for the first time, physically and stylistically consistent multi-view translations, including challenging egocentric and heterogeneous-camera setups central to world randomization for robot learning.

AI.tldr: VideoWeaver enables multimodal multi-view V2V translation by grounding multiple synchronized camera views in a shared 4D latent space derived from the Pi3 foundation model, enabling view-consistent translations and scalable autoregressive synthesis of new viewpoints.
AI.motivation: Single-view V2V models cause inconsistency across views; multi-view translation remains challenging due to cross-view attention costs; need scalable method to support multiple synchronized cameras in embodied AI tasks.
AI.method: Initialize from a single-view flow-based V2V model; extend to multi-view by grounding views in a shared 4D latent space from Pi3; train views at distinct diffusion timesteps to capture joint and conditional view distributions; enable autoregressive synthesis of new viewpoints conditioned on existing ones.
AI.result: Matches or surpasses state-of-the-art on single-view V2V benchmarks; for the first time demonstrates physically and stylistically consistent multi-view translations across egocentric and heterogeneous-camera setups; validates applicability to world randomization for robot learning.
AI.conclusion: VideoWeaver provides a scalable, view-consistent multi-view V2V translation framework, enabling better transfer of policies across environments in embodied AI and supporting multi-camera data without inconsistent appearances.

title: DC-Reg: Globally Optimal Point Cloud Registration via Tight Bounding with Difference of Convex Programming
summary: Achieving globally optimal point cloud registration under partial overlaps and large misalignments remains a fundamental challenge. While simultaneous transformation ($\boldsymbolθ$) and correspondence ($\mathbf{P}$) estimation has the advantage of being robust to nonrigid deformation, its non-convex coupled objective often leads to local minima for heuristic methods and prohibitive convergence times for existing global solvers due to loose lower bounds. To address this, we propose DC-Reg, a robust globally optimal framework that significantly tightens the Branch-and-Bound (BnB) search. Our core innovation is the derivation of a holistic concave underestimator for the coupled transformation-assignment objective, grounded in the Difference of Convex (DC) programming paradigm. Unlike prior works that rely on term-wise relaxations (e.g., McCormick envelopes) which neglect variable interplay, our holistic DC decomposition captures the joint structural interaction between $\boldsymbolθ$ and $\mathbf{P}$. This formulation enables the computation of remarkably tight lower bounds via efficient Linear Assignment Problems (LAP) evaluated at the vertices of the search boxes. We validate our framework on 2D similarity and 3D rigid registration, utilizing rotation-invariant features for the latter to achieve high efficiency without sacrificing optimality. Experimental results on synthetic data and the 3DMatch benchmark demonstrate that DC-Reg achieves significantly faster convergence and superior robustness to extreme noise and outliers compared to state-of-the-art global techniques.

AI.tldr: DC-Reg introduces a holistic DC-programming based bound for joint rotation/assignment in point-cloud registration, enabling a fast, globally optimal Branch-and-Bound search that tightens lower bounds via solving LAPs at search-box vertices.
AI.motivation: To overcome non-convexity and loose bounds in global registration with partial overlaps and large misalignments, achieving robust, globally optimal solutions without prohibitive computation.
AI.method: Derives a holistic concave underestimator of the coupled transformation and assignment objective using Difference-of-Convex (DC) programming. Unlike term-wise relaxations, this captures the joint θ–P interaction. Lower bounds are obtained by solving Linear Assignment Problems at the vertices of search boxes within a Branch-and-Bound framework. Demonstrated on 2D similarity and 3D rigid registration using rotation-invariant features.
AI.result: DC-Reg yields significantly faster convergence and improved robustness to extreme noise and outliers, outperforming state-of-the-art global methods on synthetic data and the 3DMatch benchmark.
AI.conclusion: A holistic DC-based underestimator enables tight lower bounds for the coupled registration problem, making globally optimal registration under challenging conditions practical and efficient.

title: CIAR: Interval-based Collaborative Decoding for Image Generation Acceleration
summary: Auto-regressive (AR) models have recently made notable progress in image generation, achieving performance comparable to diffusion-based approaches. However, their computational intensity and sequential nature impede on-device deployment, causing disruptive latency. We address this via a cloud-device collaboration framework \textbf{CIAR}, which utilizes on-device self-verification to handle two key properties of visual synthesis: \textit{the vast token vocabulary} required for high-fidelity images and \textit{inherent spatial redundancy} which leads to extreme predictability in homogeneous regions, while object boundaries exhibit high uncertainty. Uniform verification wastes resources on such redundant tokens. Our solution centers on an on-device token uncertainty quantifier, which adopts continuous probability intervals to accelerate processing and make it feasible for large visual vocabularies instead of conventional discrete solution sets. Additionally, we incorporate a Interval-enhanced decoding module to further speed up decoding while maintaining visual fidelity and semantic consistency via a distribution alignment training strategy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CIAR achieves a 2.18x speed-up and reduces cloud requests by 70\%, while preserving image quality compared to existing methods.

AI.tldr: CIAR enables on-device AR image synthesis with cloud-device collaboration using an on-device token uncertainty quantifier and interval-enhanced decoding; achieves 2.18x speed-up and 70% fewer cloud requests while preserving quality.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive image generators offer high fidelity but are computationally heavy and inherently sequential, hindering on-device deployment. Exploiting inhomogeneous visual regions via uncertainty and redundancy can reduce compute and latency.
AI.method: Proposes on-device token uncertainty quantifier using continuous probability intervals to handle large vocabularies and exploit spatial redundancy; introduces interval-enhanced decoding; joint distribution alignment training to maintain fidelity; CIAR framework orchestrates cloud-device collaboration.
AI.result: Empirical results show 2.18x speed-up and 70% reduction in cloud requests with preserved image quality compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: CIAR demonstrates a viable cloud-device collaboration approach for autoregressive image synthesis, balancing speed, resource usage, and output quality, and enabling scalable deployment to large vocabularies.

title: AdaSFormer: Adaptive Serialized Transformers for Monocular Semantic Scene Completion from Indoor Environments
summary: Indoor monocular semantic scene completion (MSSC) is notably more challenging than its outdoor counterpart due to complex spatial layouts and severe occlusions. While transformers are well suited for modeling global dependencies, their high memory cost and difficulty in reconstructing fine-grained details have limited their use in indoor MSSC. To address these limitations, we introduce AdaSFormer, a serialized transformer framework tailored for indoor MSSC. Our model features three key designs: (1) an Adaptive Serialized Transformer with learnable shifts that dynamically adjust receptive fields; (2) a Center-Relative Positional Encoding that captures spatial information richness; and (3) a Convolution-Modulated Layer Normalization that bridges heterogeneous representations between convolutional and transformer features. Extensive experiments on NYUv2 and Occ-ScanNet demonstrate that AdaSFormer achieves state-of-the-art performance. The code is publicly available at: https://github.com/alanWXZ/AdaSFormer.

AI.tldr: AdaSFormer introduces a serialized transformer framework for indoor monocular semantic scene completion, featuring Adaptive Serialized Transformer with learnable shifts, Center-Relative Positional Encoding, and Convolution-Modulated LayerNorm, achieving state-of-the-art results on NYUv2 and Occ-ScanNet; code released.
AI.motivation: Indoor MSSC is more challenging due to occlusions and complex spatial layouts. Transformers incur high memory costs and struggle with reconstructing fine-grained details, necessitating a design tailored for efficiency and accurate spatial modeling.
AI.method: Three contributions: (1) Adaptive Serialized Transformer with learnable shifts that dynamically adjust receptive fields; (2) Center-Relative Positional Encoding to enrich spatial information; (3) Convolution-Modulated Layer Normalization bridging convolutional and transformer representations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on NYUv2 and Occ-ScanNet show state-of-the-art performance for indoor MSSC; code is publicly available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: AdaSFormer effectively addresses memory and reconstruction challenges in indoor MSSC, delivering improved accuracy and efficiency through its three design components and enabling practical indoor 3D scene understanding.

title: RealRestorer: Towards Generalizable Real-World Image Restoration with Large-Scale Image Editing Models
summary: Image restoration under real-world degradations is critical for downstream tasks such as autonomous driving and object detection. However, existing restoration models are often limited by the scale and distribution of their training data, resulting in poor generalization to real-world scenarios. Recently, large-scale image editing models have shown strong generalization ability in restoration tasks, especially for closed-source models like Nano Banana Pro, which can restore images while preserving consistency. Nevertheless, achieving such performance with those large universal models requires substantial data and computational costs. To address this issue, we construct a large-scale dataset covering nine common real-world degradation types and train a state-of-the-art open-source model to narrow the gap with closed-source alternatives. Furthermore, we introduce RealIR-Bench, which contains 464 real-world degraded images and tailored evaluation metrics focusing on degradation removal and consistency preservation. Extensive experiments demonstrate our model ranks first among open-source methods, achieving state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: Open-source image restoration model trained on a large-scale real-world degradation dataset and evaluated on RealIR-Bench, achieving state-of-the-art performance among open-source methods and narrowing the gap to closed-source models.
AI.motivation: Generalization gap in real-world restoration due to limited training data and distribution shift; need scalable, open alternatives and robust evaluation for real degradations.
AI.method: Construct a large-scale dataset covering nine real-world degradation types and train a state-of-the-art open-source restoration model; propose RealIR-Bench with 464 real degraded images and tailored metrics emphasizing degradation removal and consistency preservation.
AI.result: The open-source model ranks first among open-source methods and achieves state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating improved generalization and reduced gap to closed-source models.
AI.conclusion: Open-source restoration can approach closed-source performance when trained on extensive real-world data; RealIR-Bench provides meaningful evaluation and a practical benchmark for real-world degradation removal and consistency.

title: Beyond the Golden Data: Resolving the Motion-Vision Quality Dilemma via Timestep Selective Training
summary: Recent advances in video generation models have achieved impressive results. However, these models heavily rely on the use of high-quality data that combines both high visual quality and high motion quality. In this paper, we identify a key challenge in video data curation: the Motion-Vision Quality Dilemma. We discovered that visual quality and motion intensity inherently exhibit a negative correlation, making it hard to obtain golden data that excels in both aspects. To address this challenge, we first examine the hierarchical learning dynamics of video diffusion models and conduct gradient-based analysis on quality-degraded samples. We discover that quality-imbalanced data can produce gradients similar to golden data at appropriate timesteps. Based on this, we introduce the novel concept of Timestep selection in Training Process. We propose Timestep-aware Quality Decoupling (TQD), which modifies the data sampling distribution to better match the model's learning process. For certain types of data, the sampling distribution is skewed toward higher timesteps for motion-rich data, while high visual quality data is more likely to be sampled during lower timesteps. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that TQD enables training exclusively on separated imbalanced data to achieve performance surpassing conventional training with better data, challenging the necessity of perfect data in video generation. Moreover, our method also boosts model performance when trained on high-quality data, showcasing its effectiveness across different data scenarios.

AI.tldr: Timestep-aware Quality Decoupling (TQD) for video diffusion models to address the Motion-Vision Quality Dilemma; it reweights data sampling across training timesteps to decouple high motion from high visual quality, enabling effective training on imbalanced data and improving performance even with non-ideal data.
AI.motivation: Visual quality and motion intensity in video data are negatively correlated, making it hard to curate datasets that are simultaneously high-quality in both aspects. The paper argues that current data scarcity and the learning dynamics necessitate a new training strategy.
AI.method: Gradient-based analysis of quality-degraded samples to understand training dynamics; design of TQD that adjusts sampling distribution across timesteps: skew towards higher timesteps for motion-rich data and towards lower timesteps for high-visual-quality data; train with imbalanced data, evaluate across data scenarios.
AI.result: TQD achieves performance surpassing conventional training with better data when trained on imbalanced data; also improves performance when trained on high-quality data, showing broad effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: TQD demonstrates that carefully aligning data sampling with the model's learning dynamics can overcome the need for perfectly balanced data, offering a path to more data-efficient video generation models.

title: BFMD: A Full-Match Badminton Dense Dataset for Dense Shot Captioning
summary: Understanding tactical dynamics in badminton requires analyzing entire matches rather than isolated clips. However, existing badminton datasets mainly focus on short clips or task-specific annotations and rarely provide full-match data with dense multimodal annotations. This limitation makes it difficult to generate accurate shot captions and perform match-level analysis. To address this limitation, we introduce the first Badminton Full Match Dense (BFMD) dataset, with 19 broadcast matches (including both singles and doubles) covering over 20 hours of play, comprising 1,687 rallies and 16,751 hit events, each annotated with a shot caption. The dataset provides hierarchical annotations including match segments, rally events, and dense rally-level multimodal annotations such as shot types, shuttle trajectories, player pose keypoints, and shot captions. We develop a VideoMAE-based multimodal captioning framework with a Semantic Feedback mechanism that leverages shot semantics to guide caption generation and improve semantic consistency. Experimental results demonstrate that multimodal modeling and semantic feedback improve shot caption quality over RGB-only baselines. We further showcase the potential of BFMD by analyzing the temporal evolution of tactical patterns across full matches.

AI.tldr: First dense Badminton Full Match Dense (BFMD) dataset and a VideoMAE-based multimodal captioning framework with Semantic Feedback; enables full-match tactical analysis with 19 broadcast matches (~20 hours), 1,687 rallies, 16,751 hit events, plus hierarchical annotations (match segments, rallies, shot types, shuttle trajectories, pose keypoints, captions). Shows that multimodal modeling with semantic feedback improves shot-caption quality over RGB-only baselines and enables analysis of tactical dynamics over entire matches.
AI.motivation: Existing badminton datasets focus on short clips or task-specific annotations and lack dense full-match multimodal annotations, hindering accurate shot captioning and match-level tactical analysis.
AI.method: Assemble BFMD dataset with 19 broadcast matches, dense rally-level multimodal annotations (shot types, shuttle trajectories, player pose keypoints, shot captions); develop a VideoMAE-based multimodal captioning framework enhanced by a Semantic Feedback mechanism that uses shot semantics to guide caption generation.
AI.result: Multimodal modeling plus semantic feedback improves shot-caption quality over RGB-only baselines; demonstrates potential to analyze temporal evolution of tactical patterns across full matches.
AI.conclusion: BFMD provides a comprehensive resource for full-match tactical analysis in badminton and validates the benefit of semantic feedback in multimodal captioning.

title: PAWS: Perception of Articulation in the Wild at Scale from Egocentric Videos
summary: Articulation perception aims to recover the motion and structure of articulated objects (e.g., drawers and cupboards), and is fundamental to 3D scene understanding in robotics, simulation, and animation. Existing learning-based methods rely heavily on supervised training with high-quality 3D data and manual annotations, limiting scalability and diversity. To address this limitation, we propose PAWS, a method that directly extracts object articulations from hand-object interactions in large-scale in-the-wild egocentric videos. We evaluate our method on the public data sets, including HD-EPIC and Arti4D data sets, achieving significant improvements over baselines. We further demonstrate that the extracted articulations benefit downstream tasks, including fine-tuning 3D articulation prediction models and enabling robot manipulation. See the project website at https://aaltoml.github.io/PAWS/.

AI.tldr: PAWS learns 3D articulations by mining hand-object interactions from large-scale in-the-wild egocentric videos, reducing reliance on labeled 3D data and improving downstream robotics tasks.
AI.motivation: Current supervised methods require high-quality 3D data and annotations, which limits scalability and diversity.
AI.method: Direct extraction of object articulations from hand-object interactions in egocentric videos; evaluated on public datasets (HD-EPIC, Arti4D); demonstrates downstream benefits and cites a project website.
AI.result: Significant improvements over baselines on the evaluated datasets; articulations aid downstream tasks such as fine-tuning 3D articulation models and enabling robot manipulation.
AI.conclusion: PAWS demonstrates that meaningful articulations can be recovered from naturalistic video without heavy labeling, enabling scalable 3D understanding and practical robotics applications.

title: Towards Comprehensive Real-Time Scene Understanding in Ophthalmic Surgery through Multimodal Image Fusion
summary:
Purpose: The integration of multimodal imaging into operating rooms paves the way for comprehensive surgical scene understanding. In ophthalmic surgery, by now, two complementary imaging modalities are available: operating microscope (OPMI) imaging and real-time intraoperative optical coherence tomography (iOCT). This first work toward temporal OPMI and iOCT feature fusion demonstrates the potential of multimodal image processing for multi-head prediction through the example of precise instrument tracking in vitreoretinal surgery.
  Methods: We propose a multimodal, temporal, real-time capable network architecture to perform joint instrument detection, keypoint localization, and tool-tissue distance estimation.
