Our network design integrates a cross-attention fusion module to merge OPMI and iOCT image features, which are efficiently extracted via a YoloNAS and a CNN encoder, respectively. Furthermore, a region-based recurrent module leverages temporal coherence.
  Results: Our experiments demonstrate reliable instrument localization and keypoint detection (95.79% mAP50) and show that the incorporation of iOCT significantly improves tool-tissue distance estimation, while achieving real-time processing rates of 22.5 ms per frame. Especially for close distances to the retina (below 1 mm), the distance estimation accuracy improved from 284 $μm$ (OPMI only) to 33 $μm$ (multimodal).
  Conclusion: Feature fusion of multimodal imaging can enhance multi-task prediction accuracy compared to single-modality processing and real-time processing performance can be achieved through tailored network design. While our results demonstrate the potential of multi-modal processing for image-guided vitreoretinal surgery, they also underline key challenges that motivate future research toward more reliable, consistent, and comprehensive surgical scene understanding.

AI.tldr: Multimodal temporal fusion of OPMI and iOCT for real-time instrument detection, keypoint localization, and distance estimation in vitreoretinal surgery, achieving high accuracy and improved distance measurement with real-time performance.
AI.motivation: In ophthalmic surgery, combining OPMI and iOCT can provide richer surgical scene understanding. Real-time, multi-task predictions are challenging due to modality differences and temporal dynamics.
AI.method: A multimodal, temporal network with cross-attention fusion between OPMI features (YoloNAS detector) and iOCT features (CNN encoder); region-based recurrent module to exploit temporal coherence; tasks: instrument detection, keypoint localization, distance estimation; real-time inference at 22.5 ms/frame.
AI.result: Instrument detection accuracy mAP50 95.79%; distance estimation improves when fusing modalities, especially within 1 mm retina, improving from 284 μm to 33 μm; real-time performance.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal fusion improves multi-task prediction vs single modality; demonstrates potential for image-guided vitreoretinal surgery; challenges remain for reliability, consistency, and comprehensive scene understanding; future work to address these.

title: GeoHeight-Bench: Towards Height-Aware Multimodal Reasoning in Remote Sensing
summary: Current Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) in Earth Observation typically neglect the critical "vertical" dimension, limiting their reasoning capabilities in complex remote sensing geometries and disaster scenarios where physical spatial structures often outweigh planar visual textures. To bridge this gap, we introduce a comprehensive evaluation framework dedicated to height-aware remote sensing understanding. First, to overcome the severe scarcity of annotated data, we develop a scalable, VLM-driven data generation pipeline utilizing systematic prompt engineering and metadata extraction. This pipeline constructs two complementary benchmarks: GeoHeight-Bench for relative height analysis, and a more challenging GeoHeight-Bench+ for holistic, terrain-aware reasoning. Furthermore, to validate the necessity of height perception, we propose GeoHeightChat, the first height-aware remote sensing LMM baseline. Serving as a strong proof of concept, our baseline demonstrates that synergizing visual semantics with implicitly injected height geometric features effectively mitigates the "vertical blind spot", successfully unlocking a new paradigm of interactive height reasoning in existing optical models.

AI.tldr: We propose a height-aware evaluation framework for Earth Observation LMMs, introducing two synthetic benchmarks (GeoHeight-Bench and GeoHeight-Bench+) generated via a VLM-driven data pipeline, and a height-aware baseline GeoHeightChat that integrates height geometry to mitigate the vertical blind spot.
AI.motivation: Vertical spatial information is essential for accurate remote sensing reasoning but is largely neglected by current large multimodal models. The scarcity of annotated height data motivates a scalable data-generation approach and a dedicated evaluation framework to enable height-aware reasoning.
AI.method: A scalable data-generation pipeline using systematic prompt engineering and metadata extraction to create two benchmarks: GeoHeight-Bench for relative height analysis and GeoHeight-Bench+ for holistic, terrain-aware reasoning. Introduces GeoHeightChat as the first height-aware remote sensing LMM baseline, designed to fuse visual semantics with implicitly injected height geometric features.
AI.result: Experimental evidence from the baseline indicates that combining visual semantics with height geometry mitigates the vertical blind spot and enables interactive height reasoning in optical LMMs.
AI.conclusion: Height-aware reasoning is essential for Earth observation LMMs. The proposed benchmarks and baseline establish a new paradigm for height-aware remote sensing, enabling improved reasoning in vertical dimensions.

title: UNIC: Neural Garment Deformation Field for Real-time Clothed Character Animation
summary: Simulating physically realistic garment deformations is an essential task for virtual immersive experience, which is often achieved by physics simulation methods. However, these methods are typically time-consuming, computationally demanding, and require costly hardware, which is not suitable for real-time applications. Recent learning-based methods tried to resolve this problem by training graph neural networks to learn the garment deformation on vertices, which, however, fail to capture the intricate deformation of complex garment meshes with complex topologies. In this paper, we introduce a novel neural deformation field-based method, named UNIC, to animate the garments of an avatar in real time, given the motion sequences. Our key idea is to learn the instance-specific neural deformation field to animate the garment meshes. Such an instance-specific learning scheme does not require UNIC to generalize to new garments but only to new motion sequences, which greatly reduces the difficulty in training and improves the deformation quality. Moreover, neural deformation fields map the 3D points to their deformation offsets, which not only avoids handling topologies of the complex garments but also injects a natural smoothness constraint in the deformation learning. Extensive experiments have been conducted on various kinds of garment meshes to demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of UNIC over baseline methods, making it potentially practical and useful in real-world interactive applications like video games.

AI.tldr: UNIC introduces an instance-specific neural deformation field to animate garment meshes in real time, avoiding complex topology handling and generalizing to new motions rather than new garments, yielding efficient and high-quality deformations for interactive applications.
AI.motivation: Physics-based garment simulation is slow and resource-intensive; existing graph-based learning methods struggle with complex garment topologies and real-time constraints; there is a demand for fast, realistic garment animation suitable for real-time applications like games.
AI.method: Train an instance-specific neural deformation field that maps 3D points to deformation offsets for a given garment and motion sequence. This approach avoids dealing with garment topology and imposes natural smoothness in deformations. The model is specialized to a particular garment but can generalize to unseen motion sequences for that garment; it does not require generalization across different garments. Inference is designed for real-time performance.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation on various garment meshes demonstrates improved deformation quality and efficiency over baselines, indicating practical viability for real-time interactive applications such as video games.
AI.conclusion: Instance-specific neural deformation fields offer a practical and effective route for real-time garment animation by simplifying topology handling, enhancing deformation quality, and reducing training complexity, with strong potential for deployment in interactive settings.

title: LanteRn: Latent Visual Structured Reasoning
summary: While language reasoning models excel in many tasks, visual reasoning remains challenging for current large multimodal models (LMMs). As a result, most LMMs default to verbalizing perceptual content into text, a strong limitation for tasks requiring fine-grained spatial and visual understanding. While recent approaches take steps toward thinking with images by invoking tools or generating intermediate images, they either rely on external modules, or incur unnecessary computation by reasoning directly in pixel space. In this paper, we introduce LanteRn, a framework that enables LMMs to interleave language with compact latent visual representations, allowing visual reasoning to occur directly in latent space. LanteRn augments a vision-language transformer with the ability to generate and attend to continuous visual thought embeddings during inference. We train the model in two stages: supervised fine-tuning to ground visual features in latent states, followed by reinforcement learning to align latent reasoning with task-level utility. We evaluate LanteRn on three perception-centric benchmarks (VisCoT, V*, and Blink), observing consistent improvements in visual grounding and fine-grained reasoning. These results suggest that internal latent representations provide a promising direction for more efficient multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: LanteRn enables large multimodal models to perform visual reasoning in a compact latent space by augmenting vision-language transformers with continuous visual thought embeddings, trained in two stages (supervised grounding then reinforcement learning). It yields consistent improvements on perception-oriented benchmarks, suggesting latent representations enable more efficient multimodal reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current LMMs often verbalize perceptual content, limiting fine-grained spatial/visual understanding and efficiency. Latent, continuous visual representations offer a way to perform reasoning without relying on external tools or pixel-space processing.
AI.method: Extend a vision-language transformer with the ability to generate and attend to continuous latent visual thought embeddings. Train in two stages: (1) supervised fine-tuning to ground visual features in latent states, (2) reinforcement learning to align latent reasoning with task utility.
AI.result: On three perception-centric benchmarks (VisCoT, V*, Blink), LanteRn shows consistent improvements in visual grounding and fine-grained reasoning, indicating latent representations support more effective visual reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Internal latent representations are a promising direction for more efficient multimodal reasoning in LMMs, potentially reducing reliance on external modules and computation in pixel space.

title: Designing Any Imaging System from Natural Language: Agent-Constrained Composition over a Finite Primitive Basis
comment: 28 pages, 7 figures, 8 tables, includes Supplementary Information (sections S1-S6)
summary:
Designing a computational imaging system -- selecting operators, setting parameters, validating consistency -- requires weeks of specialist effort per modality, creating an expertise bottleneck that excludes the
  broader scientific community from prototyping imaging instruments. We introduce spec.md, a structured specification format, and three autonomous agents -- Plan, Judge, and Execute -- that translate a one-sentence
  natural-language description into a validated forward model with bounded reconstruction error. A design-to-real error theorem decomposes total reconstruction error into five independently bounded terms, each linked
  to a corrective action. On 6 real-data modalities spanning all 5 carrier families, the automated pipeline matches expert-library quality (98.1 +/- 4.2%). Ten novel designs -- composing primitives into chains from 3D
  to 5D -- demonstrate compositional reach beyond any single-modality tool.

AI.tldr: Introduces spec.md and three autonomous agents (Plan, Judge, Execute) to turn a one-sentence NL description into a validated forward model with bounded reconstruction error. Achieves expert-level performance across 6 modalities (98.1% ± 4.2%) and demonstrates compositional design (3D–5D) beyond any single tool.
AI.motivation: The design of computational imaging systems currently requires weeks of specialist effort per modality, creating an expertise bottleneck that hinders broad prototyping and reproducibility. Automating specification, planning, evaluation, and execution aims to democratize instrument design and accelerate innovation by turning NL descriptions into validated forward models with provable error bounds.
AI.method: Introduce spec.md as a structured specification format. Develop three autonomous agents—Plan, Judge, and Execute—that translate a one-sentence natural-language description into a forward model. Propose a design-to-real error theorem that decomposes total reconstruction error into five independently bounded terms, each linked to a corrective action. Validate on six real-data modalities across all five carrier families; the automated pipeline matches expert-library quality (98.1 ± 4.2%). Demonstrate compositional reach by creating ten novel designs that chain primitives from 3D to 5D.
AI.result: A fully automated design-to-imaging-pipeline that outputs validated forward models with bounded reconstruction error, achieving expert-quality performance and demonstrating compositional scalability across modalities.
AI.conclusion: The framework substantially lowers the engineering burden for computational imaging design, provides actionable error-bounding guidance via the theorem, and generalizes to multi-modality settings. Future work could broaden primitive libraries, enhance robustness, and explore deployment in real-world instrument prototyping.

title: LEMMA: Laplacian pyramids for Efficient Marine SeMAntic Segmentation
comment: Accepted at the MaCVi Workshop, CVPR 2026
summary:
Semantic segmentation in marine environments is crucial for the autonomous navigation of unmanned surface vessels (USVs) and coastal Earth Observation events such as oil spills. However, existing methods, often relying on deep CNNs and transformer-based architectures, face challenges in deployment due to their high computational costs and resource-intensive nature. These limitations hinder the practicality of real-time, low-cost applications in real-world marine settings.
  To address this, we propose LEMMA, a lightweight semantic segmentation model designed specifically for accurate remote sensing segmentation under resource constraints. The proposed architecture leverages Laplacian Pyramids to enhance edge recognition, a critical component for effective feature extraction in complex marine environments for disaster response, environmental surveillance, and coastal monitoring. By integrating edge information early in the feature extraction process, LEMMA eliminates the need for computationally expensive feature map computations in deeper network layers, drastically reducing model size, complexity and inference time. LEMMA demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across datasets captured from diverse platforms while reducing trainable parameters and computational requirements by up to 71x, GFLOPs by up to 88.5\%, and inference time by up to 84.65\%, as compared to existing models. Experimental results highlight its effectiveness and real-world applicability, including 93.42\% IoU on the Oil Spill dataset and 98.97\% mIoU on Mastr1325.

AI.tldr: LEmma is a lightweight semantic segmentation model for marine remote sensing that uses Laplacian pyramids to encode edge information early, reducing computation and model size while achieving strong IoU on oil spill and Mastr1325 datasets.
AI.motivation: Need for real-time, low-resource semantic segmentation in marine settings due to the heavy computational cost of CNNs/transformers and the importance of accurate edge delineation for coastal/ocean scenes.
AI.method: Incorporates Laplacian pyramids to enhance edge recognition and injects edge information early in feature extraction, reducing reliance on deep feature maps and thus cutting parameters, FLOPs, and inference time.
AI.result: Claims state-of-the-art performance on diverse marine datasets with substantial efficiency gains: up to 71x fewer trainable parameters, up to 88.5% fewer GFLOPs, and up to 84.65% faster inference; IoU 93.42% on Oil Spill and 98.97% mIoU on Mastr1325.
AI.conclusion: LEMM A demonstrates practical viability for real-world, resource-constrained marine semantic segmentation, addressing deployment barriers and enabling real-time monitoring, though independent validation and analysis of generalization and edge fidelity are warranted.

title: Wan-Weaver: Interleaved Multi-modal Generation via Decoupled Training
summary: Recent unified models have made unprecedented progress in both understanding and generation. However, while most of them accept multi-modal inputs, they typically produce only single-modality outputs. This challenge of producing interleaved content is mainly due to training data scarcity and the difficulty of modeling long-range cross-modal context. To address this issue, we decompose interleaved generation into textual planning and visual consistency modeling, and introduce a framework consisting of a planner and a visualizer. The planner produces dense textual descriptions for visual content, while the visualizer synthesizes images accordingly. Under this guidance, we construct large-scale textual-proxy interleaved data (where visual content is represented in text) to train the planner, and curate reference-guided image data to train the visualizer. These designs give rise to Wan-Weaver, which exhibits emergent interleaved generation ability with long-range textual coherence and visual consistency. Meanwhile, the integration of diverse understanding and generation data into planner training enables Wan-Weaver to achieve robust task reasoning and generation proficiency. To assess the model's capability in interleaved generation, we further construct a benchmark that spans a wide range of use cases across multiple dimensions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that, even without access to any real interleaved data, Wan-Weaver achieves superior performance over existing methods.

AI.tldr: Wan-Weaver introduces a planner+visualizer framework for interleaved text-and-image generation, achieving emergent interleaved capabilities without real interleaved data by training on textual proxies and reference-guided images; it outperforms existing methods in a new multi-use benchmark.
AI.motivation: To overcome the scarcity of interleaved (text+image) training data and the difficulty of modeling long-range cross-modal context required for producing coherent interleaved outputs.
AI.method: A two-module system where the planner generates dense textual descriptions of visual content and the visualizer renders images accordingly. The planner is trained on textual-proxy interleaved data (visual content represented in text), while the visualizer is trained on reference-guided image data. A new benchmark across diverse use cases evaluates interleaved generation capabilities.
AI.result: Wan-Weaver demonstrates emergent interleaved generation with strong long-range textual coherence and visual consistency. It benefits from diverse understanding and generation data during planner training, achieving robust task reasoning and generation and surpassing existing methods even without real interleaved data.
AI.conclusion: The planner-visualizer decomposition enables effective interleaved generation by separating planning and visual synthesis, enabling training on proxy data and references to achieve coherent, multi-modal outputs. This approach provides a scalable pathway to interleaved generation and a benchmark for evaluating such systems.

title: TRACE: Object Motion Editing in Videos with First-Frame Trajectory Guidance
summary: We study object motion path editing in videos, where the goal is to alter a target object's trajectory while preserving the original scene content. Unlike prior video editing methods that primarily manipulate appearance or rely on point-track-based trajectory control, which is often challenging for users to provide during inference, especially in videos with camera motion, we offer a practical, easy-to-use approach to controllable object-centric motion editing. We present Trace, a framework that enables users to design the desired trajectory in a single anchor frame and then synthesizes a temporally consistent edited video. Our approach addresses this task with a two-stage pipeline: a cross-view motion transformation module that maps first-frame path design to frame-aligned box trajectories under camera motion, and a motion-conditioned video re-synthesis module that follows these trajectories to regenerate the object while preserving the remaining content of the input video. Experiments on diverse real-world videos show that our method produces more coherent, realistic, and controllable motion edits than recent image-to-video and video-to-video methods.

AI.tldr: Trace is a two-stage framework for easy, frame-to-video object motion editing: user draws a trajectory on a single anchor frame; a cross-view transformation maps it to frame-aligned trajectories under camera motion; a motion-conditioned re-synthesis module renders a temporally coherent edited video, outperforming image-to-video and video-to-video baselines.
AI.motivation: There is a need to edit object motion in videos with camera motion in a user-friendly manner. Prior methods focus on appearance edits or rely on point tracks, which are error-prone and hard for users to provide during inference.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) cross-view motion transformation that maps the first-frame path design to frame-aligned box trajectories under camera motion, and (2) a motion-conditioned video re-synthesis module that follows these trajectories to regenerate the object while preserving the rest of the scene content.
AI.result: Experiments on diverse real-world videos show Trace produces more coherent, realistic, and controllable motion edits than recent image-to-video and video-to-video methods.
AI.conclusion: Trace enables practical, trajectory-based object-centric motion editing in videos, offering easy user control via a single anchor frame and temporal consistency under camera motion, with improved realism and controllability over baselines.

title: Seeing to Ground: Visual Attention for Hallucination-Resilient MDLLMs
summary: Multimodal Diffusion Large Language Models (MDLLMs) achieve high-concurrency generation through parallel masked decoding, yet the architectures remain prone to multimodal hallucinations. This structural vulnerability stems from an algorithmic flaw: the decoder ranks candidate tokens based on textual likelihood without verifying localized visual support. We establish that this language-only ranking induces an objective mismatch, where language probability mass acts as a misspecified proxy for the intended multimodal task. Consequently, we reinterpret hallucination as a localized optimization error, a phenomenon where the decoder exploits language shortcuts to maximize a proxy score at the expense of visual grounding. To address this objective mismatch, we introduce VISAGE, a training-free decoding framework that calibrates the objective at inference time. VISAGE estimates the proxy discrepancy by quantifying the spatial entropy of cross-attention distributions. By enforcing a localization consensus across attention heads, the method penalizes spatially uniform distributions and re-ranks token commitments to favor visually grounded outcomes. We provide an analytical stability guarantee establishing that VISAGE maintains a bounded objective loss under estimation error. Evaluations across hallucination-sensitive and general-purpose benchmarks demonstrate the robustness of the framework, yielding relative gains of 8.59% on MMMU-val and 7.75% on HallusionBench.

AI.tldr: VISAGE is a training-free decoding framework that mitigates multimodal hallucinations in multimodal diffusion LLMs by aligning token ranking with visual grounding via cross-attention entropy and head-consensus, achieving notable gains.
AI.motivation: MDLLMs' decoding relies on language likelihood, neglecting localized visual support, causing objective mismatch and multimodal hallucinations.
AI.method: At inference, VISAGE estimates the proxy discrepancy by measuring spatial entropy of cross-attention distributions and enforces localization consensus across attention heads to penalize spatially uniform patterns, re-ranking token commitments without retraining.
AI.result: Analytical stability guarantee; robust improvements on MMMU-val (8.59% relative) and HallusionBench (7.75% relative).
AI.conclusion: A principled, training-free inference-time calibration that improves visual grounding and restricts objective loss under estimation error, enhancing hallucination robustness across benchmarks.

title: AnyHand: A Large-Scale Synthetic Dataset for RGB(-D) Hand Pose Estimation
summary: We present AnyHand, a large-scale synthetic dataset designed to advance the state of the art in 3D hand pose estimation from both RGB-only and RGB-D inputs. While recent works with foundation approaches have shown that an increase in the quantity and diversity of training data can markedly improve performance and robustness in hand pose estimation, existing real-world-collected datasets on this task are limited in coverage, and prior synthetic datasets rarely provide occlusions, arm details, and aligned depth together at scale. To address this bottleneck, our AnyHand contains 2.5M single-hand and 4.1M hand-object interaction RGB-D images, with rich geometric annotations. In the RGB-only setting, we show that extending the original training sets of existing baselines with AnyHand yields significant gains on multiple benchmarks (FreiHAND and HO-3D), even when keeping the architecture and training scheme fixed. More impressively, the model trained with AnyHand shows stronger generalization to the out-of-domain HO-Cap dataset, without any fine-tuning. We also contribute a lightweight depth fusion module that can be easily integrated into existing RGB-based models. Trained with AnyHand, the resulting RGB-D model achieves superior performance on the HO-3D benchmark, showing the benefits of depth integration and the effectiveness of our synthetic data.

AI.tldr: AnyHand is a large-scale synthetic RGB-D hand dataset (2.5M single-hand + 4.1M hand-object images) with rich annotations, plus a lightweight depth fusion module; it improves RGB-only and RGB-D 3D hand pose estimation and generalizes to out-of-domain data without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Real hand pose datasets are limited in coverage and existing synthetic datasets lack occlusions, detailed arms, and aligned depth at scale, hindering robustness and generalization of hand pose models.
AI.method: Create AnyHand dataset with extensive RGB-D images and geometric annotations; validate by augmenting RGB baselines (FreiHAND, HO-3D) with AnyHand, introduce a depth fusion module for RGB-based models, and demonstrate superior RGB-D performance on HO-3D, plus strong HO-Cap generalization without fine-tuning.
AI.result: RGB-only models show significant gains on FreiHAND and HO-3D when trained with AnyHand; strong generalization to HO-Cap without fine-tuning; RGB-D model trained with AnyHand achieves superior HO-3D performance, highlighting benefits of depth integration and synthetic data.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic, large-scale, richly annotated RGB-D hand data, together with a lightweight depth fusion component, can substantially boost 3D hand pose estimation across RGB-only and RGB-D pipelines and improve cross-domain generalization.

title: BizGenEval: A Systematic Benchmark for Commercial Visual Content Generation
summary: Recent advances in image generation models have expanded their applications beyond aesthetic imagery toward practical visual content creation. However, existing benchmarks mainly focus on natural image synthesis and fail to systematically evaluate models under the structured and multi-constraint requirements of real-world commercial design tasks. In this work, we introduce BizGenEval, a systematic benchmark for commercial visual content generation. The benchmark spans five representative document types: slides, charts, webpages, posters, and scientific figures, and evaluates four key capability dimensions: text rendering, layout control, attribute binding, and knowledge-based reasoning, forming 20 diverse evaluation tasks. BizGenEval contains 400 carefully curated prompts and 8000 human-verified checklist questions to rigorously assess whether generated images satisfy complex visual and semantic constraints. We conduct large-scale benchmarking on 26 popular image generation systems, including state-of-the-art commercial APIs and leading open-source models. The results reveal substantial capability gaps between current generative models and the requirements of professional visual content creation. We hope BizGenEval serves as a standardized benchmark for real-world commercial visual content generation.

AI.tldr: BizGenEval is a new benchmark for commercial visual content generation, evaluating text rendering, layout control, attribute binding, and knowledge-based reasoning across slides, charts, webpages, posters, and scientific figures with 400 prompts and 8000 checklist questions over 26 models.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on natural image synthesis and do not capture the structured, multi-constraint demands of professional commercial design tasks; a standardized, task-driven benchmark is needed to assess real-world production capabilities.
AI.method: Introduce BizGenEval with five document types, four capability dimensions, totaling 20 tasks. Curate 400 prompts and 8000 human-verified checklist questions. Benchmark 26 image generation systems (commercial APIs and open-source models) to evaluate compliance with complex visual/semantic constraints.
AI.result: The benchmark reveals substantial capability gaps between current generative models and professional design requirements, across multiple tasks and document types.
AI.conclusion: BizGenEval provides a standardized framework for evaluating real-world commercial visual content generation and can guide model improvement and selection for professional use.

title: SlotVTG: Object-Centric Adapter for Generalizable Video Temporal Grounding
comment: Accepted to GRAIL-V workshop at CVPR 2026
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown strong performance on Video Temporal Grounding (VTG). However, their coarse recognition capabilities are insufficient for fine-grained temporal understanding, making task-specific fine-tuning indispensable. This fine-tuning causes models to memorize dataset-specific shortcuts rather than faithfully grounding in the actual visual content, leading to poor Out-of-Domain (OOD) generalization. Object-centric learning offers a promising remedy by decomposing scenes into entity-level representations, but existing approaches require re-running the entire multi-stage training pipeline from scratch. We propose SlotVTG, a framework that steers MLLMs toward object-centric, input-grounded visual reasoning at minimal cost. SlotVTG introduces a lightweight slot adapter that decomposes visual tokens into abstract slots via slot attention and reconstructs the original sequence, where objectness priors from a self-supervised vision model encourage semantically coherent slot formation. Cross-domain evaluation on standard VTG benchmarks demonstrates that our approach significantly improves OOD robustness while maintaining competitive In-Domain (ID) performance with minimal overhead.

AI.tldr: SlotVTG adds a lightweight slot adapter for MLLMs that yields object-centric, input-grounded visual reasoning for Video Temporal Grounding (VTG) with minimal overhead, improving OOD generalization while preserving ID performance.
AI.motivation: MLLMs excel at coarse VTG but lack fine-grained temporal understanding; fine-tuning can cause dataset shortcuts and poor out-of-domain generalization.
AI.method: Introduce a lightweight slot adapter that uses slot attention to decompose visual tokens into abstract slots and reconstructs the original sequence. Objectness priors from a self-supervised vision model guide semantically coherent slot formation, enabling object-centric representations with minimal training cost.
AI.result: Cross-domain VTG benchmarks show significantly improved out-of-domain robustness with competitive in-domain performance and low computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: Object-centric, input-grounded reasoning via slot-based representations effectively improves VTG OOD generalization for MLLMs at a small additional cost.

title: PSDesigner: Automated Graphic Design with a Human-Like Creative Workflow
summary: Graphic design is a creative and innovative process that plays a crucial role in applications such as e-commerce and advertising. However, developing an automated design system that can faithfully translate user intentions into editable design files remains an open challenge. Although recent studies have leveraged powerful text-to-image models and MLLMs to assist graphic design, they typically simplify professional workflows, resulting in limited flexibility and intuitiveness. To address these limitations, we propose PSDesigner, an automated graphic design system that emulates the creative workflow of human designers. Building upon multiple specialized components, PSDesigner collects theme-related assets based on user instructions, and autonomously infers and executes tool calls to manipulate design files, such as integrating new assets or refining inferior elements. To endow the system with strong tool-use capabilities, we construct a design dataset, CreativePSD, which contains a large amount of high-quality PSD design files annotated with operation traces across a wide range of design scenarios and artistic styles, enabling models to learn expert design procedures. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PSDesigner outperforms existing methods across diverse graphic design tasks, empowering non-specialists to conveniently create production-quality designs.

AI.tldr: PSDesigner automates graphic design by emulating human design workflows, collecting assets, and autonomously executing tool calls to edit PSD files, trained on CreativePSD; it outperforms baselines and enables non-experts to produce production-quality designs.
AI.motivation: Current text-to-image and MLLM-based design aids often omit professional workflows or editable outputs, limiting flexibility and intuitive control. There is a need for an end-to-end system that translates user intent into editable design files and learns from real design procedures.
AI.method: A modular system that gathers theme-related assets from user instructions and autonomously infers and executes tool calls to manipulate PSDs (e.g., integrating assets, refining elements). It is trained/validated on CreativePSD, a PSD-centered dataset annotated with operation traces across diverse design scenarios and styles to teach expert design procedures.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show PSDesigner outperforms existing approaches across diverse graphic design tasks, enabling non-specialists to create production-quality designs.
AI.conclusion: PSDesigner demonstrates strong tool-use and end-to-end workflow automation for graphic design, bridging user intent and editable PSD manipulation, and the CreativePSD dataset enables learning of professional procedures.

title: MegaFlow: Zero-Shot Large Displacement Optical Flow
summary: Accurate estimation of large displacement optical flow remains a critical challenge. Existing methods typically rely on iterative local search or/and domain-specific fine-tuning, which severely limits their performance in large displacement and zero-shot generalization scenarios. To overcome this, we introduce MegaFlow, a simple yet powerful model for zero-shot large displacement optical flow. Rather than relying on highly complex, task-specific architectural designs, MegaFlow adapts powerful pre-trained vision priors to produce temporally consistent motion fields. In particular, we formulate flow estimation as a global matching problem by leveraging pre-trained global Vision Transformer features, which naturally capture large displacements. This is followed by a few lightweight iterative refinements to further improve the sub-pixel accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MegaFlow achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance across multiple optical flow benchmarks. Moreover, our model also delivers highly competitive zero-shot performance on long-range point tracking benchmarks, demonstrating its robust transferability and suggesting a unified paradigm for generalizable motion estimation. Our project page is at: https://kristen-z.github.io/projects/megaflow.

AI.tldr: MegaFlow provides zero-shot large-displacement optical flow by formulating flow estimation as global matching using pre-trained Vision Transformer features, followed by lightweight iterative refinements; it achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance on optical flow benchmarks and strong zero-shot results on long-range point tracking.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of traditional optical flow methods that rely on iterative local search or task-specific fine-tuning, which hinder large-displacement handling and zero-shot generalization; leverage powerful pre-trained priors to enable robust, generalizable motion estimation.
AI.method: Formulate flow estimation as a global matching problem using pre-trained global Vision Transformer features to capture large displacements, then apply a small number of lightweight iterative refinements to achieve sub-pixel accuracy.
AI.result: MegaFlow achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot performance across multiple optical flow benchmarks and shows competitive zero-shot results on long-range point tracking benchmarks, indicating strong transferability and suggesting a unified paradigm for generalizable motion estimation.
AI.conclusion: A simple, priors-driven approach that treats motion estimation as global matching with ViT features can achieve robust, zero-shot generalization for large-displacement optical flow and related motion tasks, reducing the need for task-specific architectural complexity.

title: RefAlign: Representation Alignment for Reference-to-Video Generation
summary: Reference-to-video (R2V) generation is a controllable video synthesis paradigm that constrains the generation process using both text prompts and reference images, enabling applications such as personalized advertising and virtual try-on. In practice, existing R2V methods typically introduce additional high-level semantic or cross-modal features alongside the VAE latent representation of the reference image and jointly feed them into the diffusion Transformer (DiT). These auxiliary representations provide semantic guidance and act as implicit alignment signals, which can partially alleviate pixel-level information leakage in the VAE latent space. However, they may still struggle to address copy--paste artifacts and multi-subject confusion caused by modality mismatch across heterogeneous encoder features. In this paper, we propose RefAlign, a representation alignment framework that explicitly aligns DiT reference-branch features to the semantic space of a visual foundation model (VFM). The core of RefAlign is a reference alignment loss that pulls the reference features and VFM features of the same subject closer to improve identity consistency, while pushing apart the corresponding features of different subjects to enhance semantic discriminability. This simple yet effective strategy is applied only during training, incurring no inference-time overhead, and achieves a better balance between text controllability and reference fidelity. Extensive experiments on the OpenS2V-Eval benchmark demonstrate that RefAlign outperforms current state-of-the-art methods in TotalScore, validating the effectiveness of explicit reference alignment for R2V tasks.

AI.tldr: RefAlign trains a representation alignment loss to align DiT's reference features with a visual foundation model (VFM) semantic space, improving identity fidelity and semantic discriminability in R2V without extra inference cost, achieving higher TotalScore on OpenS2V-Eval.
AI.motivation: R2V methods struggle with copy-paste artifacts and multi-subject confusion due to modality mismatch across heterogeneous encoder features; explicit alignment to a semantic space can improve identity consistency and discriminability.
AI.method: Introduce RefAlign: a training-only reference alignment loss that pulls together DiT reference-branch features and VFM features of the same subject while pushing apart features of different subjects, achieving better identity consistency and semantic discriminability without increasing inference cost.
AI.result: On the OpenS2V-Eval benchmark, RefAlign achieves higher TotalScore than current state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of explicit reference alignment for R2V.
AI.conclusion: Explicit alignment between reference representations and a visual foundation model during training improves R2V fidelity and controllability with no inference-time overhead, suggesting a promising direction for cross-modal alignment in diffusion-based video generation.

title: MuRF: Unlocking the Multi-Scale Potential of Vision Foundation Models
summary: Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) have become the cornerstone of modern computer vision, offering robust representations across a wide array of tasks. While recent advances allow these models to handle varying input sizes during training, inference typically remains restricted to a single, fixed scale. This prevalent single-scale paradigm overlooks a fundamental property of visual perception: varying resolutions offer complementary inductive biases, where low-resolution views excel at global semantic recognition and high-resolution views are essential for fine-grained refinement. In this work, we propose Multi-Resolution Fusion (MuRF), a simple yet universally effective strategy to harness this synergy at inference time. Instead of relying on a single view, MuRF constructs a unified representation by processing an image at multiple resolutions through a frozen VFM and fusing the resulting features. The universality of MuRF is its most compelling attribute. It is not tied to a specific architecture, serving instead as a fundamental, training-free enhancement to visual representation. We empirically validate this by applying MuRF to a broad spectrum of critical computer vision tasks across multiple distinct VFM families - primarily DINOv2, but also demonstrating successful generalization to contrastive models like SigLIP2.

AI.tldr: MuRF enables inference-time, multi-resolution fusion for frozen Vision Foundation Models, creating a unified representation by processing an image at several resolutions and fusing the features. It is training-free and architecture-agnostic, demonstrated across multiple VFMs (notably DINOv2 and SigLIP2) and across diverse CV tasks.
AI.motivation: Single-scale inference in VFMs overlooks complementary biases that different resolutions provide (global semantic vs. fine-grained details). A simple, training-free method to exploit multi-scale information at test time can improve representation quality broadly.
AI.method: For each input image, run a frozen VFM at multiple resolutions, extract features, and fuse them into a unified representation at inference time. The fusion mechanism is training-free and architecture-agnostic, enabling universal applicability.
AI.result: Empirical validation across a broad spectrum of CV tasks and multiple VFM families (primarily DINOv2; also generalizes to SigLIP2). MuRF consistently improves representations without retraining, demonstrating strong generalization across architectures.
AI.conclusion: MuRF is a simple, universal, training-free enhancement for visual representations that leverages multi-resolution information at inference. It broadens the applicability of VFMs by enabling effective cross-resolution fusion without architectural changes or additional training.

title: Less Gaussians, Texture More: 4K Feed-Forward Textured Splatting
summary: Existing feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting methods predict pixel-aligned primitives, leading to a quadratic growth in primitive count as resolution increases. This fundamentally limits their scalability, making high-resolution synthesis such as 4K intractable. We introduce LGTM (Less Gaussians, Texture More), a feed-forward framework that overcomes this resolution scaling barrier. By predicting compact Gaussian primitives coupled with per-primitive textures, LGTM decouples geometric complexity from rendering resolution. This approach enables high-fidelity 4K novel view synthesis without per-scene optimization, a capability previously out of reach for feed-forward methods, all while using significantly fewer Gaussian primitives. Project page: https://yxlao.github.io/lgtm/

AI.tldr: LGTM introduces a scalable, feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting framework using compact Gaussians with per-primitive textures, decoupling geometry from rendering resolution to enable 4K novel-view synthesis without per-scene optimization.
AI.motivation: Current feed-forward 3D Gaussian Splatting methods require pixel-aligned primitives and exhibit quadratic growth of primitive count with image resolution, making high-res rendering intractable; a method is needed to overcome the resolution scaling bottleneck and avoid per-scene optimization.
AI.method: Predict compact Gaussian primitives and per-primitive textures in a feed-forward network; decouple geometric complexity from output resolution; render at target resolution with fewer primitives; operates without per-scene optimization.
AI.result: Demonstrates 4K novel-view synthesis with high fidelity using significantly fewer Gaussians than previous methods; achieves scalability in a feed-forward pipeline; project page provided.
AI.conclusion: LGTM validates that decoupling geometry from rendering resolution via compact Gaussians and per-primitive textures enables scalable, high-resolution NVS in a purely feed-forward framework, addressing a key scalability bottleneck.

title: ShotStream: Streaming Multi-Shot Video Generation for Interactive Storytelling
summary: Multi-shot video generation is crucial for long narrative storytelling, yet current bidirectional architectures suffer from limited interactivity and high latency. We propose ShotStream, a novel causal multi-shot architecture that enables interactive storytelling and efficient on-the-fly frame generation. By reformulating the task as next-shot generation conditioned on historical context, ShotStream allows users to dynamically instruct ongoing narratives via streaming prompts. We achieve this by first fine-tuning a text-to-video model into a bidirectional next-shot generator, which is then distilled into a causal student via Distribution Matching Distillation. To overcome the challenges of inter-shot consistency and error accumulation inherent in autoregressive generation, we introduce two key innovations. First, a dual-cache memory mechanism preserves visual coherence: a global context cache retains conditional frames for inter-shot consistency, while a local context cache holds generated frames within the current shot for intra-shot consistency. And a RoPE discontinuity indicator is employed to explicitly distinguish the two caches to eliminate ambiguity. Second, to mitigate error accumulation, we propose a two-stage distillation strategy. This begins with intra-shot self-forcing conditioned on ground-truth historical shots and progressively extends to inter-shot self-forcing using self-generated histories, effectively bridging the train-test gap. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ShotStream generates coherent multi-shot videos with sub-second latency, achieving 16 FPS on a single GPU. It matches or exceeds the quality of slower bidirectional models, paving the way for real-time interactive storytelling. Training and inference code, as well as the models, are available on our

AI.tldr: ShotStream is a causal, real-time multi-shot video generation model enabling interactive storytelling. It uses dual-cache memory and Distribution Matching Distillation to match bidirectional models' quality at ~16 FPS on a single GPU, enabling on-the-fly frame generation.
AI.motivation: Current bidirectional video generation models offer inter-shot coherence but suffer from high latency and limited interactivity for long narratives. A causal, real-time approach is needed to support interactive, streaming storytelling while maintaining coherence across shots.
AI.method: 1) Reformulate as next-shot generation conditioned on historical context. 2) Fine-tune a text-to-video model into a bidirectional next-shot generator. 3) Distill into a causal student via Distribution Matching Distillation. 4) Introduce a dual-cache memory: a global context cache for inter-shot coherence and a local context cache for intra-shot coherence, with a RoPE discontinuity indicator to distinguish caches. 5) Two-stage distillation: intra-shot self-forcing on ground-truth history, then inter-shot self-forcing with self-generated histories to bridge train-test gaps.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show coherent multi-shot videos with sub-second latency, achieving 16 FPS on a single GPU and matching or exceeding the quality of slower bidirectional models. This enables real-time interactive storytelling; code and models are released in a public repository.
AI.conclusion: ShotStream offers a practical, fast solution for interactive, long-form video storytelling by combining dual-cache memory and a two-stage distillation strategy to mitigate error accumulation, bringing real-time generation closer to deployment.

title: Amplified Patch-Level Differential Privacy for Free via Random Cropping
summary: Random cropping is one of the most common data augmentation techniques in computer vision, yet the role of its inherent randomness in training differentially private machine learning models has thus far gone unexplored. We observe that when sensitive content in an image is spatially localized, such as a face or license plate, random cropping can probabilistically exclude that content from the model's input. This introduces a third source of stochasticity in differentially private training with stochastic gradient descent, in addition to gradient noise and minibatch sampling. This additional randomness amplifies differential privacy without requiring changes to model architecture or training procedure. We formalize this effect by introducing a patch-level neighboring relation for vision data and deriving tight privacy bounds for differentially private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) when combined with random cropping. Our analysis quantifies the patch inclusion probability and shows how it composes with minibatch sampling to yield a lower effective sampling rate. Empirically, we validate that patch-level amplification improves the privacy-utility trade-off across multiple segmentation architectures and datasets. Our results demonstrate that aligning privacy accounting with domain structure and additional existing sources of randomness can yield stronger guarantees at no additional cost.

AI.tldr: Random cropping adds a third source of stochasticity to DP-SGD by randomly excluding sensitive content, leading to amplified privacy guarantees without changing training. A patch-level neighboring relation is formalized to derive tight DP bounds when combined with random cropping, showing reduced effective sampling rate and improved privacy-utility trade-offs empirically across segmentation tasks.
AI.motivation: Address unexplored stochasticity in DP-SGD due to spatially localized sensitive content in images (e.g., faces, license plates) and to quantify its impact on privacy guarantees.
AI.method: Introduce a patch-level neighboring relation for vision data and derive tight DP bounds for DP-SGD when coupled with random cropping. Compute patch inclusion probabilities, show how they compose with minibatch sampling to reduce effective sampling rate, and validate empirically on multiple segmentation architectures/datasets.
AI.result: Theoretical privacy amplification via patch-level inclusion probabilities, yielding a lower effective sampling rate; empirical improvements in privacy-utility trade-offs across architectures/datasets; privacy accounting aligns with domain structure and existing randomness.
AI.conclusion: Aligning privacy accounting with domain structure and extra randomness (random cropping) yields stronger DP guarantees at no additional training cost, suggesting broader applicability to domain-specific augmentations.

title: Can MLLMs Read Students' Minds? Unpacking Multimodal Error Analysis in Handwritten Math
comment: Accepted by the 27th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED'26)
summary: Assessing student handwritten scratchwork is crucial for personalized educational feedback but presents unique challenges due to diverse handwriting, complex layouts, and varied problem-solving approaches. Existing educational NLP primarily focuses on textual responses and neglects the complexity and multimodality inherent in authentic handwritten scratchwork. Current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) excel at visual reasoning but typically adopt an "examinee perspective", prioritizing generating correct answers rather than diagnosing student errors. To bridge these gaps, we introduce ScratchMath, a novel benchmark specifically designed for explaining and classifying errors in authentic handwritten mathematics scratchwork. Our dataset comprises 1,720 mathematics samples from Chinese primary and middle school students, supporting two key tasks: Error Cause Explanation (ECE) and Error Cause Classification (ECC), with seven defined error types. The dataset is meticulously annotated through rigorous human-machine collaborative approaches involving multiple stages of expert labeling, review, and verification. We systematically evaluate 16 leading MLLMs on ScratchMath, revealing significant performance gaps relative to human experts, especially in visual recognition and logical reasoning. Proprietary models notably outperform open-source models, with large reasoning models showing strong potential for error explanation. All evaluation data and frameworks are publicly available to facilitate further research.

AI.tldr: ScratchMath introduces a handwriting-based error analysis benchmark for mathematics; it reveals gaps between multimodal LLMs and expert human error explanations across 1,720 Chinese student samples using two tasks and seven error types.
AI.motivation: Current educational NLP emphasizes textual answers and neglects authentic handwritten scratchwork, which is multimodal and prone to diverse error patterns. A benchmark is needed to study error explanation/classification and improve MLLMs in educational diagnostics.
AI.method: Collect 1,720 authentic handwritten math scratchwork samples from Chinese primary/middle school students. Provide two tasks: Error Cause Explanation (ECE) and Error Cause Classification (ECC) with seven error types. Use rigorous human-machine collaboration with multi-stage labeling, review, and verification. Evaluate 16 leading multimodal LLMs, analyze performance gaps relative to experts, and compare proprietary vs open-source models. Publicly release evaluation data and frameworks.
AI.result: Significant performance gaps remain between MLLMs and human experts, especially in visual recognition and logical reasoning tasks. Proprietary models outperform open-source ones, with large reasoning models showing promise for error explanation. The benchmark and evaluation framework enable reproducible research and benchmarking for educational error analysis.
AI.conclusion: ScratchMath fills a gap in multimodal educational AI by focusing on error explanation and classification for handwritten work. It highlights the need for stronger multimodal comprehension and reasoning in LLMs and provides a valuable resource for ongoing improvement and benchmarking in educational diagnostics.

title: Intern-S1-Pro: Scientific Multimodal Foundation Model at Trillion Scale
summary: We introduce Intern-S1-Pro, the first one-trillion-parameter scientific multimodal foundation model. Scaling to this unprecedented size, the model delivers a comprehensive enhancement across both general and scientific domains. Beyond stronger reasoning and image-text understanding capabilities, its intelligence is augmented with advanced agent capabilities. Simultaneously, its scientific expertise has been vastly expanded to master over 100 specialized tasks across critical science fields, including chemistry, materials, life sciences, and earth sciences. Achieving this massive scale is made possible by the robust infrastructure support of XTuner and LMDeploy, which facilitates highly efficient Reinforcement Learning (RL) training at the 1-trillion parameter level while ensuring strict precision consistency between training and inference. By seamlessly integrating these advancements, Intern-S1-Pro further fortifies the fusion of general and specialized intelligence, working as a Specializable Generalist, demonstrating its position in the top tier of open-source models for general capabilities, while outperforming proprietary models in the depth of specialized scientific tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces Intern-S1-Pro, a 1T-parameter scientific multimodal foundation model that blends broad general intelligence with extensive scientific capabilities, backed by XTuner/LMDeploy for efficient trillion-parameter RL training; positioned as a top open-source generalist with deep science proficiency.
AI.motivation: Scale to 1T parameters to unify general reasoning and broad scientific expertise, enabling advanced agent capabilities and wide-ranging reasoning across chemistry, materials, life and earth sciences.
AI.method: Scale architecture to 1T parameters; integrate XTuner and LMDeploy for efficient RL training with high precision consistency between training and inference; train to master 100+ specialized science tasks; design as a Specializable Generalist combining generalist and specialist capabilities.
AI.result: Claims strong improvements across general and scientific domains; surpasses proprietary models in depth of specialized tasks; open-source tier; robust infrastructure enables efficient training at trillion scale.
AI.conclusion: Intern-S1-Pro demonstrates the feasibility and advantage of a trillion-parameter open-source multimodal model that fuses general and specialized intelligence, setting a new benchmark for scalable scientific AI.
